```
I am the so called "Contract" involved with gvwmoore and paulr.  If anyone
wants to know
the "other side of the story", email me.  I always hear the same old story.
Everyone's a
good worker or too busy.  But, when 6 months go by and there is nothing more
done
than a few lines of code, I need better results than that.  Especially when
there is money
paid up front.  If you can't handle the load, then don't take it on.
Dan Mentzer
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Unfeasibly large COBOL programmer salaries

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1999-02-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<79ib2g$j7s$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<36af3b9e.0@news1.mweb.co.za> <36b16be4.814098@nntp.ix.netcom.com> <78u6dp$vt$1@juliana.sprynet.com> <MWJs2.1351$Xl5.1376067@news1.mia> <790ku1$rnb@lotho.delphi.com> <79514t$bpt@dfw-ixnews8.ix.netcom.com> <795j42$gp@lotho.delphi.com> <36bc8a5a.0@news.desupernet.net>`

```
It sounds like a story that has been heard many times before
by anybody with a lot of experience.  Unfortunately, estimating
work load is a black art. I have found, over the years, that I can
do so for myself, and for somebody that I have worked with for
at least a year, but otherwise it is a guess.

When there is design work involved, it is even worse.  Nothing
can be estimated without a clear and complete specification. If
the job is to create such a document, then I have always quoted
that as a separate job, and quoted a fixed price and time. I then
encourage the customer to get at least three quotations based
on the specification.  If they are not willing to do that, then I will
not take the contract.

>wants to know

>good worker or too busy.  But, when 6 months go by and there is nothing
more
>done
>than a few lines of code, I need better results than that.  Especially when
…[46 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Unfeasibly large COBOL programmer salaries

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** paulr@bix.com (paulr)
- **Date:** 1999-02-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<79j7hi$j9f@lotho.delphi.com>`
- **References:** `<36af3b9e.0@news1.mweb.co.za> <36b16be4.814098@nntp.ix.netcom.com> <78u6dp$vt$1@juliana.sprynet.com> <MWJs2.1351$Xl5.1376067@news1.mia> <790ku1$rnb@lotho.delphi.com> <79514t$bpt@dfw-ixnews8.ix.netcom.com> <795j42$gp@lotho.delphi.com> <36bc8a5a.0@news.desupernet.net> <79ib2g$j7s$1@news.igs.net>`

```
Oh boy - one royal f*** up and I am not going to be able to 
sleep the rest of my life.  To be fair, a lot happened, as in
my fulltime job moved to Baltimore, but my wife wouldn't go.
A good three months of my email, which was forward from all 
over to my work email box, got trashed by an overzealous
system admin, and I never saw it. 

Indeed, I didn't even know that it had arrived. 

And on top of all that, my *outbound* email went through 
work as well. Guess what happened to it? 

I was in ignorance of just about all the normal email
communciations I was used to receiving. (Interestingly enough,
internal company email still got delivered to me - fancy that...)

Add two contracts on top of changing a full time job, 
communting between Lancaster PA, Richmond Va, and Baltimore Md
all week long, a daughter needing back surgery, and of 
course, little things like flying around the country interviewing,
and the next thing you know - zappp. 

This is still a F*U* on my part, and there is no 
justification for it that I can think of. Reasons, but not 
justifications. 

On the other paw, my other half and the accountant in the family 
tells me she mailed back checks to everyone involved, and I see
them recorded here in the accounting system. That cost me well 
over a grand. Not to mention the cost of an additional AccuCOBOL
compiler.            

If anyone didn't get them let me know and (via email to paulr@bix.com)
and I'll handcarry the darn things to you. In the evening though, since
right now I have a large CICS project on my hands, and am spending
well over 100 hours per week on that. In fact, I am supposed to 
be doing that right now.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ It's estimation, I know.

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com>
- **Date:** 1999-02-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36BF066A.FCE752A5@NOSPAMhome.com>`
- **References:** `<36af3b9e.0@news1.mweb.co.za> <36b16be4.814098@nntp.ix.netcom.com> <78u6dp$vt$1@juliana.sprynet.com> <MWJs2.1351$Xl5.1376067@news1.mia> <790ku1$rnb@lotho.delphi.com> <79514t$bpt@dfw-ixnews8.ix.netcom.com> <795j42$gp@lotho.delphi.com> <36bc8a5a.0@news.desupernet.net> <79ib2g$j7s$1@news.igs.net>`

```
Donald Tees wrote:
> 
> It sounds like a story that has been heard many times before
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> at least a year, but otherwise it is a guess.

What programmers/analysts and companies need to do is to require
programmers & analysts to estimate for every single job, putting down
results & updates in a spread sheet.  Then the companies should require
that the programmers review their estimates but NOT TURN THEIR REVIEWS
in to their bosses.  Those spread sheets are there only to increase
their skills in estimating, not to effect their reviews.

Of course, as soon as we get a project twice as large as the largest
project we have done in the past, we assume it will take twice as long. 
We all have a tendency to grossly underestimate like that!

But why don't we have even our rookie programmers learn & practice the
skill of estimating?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: It's estimation, I know.

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** "Jerry Peacock" <bismail@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 1999-02-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<79o4ok$131$1@ash.prod.itd.earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<36af3b9e.0@news1.mweb.co.za> <36b16be4.814098@nntp.ix.netcom.com> <78u6dp$vt$1@juliana.sprynet.com> <MWJs2.1351$Xl5.1376067@news1.mia> <790ku1$rnb@lotho.delphi.com> <79514t$bpt@dfw-ixnews8.ix.netcom.com> <795j42$gp@lotho.delphi.com> <36bc8a5a.0@news.desupernet.net> <79ib2g$j7s$1@news.igs.net> <36BF066A.FCE752A5@NOSPAMhome.com>`

```

Howard Brazee wrote in message <36BF066A.FCE752A5@NOSPAMhome.com>...
>Donald Tees wrote:
>>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>


Reminds me of a book called "SystemAntics" full of pithy gems
of wisdom such as:

1. A large system designed from scratch will not work and
cannot be made to work.
2. Large systems that do work are invariably found to have
evolved from smaller systems that worked.
2a. A small system that works can be developed by copying
a manual system.
3. The system fights back - pushing on the system does not help.
4. People in systems do not do what the system says they
do (a "Payroll Clerk" is not a clerk and has very little to do with
the payroll).
5. A system under development that is late will be made even
later by adding more resources to its development.
6. Systems fail in ways that often undetectable for long periods,
if ever.
7. A fail-safe system often fails by failing to fail-safe.
8. Introducing fail-safe methodology always introduces more
things that can go wrong - and they often do.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: It's estimation, I know.

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1999-02-08T00:00:00

- **Message-ID:** `<79nhgg$n44$1@news.igs.net>`

```
I agree.  Estimating skills are important to every programmer. Your
remark regarding the non-linearity is right on, but there is another
factor that most people miss as well.  Doubling the workers does
*not* half the time ... a point that managers cannot seem to
understand.  I have had projects where I have said "Six weeks alone,
four weeks with one other, six weeks with three, and eight weeks with
more than three ..."

Howard Brazee wrote in message <36BF066A.FCE752A5@NOSPAMhome.com>...
>Donald Tees wrote:
>>
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
>skill of estimating?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: It's estimation, I know.

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com>

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36BF4E33.10896B01@NOSPAMhome.com>`
- **References:** `<36af3b9e.0@news1.mweb.co.za> <36b16be4.814098@nntp.ix.netcom.com> <78u6dp$vt$1@juliana.sprynet.com> <MWJs2.1351$Xl5.1376067@news1.mia> <790ku1$rnb@lotho.delphi.com> <79514t$bpt@dfw-ixnews8.ix.netcom.com> <795j42$gp@lotho.delphi.com> <36bc8a5a.0@news.desupernet.net> <79ib2g$j7s$1@news.igs.net> <36BF066A.FCE752A5@NOSPAMhome.com> <79nhgg$n44$1@news.igs.net>`

```
Donald Tees wrote:
> 
> I agree.  Estimating skills are important to every programmer. Your
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> more than three ..."

Which is why the estimating should not stop with programming and
analysis.  Managers need to keep honing their skills also.  But to add
to your example, quite often management wants to add the extra bodies
only after the project is late.  Extra bodies after the project is late
slows the project down much more than extra bodies early in the process!
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: It's estimation, I know.

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1999-02-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<paEv2.2842$wj1.5704436@news2.mia>`
- **References:** `<36af3b9e.0@news1.mweb.co.za> <36b16be4.814098@nntp.ix.netcom.com> <78u6dp$vt$1@juliana.sprynet.com> <MWJs2.1351$Xl5.1376067@news1.mia> <790ku1$rnb@lotho.delphi.com> <79514t$bpt@dfw-ixnews8.ix.netcom.com> <795j42$gp@lotho.delphi.com> <36bc8a5a.0@news.desupernet.net> <79ib2g$j7s$1@news.igs.net> <36BF066A.FCE752A5@NOSPAMhome.com>`

```
Howard Brazee wrote:
>
>What programmers/analysts and companies need to do is to require
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>We all have a tendency to grossly underestimate like that!

Nothing wrong with your suggestion, except that it won't work.  Not
saying that there aren't estimating skills, just that there are too
many variables to ever be accurate.  You acknowledge the problem with
estimating large projects.  On small projects you can get zapped by a
bad bug or two.  How many experienced programmers here have not had a
subtle bug which took them three or four days to find and fix?  That
would kill you on a two week project!

>But why don't we have even our rookie programmers learn & practice the
>skill of estimating?

Because there is only one way that works: make your estimate so large
that you could not possibly go over.  Then hope and pray that it was
big enough. :-)  Whenever possible, I prefer to give my clients a very
rough estimate and do the job by the hour.  That way I don't mind when
they ask for additional features or changes, and they get the system
for exactly what it cost to do it.  Fair all around, they just have to
trust you.  Once you build a reputation for rock-solid honesty it gets
a lot easier. :-)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: It's estimation, I know.

_(thread depth: 12)_

- **From:** Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>
- **Date:** 1999-02-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36C0234D.E8C0813F@zip.com.au>`
- **References:** `<36af3b9e.0@news1.mweb.co.za> <36b16be4.814098@nntp.ix.netcom.com> <78u6dp$vt$1@juliana.sprynet.com> <MWJs2.1351$Xl5.1376067@news1.mia> <790ku1$rnb@lotho.delphi.com> <79514t$bpt@dfw-ixnews8.ix.netcom.com> <795j42$gp@lotho.delphi.com> <36bc8a5a.0@news.desupernet.net> <79ib2g$j7s$1@news.igs.net> <36BF066A.FCE752A5@NOSPAMhome.com> <paEv2.2842$wj1.5704436@news2.mia> <36C00A00.D4F648D2@conexion.com.py>`

```
In order to do an accurate estimate you must have accurate information.
The bottom line is that unless you are in you own subject domain with a
customer that you know well with systems that you know well you just
cannot do this.

If you look at a realistic quote you can say that the project will take
between 3 and 9 months.  If you say 6 months then you are wrong, as the
details become clear then the qoute will go up or down.  Once you have
generated the functional specification from the user requirements you
are in a better position to refine the quote.  We have used a month
doing the analysis and we need between 10 and 14 weeks to complete the
project.  And the story goes on.

The problem is that people expect a fixed price quote.  In this case
give them the high figure with the true estimates and let them figure it
out.  Going into to much precision to early can be a fatal mistake.

There are many things that affect the outcome of a project.  If you look
at adding people DO IT EARLY.  Added them as an after thought slows
people down and they often do not repay the education cost.

Micro manage.  If you break down the project into small chunks then you
can accurately see whether there is a problem with the estimate or just
a problem.  If the tasks cannot be handled quickly by developers then
you may be in for surprises.

Do not allow 50% complete to stay on the schedule.  Ensure that a task
is fully complete before proceding to the next.  90% of the time is
spent on the first 90% and then another 90% is spent on the last 10%.

Just some ideas.   This is really worth investigating in it's own right.

Ken
```

```
Ken Foskey wrote:
>
>Y2K insurance premiums are 25% of contracting rates here in Aus.  Y2K no

Whew!  I write a clause in my contracts which limits my liability to
correcting my code, and specifically excludes consequential damage.
Anything else, don't touch!
```

```
In article <MWJs2.1351$Xl5.1376067@news1.mia>,
  "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net> wrote:
> darnold02@sprynet.com wrote:
> >and i thought my company was bad charging $80 per hour programming fees
…[34 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Isn't that what insurance is for?

Any contractor giving away more that 10% to his/her contracting firm with out
any benefits, is a fool.

The contracting firm is nothing more than an agent.  And agent by all rights
only deserve 10%.  If they make any more than that there is another phase we

-----------== Posted via Deja News, The Discussion Network ==----------
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Unfeasibly large COBOL programmer salaries

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1999-02-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<%0Ct2.2536$Xl5.2900975@news1.mia>`
- **References:** `<36af3b9e.0@news1.mweb.co.za> <36b16be4.814098@nntp.ix.netcom.com> <78u6dp$vt$1@juliana.sprynet.com> <MWJs2.1351$Xl5.1376067@news1.mia> <7957df$jiv$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
DOUG-PARTCH@WORLDNET.ATT.NET wrote:
>
>Isn't that what insurance is for?

Duh!  Surprise!  Insurance costs money.  Where do you think the money
comes from, if the contractor pays it all to the programmer?  The
insurance alone for that kind of coverage would probably be more than
10%.  Insurance companies aren't stupid.

>Any contractor giving away more that 10% to his/her contracting firm
>with out any benefits, is a fool.

He isn't *giving* away anything.  He is being *paid* by the contractor.
The contractor is providing him a job.  One assumes he wanted the job to
have accepted it in the first place, no?  Are you saying he was forced
into accepting a job that was unacceptable?

>The contracting firm is nothing more than an agent.  And agent by all
>rights only deserve 10%.  If they make any more than that there is
>another phase we could use (prostitute and pimp).

It depends on the contractor, I suppose.  In my case, the people hiring
me do so because they want me.  They want me because they know I will
make sure their work is done right, no matter what.  The programmers I
hire would have zip chance of getting work there, except working for me.
It is not their name or their reputation the client is paying for.  I do
not advertize.  People come to me because of my reputation.  I do almost
all the client interface, the documentation, the design work, and the
most difficult programming.  I take responsibility that it is done right,
and the programmer simply codes.  In simple terms, I actively manage the
project.  If anybody gets sued, it will be me.  If the programmer screws
up, it will be me that makes it right with the client, out of my pocket
or my time.  That's not being 'an agent' in my book.  I also pay my
programmers what they ask, I don't quibble with them.  I have never once
tried to get a programmer to take less money then he asked for, and have
often paid them better than they asked.  If I can't afford them, I can't
afford them.  But I never ask them to take less than they believe they
are worth.  Yet you would call me a 'prostitute' or 'pimp' just because
I gave programmers jobs they wouldn't have had a prayer of getting
otherwise, paying them what they ask or more, but keep more than 10% of
what the client pays me?  Thanks for such a fair and unbiased appraisal!
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: level numbers 77/78

- **From:** scm@bolo.freeserve.co.uk (Shaun C. Murray)
- **Date:** 1999-02-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36b5e828.538780956@news.freeserve.net>`
- **References:** `<36AD9C41.578CD49B@SPAM-NO.thewatercooler.com> <36AE0CE8.6E38BD9A@home.com> <36AE0DC5.69844F77@home.com> <78s4fe$6tb$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <36B1C317.3780000@ASPMnetdoor.com>`

```
On Fri, 29 Jan 1999 08:17:59 -0600, Warren Porter
<warrenp@ASPMnetdoor.com> wrote:


>  My only occasion to use 78 is to hold a
>count of the number of entries in a table defined later in the code.

Under which compiler. 78 in Micro Focus terminology is a constant like
you'd use #define in C. You couldn't use it 'hold' anything as it's
replaced at compile time.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: level numbers 77/78

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Warren Porter <warrenp@ASPMnetdoor.com>
- **Date:** 1999-02-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36B5F7DD.B07432E2@ASPMnetdoor.com>`
- **References:** `<36AD9C41.578CD49B@SPAM-NO.thewatercooler.com> <36AE0CE8.6E38BD9A@home.com> <36AE0DC5.69844F77@home.com> <78s4fe$6tb$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <36B1C317.3780000@ASPMnetdoor.com> <36b5e828.538780956@news.freeserve.net>`

```
Shaun C. Murray wrote:

> >  My only occasion to use 78 is to hold a
> >count of the number of entries in a table defined later in the code.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> replaced at compile time.

It's AcuCobol85.  Also I use it just like the #define in C since I will never
change the contents of the table or number of entries while the program is
running.  If I add or remove a table entry, I change the value of the 78 level
and in only one place.

78  TABLE-SIZE   VALUE 32.
----
     05 TABLE-ENTRY OCCURS TABLE-SIZE.
----
       PERFORM  VARYING SUB1 FROM 1 BY 1 UNTIL SUB1 > TABLE-SIZE ... .
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: level numbers 77/78

- **From:** scm@bolo.freeserve.co.uk (Shaun C. Murray)
- **Date:** 1999-02-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36b5e8c8.538940876@news.freeserve.net>`
- **References:** `<36AD9C41.578CD49B@SPAM-NO.thewatercooler.com> <36AE0CE8.6E38BD9A@home.com> <36AE0DC5.69844F77@home.com> <78s4fe$6tb$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <36b36ef5.845666@news3.ibm.net>`

```
On Sat, 30 Jan 1999 20:46:27 GMT, vbandke@ibm.net (Volker Bandke)
wrote:


>
>Yes, always (that is, when the data  item is not subdivided)  If the compiler doesn't make
>a differencebetween  01 and 77, why shouldn't I use 77?  And if the compiler DOES make a
>difference, I definitely SHOULD use 77 -: This implies: USE 77 levels whenever allowed
>

A valid answer but some compilers optimize better with group items and
it's not uncommon for programmers to have to think about the alignment
structure in memory on PC's. Particularly if you are using windows
API's or talking to foreign languages. A group item works better for
this than seperate 01's or 77's. 

You can also group items of a similar size together too for
compactness in memory and speed of access.
```

```
If it is Isam, see if there is a rebuild facility.  That may
have the ability to dump it.

If not, you can read it fairly easily anyway.  The MF
Isam keeps the index in a separate file, so the records
in the data file are fairly simple.  Five minutes with debug
will usually get you there.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: data files micro focus cobol

- **From:** areymond@csi.com (Alain Reymond)
- **Date:** 1999-02-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36c332c8.37714940@news.skynet.be>`
- **References:** `<794vtm$dlm$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <BkLt2.394$VQ5.5850327@news1.i1.net> <F7017u.3u6@news.boeing.com>`

```
In fact, if you have Microsoft Cobol 4.x, you should be able to read
the files. MS Cobol was a revamped MF compiler. Then export the files
to sequential and import it ti your new application.

I asked Fujitsu if they plan to integrate in their FJ Cobol compiler
or PowerCobol a direct access to MF files. The answer was no. I find
it a pitty, because I think that we are many programmers trying to
access MF files in native format from a FJ compiler. It would
certainly broaden the number of possible programmers using FJ
programs. 

Truly,

Alain Reymond
```

<a id="t-a09a2f6cbd"></a>
## Thread: File Limitation — 3 msgs

### File Limitation

- **From:** "stcheong" <stcheong@mbox2.singnet.com.sg>
- **Date:** 1999-02-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<796ado$pm1$1@mawar.singnet.com.sg>`

```
It had been more than a month since I encounter the error code 34,01 in
RM/COBOL-85.
During this period, I have
a.    increase the disk block size from 1024 to 4094 so that the file would
be able to grow bigger.
b.    perform a reorganisation of the old file to the new disk.
c.    I had get my hardware vendor to increase the hard limit of my
ystem( which runs on ATT Unix System V) to 0x70000000 and my soft limit of
the system from 4194304 to 8192000.
d.    when I re-run my Month End Program, I alway stop at 1.07GB. It alway
stopped at the same byte size.
I believe that there are some setting in the unix system that restrict the
size of my file to 1.07GB but my vendor seems to run out of idea on how to
proceed from here.
If I multiple 4194304 * 256, I end up with the byte size 1073741824. This is
where it stopped.
Can some expert out there able to offer some form of fruitful advice?
Thank You.
```

#### ↳ Re: File Limitation

- **From:** "Jeff" <a@a.com>
- **Date:** 1999-02-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<i1St2.2452$fz.8417932@storm.twcol.com>`
- **References:** `<796ado$pm1$1@mawar.singnet.com.sg>`

```

>Can some expert out there able to offer some form of fruitful advice?
>Thank You.


Never used a UNIX system to do COBOL, but here is my uninformed guess.

Under MVS I have run into a similar problem and it was due to the number of
extensions of the file. Under MVS when a file is allocated there is a
primary and a secondary allocation amount. When the file is first allocated
the primary amount is used. When the that primary extension is exhausted,
extension allocations are added to the file in the secondary increment. This
can be done up to 16 times. Thus this may be your problem. There may be a
similar secondary allocation setting on your system.

The block size you mentioned, doesn't seem quite right. Increasing block
size should be dependant on the disk drive unit being used, not simply
increasing it. All disk units I have used had a block size of somewhere
around 27000. I believe you need to determine this before re-sizing your
block size.

If all else fails, run the report to a tape drive then determine the actual
file size. Then copy it back to the disk drive forcing it to an appropriate
block size.

That is just my uninformed opinion. I may be wrong.
```

#### ↳ Re: File Limitation

- **From:** Chris Carroll <ccadmin@idis.com.au>
- **Date:** 1999-02-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36BC53FA.7CCF@idis.com.au>`
- **References:** `<796ado$pm1$1@mawar.singnet.com.sg>`

```
On Unix, 2 Gig is the limit as I recall.
You'll more than likely end up splitting the file up into 2, 4, etc
hunks and have an I/O handler program routine to cater for a huge file.
Why you have a 1 Gig limit I don't know but this I suspect to be a Unix
setup issue as you suggest. Another trick is to try the COMMIT command
during processing, say after every record, 10,000 records or what you
feel is reasonable.    
Personally, I'd give AT&T a rev up and get some support that I'm sure
you're already paying for.
Chris.

stcheong wrote:
> 
> It had been more than a month since I encounter the error code 34,01 in
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
> Thank You.
```

```
Helen
```

```
Stu.
```

```
In message <<793vl6$n23$1@SOLAIR2.EUnet.yu>> "Joca" <jocadj@www.yu> writes:
> 
> Y2K  solution  ???   accept xxx  from date  ; yymmdd -> yyyymmdd ???
> 
> 

         IF ( yy > 90 )
             MOVE 19      TO cc
         ELSE
             MOVE 20      TO cc
             .
```

#### ↳ Re: Honeywell Bull X20-X40

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1999-02-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<xYFt2.2575$Xl5.3020938@news1.mia>`
- **References:** `<793vl6$n23$1@SOLAIR2.EUnet.yu>`

```
Joca wrote:
>Working anywhere ?  AT&T  V3.1  VS/25    with Micro Focus COBOL/2  v1.1
>
>Y2K  solution  ???   accept xxx  from date  ; yymmdd -> yyyymmdd ???

ACCEPT mmddyy FROM DATE.
IF yy < 99
   cc = 20
ELSE
   cc = 19
END IF.

Good for 99 more years.
```

<a id="t-270938ce99"></a>
## Thread: Is there a way? — 8 this month / 32 total

_Thread started [1999-01](1999-01.md#t-270938ce99)._

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Is there a way?

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** cwestbury@giant.intranet.com (Chris Westbury)
- **Date:** 1999-02-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1999Feb1.211555.19497@giant>`
- **References:** `<78nkm0$d0n@dfw-ixnews4.ix.netcom.com> <36b2a5c8.1257782@nntp.ix.netcom.com>`

```
In article <36b2a5c8.1257782@nntp.ix.netcom.com>,
gvwmoore@ix.netcom.com (G Moore) wrote:
>
> I write alot better when I get paid.

I'm afraid you have a misconception here.  It's a common misconception,
but a misconception nonetheless.

You see, there are only twenty-four hours in a day.  Whatever you do on a
regular basis, you get better at.  Whatever you don't do, you get worse
at.

Now, some of the candidates I don't hire say something like, "I'm careful
on the important things.  Why should I be careful on the unimportant
things?"

Well, there are two reasons.

First, you can't tell for sure what's important except in hindsight.  If
you mess up something important because you're too lazy to be careful all
the time, that's a serious problem, the more so because it's so easily
avoidable.

Second, it's hard to be careful and it's easy to be careless.  If you are
careful with something, and it turns out in hindsight not to be important
after all, you might say you wasted that effort.  But you'd be wrong. 
What you did was PRACTICE CAREFULNESS.  That practice made it a little
bit easier to be careful next time, when it might be important.

If you are careless with something, and it turns out in hindsight not to
be important after all, you might say "See?  I told you so!"  But you'd
be wrong.  What you did was PRACTICE CARELESSNESS.  That practice made it
a little bit _harder_ to be careful next time, when it might be
important.

Based on what you have posted in this newsgroup and in comp.lang.c, you
have been practicing carelessness for so long that you are doubtless
quite good at it.  Conversely, I would say that, contrary to your
statement quoted above, you do _not_ write a lot better when you get
paid, because you haven't been practicing carefulness.

You have gotten much excellent advice here from those who know whereof
they speak, and you have ignored it because it doesn't fit your
misconceptions.  Your idea of the way the world "ought" to be is very far
from the way the world is.  You don't seem like a bad fellow; try working
backward from the advice and changing your premises, instead of rejecting
the advice because it conflicts with your premises.


> But then, you probably have sat on your high ivory tower, pulling in
> dollars all day, and don't know what it's like to have zero contacts.

Unfortunately, sentences like this are typical of what you post here.  No
one, and I mean _no_ one, who reads this sentence would _ever_ hire you
to program.

"On your high horse" is a metaphor referring to the arbitrary exercise of
power, but no one in a newsgroup issues orders.  "In your ivory tower" is
a metaphor referring to impractical academics, but most of the posters in
this newsgroup are on the front lines and have given you extremely
practical advice.  You can't sit "in" your high horse or "on" your ivory
tower.  "Pulling in dollars" is an idiom referring to easy money, but I
sweat blood over my programs, and I get paid because they always work. 
Lastly, I certainly do know what it's like to have zero contacts, but
it's irrelevant, because I can program anything in any language on any
machine.

In other words, you need to _demonstrate_ basic competence before anyone
will hire you for anything.  I don't mean experience, I mean competence. 
If you can't or won't write a simple declarative sentence, you certainly
can't write a commercial program that works, and it's no use saying that
you could if you were paid, because you couldn't.


> [...] I have tried every method known to man to work legitimately.

No, you haven't.  Somehow, you missed method number one, "develop
competence and demonstrate it".
```

- **References:** `<78nkm0$d0n@dfw-ixnews4.ix.netcom.com> <JHKr2.534$kA1.5643@news.rdc1.on.wave.home.com> <EsLr2.2431$VB.3684117@news4.mia> <NMLr2.564$kA1.5899@news.rdc1.on.wave.home.com> <FTLr2.2441$VB.3699481@news4.mia> <36afa42b.47947108@netnews> <78sdih$gbj$1@server.cntfl.com> <36b20d9f.8408193@netnews>`

```

Randall Bart wrote in message <36b20d9f.8408193@netnews>...
[...]
>
>...  But I prefer to *write* code which is tediously obvious.
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>
At least "obvious" and "rewrite ... in microcode," were not in the same
paragraph. ;-)

Why is it that, in COBOL, so many solutions have "INPUT-???" and
"OUTPUT-???" ? (I am guilty, as well!)

Since the problem deals with elementary variables, one could visuallize
a COBOL-like instruction, such as:

    SQUEEZE identifier-1 REMOVING identifier-2 CHARACTERS
        FILLING WITH literal-1

The ANS85 CALL then becomes something like:

    CALL "SQUEEZE" USING
        BY REFERENCE  THE-STRING
        BY CONTENT        LENGTH-OF-THE-STRING
                                         REMOVE-THESE
                                         LENGTH-OF-REMOVE-THESE
                                         FILL-CHAR.

This is a general solution for "squeezing" strings. Furthermore, since
this is essentially a library program, virtually all optimization techniques
become available, including less obvious, but faster, solutions in
assembler or microcode.
-------------------------------
Rick Smith
e-mail: < ricksmith@aiservices.com >
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Is there a way?

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1999-02-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36b50dab.1115617552@news1.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<78nkm0$d0n@dfw-ixnews4.ix.netcom.com> <JHKr2.534$kA1.5643@news.rdc1.on.wave.home.com> <36afdd44.775495466@news1.ibm.net> <01be4d68$36e44660$0100007f@vaagshaugen>`

```
On 31 Jan 1999 23:32:28 GMT, "Gunnar Opheim" <gunnar.opheim@eunet.no>
wrote:

Code snipped.
>
>Was this any close to what you had in mind?
>
>Gunnar.

Similar.  There's obviously some waste as the non space data rarely
fills up the maximum input field.  The problem is that the routine
necessary to find out where to stop (or start if going in reverse)
taks as much time as just moving the fields.  A compare of the
remaining characters to determine if one should stop, after each move,
is likewise inefficient.    

The routine that exists in the systems I have (not one I wrote, I
inherited it) uses Tables to redefine the input string and performs a
loop that increments the index values.  No reference modification is
used.   

I'll have to look at the program - the data is constructed from a
called routine - that just might be returning the length - I'll have
to see.  If so, the space squisher could be made more efficient by
knowing when to quit.  But that's about all I can see that you can do
to it to make it better.   In either case the spaces are not moved.  I
suppose one could limit the moves.  

Maybe I'm dense but I don't see where your code killed the embedded
spaces.  I should run it I suppose.

PS.  Here's your code as I compiled it - it doesn't squish out the
spaces!  What am I missing?

       IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
       PROGRAM-ID. TT1.
       DATA DIVISION.
       WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
       01  TEXT-DATA PIC X(80) VALUE "This is the phrase!".
       01  I PIC 9(4) VALUE ZEROS.
       01  J PIC 9(4) VALUE ZEROS.
       01  STRIP-FLAG PIC X VALUE "Y".
           88  STRIP-INITIAL-SPACES VALUE "Y".
       PROCEDURE DIVISION.
       Compress-text.
	   perform varying I from 80 by -1
	       until I < 1
	       or TEXT-DATA(I:1) not = space
	   end-perform
	   perform until I < 1
	     perform varying J from I by -1
		 until J < 1
		 or TEXT-DATA(J:1) = space
	     end-perform
	     if J > 0
	       perform varying I from J by -1
		   until I < 1
		   or TEXT-DATA(I:1) not = space
	       end-perform
	       if I > 0
		 if J > I + 1
		   move TEXT-DATA(J:) to TEXT-DATA(I + 1:)
		 end-if
	       else
		 if STRIP-INITIAL-SPACES
		   move TEXT-DATA(J + 1:) to TEXT-DATA(1:)
		 end-if
	       end-if
	     else
	       move zero to I
	     end-if
	   end-perform
	   DISPLAY TEXT-DATA
	   .

Here's an interesting alternative - working from the front.  It might
be slightly more efficient - only by virtue of not moving a character
at a time.  However, there are a lot of comparisons going on.  In my
testing it's only slightly slower than the main alternative:
      *$CALL
       IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
       PROGRAM-ID. TT2.
       DATA DIVISION.
      * space squisher.
       WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
       01  TEXT-DATA PIC X(80) VALUE "This is the phrase!".
       01  text-out  pic x(80) value spaces.
       01  START-POS PIC 9(2) VALUE ZEROS.
       01  out-pos   pic 9(2) value 1.
       01  MOVE-LENGTH PIC 9(2) VALUE ZEROS.
       01  cntr pic 99 value zeros.
       01  last-type pic x value spaces.
           88  last-spaces value "S".
           88  last-char   value "C".
       01 t-time pic 9(8).
       PROCEDURE DIVISION.
       Compress-text.
	   accept t-time from time
	   display t-time
	   perform 99999 times
	   move spaces to text-out last-type
	   move 1 to out-pos
	   perform varying cntr from 1 by 1 until cntr > 80
	     if text-data (cntr:1) = spaces
		if last-char
		   move text-data (start-pos:move-length) to
			text-out (out-pos:move-length)
			add move-length to out-pos
			move zeros to move-length
		   set last-spaces to true
		end-if
	     else
		if not last-char
		   move cntr to start-pos
		end-if
		set last-char to true
		add 1 to move-length
	     end-if
	   end-perform
	   if last-char
	      move text-data (start-pos:move-length) to
		   text-out (out-pos:move-length)
	   end-if
	   end-perform
	   accept t-time from time
	   display t-time
	   DISPLAY TEXT-out
	   .

Here is the fastest method I have found.  Is there a faster way?

      *$CALL
       IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
       PROGRAM-ID. TT3.
       DATA DIVISION.
      * space squisher.
       WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
       01  TEXT-DATA PIC X(80) VALUE "This is the phrase!".
       01  text-out  pic x(80) value spaces.
       01  PROCESS-SWITCH PIC X VALUE SPACES.
       01  START-POS PIC 9(2) VALUE ZEROS.
       01  out-pos   pic 9(2) value 0.
       01  MOVE-LENGTH PIC 9(2) VALUE ZEROS.
       01  cntr pic 99 value zeros.
       01  last-type pic x value spaces.
           88  last-spaces value "S".
           88  last-char   value "C".
       01  t-time pic 9(8).
       PROCEDURE DIVISION.
       Compress-text.
	   accept t-time from time
	   display t-time
	   perform 99999 times
	     move spaces to text-out
	     move zeros to out-pos
	     perform varying cntr from 1 by 1 until cntr > 80
	       if text-data (cntr:1) not = space
		  add 1 to out-pos
		  move text-data (cntr:1) to text-out (out-pos:1)
	       end-if
	     end-perform
	   end-perform
	   accept t-time from time
	   display t-time
	   display text-out
	   .
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Is there a way?

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Gunnar Opheim" <gunnar.opheim@eunet.no>
- **Date:** 1999-02-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01be4e2a$07f62f40$0100007f@vaagshaugen>`
- **References:** `<78nkm0$d0n@dfw-ixnews4.ix.netcom.com> <JHKr2.534$kA1.5643@news.rdc1.on.wave.home.com> <36afdd44.775495466@news1.ibm.net> <01be4d68$36e44660$0100007f@vaagshaugen> <36b50dab.1115617552@news1.ibm.net>`

```
Thane Hubbell <redsky@ibm.net> wrote in article
<36b50dab.1115617552@news1.ibm.net>...
> PS.  Here's your code as I compiled it - it doesn't squish out the
> spaces!  What am I missing?

I think we attacked two different tasks.

My routine will remove _duplicate_ blanks, leaving a single blank between
non-blank words.  It was designed to compress text lines that contained
unnecessary space (it has been snipped from a production program).  In many
cases there were a need to keep indentation, so the STRIP-INITIAL-SPACES
was made optional.

To remove _all_ intermediate blanks, change

		 if J > I + 1
		   move TEXT-DATA(J:) to TEXT-DATA(I + 1:)
		 end-if
to
		 if J > I
		   move TEXT-DATA(J + 1:) to TEXT-DATA(I + 1:)
		 end-if

in the routine.  It will move the accumulated text forward once for each
string of blanks encountered and blank out the remainder of TEXT-DATA. 
This repeated blanking is the bad thing with this solution.

If you add your timing statements, you could compare performance.  As the
compression is done in-place, and you execute the routine several times for
timing purposes, the string should be initialized between each execution
(and the cost of that deducted from the time).

By the way, this way of timing may be disturbed by interrupts.  You should
run it several times and regard the fastest executions to be correctly
measured.  For the same reason the loop count should not be greater than
necessary.

Your last example is just what I believed to be the fastest algorithm.  The
loop could be made a little bit tighter in assembler, but it should be used
a lot to justify the inconvenience.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Is there a way?

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 1999-02-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<795r2g$pu6$1@server.cntfl.com>`
- **References:** `<78nkm0$d0n@dfw-ixnews4.ix.netcom.com> <JHKr2.534$kA1.5643@news.rdc1.on.wave.home.com> <36afdd44.775495466@news1.ibm.net> <01be4d68$36e44660$0100007f@vaagshaugen> <36b50dab.1115617552@news1.ibm.net>`

```

Thane Hubbell wrote in message <36b50dab.1115617552@news1.ibm.net>...
>
>Here is the fastest method I have found.  Is there a faster way?
[... code snipped]
>
I boosted the performance by about 30 percent by partially unrolling
the loop. The times on my computer are about 1.60s vs 1.21s, or
32 percent faster. YMMV :-)
-----------------
       identification division.
       program-id. tt4.
       data division.
       working-storage section.
       01  text-data pic x(80) value "This is the phrase!".
       01  text-out pic x(80) value all "A".
       01  out-pos pic s99 comp.
       01  in-pos pic s99 comp.
       01  t-time pic 9(8).
       procedure division.
       Compress-text.
           accept t-time from time
           display t-time
           perform 99999 times
             move spaces to text-out
             move 1 to out-pos
             perform varying in-pos from 1 by 1 until in-pos > 80
               if text-data(in-pos:1) not = space
                 move text-data(in-pos:1) to text-out(out-pos:1)
                 add 1 to out-pos
               end-if
             end-perform
           end-perform
           accept t-time from time
           display t-time
           display text-out
      * faster code with partially unrolled loop
           accept t-time from time
           display t-time
           perform 99999 times
             move spaces to text-out
             move 1 to out-pos
             perform varying in-pos from 1 by 4 until in-pos > 80
               if text-data(in-pos:1) not = space
                 move text-data(in-pos:1) to text-out(out-pos:1)
                 add 1 to out-pos
               end-if
               if text-data(in-pos + 1:1) not = space
                 move text-data(in-pos + 1:1) to text-out(out-pos:1)
                 add 1 to out-pos
               end-if
               if text-data(in-pos + 2:1) not = space
                 move text-data(in-pos + 2:1) to text-out(out-pos:1)
                 add 1 to out-pos
               end-if
               if text-data(in-pos + 3:1) not = space
                 move text-data(in-pos + 3:1) to text-out(out-pos:1)
                 add 1 to out-pos
               end-if
             end-perform
           end-perform
           accept t-time from time
           display t-time
           display text-out
           stop run
           .
---------------
Have fun!
-------------------------------
Rick Smith
e-mail: < ricksmith@aiservices.com >
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Is there a way?

- **From:** Christoph Daeppen <christoph.daeppen@ubs.com>
- **Date:** 1999-02-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36B5C845.47BCCADE@ubs.com>`
- **References:** `<78nkm0$d0n@dfw-ixnews4.ix.netcom.com> <JHKr2.534$kA1.5643@news.rdc1.on.wave.home.com> <36afdd44.775495466@news1.ibm.net>`

```
Thane Hubbell wrote:
> 
> What I would love to see is an efficient/fast space squisher.

If you know the upper limit of non-space tokens in your input this sort
of code will be the fastest:

...
       01 
UNSTRING-TBL.                                                                        
           05 UT-TAB                   VALUE
SPACE.                             
              10 UT-ITM                OCCURS upper-limit
TIMES.                         
                 15 U-T                PIC
X(max-token-size).                              
...
           UNSTRING IN-BUFFER            DELIMITED BY ALL
SPACES                
                                         INTO U-T
(01),                         
                                              U-T
(02),                         
                                              U-T
(03),                         
                                              U-T
(04),                         
                                              U-T
(05),                         
                                              U-T (06), 
...
Don't think in C (or algorithms!) when programming COBOL. Think in data
structures! Let the compiler do the work! This code works very well and
VERY FAST (like assembler...) for most of the space-parsing-problems in
a typical COBOL-environment.

CD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: advice needed...

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>
- **Date:** 1999-02-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36B58B46.C64BB127@zip.com.au>`
- **References:** `<78fukt$oen$1@news8.svr.pol.co.uk> <78ovso$gqe$1@news.fsu.edu> <36b32cb4.4114943@news.trxinc.com> <78vbrc$uhb$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <IRIs2.1343$Xl5.1344622@news1.mia>`

```
Judson McClendon wrote:
> 
> WOB@my-dejanews.com wrote:
> >cyndyh@hotmail.com wrote:
..snippage...
>>To appreciate any High-Level Language, one should understand the 
>>basics. Every programmer should have a general understanding of 
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> --

Judson

I am not sure you are recommending this but to learn Assembler first is
just silly.  I agree with a general understanding is great but actually
learning assembler is probably a waste of time unless you own a
propeller cap.

How memory is allocated and pointed to there is no worse language than
Cobol.  It (thank god or designers) deliberately hides these details
from the programmer.  If you really want to understand this stuff then
you cannot beat C or assembler (C being high level structured assembler
in a lot of ways).  I have tried to get the concept of pointers across
to experienced cobol programmers but just get vague looks.

I learnt Pascal first and I think that it was a very good start it is
more readable than C (closer to cobol) and has more low level features
than Cobol (real pointers that work!).  I do not know who is still using
Pascal apart from Delphi (toy systems) and historical applications.

Ken
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: advice needed...

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1999-02-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<kRht2.2392$OS5.2308141@news3.mia>`
- **References:** `<78fukt$oen$1@news8.svr.pol.co.uk> <78ovso$gqe$1@news.fsu.edu> <36b32cb4.4114943@news.trxinc.com> <78vbrc$uhb$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <IRIs2.1343$Xl5.1344622@news1.mia> <36B58B46.C64BB127@zip.com.au>`

```
Ken Foskey wrote:
>
>Judson
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>to experienced cobol programmers but just get vague looks.

Perhaps it is the way you have been presenting the concepts.  A person
must be presented with new concepts in a way that relates to their
current knowledge.  Not everyone can make the conceptual leap to new
ideas presented with little relation to what they already know.  How
could a COBOL programmer not understand a presentation of pointers, if
the instructor said something like "Imagine all of memory REDEFINED as
one table, each entry having a PICTURE X, with OCCURS the number of
bytes in memory.  The subscript into that table would be equivalent to
a pointer."?  How many COBOL programmers would give you a blank stare
at that?  Remember, we're talking low-level assembly-type pointers or
vectors here (pure addresses), not C-type pointers, where a length and
data type are associated.

I don't suggest that every programmer must learn assembly language as
a first language, only that assembly language is a very good starting
place if you want to *really* understand what's going on.  I think that
every 'good' programmer should know at least the basics of assembly.
Donald Knuth says in the preface to "The Art of Computer Programming":
"A person who is more than casually interested in computers should be
well schooled in machine language, since it is a fundamental part of a
computer."  Knuth is one of the real giants in our field.  I have yet
to meet a programmer that I thought was *really* good, who did not have
a very good knowledge of assembly language.  How many top notch race
car drivers do you think there are who do not have intimate knowledge
of how engines and drive trains work?

As far as C being better than COBOL to teach basic concepts, I would
disagree based on significant experience teaching programmers.  C is
far more difficult to master than COBOL, so the 'typical' C programmer
may be more skilled than the 'typical' COBOL programmer.  Understand,
I program mostly in COBOL myself, and am not knocking COBOL programmers.
But all of us know COBOL programmers who simply could not master C.  It
is simply that COBOL is easier to use, and there are those who can be
productive in COBOL, but who could never do so in C.  In my opinion,
that is a strong point in COBOL's favor.  And yes, you must learn about
pointers to use C.  In fact, you *better* know pointers, because you
are going to be spending a lot of time being shot in the foot and head
by pointer problems! :-)  But pointers are not everything, and there is
more to memory allocation and management than pointers (C programmers
often don't realize this).  When a COBOL programmer creates a record,
the process is very close to how you would create a record layout in
assembly or machine language.  C is very weak in this.  Struc and union
are very clumsy and kludgy by comparison.  I have taught a fair number
of programmers over the years, and have never found it difficult to
convey low-level hardware concepts to COBOL programmers.  They are all
used to the concept of subscripts, which is easy to relate to pointers.
Most COBOL programmers are also familiar with record numbers, also easy
to relate to memory pointers.  I can remember classes where some of the
students were what I would call marginal COBOL programmers; comfortable
enough in COBOL, but who would find it difficult, if not impossible,
to master C.  None of them ever had any real trouble understanding low
level hardware concepts, or assembly language.  To me, that is a clear
indication that programming in COBOL had given them a solid perceptual
basis upon which they could assimilate the new concepts.  I have yet to
see that so well displayed in any other language than assembly.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: advice needed...

_(thread depth: 7)_

```
In message <<kRht2.2392$OS5.2308141@news3.mia>> "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net> writes:
> 
> could a COBOL programmer not understand a presentation of pointers, if
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> a pointer."?  How many COBOL programmers would give you a blank stare

Of course a COBOL programmer would already know that a POINTER
was used in the SRTING and UNSTRING and should have a good
idea about that.  Now you want to tell them it is something
different.

> at that?  Remember, we're talking low-level assembly-type pointers or
> vectors here (pure addresses), not C-type pointers, where a length and
> data type are associated.

In the implementations that I use, a C pointer is pure address.
C doesn't know about length from its pointer and the typing is
done entirely by the compiler.  Maybe mainframe C is different.
If you told a C programmer that a C pointer carried size and type
information you would get "a blank stare".


> 
> I don't suggest that every programmer must learn assembly language as
> a first language, only that assembly language is a very good starting
> place if you want to *really* understand what's going on.  I think that

I prefer programmers who 'really understand what's going on' by
knowing what a credit note is and the difference between a
picking slip and a packing slip.

> every 'good' programmer should know at least the basics of assembly.
> Donald Knuth says in the preface to "The Art of Computer Programming":
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> of how engines and drive trains work?

We are not discussing the few 'top notch race car drivers' we
are discussing the average trainee programmer.  How many
ordinary drivers, or even professional ones, such as taxi
or delivery van drivers know what a gudgeon pin is, or 
would care ?  Do you know ? you drive a car don't you ?

> 
> As far as C being better than COBOL to teach basic concepts, I would
> disagree based on significant experience teaching programmers.  C is
> far more difficult to master than COBOL, so the 'typical' C programmer
> may be more skilled than the 'typical' COBOL programmer.  Understand,

I doubt that C is 'more difficult' to master.  It is a lower
level language and needs the programmer to look after some
of the details that a Cobol programmer never needs to even
know about, but Cobol has its quirks too.

> I program mostly in COBOL myself, and am not knocking COBOL programmers.
> But all of us know COBOL programmers who simply could not master C.  It
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> by pointer problems! :-)  But pointers are not everything, and there is

It is entirely possible to write substancial programs in C
without knowing what a pointer is (ie not using them).

> more to memory allocation and management than pointers (C programmers
> often don't realize this).  When a COBOL programmer creates a record,
> the process is very close to how you would create a record layout in
> assembly or machine language.  C is very weak in this.  Struc and union
> are very clumsy and kludgy by comparison.  I have taught a fair number

In what way do you find struc and union 'kludgy' ?
Given that it must work on several different architectures and
your assembler only needs to work on one specific machine
how would you 'improve' struc ?

> of programmers over the years, and have never found it difficult to
> convey low-level hardware concepts to COBOL programmers.  They are all
> used to the concept of subscripts, which is easy to relate to pointers.

Subscripts don't relate to pointers _at_all_.  Subscripts are
relative to the start of the table, the first item is 1, the
step is the item size.

C pointers are completely different, and also different from
memory addresses in assembler.

> Most COBOL programmers are also familiar with record numbers, also easy
> to relate to memory pointers.  I can remember classes where some of the

You must have very strange 'record numbers' - physical sector
addresses ?  Doesn't this confuse them into then thinking
that ram (core?) and disk are both the same sort of memory ?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: advice needed...

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>

```
Richard Plinston wrote:
>
>In the implementations that I use, a C pointer is pure address.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>information you would get "a blank stare".

No doubt about it.  Any C programmer in the world, presented with

int *temp;

would know that there is absolutely *no* type associated with that
pointer.  They would also know that

  temp++;

would *not* increment the pointer by the size of an int variable.
And if you suggested such a thing, you would certainly get "a
blank stare."

Richard, are you living in the same universe as the rest of us? ;-)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: advice needed...

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com>
- **Date:** 1999-02-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36B76FF1.D90D3715@NOSPAMhome.com>`
- **References:** `<kRht2.2392$OS5.2308141@news3.mia> <329932.68331.17001@kcbbs.gen.nz>`

```
> > I don't suggest that every programmer must learn assembly language as
> > a first language, only that assembly language is a very good starting
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> picking slip and a packing slip.

Couldn't agree more.  (adjusted to fit your business model).

If you went to a COBOL shop and looked at the code, you would come away
having a picture of the business model.  Go to a JAVA shop, and you will
have a much more difficult time doing this.
 
> > every 'good' programmer should know at least the basics of assembly.
> > Donald Knuth says in the preface to "The Art of Computer Programming":
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> would care ?  Do you know ? you drive a car don't you ?
 
Another good point.

I worked in a shop which at one time was RPG only.  They had only
recently converted to COBOL.  An earlier programmer knew all the tricks
which can be done with RPG.  His programs were a nightmare to maintain,
and when we needed to change them, we usually re-wrote them in COBOL. 
Don't get me wrong.  I like using a sort utility and Easytrieve to do
certain tasks.  But for enterprise business applications, give me COBOL
every time.  

But make sure the COBOL programmer is primarily a business language
programmer - maintenance and clarity win over elegance and efficiency
virtually whenever they conflict.

> > As far as C being better than COBOL to teach basic concepts, I would
> > disagree based on significant experience teaching programmers.  C is
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> know about, but Cobol has its quirks too.

I think it is easier to learn to code bad C, but harder to master. 
After all, mastering C means mastering all of the libraries which could
be used.  And C tends to be more removed from the business paradigm. 
You are not a master of the language when the product you produce does
not fit well with the business functions and is easy to maintain.
 
> > of programmers over the years, and have never found it difficult to
> > convey low-level hardware concepts to COBOL programmers.  They are all
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> step is the item size.

COBOL relates to business data.  Business functions start counting with
#1.  Users want business solutions, so let's think in their language!

> > Most COBOL programmers are also familiar with record numbers, also easy
> > to relate to memory pointers.  I can remember classes where some of the
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> that ram (core?) and disk are both the same sort of memory ?

Amazing how hard it is to get the average user to understand the
difference between disk space and computer chip RAM.  But they don't
care - and modern operating systems are making this distinction less
important -  just as it is getting to be less important to know the most
efficient way to handle an array in COBOL.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: advice needed...

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 1999-02-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7982kt$sra$1@server.cntfl.com>`
- **References:** `<kRht2.2392$OS5.2308141@news3.mia> <329932.68331.17001@kcbbs.gen.nz>`

```

Richard Plinston wrote in message <329932.68331.17001@kcbbs.gen.nz>...
>In message <<kRht2.2392$OS5.2308141@news3.mia>> "Judson McClendon"
<judmc123@bellsouth.net> writes:
>>
[...]
>> at that?  Remember, we're talking low-level assembly-type pointers or
>> vectors here (pure addresses), not C-type pointers, where a length and
>> data type are associated.
>
>In the implementations that I use, a C pointer is pure address.

Only a pointer to void (void *) is pure address -- all other pointers
have type associated with the pointer (the type has a length).

>C doesn't know about length from its pointer and the typing is
>done entirely by the compiler.

I have no idea how you could possiblly disassociate (or distinguish
between) the C language and its compiler. The compiler (actually,
translator, in the C language standard) must conform to the
language. Typing is done in the language and implemented in
the translator.

>  Maybe mainframe C is different.

Mainframe C cannot be different and still conform to ANSI/ISO.

>If you told a C programmer that a C pointer carried size and type
>information you would get "a blank stare".

You seem to have misinterpreted (or missed) the word "associated"
in Judson's comments. A pointer in C does not carry size and type
but it is associated with a size and type.

>> As far as C being better than COBOL to teach basic concepts, I would
>> disagree based on significant experience teaching programmers.  C is
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>know about, but Cobol has its quirks too.

But it is the "look(ing) after some of the details" that makes C more
difficult to master.
-------------------------------
Rick Smith
e-mail: < ricksmith@aiservices.com >
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: advice needed...

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** Barticus@att.spam.net (Randall Bart)
- **Date:** 1999-02-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36b65337.28163123@netnews>`
- **References:** `<78fukt$oen$1@news8.svr.pol.co.uk> <78ovso$gqe$1@news.fsu.edu> <36b32cb4.4114943@news.trxinc.com> <78vbrc$uhb$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <IRIs2.1343$Xl5.1344622@news1.mia> <36B58B46.C64BB127@zip.com.au> <kRht2.2392$OS5.2308141@news3.mia>`

```
'Twas Mon, 01 Feb 1999 13:19:12 GMT, when "Judson McClendon"
<judmc123@bellsouth.net> illuminated comp.lang.cobol thusly:

>I don't suggest that every programmer must learn assembly language as
>a first language, only that assembly language is a very good starting
>place if you want to *really* understand what's going on.  I think that
>every 'good' programmer should know at least the basics of assembly.

I do think every programmer should have a casual aquaintance with three
(or more) radically different computer architectures.  I think every
programmer should sit through a two hour lecture about this crazy computer
where for every 48-bits of data there's a 4-bit tag and a word only points
at data if its tag *says* it points at data.  They'll leave knowing
nothing about reading an A Series dump, but they'll leave knowing more
about all the stuff going on below the application program.  Most of the
"magic" done by the A Series instruction set is done by all modern
operating systems at some level.  I also think every good programmer needs
to learn one assembly language in enough depth to write code in it.

>Donald Knuth says in the preface to "The Art of Computer Programming":
>"A person who is more than casually interested in computers should be
>well schooled in machine language, since it is a fundamental part of a
>computer."  Knuth is one of the real giants in our field.

Knuth's advice was blatantly obvious when he started programming forty
years ago.  By the 1970s it was sage advice to the programmers of my era.
Now I think the words "well schooled" no longer apply, in large part
because machine language is less fundamental in relation to the operating
system.   

I would want programmers to have a little more depth (four hour lecture)
in the hardware they're working on, but I would rather the programmers
were well schooled in file attributes and system utilities.  Clearly a
Java programmer doesn't need to know about registers and op-codes.  I've
seen no end of programs written in Visual Basic which make very poor use
of the GUI environment.  In fact everywhere I look I see programs written
by programmers who clearly hadn't a clue how to use the tools around them.

At a recent job a very bright programmer would bring CANDE to its knees
with schedule sessions before I showed him SYSTEM/PDIR.  The same job
asked me to fix some QBasic programs.  I would be embarassed to take my 
A Series consultant rates for QBasic work except in six days I fixed five
programs which their previous guy took a month each to write poorly.  One
program would blow up whenever the report exceeded the 64K QBasic string
space.  He was sorting the records in memory.  The programmer may have
spent weeks adding that sort (the user says they waited many months for
that enhancement) only to leave the user with no report at all when it's
too large.  BTW, to all you MS-DOS programmers out there:  There's a
program called SORT.EXE in your \DOS (or \WINDOWS\COMMAND) directory.
Knowledge is power.

It's not that being well schooled in machine language isn't helpful to a
programmer anymore, but there is now such a wealth of software between the
application programmer and that machine language, and a programmer needs
to well schooled in all of that.

>How many top notch race
>car drivers do you think there are who do not have intimate knowledge
>of how engines and drive trains work?

They may even know how to program the computer which controls the timing,
but do they know its machine language?

>As far as C being better than COBOL to teach basic concepts, I would
>disagree based on significant experience teaching programmers.

I've been thinking about this since you mentioned that teaching someone
RPG first ruined them as a programmer.  I always hated RPG.  My computer
school made me take four weeks of RPG.  When they needed bodies to throw
at the RPG compiler that was experience.  RPG was instigated by IBM in
1959 as a language for plug board programmers.  Since the 1920s many
companies had card processing machines which were programmed with
plugboards, which were not very different from the punched card programs
Jacquard wrote a hundred years before that.  RPG was needed as a
programming language for people who already had this plug board mentality.
Unfortuantely it teaches that plug board mentality.  I think my school was
doing its students a disservice teaching us RPG.

I learned Basic first and Fortran second; both are poor choices.
Variables which exist by reference are good when you're writing quick and
dirty programs, but they have no place in serious programming.  A
programmer should learn that nothing may be used unless it is declared.  

I think assembler is a poor first language simply because it's so wide
open.  An apprentice should not start with the most dangerous tools.  C is
similarly flawed because it is so much like assembler.  C is a language
where the left out what features which assembler programmers could do
without.  There are no by reference parameters in C, you pass a pointer by
value instead.  This necessitates learning about pointers and references
to do what should be more basic.  

I find Cobol better than all these, but I think the best choice for
teaching new programmers is Pascal, which is what Wirth designed it for.
```

```
Various plug-board machines had RPG as a language.  The Univac
1004 had 1000 bytes of core memory and a plug board.  The
RPG compiler was loaded - it was about 2 foot square and weighed
30 lbs, and then the RPG card pack was read.

This was before computers implemented RPG in software only.

> 
> I think assembler is a poor first language simply because it's so wide
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> to do what should be more basic.  

I quite agree.  It is like teaching driving by starting with
the distributor and the principles of the carbruettor.  It
was necessary 70 years ago or more when a drive usually
required the engine to be fixed before you could return,
but these days many good drivers wouldn't know how to
open the bonnet.

> 
> I find Cobol better than all these, but I think the best choice for
> teaching new programmers is Pascal, which is what Wirth designed it for.

They probably don't have Pascal on their IBM 360s.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: advice needed...

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 1999-02-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<797ege$1ai$1@server.cntfl.com>`
- **References:** `<78fukt$oen$1@news8.svr.pol.co.uk> <78ovso$gqe$1@news.fsu.edu> <36b32cb4.4114943@news.trxinc.com> <78vbrc$uhb$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <IRIs2.1343$Xl5.1344622@news1.mia> <36B58B46.C64BB127@zip.com.au> <kRht2.2392$OS5.2308141@news3.mia>`

```

Judson McClendon wrote in message ...
[...]
>... How
>could a COBOL programmer not understand a presentation of pointers, if
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>

As you well know, but may not have considered, with a definition
of PICTURE X, you defined the C-equivalent 'char'. In fact, the
PICTURE (and USAGE) clause defines both a length and data type
in COBOL, the same as in C.

Therefore a reference to a PICTURE X memory location in COBOL
is same as a (char *) cast on a void (or pure address) pointer in C.
Both references consider the length and data type.
-------------------------------
Rick Smith
e-mail: < ricksmith@aiservices.com >
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: advice needed...

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1999-02-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<MKHt2.2596$Xl5.3081548@news1.mia>`
- **References:** `<78fukt$oen$1@news8.svr.pol.co.uk> <78ovso$gqe$1@news.fsu.edu> <36b32cb4.4114943@news.trxinc.com> <78vbrc$uhb$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <IRIs2.1343$Xl5.1344622@news1.mia> <36B58B46.C64BB127@zip.com.au> <kRht2.2392$OS5.2308141@news3.mia> <797ege$1ai$1@server.cntfl.com>`

```
Rick Smith wrote:
>
>Judson McClendon wrote in message ...
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
>in COBOL, the same as in C.

Did my above statement "Remember, we're talking low-level assembly-type
pointers or vectors here (pure addresses), not C-type pointers, where a
length and data type are associated" not ring a bell? ;-)

>Therefore a reference to a PICTURE X memory location in COBOL
>is same as a (char *) cast on a void (or pure address) pointer in C.
>Both references consider the length and data type.

I did not say "a reference to the table" I said "the subscript into
that table".  The subscript carries no data type or length with it.
Did you think I was suggesting that once the above comments were made,
that the instructor walk out of the room and say nothing else? :-)  In
teaching, you must present the student with a comprehensible reference,
then refine it.  Giving them a bunch of accurate technical verbage for
which they have no frame of reference to understand is equivalent to
speaking gibberish.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: advice needed...

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Gunnar Opheim" <gunnar.opheim@eunet.no>
- **Date:** 1999-02-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01be4e2e$448372c0$0100007f@vaagshaugen>`
- **References:** `<78fukt$oen$1@news8.svr.pol.co.uk> <78ovso$gqe$1@news.fsu.edu> <36b32cb4.4114943@news.trxinc.com> <78vbrc$uhb$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <IRIs2.1343$Xl5.1344622@news1.mia> <36B58B46.C64BB127@zip.com.au>`

```
Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au> wrote in article
<36B58B46.C64BB127@zip.com.au>...
> 
> How memory is allocated and pointed to there is no worse language than
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> to experienced cobol programmers but just get vague looks.

Referring to IBM mainframe systems, the OS/VS Cobol had no pointers visible
to the programmer.  When Cobol was used for the CICS online system, the BLL
cells of LINKAGE SECTION (yes, those are the pointers) was manipulated by
CICS, and the programmer had to keep track of changes to them (using
SERVICE RELOAD statements).  It must have been a real nightmare to Cobol
programmers.  Since it is history now, I won't bring up all the nasty
details.

The VS Cobol II has the concept of pointers, and some nicer way of dealing
with them, but it still only works for LINKAGE SECTION.

Maybe it is not polite to bring it up in this group, but the PL/I language
is an excellent example of the use of pointers and pointer-based
structures.  And has been for at about 30 years.

Gunnar.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: mod equivalent in cobol

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1999-02-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-STvdgGJeUjoE@Dwight_Miller.iix.com>`
- **References:** `<j4Tp2.1911$PD.11130759@news.magma.ca> <36a9dbaf.381814714@news1.ibm.net> <01be47d6$765aace0$0100007f@vaagshaugen> <36aba85e.499767707@news1.ibm.net> <01be4887$46fdb710$0100007f@vaagshaugen> <36ad291e.598279063@news1.ibm.net> <01be4d54$3a874dd0$0100007f@vaagshaugen>`

```
On Sun, 31 Jan 1999 23:32:25, "Gunnar Opheim" <gunnar.opheim@eunet.no>
wrote:
> I am aware of those two definitions, and even a third one concerning math:
> the number by which a logarithm to one base is multiplied to give the
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> theme of this thread.

I hate to belabor the point but... I'm not arguing that the results 
for positive numbers are different.  What I am saying is that MOD and 
REM "ARE" different and that one should not use MOD and REM 
interchangably - else MOD (as it has in other languages -- apparantly)
will lose it's proper meaning.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: mod equivalent in cobol

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "Gunnar Opheim" <gunnar.opheim@eunet.no>
- **Date:** 1999-02-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01be4e5e$3fcaf2f0$0100007f@vaagshaugen>`
- **References:** `<j4Tp2.1911$PD.11130759@news.magma.ca> <36a9dbaf.381814714@news1.ibm.net> <01be47d6$765aace0$0100007f@vaagshaugen> <36aba85e.499767707@news1.ibm.net> <01be4887$46fdb710$0100007f@vaagshaugen> <36ad291e.598279063@news1.ibm.net> <01be4d54$3a874dd0$0100007f@vaagshaugen> <Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-STvdgGJeUjoE@Dwight_Miller.iix.com>`

```
Thane Hubbell <redsky@ibm.net> wrote in article
<Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-STvdgGJeUjoE@Dwight_Miller.iix.com>...
> I hate to belabor the point but... I'm not arguing that the results 
> for positive numbers are different.  What I am saying is that MOD and 
> REM "ARE" different and that one should not use MOD and REM 
> interchangably - else MOD (as it has in other languages -- apparantly)
> will lose it's proper meaning.

I finally have consulted a book on number theory, and I rush to admit that
you are quite right.

Gauss (1777-1855) introduced the idea of congruence, and he defined that A
is congruent to B for the modulus M if both yields the same non-negative
remainder R when divided by the modulus.

So, in the Cobol sentence
   COMPUTE R = MOD(A,M)
M is the modulus and R is the remainder when A is divided by M.  But note
that R is defined to be non-negative, while in
   COMPUTE R = REM(A,D)
R may be negative (if A or D, but not both, are negative).

So R for both functions is a remainder.  In the MOD function the second
argument is called modulus, while in the REM function the second argument
is called divisor.

When applied to determination of leap years, both functions will work.  If
we do it for negative years AD (God forbid) they yield different results
for non-leap years.  Which of them is correct is a matter of taste.

The bottom line: The function name MOD must not be confused with the
modulus argument.

Now, if that doesn't solve it, I give up.
```

```
In a previous article, NOSPAMbrazee@home.com (Howard Brazee) says:

>> Sorry that I have to disappoint you.  The Julian calendar was introduced in
>> the year 46 BC in the Roman Empire and included the use of leap years.  
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Calendrical Calculations
Nachum Dershowitz and Edward M. Reingold
ISBN 0-521-56413-1 hardback
ISBN 0-521-56474-3 paperback
"The purpose of this book is to present, in a unified,
completely algorithmic form, a description of fourteen
calendars and how they relate to one another: the
present civil calendar (Gregorian), the recent ISO
commercial calendar, the old civil calendar (Julian),
the Coptic and Ethiopic calendars, the Islamic (Moslem)
calendar, the modern Persian (solar) calendar, the
Baha'i calendar, the Hebrew (Jewish) calendar, the
Mayan calendars, the French Revolutionary calendar,
the Chinese calendar, and both the old (mean) and new
(true) Hindu (Indian) calendars. Easy conversion among
these calendars is a by-product of the approach, as is
the determination of secular and religious holidays.
Calendrical Calculations makes accurate calendrical
algorithms readily available for computer use.
This volume will be a valuable resource for working
programmers, as well as a source of useful algorithmic
tools for computer scientists. It also includes a
wealth of historical material of value to anyone
interested in chronology."
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: mod equivalent in cobol

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** riplin@kcbbs.gen.nz (Richard Plinston)
- **Date:** 1999-02-01T00:00:00

```
In message <<01be4d5d$3bc1da90$0100007f@vaagshaugen>> "Gunnar Opheim" <gunnar.opheim@eunet.no> writes:
> <78kgmr$mfs$1@news.igs.net>...
> > I can absolutely assure you, that he did *not* declare the year
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

Quite, but leap years in the past are a matter of historical
record rather than of calculation.  I understand that in Rome
the years 45BC, 42BC and 39BC were leap years.

Calculations are fine for the last couple of centuries and
for the next few.  For example whether 1600 was a leap year
or not depends on the country.

When did the October 1917 revotion occur ?  The answer is
November (if you lived in Europe).
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: mod equivalent in cobol

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1999-02-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7954ps$o86$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<j4Tp2.1911$PD.11130759@news.magma.ca> <01be47d6$765aace0$0100007f@vaagshaugen> <1999Jan24.214510.19488@giant> <78h38o$ne5$1@news.igs.net> <01be488c$9348b6b0$0100007f@vaagshaugen> <78kgmr$mfs$1@news.igs.net> <01be4d5d$3bc1da90$0100007f@vaagshaugen>`

```
Depends on what you mean as a "concept".  Certainly
there were years, and they had a number of days in them,
and the number may have been 365 or 366.  However,
the concept of a leap year is that a normal year has
365 days, while a leap year has 366, and is added at
specific intervals.  That took another 16 centuries to
evolve.

Gunnar Opheim wrote in message <01be4d5d$3bc1da90$0100007f@vaagshaugen>...
>Donald Tees <donald@willmack.com> wrote in article
><78kgmr$mfs$1@news.igs.net>...
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: mod equivalent in cobol

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** riplin@kcbbs.gen.nz (Richard Plinston)
- **Date:** 1999-02-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<329932.69132.17560@kcbbs.gen.nz>`
- **References:** `<7954ps$o86$1@news.igs.net>`

```
In message <<7954ps$o86$1@news.igs.net>> "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com> writes:
> there were years, and they had a number of days in them,
> and the number may have been 365 or 366.  However,
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> evolve.

Simply not true.  What took until 1584 was establishing the
rules that we now use that keeps the years in sync with
the earth's orbit to an adequate accuracy.

A leap year every 4 years was well established by the Julian
calendar.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: mod equivalent in cobol

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** "Gunnar Opheim" <gunnar.opheim@eunet.no>
- **Date:** 1999-02-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01be4e40$93c928e0$0100007f@vaagshaugen>`
- **References:** `<j4Tp2.1911$PD.11130759@news.magma.ca> <01be47d6$765aace0$0100007f@vaagshaugen> <1999Jan24.214510.19488@giant> <78h38o$ne5$1@news.igs.net> <01be488c$9348b6b0$0100007f@vaagshaugen> <78kgmr$mfs$1@news.igs.net> <01be4d5d$3bc1da90$0100007f@vaagshaugen> <7954ps$o86$1@news.igs.net>`

```
Donald Tees <donald@willmack.com> wrote in article
<7954ps$o86$1@news.igs.net>...
> Depends on what you mean as a "concept".  Certainly
> there were years, and they had a number of days in them,
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> evolve.

I detailed this in an earlier posting (which you probably will have to go
to Dejanews to find now).  If my sources are not completely wrong, the
Julian calendar was decided in 46 BC (which they then called year 708 after
the foundation of Rome).  The first year of that calendar, 45 BC in our
terms, was to have 366 days (leap year), as well as every 4. year
thereafter.  The other years were 365 days.  So it has been since, and
still is, except that the adjustment of leaving out the century years not
divisible by 400 was declared by Pope Gregory XIII in 1582 (introduced
later in non-Roman-Catholic western countries).  So leap years have existed
(in the Julian/Gregorian calendar) since 45 BC.

Since this thread long since diverted from the original Cobol question,
this is my last posting on the theme.
```

```
though we have to repunch cards, our editors can handle it.  But it
makes it easier to browse a program listing and can create implicit
paragraph groups without sections.  

(I use 4 digit prefixes, allowing more room for inserted paragraphs)

…[22 more quoted lines elided]…
```
