## Thread: Roman Numerals revisited — 2 this month / 52 total

_Thread started [1999-01](1999-01.md#t-3818b7b61d)._

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Alphabetical Order

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** cwestbury@giant.intranet.com (Chris Westbury)
- **Date:** 1999-02-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1999Feb2.205236.19498@giant>`
- **References:** `<qlnk2.656$EC3.507828@news3.mia> <1999Jan8.174834.19474@giant> <36b0fee2.11993423@netnews>`

```
In article <36b0fee2.11993423@netnews>:
Barticus@att.spam.net (Randall Bart) wrote:
>
> In article <369ced51.91062738@netnews>,
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
> symbol look like?

Sorry, I was out skiing and missed the discussion.  Friedlein says
nothing about where sampi came from or how it was pronounced.  AFAIK, it
was not from an older alphabet, nor did it make it into any of the newer
alphabets.  My guess is that it is simply a symbol made up because one
was needed.  The earliest symbol used for 900 in the Greek bookkeeping
system (3rd century BC) is an upside-down psi.  Sampi looks to me sort of
like a pi rotated 45 degrees clockwise, which would partially account for
the name.


[...]
> The Latin Alphabet is 26 letters.  If we add a 27th between N and O
> (the xi between nu and omicron) there is an amazing correspondence with
> the chart above, all the way thru the letter upsilon.
[...]

I'm no expert on this.  Everything I posted was taken from Friedlein. 
But what you wrote, including the parts I snipped for brevity, looks spot
on to me.  I can remember my own astonishment when I first saw delta
epsilon vau and omicron pi qoph rho sigma tau upsilon.  The Latin
alphabetical order came from the Greek numerical order!  As you say,

> clearly the use of letters for numbers pre-dates alphabetical order. 
> If alphabetical order had come first, C and G would be adjacent.
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> > learn the alphabet anyway.

I really should have mentioned that many Roman bookkeepers were Greek
slaves.
```

```
>>>The Greek bookkeeping system starts with three cycles:
>>>
>>>             1     2     3      4       5       6   7
8     9
>>>    units    ALPHA BETA  GAMMA  DELTA   EPSILON VAU ZETA
ETA   THETA
>>>    tens     IOTA  KAPPA LAMBDA MU      NU      XI
OMICRON PI    QOPH
>>>    hundreds RHO   SIGMA TAU    UPSILON PHI     CHI PSI
OMEGA SAMPI

Alphabet has 24

does the symbol
>look like?

answered each other:

character, why
>aren't they adjacent in alphabetical order, like I-J, and
U-V-W?
>AND
>Which came first, the use of letters as numbers, or
alphabetical order?
,snip>
```

```
let your download (or send you a demo) good for 30 days or so. 
You can also get a copy in one of the newer RPG books our, though

who are BIG on the AS/400), is a small blue book, and costs about $40.
```

```
experience.
```

<a id="t-426540e155"></a>
## Thread: Computer&Internet quiz from Inquizitive.com — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`Web, XML, modern integration`](topics.md#web)_

### Computer&Internet quiz from Inquizitive.com

- **From:** naras@my-dejanews.com
- **Date:** 1999-02-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** rec.games.computer,comp.lang.java,comp.lang.cobol,alt.comp.sys
- **Message-ID:** `<7971s5$3pn$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
Questions

1. In China, you need to wait for about 2 months to get a telephone
connection. In India, it is about one and a half years. On an average, how
long would you need to wait to get a telephone connection in Bangladesh,
according to a report in the Tele.com magazine published in December 1997?

2. In 1939, Konrad Zuse and Helmut Shreyer went to a German government
official and told him that they had an idea of how to build a computer that
would decipher enemy codes, but the official after conferring with Hitler
rejected their proposal. What was the reported reason for the refusal?

3. It is said that as a schoolboy, this famous person once hacked his way
into a University of Washington computer, and got caught. Who?

4. How is MAE West related to the Internet?

5. In 1990, Patrick Naughton sent an angry resignation letter to the CEO of
Sun Microsystems detailing the woeful state of the company's operating
systems. As a result of this letter, the company asked Naughton, Bill Joy,
James Gosling, and three others to create a solution to the problem. They
created a simple object-oriented programming language named Oak. What would
Oak evolve into a few years later?

(Answers are given at the bottom).

This quiz was provided by Inquizitive.com, world�s finest quiz.
Inquizitive.com also runs a Computers & Internet Quiz mailing list that has
great questions on computers & internet. You can register with
Inquizitive.com computers & internet quiz mailing list at:
http://Inquizitive.com_Cmp.Listbot.com

Please forward these questions and answers to any friends who could be
interested. Check out how many they got right!(You can do this easily either
by using the option provided by DejaNews at the bottom of this mail, or by
choosing the Send File option under File, in your browser)

You can also cut and paste any questions and answers from this quiz to the end
of your e-mails to make them more interesting. It will be great if you could
mention that the content was provided by http://www.inquizitive.com

Answers

1. More than 10 years!

2. They said it would take them a year to build the machine. Hitler thought
that it was too long a time because in his opinion the war would end within
six months!

3. Bill Gates.

4. MAE West ( standing for Metropolitan Area Exchange West) is a key Network
Access Point for Internet in the United States. ( It has nothing to do with
the actress).

5. Java.
```

<a id="t-4811c1034d"></a>
## Thread: deleted records — 4 this month / 18 total

### deleted records

- **From:** Carlos Garcia de Paredes <chalula@sinfo.net>
- **Date:** 1999-02-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<798cif$bt6$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
We use RM Cobol running on SCO Open Server 5.  When records are deleted from
a database file, the deleted records are still physically present, occupying
valuable disk space.  To physically remove these deleted records, we copy the
file.  There must be an easier way to physically remove the deleted records. 
I hope somebody can help. Dr. Carlos Garcia de Paredes - Panama

-----------== Posted via Deja News, The Discussion Network ==----------
http://www.dejanews.com/       Search, Read, Discuss, or Start Your Own
```

#### ↳ Re: deleted records

- **From:** rkrayhawk@aol.com (RKRayhawk)
- **Date:** 1999-02-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19990207171708.21407.00001203@ng23.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<798cif$bt6$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
(Original post quoted in toto infra).

Many database products require some kind of reorg to reclaim space occupied by
'deleted' records.  (Sometimes copying a file does this, sometimes a reorg
utility is needed).  Dont' feel bad about the product you are using. The
situation is very common.

The motivation is that just posting a record as logically deleted is fast,
typically requiring just a single byte or even a single bit flip. 

Physically removing a record in a complex file access methodology could require
fixups to the previous record's forward pointer, and fixups to the following
record's backwards pointers. Also index pointers to the specific record in
question would need to be sponged up too - THAT could require multiple index
record fixups if the record in question was the lowest or highest record in a
group.

In short, physical deletes can create serious performance bogs. Generally, a
dbs intended for 'online' use (and most PC work is actually 'online') will
avoid physical delete, and just go with logical delete, tricking any subsequent
access by making the deleted record invisible.

File reorganization is usually done offline at a time when performance is less
critical. The culture that is out there is to do reorg at roughly the same time
as you do file backups, so 'copying' the file for reorg purposes is a trivial
additional step.

Some access methods do dynamic space retrieval; but this introduces performance
issues as outlined above, and introduces reliability issues, and makes forward
recovery a much more dubious adventure.

Recoveing the valuable disk space is important. More frequent reorgs are the
usual prescription. But like anything else in this biz it is a trade-off. There
is certainly a subset of applications that involve very frequent record
deletion, in which cases a less typical access method strategy involving
dynamic space recovery could be indicated.

Some extremely advanced access methods organize your single db intro numerous
physical files, any one of which can be varied offline and reorganized or
recovered independently (even while the rest of the db stays online).  But you
need a megabucks application to justfy the extra cost of dealing with data that
way.

Best Wishes
Bob Rayhawk
RKRayhawk@aol.com

Carlos Garcia de Paredes <chalula@sinfo.net>
Date: Wed, 03 Feb 1999 02:33:52 GMT
posted
<<
We use RM Cobol running on SCO Open Server 5.  When records are deleted from
a database file, the deleted records are still physically present, occupying
valuable disk space.  To physically remove these deleted records, we copy the
file.  There must be an easier way to physically remove the deleted records. 
I hope somebody can help. Dr. Carlos Garcia de Paredes - Panama


>>
```

#### ↳ Re: deleted records

- **From:** pduboisREMOVETHIS@enteract.com (PAL3000)
- **Date:** 1999-02-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36bf1dc1.8177831@news.enteract.com>`
- **References:** `<798cif$bt6$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
Just copying the file will not remove the "Deleted" records.  To
actually remove them, write a program that:

   Writes the good records to a temp file
   Open the production file as output
   Put back into the production file the records in the temp file.

The accounting software I work with comes with pr-written programs
that do just this.  They provide an ascii export for each file, and
also a corrosponding import. Thus, to reorg, we export the file, then
re-import it. 

Hope this helps,

Paul

On Wed, 03 Feb 1999 02:33:52 GMT, Carlos Garcia de Paredes
<chalula@sinfo.net> wrote:

>We use RM Cobol running on SCO Open Server 5.  When records are deleted from
>a database file, the deleted records are still physically present, occupying
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>http://www.dejanews.com/       Search, Read, Discuss, or Start Your Own    

--------------------------------------
To reply by email, please take out the
words REMOVETHIS from my email address
```

#### ↳ Re: deleted records

- **From:** HEBERT OROFINO <H.OROFINO@NEGOCIOS.COM.UY>
- **Date:** 1999-02-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<79q95j$u94$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`
- **References:** `<798cif$bt6$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
In article <798cif$bt6$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>,
  Carlos Garcia de Paredes <chalula@sinfo.net> wrote:
> We use RM Cobol running on SCO Open Server 5.  When records are deleted from
> a database file, the deleted records are still physically present, occupying
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>

No creo que estes usando una base de datos como tu dices. Me parece que debes
estar utilizando la estructura de archivos propia de RM/Cobol. Te dire que
los archivos que RM/Cobol utiliza unicamente recuperan el espacio asignado a
registro eliminados una vez que tu reorganizas el archivo o se tienes que
hacer un programa que lea el archivo y cree otro igual. De esta manera se
recupera el espacio. Asimismo te digo que los registros desaparecen luego del
"Delete" pero el espacio queda asignado. De todas formas ese espacio sera
utilizado por nuevos registros y el archivo no crece tanto mientras el area
de registros eliminados no sea completa. Unicamente puede crecer debido al
arbol del indice del archivo que puede quedar desbalanceado.

Saludos
H.O.

http://www.dejanews.com/       Search, Read, Discuss, or Start Your Own
```

- **Message-ID:** `<36BB3AC2.742B3453@NOSPAMhome.com>`
- **References:** `<796uhg$e54$1@oak.prod.itd.earthlink.net> <19990205095337.21963.00000721@ng96.aol.com> <36BB16D8.7F5E8899@bdssoftware.com> <NvGu2.3635$Xl5.4988494@news1.mia>`

```
> Visual SlickEdit is a great editor.  Multi-Edit is also very good, and
> costs less.  I wish Borland had not bought and buried Brief. Sigh.  I
> still use Brief for a DOS editor.  Adopting a new editor is like getting
> married.  Changing editors is like getting divorced and remarried.  Very
> traumatic! :-)

I have always been tempted to try SlickEdit, but couldn't see paying
that much money.  Now if they would bundle their OS/2, DOS, & Windows
versions.

I have had Multi-Edit forever, but I never got the hang of using the
Windows version.  In the DOS version, I could say ME FILE.COB and if
FILE.COB did not exist, it would open it in the correct directory.  But
it wouldn't handle long filenames which is a pain in OS/2.  (I would use
batch jobs to rename my long JAVA extensions).  I have two different
Windows versions, but probably will delete the one which runs on Windows
3.1.  Again, it doesn't handle long filenames.  When I use OS/2, I will
use ME for DOS, which I use 9/10 of the time even under NT.  The only
time I use the GUI version are when I need long filenames under NT, or
when I need to cut and paste a document into Netscape (for instance in
the comp.lang.cobol newsgroup).

GUIs have a lot of advantages, but they are also more work.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Editor Suggestions

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com>
- **Date:** 1999-02-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36BB3C7A.D64067F1@NOSPAMhome.com>`
- **References:** `<796uhg$e54$1@oak.prod.itd.earthlink.net> <19990205095337.21963.00000721@ng96.aol.com> <36BB16D8.7F5E8899@bdssoftware.com> <NvGu2.3635$Xl5.4988494@news1.mia> <36BB3AC2.742B3453@NOSPAMhome.com>`

```
Oh, one more thing about Multi-Edit.

I have bought upgrades via the internet.  When I install, I keep the
MEWSETUP.EXE file.  I can run it any time to create all of the icons and
registry settings I need.  When I added a second NT partition, I ran
this and was able to run Multi-Edit without installing from scratch.  

Most Windows applications force you to install from scratch when you
want a second OS to see it.  Or when you reinstall Windows or move the
application from an old computer to a new one.  This is a terrible idea,
especially in the era of web based upgrades.

Please install a SETUP file in your applications which will correct this
terrible design deficiency!!
```

```
called Kexx, and many built-in functions.

good reputation, and of course, the price is right!
```

```
Judson McClendon wrote:
> 
> Howard Brazee wrote:
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> kidding! :-)

Some languages are easy to read with color typing of various words.  (I
used one though which wouldn't PRINT in color on my printer).  Still, I
can type.

One place where PC editors seem to fall short in is in column control. 
Maybe it is my COBOL mindset, but I like being able to easily control
what column I am in.  There's a thread here comparing tab delimited data
which C programmers like with positional data COBOL programmers like. 
Well, we feel the same way with our editors.

How would you like to use a non-column oriented editor to do RPG?
```

```
Howard Brazee wrote:
>Judson McClendon wrote:
>> Howard Brazee wrote:
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>can type.

Actually, when typing text such as this I am pretty slow.  For some reason
it is when I am programming that my typing speeds up dramatically.  And I
can't touch type, I use my own method, but I have to occasionally glance
at the keyboard. :-(

>One place where PC editors seem to fall short in is in column control.

Wow!  That comment surprises me, Howard.  One of the reasons I like
the PC editors I use is their column control.  To my mind, that is the
most important difference in programmer's editors from other text
editors.  I don't use code templates, though I can see where they might
be very helpful.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Editor Suggestions

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com>
- **Date:** 1999-02-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36BF4D85.1476D1D3@NOSPAMhome.com>`
- **References:** `<796uhg$e54$1@oak.prod.itd.earthlink.net> <19990205095337.21963.00000721@ng96.aol.com> <36BB16D8.7F5E8899@bdssoftware.com> <NvGu2.3635$Xl5.4988494@news1.mia> <36BB3AC2.742B3453@NOSPAMhome.com> <MhIu2.3722$Xl5.5049753@news1.mia> <36BEFA13.DCBF4B15@NOSPAMhome.com> <rfDv2.2828$wj1.5671075@news2.mia>`

```
> >One place where PC editors seem to fall short in is in column control.
> 
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> be very helpful.

Line control is worse, but I like being able to type in the following
commandS:

X ALL;CHANGE P'<' P'>' 7 72 ALL  (exclude all lines, change capitalize
everything between columns 7 & 72 while unexcluding thos lines with
changes!)

With TSO, I use the exclude and flip commands a lot:

X ALL; F P'#' 8 ALL    (This finds every numeric in column 7.  I then
exclude everything from the top to PROCEDURE, and can see all paragraph
names, procided they are in the format of 0000-M-PARAGRAPH.

Some editors let me automatically put my initials and change request in
columns 73-80 where I make changes.

I haven't yet seen a PC based editor which excluded lines the way I want
(but I have seen some of the other stuff I wrote above).
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Editor Suggestions

```
>> >One place where PC editors seem to fall short in is in column control.

…[23 more quoted lines elided]…

Then you haven't tried KEDIT by Mansfield. It supports ALL, MORE, LESS and
is extensible using KEXX, which is based on REXX. When combined with the
'ALTERED' command it is trivial to write a macro to place modcodes in
columns 73-80 when the file is saved.
```

```
for Windows for just 50 US Dollars, before the rise to 80 US Dollars on
Monday 8th February.

With its simple GUI designer and COBOL editor, Casegen makes it really easy
to write small to large commercial Windows programs in COBOL that run at top
speed due to generating all those complex 32-bit Windows calls directly
inside your program. We have included the FREE Fujitsu Version 3 for your
review so you can start building and running your Windows programs without
buying additional products.

There are lots of examples and stong help facilities to get your first
Windows program up and running in just 30 minutes of receiving the CD. Start
by customising these programs and reviewing the "Getting Started" book on
the CD.

PROFESSIONAL version with even more features including easy-to-specify ODBC
access for just 50 US Dollars more (when it is released).

Email us now on sales@casegen.co.uk or see our web site at

Cheque for 50 US Dollars drawn on a US Bank

Direct to our bank account - ask for details.
50 USD cash or equivalent in all major currencies.

Cheques should be made payable to: Casegen Systems Ltd.
Cash may be sent "recorded delivery", if required.
30-day money-back guarantee - less postage costs.
Sorry, no credit cards.

Please order from:

Casegen CD Offer
Swan House
White Hart Street
High Wycombe
Bucks
UK HP11 2HL

enclosing your payment and delivery address.  CDs are despatched normally
same day.

Thank you.
```

<a id="t-94f6eead98"></a>
## Thread: Difference between REM and MOD — 7 msgs

### Re: Difference between REM and MOD

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1999-02-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<hqGu2.3627$Xl5.4985628@news1.mia>`
- **References:** `<79f0pn$rl4$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
jasonc@collegeestrie.com wrote:
>Would someone please explain to me the difference between MOD and REM.

MOD(argument-1, argument-2) returns the integer value defined by the
modulo arithmetic function (argument-1 modulo argument-2).  For positive
arguments, it is equivalent to REM (remainder).  But unless you are math-
aware, you may not get the result for negative arguments that you expect.
The ANSI Intrinsic Functions documentation gives this table:

  FUNCTION MOD(argument-1, argument-2)

  Argument-1    Argument-2    Return
     11             5            1
    -11             5            4
     11            -5           -4
    -11            -5           -1

REM(argument-1, argument-2) returns the integer value which is the
remainder after dividing argument-1 (dividend) by argument-2 (divisor).
The arguments in the above table would give the following results when
used with FUNCTION REM:

  FUNCTION REM(argument-1, argument-2)

  Argument-1    Argument-2    Return
     11             5            1
    -11             5           -1
     11            -5            1
    -11            -5           -1

Since FUNCTION REM returns the 'remainder' after integer division,
FUNCTION REM has the sign of the dividend (argument-1).
```

#### ↳ Re: Difference between REM and MOD

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com>
- **Date:** 1999-02-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36BB3934.A92F14E7@NOSPAMhome.com>`
- **References:** `<79f0pn$rl4$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <hqGu2.3627$Xl5.4985628@news1.mia>`

```

REM?
```

- **From:** Barticus@att.spam.net (Randall Bart)
- **Date:** 1999-02-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36be134a.8756802@netnews>`
- **References:** `<79f0pn$rl4$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <hqGu2.3627$Xl5.4985628@news1.mia> <36BB3934.A92F14E7@NOSPAMhome.com>`

```
'Twas Fri, 05 Feb 1999 11:32:20 -0700, when Howard Brazee
<brazee@NOSPAMhome.com> illuminated comp.lang.cobol thusly:

>What types of COBOL (business) needs might I want to use MOD instead of
>REM?

Most uses of MOD and REM are with positive arguments, and in such uses the
functions are identical.  Because of my math background I prefer MOD.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Difference between REM and MOD

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com>
- **Date:** 1999-02-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36BEF8B0.6B0200C1@NOSPAMhome.com>`
- **References:** `<79f0pn$rl4$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <hqGu2.3627$Xl5.4985628@news1.mia> <36BB3934.A92F14E7@NOSPAMhome.com> <36be134a.8756802@netnews>`

```
Randall Bart wrote:
> 
> 'Twas Fri, 05 Feb 1999 11:32:20 -0700, when Howard Brazee
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> functions are identical.  Because of my math background I prefer MOD.

Where they are identical, I still prefer using the function which has
the correct INTENT.  If I extend my logic to where they are not
identical, I want to be using the correct word with the correct mindset.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Difference between REM and MOD

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Barticus@att.spam.net (Randall Bart)
- **Date:** 1999-02-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36bf6ade.42835568@netnews>`
- **References:** `<79f0pn$rl4$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <hqGu2.3627$Xl5.4985628@news1.mia> <36BB3934.A92F14E7@NOSPAMhome.com> <36be134a.8756802@netnews> <36BEF8B0.6B0200C1@NOSPAMhome.com>`

```
'Twas Mon, 08 Feb 1999 07:46:08 -0700, when Howard Brazee
<brazee@NOSPAMhome.com> illuminated comp.lang.cobol thusly:

>Randall Bart wrote:

>> Most uses of MOD and REM are with positive arguments, and in such uses the
>> functions are identical.  Because of my math background I prefer MOD.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>identical, I want to be using the correct word with the correct mindset.

Okay.  If you are splitting seconds into hours, minutes, and seconds, or
splitting inches into feet and inches, the correct intent is found in the
function REM.  If you are doing a check digit or encryption calculation
the correct intent is found in the function MOD.
```

```
> >> functions are identical.  Because of my math background I prefer MOD.
```

<a id="t-7e5c66bc66"></a>
## Thread: Variable length files — 7 msgs

_Topics: [`VSAM, files, sorting`](topics.md#files)_

### Variable length files

- **From:** campsnow@hotmail.com
- **Date:** 1999-02-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<79fpaj$i7n$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
HELP HELP I am converting to COBOL for MVS and am having difficulty reading a
variable length file. File is created in program using 4 different 01 levels
that are 44, and 92 and 150 characters in size. File is created with(
recording mode v ) (block contains 484 to 1544 character). I am aborting in
program that is trying to read this file in this program a copybook is read
in with those same 01 levels plus one with 194.. I am receiving wrong record
size in this program.. Because we are converting we are trying to minimize
code changes. This works OK  before MVS changes were put in?

-----------== Posted via Deja News, The Discussion Network ==----------
http://www.dejanews.com/       Search, Read, Discuss, or Start Your Own
```

#### ↳ Re: Variable length files

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-02-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<79fsqk$7ft@sjx-ixn9.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<79fpaj$i7n$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
Welcome to the "wonderful world of File Status 39"

This has got to be one of the biggest inhibitors to migrating from OldBOL to
NewBOL.

Bottom-Line:
  With the new  IBM compilers
     You must (absolutely positively) make certain that your FD and its
records match your DCB attributes EXACTLY.

A few solutions (all of which are documented in the IBM migration guide)

A) Make certain that all programs that reference the file (create and read)
have EXACTLY the same FD and all the same records.

B) Use the *new* RECORD VARYING IN SIZE option in the FD (both create and
read) with the minimum and maximum record sizes (minus the 4 byte RDW - as
always)

C) Make certain that you have at least ONE 01-level (record) under the FD in
each program that is the maximum size (again 4 bytes smaller than your JCL
LRECL) and still specify RECORDING MODE V in the FD.

  ****

There are probably a few other possibilities, but one or all of these should
get you thru.  (Compiling with the CMPR2 compiler option also MIGHT work -
but I would really consider both temporary and "last resort")

In general, you are talking about your program "aborting".  I do suggest
that you get used to file status code checking - in all programs for all
files - not just for VSAM (which used to be the "rule of thumb" with older
IBM compilers)
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Variable length files

- **From:** "Jeff" <a@a.com>
- **Date:** 1999-02-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<aMuv2.3575$fz.12034683@storm.twcol.com>`
- **References:** `<79fpaj$i7n$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <79fsqk$7ft@sjx-ixn9.ix.netcom.com>`

```
>C) Make certain that you have at least ONE 01-level (record) under the FD
in
>each program that is the maximum size (again 4 bytes smaller than your JCL
>LRECL) and still specify RECORDING MODE V in the FD.


I recommend C. I always recommend specifying as little as possible in the FD
statement. That way if you need to change the maximum record length you will
always be changing it in the same place, at the 01 level. Changing it in the
FD statement could possibly cause a compile error. I try to avoid these at
all cost, you never know what your response time for job submissions is
going to be.

You could also try specifying the LRECL in the JCL for the input file:

//INFILE  DD DSN=INFILE.WHATEVER,DISP=SHR,DCB=(LRECL=198)    REMEMBER 194+4
```

#### ↳ Re: Variable length files

- **From:** sff5ky@aol.comxxx123 (Sff5ky)
- **Date:** 1999-02-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19990205183931.11567.00000094@ngol03.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<79fpaj$i7n$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```

In article <79fpaj$i7n$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>, campsnow@hotmail.com writes:

>
>HELP HELP I am converting to COBOL for MVS and am having difficulty reading a
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>

How is the FD setup in the program and the DCB in the JCL?
I have seen the wrong record size message come up in relation to the JCL DCB
being set to the file versus the FD.  I would set the DCB LRECL to 1548 and
BLKSIZE to 32756 and see how that works.

HTH.  :-)
```

#### ↳ Re: Variable length files

- **From:** Warren Porter <warrenp@ASPMnetdoor.com>
- **Date:** 1999-02-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36BF0E69.484491F2@ASPMnetdoor.com>`
- **References:** `<79fpaj$i7n$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
Unless you have a record with OCCURS m TO n DEPENDING ON variable_earlier_in_FD,
it is difficult to ask COBOL to read variable length files.  Try reading the file
one byte at a time.  After four reads you will know the block length (1st 2 bytes
of the Block Descriptor Word).  The next four bytes will give you the Record
Descriptor Word (same format).  The record length - 4 will tell you the number of
consecutives reads you will need to fill out a record.  You will also have to
keep up with the total number of bytes read in each block to know when to read
the next block.

HTH

campsnow@hotmail.com wrote:

> HELP HELP I am converting to COBOL for MVS and am having difficulty reading a
> variable length file. File is created in program using 4 different 01 levels
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> http://www.dejanews.com/       Search, Read, Discuss, or Start Your Own
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Variable length files

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-02-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<79nfho$p7i@dfw-ixnews8.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<79fpaj$i7n$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <36BF0E69.484491F2@ASPMnetdoor.com>`

```
Just a "tid bit" of information.  According to the ANSI Standard (as
interpreted for the ANSI'85 Standard), if you have an FD with

      RECORD CONTAINS   n TO M

it is IMPLEMENTOR DEFINED whether the file is "variable format" or "fixed
format"  (I am NOT kidding - there were reasons for this interpretation -
but it sure surprises some).  If you want to GUARANTEE that a file is
"variable format" *and* you are using an '85 compiler,
use the following (new with '85 COBOL) format:

    Record Varying in Size

(For our IBM-limited friends, the "RECORD FORMAT V" phrase is an IBM
extension.)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Variable length files

- **From:** John Trifon <jtrifon@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-02-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36BF9643.E997F3E7@ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<79fpaj$i7n$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <36BF0E69.484491F2@ASPMnetdoor.com> <79nfho$p7i@dfw-ixnews8.ix.netcom.com>`

```
Bill Klein's recommendations are right on the money.

As far a reading a variable record one byte at a time to get the BDW* or the
RDW* in COBOL, faggetaboutit. COBOL does not give you the option of reading a
block of records and deblocking them yourself. You can do this in assembler if
you really are masochistic.  The simplest way to fix your problem is to align
the 01 length with the actual file.

*For the mainframe challenged:
BDW: block descriptor word
RDW: record descriptor word



"William M. Klein" wrote:

> Just a "tid bit" of information.  According to the ANSI Standard (as
> interpreted for the ANSI'85 Standard), if you have an FD with
…[59 more quoted lines elided]…
> >
```

<a id="t-985a969aa3"></a>
## Thread: indexed with alternate keys — 9 msgs

### Re: indexed with alternate keys

- **From:** walterm@rose.hp.com (Walter Murray)
- **Date:** 1999-02-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<79fv8o$mkp$1@ocean.cup.hp.com>`
- **References:** `<799sk4$si7$1@news1.epix.net> <799ub6$dv6@dfw-ixnews3.ix.netcom.com> <dw4u2.88$Xk.161505@client.news.psi.net> <79cb4g$327$1@news.igs.net> <79d3md$hrn@dfw-ixnews11.ix.netcom.com>`

```
William M. Klein (wmklein@nospam.netcom.com) wrote:
: I don't think that "02" is a look ahead feature, I think it is just a way of
: checking that you are STILL under the same duplicate key.  Can someone
: correct me if this isn't right?

No, Bill, I don't think you are right (if I understand what you
are saying).  When the I-O status is 02, it means that the next
record will have the same key value as the current record.  At
least that's the way it works in HP COBOL II, and I think that's
what the standard requires.

Walter Murray
Hewlett-Packard
```

#### ↳ Re: indexed with alternate keys

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1999-02-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36bc70c1.217506156@news1.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<799sk4$si7$1@news1.epix.net> <799ub6$dv6@dfw-ixnews3.ix.netcom.com> <dw4u2.88$Xk.161505@client.news.psi.net> <79cb4g$327$1@news.igs.net> <79d3md$hrn@dfw-ixnews11.ix.netcom.com> <79fv8o$mkp$1@ocean.cup.hp.com>`

```
On 5 Feb 1999 23:35:52 GMT, walterm@rose.hp.com (Walter Murray) wrote:

>William M. Klein (wmklein@nospam.netcom.com) wrote:
>: I don't think that "02" is a look ahead feature, I think it is just a way of
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>

I don't think that is necessarily true.

From my understanding --- When you operate on a record that has an
alternate key that allows duplicates, and that record duplicates
another's alternate key - the successful file status will be "02".

If you do a "Start" - then "read" sequentialy - you seem to indicate
that the first, and subsuquent reads will return an "02" - up to, but
not including the "last" record with that alternate key, which will
return what?  "00"?   That doesn't seem right to me, so let's test it.

Here's the program:

 IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
 PROGRAM-ID.  TESTC.
*
*  Test Duplicate alternate key file status values.
*
 ENVIRONMENT DIVISION.
 CONFIGURATION SECTION.
 SOURCE-COMPUTER.    IBM-PC.
 OBJECT-COMPUTER.    IBM-PC.
 INPUT-OUTPUT SECTION.
 FILE-CONTROL.
     SELECT OPTIONAL TEST-FILE ASSIGN TO "Test.Fil"
            ORGANIZATION IS INDEXED
            ACCESS IS DYNAMIC
            RECORD KEY IS PRIME-KEY
            ALTERNATE RECORD KEY IS ALT-KEY WITH DUPLICATES
            FILE STATUS IS TEST-STATUS.
 DATA DIVISION.
 FILE SECTION.
 FD  TEST-FILE.
 01  TEST-RECORD.
     03  PRIME-KEY PIC 999.
     03  ALT-KEY   PIC XXX.
     03  FILLER    PIC X.
 WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
 01  TEST-STATUS PIC XX.
 01  CNTR PIC 9(3) VALUE ZEROS.
 PROCEDURE DIVISION.
 START-OF-PROGRAM.
     OPEN I-O TEST-FILE
     PERFORM VARYING CNTR FROM 1 BY 1
             UNTIL CNTR > 9
           MOVE CNTR TO PRIME-KEY
           EVALUATE CNTR
              WHEN 1
                MOVE "AAA" TO ALT-KEY
              WHEN 2 THRU 4
                MOVE "BBB" TO ALT-KEY
              WHEN 5 THRU 8
                MOVE "CCC" TO ALT-KEY
              WHEN 9
                MOVE "DDD" TO ALT-KEY
          END-EVALUATE
          DISPLAY "WRITING " TEST-RECORD (1:6)
          WRITE TEST-RECORD 
          DISPLAY "STATUS " TEST-STATUS
     END-PERFORM
* realia needs a close/open here
     CLOSE TEST-FILE
     OPEN I-O TEST-FILE
     INITIALIZE TEST-RECORD
     MOVE "AAA" TO ALT-KEY
     START TEST-FILE KEY NOT < ALT-KEY
     DISPLAY "Start AAA " TEST-STATUS
     READ TEST-FILE NEXT RECORD
     DISPLAY "ReadNext AAA " TEST-STATUS
     MOVE "BBB" TO ALT-KEY
     START TEST-FILE KEY NOT < ALT-KEY
     DISPLAY "Start BBB " TEST-STATUS
     PERFORM 4 TIMES
       READ TEST-FILE NEXT
       DISPLAY "ReadNext BBB " TEST-STATUS " " ALT-KEY
     END-PERFORM
     CLOSE TEST-FILE
     STOP RUN
     .

Here are the results:

WRITING 001AAA
STATUS 00
WRITING 002BBB
STATUS 00
WRITING 003BBB
STATUS 00
WRITING 004BBB
STATUS 00
WRITING 005CCC
STATUS 00
WRITING 006CCC
STATUS 00
WRITING 007CCC
STATUS 00
WRITING 008CCC
STATUS 00
WRITING 009DDD
STATUS 00
Start AAA 00
ReadNext AAA 00
Start BBB 00
ReadNext BBB 02 BBB
ReadNext BBB 02 BBB
ReadNext BBB 00 BBB
ReadNext BBB 02 CCC

Well looky here.  Walter is right!  I learned something new!

If someone had bet me I would have sworn that the third "ReadNext BBB"
display would hav returned a status of "02" also.  VERY interesting.

Also - my earlier assertion that "02" would be returned for the write
is equally incorrect.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: indexed with alternate keys

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1999-02-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36bdb109.299510058@news1.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<799sk4$si7$1@news1.epix.net> <799ub6$dv6@dfw-ixnews3.ix.netcom.com> <dw4u2.88$Xk.161505@client.news.psi.net> <79cb4g$327$1@news.igs.net> <79d3md$hrn@dfw-ixnews11.ix.netcom.com> <79fv8o$mkp$1@ocean.cup.hp.com> <36bc70c1.217506156@news1.ibm.net>`

```
On Sat, 06 Feb 1999 17:51:30 GMT, redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
wrote:


>Also - my earlier assertion that "02" would be returned for the write
>is equally incorrect. 

Actually, after doing some research with some other compilers - my
assertion is correct - "02" should have been returned for the
duplicate alternate key writes - but Realia is doing it incorrectly.
The reason? - Realia does not write the alternate index records till
after the file is closed - it then does a sort and writes the records.
```

- **From:** paulr@bix.com (paulr)

```

continually...

-Paul
```

```
is NOT the way they handle alternate keys subsequent to initial load.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: indexed with alternate keys

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-02-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<79l98d$mnb@dfw-ixnews12.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<799sk4$si7$1@news1.epix.net> <799ub6$dv6@dfw-ixnews3.ix.netcom.com> <dw4u2.88$Xk.161505@client.news.psi.net> <79cb4g$327$1@news.igs.net> <79d3md$hrn@dfw-ixnews11.ix.netcom.com> <79fv8o$mkp$1@ocean.cup.hp.com> <36bc70c1.217506156@news1.ibm.net> <36bdb109.299510058@news1.ibm.net>`

```
Thane Hubbell wrote in message <36bdb109.299510058@news1.ibm.net>...
>On Sat, 06 Feb 1999 17:51:30 GMT, redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
>wrote:
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>

From my "usually reliable CA source",

"This is a known and documented "feature" - if users want the 02 to be
reported, there was a module they needed to link in (XBLDSLOW) which slowed
down indexed file load times a lot, but did report on the 02.  (basically
normally we do not build the alternate index entries when records are
written but quickly build them all when the file is closed - this works
dramatically faster but has that darned problem with the 02 status)."
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: indexed with alternate keys

- **From:** Jeffrey Friedman <jfriedm@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 1999-02-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36BE3630.6CE8755@ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<799sk4$si7$1@news1.epix.net> <799ub6$dv6@dfw-ixnews3.ix.netcom.com> <dw4u2.88$Xk.161505@client.news.psi.net> <79cb4g$327$1@news.igs.net> <79d3md$hrn@dfw-ixnews11.ix.netcom.com> <79fv8o$mkp$1@ocean.cup.hp.com> <36bc70c1.217506156@news1.ibm.net> <36bdb109.299510058@news1.ibm.net>`

```
There is a link option with Realia if you want the duplicate alternate key
file stati returned at the time of a WRITE for an indexed file with
alternate keys opened OUTPUT:  Link with XBLDSLOW.  By default we build
the alternate index entries at CLOSE time - this is dramatically faster as
we sort them and write them sequentially rather than doing the random
inserts at the time of each WRITE.  But of course, with this model we
cannot return the duplicate indication on WRITE.  Note that that module is
appropriately named:  load times for large files with alternate keys will
be slower.

Thane Hubbell wrote:

> On Sat, 06 Feb 1999 17:51:30 GMT, redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
> wrote:
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> after the file is closed - it then does a sort and writes the records.
```

<a id="t-1809e76a1b"></a>
## Thread: indexed with alternate keys — 6 msgs

### Re: indexed with alternate keys

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1999-02-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<EGGu2.3651$Xl5.4993618@news1.mia>`
- **References:** `<799sk4$si7$1@news1.epix.net> <79b7s4$i8i$1@news1.epix.net> <79cbq4$3d3$1@news.igs.net>`

```
Donald Tees wrote:
>
>I prefer the method that you outlined, avoiding the use
>of duplicate keys.  It makes file updating and locking
>much simpler.  I use duplicate keys quite often, but tend
>not to use them for random access.

In general, I try to avoid duplicate keys, because of the overhead it
can cause (from serial searches for the key) on deletes and rewrites.
If disk space is not a problem, I often append a unique record key to
the alternate key(s).  Takes a bit more space, but makes for faster
updates.  I almost never declare a single, non-unique field as a key.
```

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>

```
This is almost a complete change of topic, but I have
been working on my first Isam "object".  I am rebuilding
a heritage system, and I plan to make every file update
an invoked method.

The old system does all of it's I/O through standard
performs done on a copy book, so the basic methods
will duplicate the original paragraph names, and the
performs will be replaced by invokes. I plan to make
the object self-sufficient ... that is it will contain all version
number conversions, re-orgs, dumps, reloads, etc.
for that file for life.  I started with the create method
that creates the original Isam file when a file-status
35 is returned.

The object arguments will be the record, and a simple
"Y/N" status flag to the world. I figure that if I make the
record ASCII, sign separate, then it can be invoked by
any language that can invoke an object out of a DLL,
including every Cobol on the market.

Judson McClendon wrote in message ...
>Donald Tees wrote:
>>
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

```
Donald (and other readers of this newsgroup),
  Are you aware that in Object/Methods in the draft Standard, exactly what
file handling will be allowed and how it will be allowed and how it works
with file sharing/record locking is all still very much "up in the air"?

It might have gotten some direction at last week's J4 meeting, but at least
last December at the joint J4/WG4 meeting, there were still lots of issues
being rehashed (for the 3rd or 4th time).

I am certain that you are checking out what your specific implementation
allows and how it handles these issues, but I did think that you might want
to know that this is still a pretty "fuzzy" area in the draft and that
future upgrades and portability may well be a problem.
```

```
I am not quite sure why that applies.  Surely the reason
for making the file handler an object is so that all that
is encapsulated in the object, and transparent to the
application.  At least that is one of my reasons for the
methodology.

Version one can be very simple --- open I/O until status
zero with a one minute timeout, update the file, and close
it.  I can make that more sophistocated any time that
better locking methods become available, and the
source code I write using it should invisibly improve
in response.

<That right paul><G><?>

I do see some areas where lack of a current standard
will have to be handled (like DLL arguement length and
type conventions).  However, I think I can get arround
that by the old fashioned copy book method.  It appears
that way from my testing, anyway.

I am not using the Cobol calling conventions, BTW.  I am
creating the object as an OCX compliant control, and using
OLE to talk to it from standard Cobol.

If I want to put it in a PowerCobol program, It sits on my
toolbar, and if I need access to that Isam File in my
program, I just drag it onto my form.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: indexed with alternate keys

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1999-02-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Xwjv2.4190$Xl5.6058347@news1.mia>`
- **References:** `<799sk4$si7$1@news1.epix.net> <79b7s4$i8i$1@news1.epix.net> <79cbq4$3d3$1@news.igs.net> <EGGu2.3651$Xl5.4993618@news1.mia> <79fptc$2ji$1@news.igs.net>`

```
I wrote a large (>100k LOC) PC COBOL multi-user client-server inventory
system for a client in 1987.  I used Btrieve for the database and wrote
all the file I/O stuff in COPY files.  All the status codes are constants
in COPY files.  The application programs COPY in the files for the files
being used, and PERFORM the appropriate routines.  It works well, and is
very modular.  If I have to convert to another database, it shouldn't be
too difficult to simply rewrite the I/O handling code, and redefine the
constants as needed.  One refinement I have considered is to break the
PERFORM COPY files into individual paragraphs, then use a code scanner
to determine which specific routines are used in a given program and
insert the appropriate COPY statements.  It would reduce the compile
times, and make the programs a bit smaller.
```

<a id="t-46b91531cd"></a>
## Thread: Difference between REM and MOD — 1 msgs

### Re: Difference between REM and MOD

- **From:** Don Nelson <don.nelson@compaq.com>
- **Date:** 1999-02-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36BB30BC.3683@compaq.com>`
- **References:** `<79f0pn$rl4$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
jasonc@collegeestrie.com wrote:
> 
> Would someone please explain to me the difference between MOD and REM.
> 

The difference is in handling signed items (mainly, one negative and 
one positive).  MOD is defined as (argument-1 - (argument-2 * FUNCTION 
INTEGER (argument-1 / argument-2))) and REM the same except for 
INTEGER is INTEGER-PART.  So what, you might say.  INTEGER means the 
greatest integer less than or equal to the value of the argument.  If 
you have an argument of 1.5 you get 1.  However, -1.5 gets -2.  
INTEGER-PART just chops off the fraction.  Therefore, MOD (11, -5) 
returns -4.  REM (11, -5) returns +1.  If all you are using is 
positive numbers, there is no difference.  By the way, don't start 
ranting about the COBOL Committee defining MOD in a dumb way.  That is 
the mathematical way and is used by all the other languages and 
defined that way in the ISO standard.
```

```
I think the main reason that I never use sorts is that
I never program batch.  All my work is online interactive
and PC based.  The sort is really the realm of batch
processing, not systems that receive transactions
one at a time.
```

#### ↳ Re: Multiple RELEASE in SORTs - Newbie question...

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com>
- **Date:** 1999-02-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36BB5308.82D40226@NOSPAMhome.com>`
- **References:** `<79d6am$c9t$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <36BA6A25.CD25AA60@erols.com> <36BADC59.DCAFD39D@zip.com.au> <79eu4v$i4o$1@news.igs.net> <36BB0413.783339DA@NOSPAMhome.com> <79ff4j$r8q$1@news.igs.net>`

```
Donald Tees wrote:
> 
> I think the main reason that I never use sorts is that
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> one at a time.

There are three types of data which I have needed in order in on-line
processing.

1.  Large amounts of data - which need to be sorted before hand, either
with an indexed file or an ordered database.

2.  SQL queries which get sorted via my SQL calls (I am currently
working with IDMS, so that doesn't apply).

3.  Very small amounts of data - in which case I code the sort by hand,
figuring that the overhead of calling an external sort is larger than
the inefficiency of using COBOL procedural code.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Multiple RELEASE in SORTs - Newbie question...

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1999-02-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

- **References:** `<79d6am$c9t$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <36BA6A25.CD25AA60@erols.com> <36BADC59.DCAFD39D@zip.com.au> <79eu4v$i4o$1@news.igs.net> <36BB0413.783339DA@NOSPAMhome.com> <79ff4j$r8q$1@news.igs.net> <36BB5308.82D40226@NOSPAMhome.com>`

```
Again, a difference in working environments.  As a Var,
my product is usually going into a standalone computer,
and most of the data originates at my end.  So I get
completely random events happening, and I funnel them
into a format for a shop like yours.  I normally, for example,
would build an Isam file of them during the day, then
purge into a sequential/ordered flat ASCII file
forwarded to head office.  The Isam file is then
closed, opened output, closed, and reopened in I/O mode
for the next day.

I never see sequential input files, unless it is a conversion,
then they are dumped in order.
```

- **From:** cwestbury@giant.intranet.com (Chris Westbury)
- **Date:** 1999-02-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1999Feb5.132039.19511@giant>`
- **References:** `<xqau2.1739$ob2.4260@news.flash.net> <1999Feb4.132809.19503@giant> <6%Bu2.42$j3.54@testbox>`

```
In article <6%Bu2.42$j3.54@testbox>,
"Tom Eisenman" <Tom_Eisenman@NOJUNKbigfoot.com> wrote:
>
> In article <1999Feb4.132809.19503@giant>,
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
> exception when you execute the IF.

Nope.  Go look up the NUMERIC class condition.

If the NUMERIC class condition abended on non-numeric instead of
returning false, you'd have to be sure that a field was numeric before
testing it for NUMERIC!  Like my wife cleaning up before the cleaning
lady gets here.

It's the sign condition, not the class condition, that might abend on
"ABCDEF", which is why it needs to be guarded by a NUMERIC class
condition.
```

<a id="t-d0c93dbc6e"></a>
## Thread: UNISYS Screen Codes — 1 msgs

### Re: UNISYS Screen Codes

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1999-02-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<joIu2.3724$Xl5.5053184@news1.mia>`
- **References:** `<79cpqs$c6j4@ns2.hgo.net>`

```
Duncan Kinder wrote:
>As a school assignment, I am developing an interactive program, the menus of
>which depend upon UNISYS screen codes.  I am working with COBOL 74.
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>hexadecimal numbers and they are staring back at me.

I assume you are referring to the Unisys A Series.  The following hex codes
are EBCDIC.

  HEX NAME  FUNCTION
  1C   FS   Left Delimiter: protected transmittable field
  1D   GS   Left Delimiter: right justified unprotected transmittable field
  1F   RS   Left Delimiter: normal unprotected transmittable field
  1E   US   Right Delimiter: end transmittable field
  4A   [    Will be changed into RS (1F) when terminal enters forms mode
  5A   ]    Will be changed into US (1E) when terminal enters forms mode

When a screen is transmitted ot the terminal an Escape-'W' is appended,
which places the terminal in 'forms mode'.  While in forms mode, the
user can only enter data into the fields defined between appropriate
left and right delimiter characters.  When the 'Xmit' key is pressed in
forms mode, only the data in the defined fields are transmitted to the
A Series mainframe.  The FS, GS and RS characters are the left delimiter
characters, and they specify what type of field it is.  The FS character
begins a 'protected transmittable' field.  This field is to send data to
the mainframe that cannot be changed by the user, such as a trancode.
The US character is the universal right delimiter for all fields.  The
left and right square brackets can be used instead of the normal left
and right delimiters, if you want to deal only with printable characters.
When the terminal enters forms mode, these are changed into the actual
left and right delimiters.

  HEX NAME  FUNCTION
  0E   SO   Start Reverse Video (ends with US or end of line)
  18   CAN  Start Blink (ends with US or end of line)
  3F   SUB  Bright Video (ends with US or end of line)

In addition, there are several highlighting characters.  When used, they
start the highlighting feature, and the highlighting continues to the
right until the first US character or the end of the line is encountered.
```

```
>

Platform: OS/390

> 3)  I do both

Platform: DEC/VMS

>3)  I do both


Note:  Under OS/390 with DF SORT (and SyncSort) for that matter - I 
use the FASTSRT compiler directive - makeing the program LOOK like an 
internal sort, but in reality CREATING an external sort.

Sorts within sorts were a BAD trick CA played on the industry with 
CA-SORT - once you start you can't stop.
```

<a id="t-ba1812ee7f"></a>
## Thread: 88 Definitions and Data Validation — 1 msgs

### Re: 88 Definitions and Data Validation

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-02-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<79fo5i$bag@dfw-ixnews4.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<xqau2.1739$ob2.4260@news.flash.net> <1999Feb4.132809.19503@giant> <6%Bu2.42$j3.54@testbox>`

```

Tom Eisenman wrote in message <6%Bu2.42$j3.54@testbox>...
>But in many systems the example you have given below will cause the program
>to abend (crash) the first time one of the customer fields is non-numeric.
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>


Not true, (either part)
   The ANSI/ISO Standard explicitly says that you do NOT get an
"incompatible data" condition when doing a class test - and I know of no
compiler that does so.   You can do a  NUMERIC (or NOT NUMERIC) test against
a USAGE DISPLAY elementary numeric field.
```

<a id="t-4e34ba35d4"></a>
## Thread: Function REM — 1 msgs

### Function REM

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 1999-02-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<79fpmq$4mm$1@server.cntfl.com>`

```
In the recent discussions concerning FUNCTION REM, there was
no mention that the result of the function is NUMERIC and that the
function can accept NUMERIC (non-INTEGER) arguments.

I have included a small program with word problems to demonstrate
this capability.

-------------------
      $set ans85 flag"ans85" flagas"e"
       identification division.
       program-id. func-rem.
      * demonstrates the functions rem, integer, and numval
       data division.
       working-storage section.
      * a numeric result
       01 nresult pic -z(6)9.99.
      * an integer result
       01 iresult pic -z(9)9.
       procedure division.
       begin.
           perform problem-1
           perform problem-2
           perform problem-3
           stop run
           .

       problem-1.
           display " "
           display "How many feet and inches are in a meter?"
      * there are 100 cm per meter, 2.54 cm per inch
      * and 12 inches per foot
           compute iresult = function integer ( ( 100 / 2.54 ) / 12 )
           display iresult " feet"
           compute nresult = function rem ( ( 100 / 2.54 ), 12 )
           display nresult " inches"
           .

       problem-2.
           display " "
           display "If an 8 foot peice of lumber is cut into"
           display "sections that are 2 1/2 feet long, how"
           display "many sections are there?"
           display "How much is left (in feet)?"
           display "How much is left (in inches)?"
           compute iresult = function integer ( 8 / 2.5 )
           display iresult " sections"
           compute nresult = function rem ( 8, 2.5 )
           display nresult " feet"
           compute nresult = function numval ( nresult ) * 12
           display nresult " inches"
           .

       problem-3.
           display " "
           display "If the lumber was cut into 2 1/4 foot sections?"
           compute iresult = function integer ( 8 / 2.25 )
           display iresult " sections"
           compute nresult = function rem ( 8, 2.25 )
           display nresult " feet"
           compute nresult = function numval ( nresult ) * 12
           display nresult " inches"
           .
-------------------------------
Rick Smith
e-mail: < ricksmith@aiservices.com >
```

<a id="t-adf3f7eec4"></a>
## Thread: JAVA calling COBOL DLL's — 1 msgs

### Re: JAVA calling COBOL DLL's

- **From:** Don Braffitt <braffitt@ma.ultranet.com>
- **Date:** 1999-02-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36BB66B2.74845F5F@ma.ultranet.com>`
- **References:** `<36BA23CE.F697D934@pwgsc.gc.ca>`

```
> I have been trying to call a cobol DLL from a java program. Can
> anyone help me ?
> I am using Micro focus Cobol and JAVA 1.1.7. Any advice would be
> helpful

I tried something similar with DIGITAL COBOL on DIGITAL UNIX.
I have not tried this same example with Micro Focus COBOL.

========================================================================

class jni5 {
  public static native long   jni5long(long arg1);
  public static native String jni5string(String arg1);

  static {
    System.loadLibrary("jni5");
  }

  public static void main (String[] args) {
    System.out.println("Java JNI5 long   = " +
Long.toString(jni5long(1)));
    System.out.println("Java JNI5 string = " + jni5string("Str1"));
  }
}
========================================================================

#include "jni5.h"
#include <stdio.h>
#include <string.h>
void JNI5COBLONG(long *arg1);
void JNI5COBSTRING(char *arg1);

JNIEXPORT jlong JNICALL Java_jni5_jni5long (JNIEnv *env, jclass this,
    jlong arg1) {
  long retlong;
  retlong = arg1;
  printf("C    JNI5 long   = %d (before call to COBOL)\n", retlong);
  JNI5COBLONG(&retlong);
  printf("C    JNI5 long   = %d (after  call to COBOL)\n", retlong);
  return retlong;
}

JNIEXPORT jstring JNICALL Java_jni5_jni5string (JNIEnv *env, jclass
this,
    jstring arg1) {
  const char *tmp = (*env)->GetStringUTFChars(env, arg1, 0);
  char retstring[100];
  strcpy(retstring, tmp);
  printf("C    JNI5 string = %s (before call to COBOL)\n", retstring);
  JNI5COBSTRING(retstring);
  printf("C    JNI5 string = %s (after  call to COBOL)\n", retstring);
  (*env)->ReleaseStringUTFChars(env, arg1, tmp);
  return (*env)->NewStringUTF(env, retstring);
}
========================================================================

       identification division.
       program-id. JNI5COBLONG.
       environment division.
       data division.
       linkage section.
       01 llong pic s9(9) comp.
       procedure division using llong.
       p1.  add 1 to llong.
            exit program.
       end program JNI5COBLONG.

       identification division.
       program-id. JNI5COBSTRING.
       environment division.
       data division.
       working-storage section.
       01 wloc pic 9(9) comp.
       linkage section.
       01 lstring pic x(100).
       procedure division using lstring.
       p2.  call "FINDNULL" using lstring wloc.
            move "2" to lstring(4:1).
            exit program.
       identification division.
       program-id. FINDNULL.
       environment division.
       data division.
       working-storage section.
       01 wsub pic x(100).
       linkage section.
       01 lstring pic x(100).
       01 wloc pic 9(9) comp.
       procedure division using lstring wloc.
       p3.  unstring lstring delimited by "h" into wsub count in wloc.
            exit program.
       end program FINDNULL.
       end program JNI5COBSTRING.
========================================================================

javac jni5.java
javah -jni jni5
cc -c jni5c.c
cobol -ansi -c -names upper jni5cob.cob
ld -shared -no_archive jni5c.o jni5cob.o \
  -L/usr/shlib/osf.1 -lcob -lcurses -lFutil -lots2 -lots \
  -L/usr/lib/libisam.a -L/usr/lib/libtps_stub.a -lsort -lexc -lm -lc \
  -o libjni5.so
setenv LD_LIBRARY_PATH \
  /usr/shlib:/usr/users/braffitt/jni5
java  jni5
========================================================================

C    JNI5 long   = 1 (before call to COBOL)
C    JNI5 long   = 2 (after  call to COBOL)
Java JNI5 long   = 2
C    JNI5 string = Str1 (before call to COBOL)
C    JNI5 string = Str2 (after  call to COBOL)
Java JNI5 string = Str2
========================================================================

- Don Braffitt
  DIGITAL COBOL project leader
  Compaq Computer Corporation
  don.braffitt@compaq.com
```

```
In article <36bf02c8.0@news2.uswest.net>,
"Chris Osborne" <chris_n_osborne@yahoo.com> wrote:
>
> In article <1999Feb7.235737.19513@giant>,
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> new programmers are concerned.

There are two aspects to this.  First, the original poster is using Stern
& Stern, which does not (IMO) bungle the 88-level explanation.

More importantly, one spends most of one's professional life attempting
to ferret out information over noisy channels.  One is lucky if the
exposition is merely bungled; there are those who are actively trying to
hide the information one needs to do one's job.

The ability to get information from even a bungled presentation is an
useful skill independent of the problem domain, and it is never too early
to practice it.
```

```
It was: 10 Feb 99 and with STARTLING insight,  "cwestbury@giant.intranet.com (Chris
Westbury)" posted "Re: 88 Definitions and Data Validation" to "comp.lang.cobol" :

-->In article <36bf02c8.0@news2.uswest.net>,
-->"Chris Osborne" <chris_n_osborne@yahoo.com> wrote:

-->> In article <1999Feb7.235737.19513@giant>,

-->> > You don't need more books.  You need to learn how to use the one you
-->> > have.

-->> However, to be fair, I know at least two books (one of them a vendor
-->> manual) that completely bungle (IMO) the 88 level explanation as far as
-->> new programmers are concerned.

-->There are two aspects to this.  First, the original poster is using Stern
-->& Stern, which does not (IMO) bungle the 88-level explanation.

-->More importantly, one spends most of one's professional life attempting
-->to ferret out information over noisy channels.  One is lucky if the
-->exposition is merely bungled; there are those who are actively trying to
-->hide the information one needs to do one's job.

-->The ability to get information from even a bungled presentation is an
-->useful skill independent of the problem domain, and it is never too early
-->to practice it.

-->Christopher Westbury

It bugs me that you seem to support not just the reasoning needed to resolve
confusing issues on one's own (good), but the logic that creates and disseminates
confusion in the first place (bad).  I know that you don't come right out and say
this.  It is implicit to some degree thus your apparent (tacit) complicity.

A TEXTBOOK or PRIMER on a subject that is not clear, well written, proofread,
professional, thorough and complete is a disservice to all concerned.  I feel this
way about "Cobol From Micro to Mainframe"  Grauer, Villar & Buss 3rd edition, �1991.
This book has errors of fact, misplaced diagrams, is incomplete in explanations,
misleading on some topics, and not very clear on others.  There is bad grammar,
mispelling, incomplete index, missing topics, etc.  I have to credit our instructor
for helping to weed past some the problems created by the book.  If your professional
life entirely depended on RTFM and this was the manual, you'd be in crisis!  It may
be that no better book has ever been written, doubtful, but there is no doubt at all
the first directive for a textbook, especially a primary one, is to elucidate not
obfuscate. 
The referenced book cost more than US$75 and is used in our course for 3 quarters of
introductory Cobol.  The discussion of 88 levels provided has not really filled me in
on the topic to where I can use them successfully, and 88's appear not to be that
complicated.....  This is true on a number of basic techniques that are 'covered' in
the book.
TS

primer
1an elementary textbook
2an introductory book.

textbook
 a book for use in studying, esp. a standard account of a subject.

elucidate
 throw light on; explain.

obfuscate
1obscure or confuse (a mind, topic, etc.).
2stupefy, bewilder.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: 88 Definitions and Data Validation

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** cwestbury@giant.intranet.com (Chris Westbury)

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

```
In article <36c1b794.1559077@news>,
NoLonger@ix.netcom.com (OldUncleMe) wrote:
>
> It bugs me that you seem to support not just the reasoning needed to
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> degree thus your apparent (tacit) complicity.

I'm sorry you got that impression, because I have devoted a large part of
my life to reducing confusion (without, however, having any noticeable
effect on the supply, which is apparently infinite).

When I say, "If they give you lemons, make lemonade!", that hardly means
I approve (even tacitly) of the lemons.

Take your own horror story for example.  There is certainly a very
special place in the afterlife reserved for the morons who published
Grauer, Villar, & Buss 3e, and an even hotter place for the morons who
adopted it for your course.

But what did you do?  Did you get the book unpublished?  Did you get the
book unadopted?  What you did, actually, was do what I meant, however
poorly I may have expressed it.  Your instructor worked around the
problems with book, and you warned others.  (Not that they'll listen.  If
they had any brains, they wouldn't have adopted the book in the first
place.)

As for the original poster, he was using Stern & Stern, and I stand by my
advice to him. 

Personally, I believe it is important for students to become aware of the
conditions that prevail over (most) of the industry before they make life
decisions.  Programing is not a profession like law, or medicine, or even
engineering, where whoever hires you must ipso facto listen to you.

Programing is a craft, like carpentry, or plumbing.  Someone tells you,
okay, I want the bath over here, the toilet here, and the sink here, and
then they leave you alone to do it.  You have complete control of your
work, but you don't get to pick the sink, or even to decide where it
goes.
```

```
-->
-->Personally, I believe it is important for students to become aware of the

-->engineering, where whoever hires you must ipso facto listen to you.
-->
-->Programing is a craft, like carpentry, or plumbing.  Someone tells you,
-->okay, I want the bath over here, the toilet here, and the sink here, and
-->then they leave you alone to do it.  You have complete control of your
-->work, but you don't get to pick the sink, or even to decide where it
-->goes.

-->Christopher Westbury, Midtown Associates, 15 Fallon Place, Cambridge, MA 02138

It's a difficult question, what to do about the problems I find in this book.  If I
write the publisher, it's basically  unsupported unless I research many problems with
the book.  That's doing their work for them, for free, with no acknowledgement.
With the likely chance that they will ignore me.  I was ignored in discussing this
with the department at my school.  They have some other pretty rotten books in their
curriculum.  Take the Operating Systems book they use:  it was published in 1992.
Full of errors in fact, presentation, current use, the line taken by the department
is that it's the best available.  It's simply not that;  a few minutes online turned
up much better texts.  But the discussion was over!  Case made.  

The book for networking concepts was so out of date.  They discuss OS/2Warp as a
major innovation, and NT as a sidelight.  (Try to find an OS/2 shop.  Try not to find
an NT shop.)  Unix was presented as something that exists but is not common....  This
is so inaccurate  and unrealistic as to be laughable.  The chapter on the osi model
didn't manage to make it understandable in 40 pages.  I saw a single web page that
made it all clear in 2 paper pages worth of presentation.  Let's not even get to the
subject of subnets.  We didn't in class because nobody could follow the course by
using the book.  This gem came from 'QUE'.  I've always had so much respect for that
publisher too!  I guess there can be a rotten apple in any barrel.

I've done a lot of plumbing and carpentry.  It's true, that like in most service
fields, you have to suit the customer's expressed wishes.  But to succeed in the job
and thus the field, you have to work to code, and more than that, you have to produce
a working product in the end, on both schedule and budget, which may not include
every design element or feature orignally expressed by the client.  This may vary by
method, placement, inclusion, etc.  While some aspects may be held as constants (no
exposed pipes, buy douglas fir doors from here, use oak moldings), some must be
variables or the job is not possible (have to put in a fake wall to hide the pipes,
have to spend more on doors than contracted for:  here's the addenda to pricing &
schedule; oak is fine, but painted moldings made of oak will not be smooth and will
require special ordering and handling expenses:  would you consider poplar or white
pine/fir/spruce?)        TS


"Come to think of it, there are already a million monkeys on a million
 typewriters, and Usenet is NOTHING like Shakespeare." Blair Houghton
***********entia non sunt multiplicanda praeter necessitate***********
++++++****tauceti****@****abraxis****.****com****for***email****++++++
```

```
>may want to provide some input.
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…

<snip>

How about:
    PERFORM VARYING A FROM -8 BY 1 UNTIL A > 8
        PERFORM VARYING B FROM -8 BY 1 UNTIL B > 8
            IF A**2 + B**2 = 65
                DISPLAY A B
                END-IF
            END-PERFORM
        END-PERFORM.
```

```
<wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> illuminated comp.lang.cobol thusly:

>I really like this answer.  It seems quite "clean" and appropriate to me.

I think it's rather redundant.  There's no need to check the negative
numbers; in fact we can omit half of the remaining quadrant:

    PERFORM VARYING A FROM 1 BY 1 UNTIL A > 8
        PERFORM VARYING B FROM 1 BY 1 UNTIL B > A
            IF A**2 + B**2 = 65
                DISPLAY A B
            END-IF
        END-PERFORM
    END-PERFORM.

(I didn't like the indent style, either.)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Student need help please...

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Barticus@att.spam.net (Randall Bart)
- **Date:** 1999-02-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36bf6d5c.43473192@netnews>`
- **References:** `<79i0l4$ori$1@news.worldonline.nl> <79j3uo$mtc@dfw-ixnews8.ix.netcom.com> <79knjj$12d$1@oak.prod.itd.earthlink.net> <79l8p3$mf5@dfw-ixnews12.ix.netcom.com> <36be3456.17202839@netnews> <36BEF305.9686B8F9@NOSPAMhome.com>`

```
'Twas Mon, 08 Feb 1999 07:21:57 -0700, when Howard Brazee
<brazee@NOSPAMhome.com> illuminated comp.lang.cobol thusly:

>> I think it's rather redundant.  There's no need to check the negative
>> numbers; in fact we can omit half of the remaining quadrant:
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>Maybe not, but there is a need to DISPLAY the negative numbers.

The original request was "i want to find" not "I want to display", but if
the object is to display them, let's try:

DISPLAY "1 8" 
DISPLAY "-1 8" 
DISPLAY "1 -8" 
DISPLAY "-1 -8" 
DISPLAY "4 7"
DISPLAY "-4 7"
DISPLAY "4 -7"
DISPLAY "-4 -7"
DISPLAY "8 1" 
DISPLAY "-8 1" 
DISPLAY "8 -1" 
DISPLAY "-8 -1" 
DISPLAY "7 4"
DISPLAY "-7 4"
DISPLAY "7 -4"
DISPLAY "-7 -4"

If the object is to find and display, I suggest

    MOVE 65 TO TARGET 
    PERFORM VARYING A FROM 1 BY 1 UNTIL (A * A) > TARGET
        PERFORM VARYING B FROM 0 BY 1 UNTIL B > A
            IF A**2 + B**2 = TARGET
                DISPLAY A B
                DISPLAY "-" A B
                DISPLAY A "-" B
                DISPLAY "-" A "-" B
                IF  A NOT = B
                    DISPLAY B A
                    DISPLAY "-" B A
                    DISPLAY B "-" A
                    DISPLAY "-" B "-" A
                END-IF
            END-IF
        END-PERFORM
    END-PERFORM.

More typing than the original, but for searching I insist on minimizing
the search space.

I have also generalized the solution, by not assuming that it's within 8.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Student need help please...

- **From:** cwestbury@giant.intranet.com (Chris Westbury)
- **Date:** 1999-02-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1999Feb8.004210.19519@giant>`
- **References:** `<79i0l4$ori$1@news.worldonline.nl> <79knjj$12d$1@oak.prod.itd.earthlink.net>`

```
In article <79knjj$12d$1@oak.prod.itd.earthlink.net>,
"Jerry Peacock" <bismail@bisusa.com> wrote:
>
> In article <79j3uo$mtc@dfw-ixnews8.ix.netcom.com>,
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
>     END-PERFORM.

This is a good problem for demonstrating some simple techniques that you
need for enumerating large combinatorial spaces.

First, notice that this problem is zero-symmetric because of the
squaring.  Furthermore, there's no point in testing zero because 65 is
not a square:

      PERFORM VARYING A FROM 1 BY 1 UNTIL A > 8
          PERFORM VARYING B FROM 1 BY 1 UNTIL B > 8
              IF A*A + B*B = 65
                  DISPLAY " " A "  " B
                  DISPLAY " " A " -" B
                  DISPLAY "-" A "  " B
                  DISPLAY "-" A " -" B
              END-IF
          END-PERFORM
      END-PERFORM.

This snippet performs only 64 tests, as opposed to the 289 tests
performed by the original snippet.  Furthermore, examination of the code
emitted by this particular compiler (VAX CoBOL) shows that each of the 64
tests is almost twice as fast as each of the 289 tests in the other
snippet (primarily due to changing A and B from signed to unsigned).  Of
course, this is not something you need to care about for most programs,
but combinatorial spaces have an unpleasant habit of exploding.

Second, notice that this problem is mirror-symmetric.  We find both {1 8}
and {8 1}.  The following snippet performs only 36 tests.  Furthermore,
if you look at it carefully, you can discover for yourself why the next
optimization would be to hoist the A*A term:

      PERFORM VARYING A FROM 1 BY 1 UNTIL A > 8
          PERFORM VARYING B FROM A BY 1 UNTIL B > 8
              IF A*A + B*B = 65
                  DISPLAY " " A "  " B
                  DISPLAY " " A " -" B
                  DISPLAY "-" A "  " B
                  DISPLAY "-" A " -" B
                  DISPLAY " " B "  " A
                  DISPLAY " " B " -" A
                  DISPLAY "-" B "  " A
                  DISPLAY "-" B " -" A
              END-IF
          END-PERFORM
      END-PERFORM.

Generally, though, displaying the mirror pairs like this is considered
bad form.  It is enough to display all the unique pairs.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Student need help please...

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com>
- **Date:** 1999-02-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36BEFEEC.7735EA11@NOSPAMhome.com>`
- **References:** `<79i0l4$ori$1@news.worldonline.nl> <79knjj$12d$1@oak.prod.itd.earthlink.net> <1999Feb8.004210.19519@giant>`

```
If I were writing this program, I would not write it to solve for the
number 65.  I would use it to solve for a variable.  We don't need a
program to solve for 65, but use the program to solve for a variety of
numbers of which 65 is the test which is turned into the teacher.

Therefore, I would not hard code the number 8, nor would I use any
shortcuts which would fail when I plug in a different number.  (test for
zero)
```


##### ↳ ↳ Re: Student need help please...

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1999-02-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<5vAv2.2798$wj1.5599331@news2.mia>`
- **References:** `<79i0l4$ori$1@news.worldonline.nl> <79j3uo$mtc@dfw-ixnews8.ix.netcom.com> <79knjj$12d$1@oak.prod.itd.earthlink.net>`

```
Jerry Peacock wrote:
>
>How about:
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>        END-PERFORM.

Very clear and succinct.  Just for fun, permit me to suggest a few
'improvements'.  One potentially big one is to change A**2 and B**2
to A*A and B*B respectively.  If the compiler is not clever enough
to recognize an integer power and use a different algorithm, it may
generate code to calculate the logarithms of A and B, multiply them
by 2, then calculate the antilogarithms.  Ugh!  Hundreds of lines
of assembly, and *very* slow.  The second is to calculate A**2 (A*A)
in your outer loop, a minor speed improvement.  In some compilers
the compute statement is less efficient than an individual multiply.
The third is to only iterate B using values >= A.  This excludes
duplicate solutions such as 8,1 and 1,8.  The fourth one is subtle,
and most compilers it wouldn't matter.  On some architectures making
the IF condition 'B*B + ASQ' instead of 'ASQ + B*B' can simplify the
generated code like this:

    ASQ + B*B -> move ASQ to temp1
                 multiply B by B giving temp2
                 add temp2 to temp 1

    B*B + ASQ -> multiply B by B giving temp
                 add ASQ to temp

And finally, display a space between A and B. :-)

           PERFORM VARYING A FROM -8 BY 1 UNTIL A > 8
               MULTIPLY A BY A GIVING ASQ
               PERFORM VARYING B FROM A BY 1 UNTIL B > 8
                   IF B*B + ASQ = 65
                       DISPLAY A " " B
                   END-IF
               END-PERFORM
           END-PERFORM.

    -8 -1
    -8 +1
    -7 -4
    -7 +4
    -4 +7
    -1 +8
    +1 +8
    +4 +7

Not that this application needs it, but depending on the compiler and
system architecture, the above code could run as much as hundreds of
times faster than the original.  There are times when it is useful to
know such things. :-)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Student need help please...

- **From:** cwestbury@giant.intranet.com (Chris Westbury)
- **Date:** 1999-02-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1999Feb8.231015.19525@giant>`
- **References:** `<79i0l4$ori$1@news.worldonline.nl> <5vAv2.2798$wj1.5599331@news2.mia>`

```
In article <5vAv2.2798$wj1.5599331@news2.mia>,
"Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net> wrote:
>
> PERFORM VARYING A FROM -8 BY 1 UNTIL A > 8
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> END-PERFORM.

After hoisting the first multiplication, you can hoist the addition:

  PERFORM VARYING A FROM -8 BY 1 UNTIL A > 8
      MULTIPLY A BY A GIVING ASQ
      SUBTRACT ASQ FROM 65 GIVING K
      PERFORM VARYING B FROM A BY 1 UNTIL B > 8
          IF B*B = K
              DISPLAY A " " B
          END-IF
      END-PERFORM
  END-PERFORM.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Student need help please...

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1999-02-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<fzVv2.3418$wj1.6213267@news2.mia>`
- **References:** `<79i0l4$ori$1@news.worldonline.nl> <5vAv2.2798$wj1.5599331@news2.mia> <1999Feb8.231015.19525@giant>`

```
Chris Westbury wrote:
>Judson McClendon wrote:
>>
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
>  END-PERFORM.

That's good.  This illustrates another point about optimization: on
non trivial code, it is essentially a never-ending process which tends
to give smaller and smaller returns as you progressively refine it. :-)
```
