- **From:** William Lynch <wblynch@att.net>
- **Date:** 1999-02-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36BBE01A.AF4C88@att.net>`
- **References:** `<A3mu2.10073$3J3.2520@news2.giganews.com> <79crha$92d$1@callisto.clark.net> <36ba08bf$4$tfs$mr2ice@news>`

```
Gilbert Saint-flour wrote:
> 
(snip)
> 
> What environment you run in is important: the path length of dynamic CALLs
> associated with recent COBOL compilers and LE run-times is significantly
> shorter than what it was in VS COBOL II, which was a poor performer in
> this area.

Dynamic calls weren't new to COBOL II, was there something particularly
nasty about their implementation with this compiler? Were/are COBOL II
dynamic calls noticeably slower than ones using OS/VS COBOL?

Bill Lynch
```

#### ↳ Re: Dynamic CALL

- **From:** Gilbert Saint-flour <gsf@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 1999-02-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36bc5b22$13$tfs$mr2ice@news>`
- **References:** `<A3mu2.10073$3J3.2520@news2.giganews.com> <79crha$92d$1@callisto.clark.net> <36ba08bf$4$tfs$mr2ice@news> <36BBE01A.AF4C88@att.net>`

```
In <36BBE01A.AF4C88@att.net>, on 06 Feb 1999 at 06:24, William Lynch
<wblynch@att.net> said:

>Dynamic calls weren't new to COBOL II, was there something particularly
>nasty about their implementation with this compiler? Were/are COBOL II
>dynamic calls noticeably slower than ones using OS/VS COBOL?

Yes, COBOL II dynamic CALLs are noticeably slower than OS/VS COBOL.  I
don't remember all the reasons, but IGZCLNK is one of them.

Gilbert Saint-flour <gsf@ibm.net>
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Dynamic CALL

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-02-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<79iu6b$2tm@sjx-ixn6.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<A3mu2.10073$3J3.2520@news2.giganews.com> <79crha$92d$1@callisto.clark.net> <36ba08bf$4$tfs$mr2ice@news> <36BBE01A.AF4C88@att.net> <36bc5b22$13$tfs$mr2ice@news>`

```

Gilbert Saint-flour wrote in message <36bc5b22$13$tfs$mr2ice@news>...
>In <36BBE01A.AF4C88@att.net>, on 06 Feb 1999 at 06:24, William Lynch
><wblynch@att.net> said:
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>

A) Most IBM mainframe shops used "static" calls with older COBOL compilers.
(Various performance and other reasons - that I won't go into)

B) When VS COBOL II first was introduced ("replacing" both DOS/VS COBOL
(VSE) and OS/VS COBOL (MVS & VM)) - one of the MAJOR complaints was
"performance" - particularly performance on CALLs (dynamic and static) - but
DYNAMIC Calls were a "disaster".  By about release 3.1, this began to be
improved - but it really took until LE before even vaguely comparable
dynamic CALL performance was reported.

The major "design" flaw (as I recall) with the earlier versions was how much
of the COBOL environment was "built" with each CALL.  This was particularly
true when CALLing from OS/VS COBOL to VS COBOL II or vice versa.  There were
"performance APARs" throughout the history of VS COBOL II but I don't think
the newsgroup really cares about this (now historical) trivia.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Dynamic CALL

- **From:** Roland.Schiradin@t-online.de (Roland Schiradin)
- **Date:** 1999-02-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<79q5vv$a9o$1@news04.btx.dtag.de>`
- **References:** `<A3mu2.10073$3J3.2520@news2.giganews.com> <79crha$92d$1@callisto.clark.net> <36ba08bf$4$tfs$mr2ice@news> <36BBE01A.AF4C88@att.net> <36bc5b22$13$tfs$mr2ice@news> <79iu6b$2tm@sjx-ixn6.ix.netcom.com>`

```
Bill
>B) When VS COBOL II first was introduced ("replacing" both DOS/VS COBOL
>(VSE) and OS/VS COBOL (MVS & VM)) - one of the MAJOR complaints was
>"performance" - particularly performance on CALLs (dynamic and static) -
but
>DYNAMIC Calls were a "disaster".  By about release 3.1, this began to be
>improved - but it really took until LE before even vaguely comparable
>dynamic CALL performance was reported.
The main problem under CICS was the PUSH/POP. Now with LE
this can be set on run-unit level. Unfortunally every CICS LINK/XCTL
creates a new LE run-unit.

I turn PUSP/POP of and save aprox 30 % CPU

Roland
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ IBM (LE and) COBOL Tuning (was Re: Dynamic CALL

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-02-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<79qgsm$bal@dfw-ixnews4.ix.netcom.com>`

<a id="t-8e7060015f"></a>
## Thread: Removing duplicates [LONG] — 10 msgs

### Removing duplicates [LONG]

- **From:** scm@bolo.freeserve.co.uk (Shaun C. Murray)
- **Date:** 1999-02-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36bcb34a.983999396@news.freeserve.net>`

```
I'm having a cross language contest at the moment with a file/data
handling problem and I'm wondering if any of the locals can come up
with a better solution than I have for COBOL. It would be nice to show
how valid COBOL really still is for this kind of task. Even with my
quick afternoon hack, it's looking good though.

I have an SQL database with 7.5 million records in the table, some of
which are duplicates. The task is to weed out the duplicates.

In SQL, this can be achieved with a simple SQL statement such as
selecting all rows grouped with count(*) > 1. This however requires
huge amounts of time and resources. More than the server, a dual
Pentium Pro 200 with 1Gb RAM, NT Server and MS SQL Server.

So, more traditional methods are needed.

I've got three solutions, one of which has completed already and I
know works. A Delphi app selects data down in manageable chunks into a
table and looks for duplicates (with SELECT count(*) etc)  before
creating a clean table of new data and putting it back onto the
database in another table. I'll copy the table across once I'm sure
it's correct. This took 29 hours to run.

As this was taking a long time, I wrote a COBOL app to run along side
on a similar machine. The 7.5 million records are comma delimited
lines straight from SQL Servers BCP command. They contain variable
length data.

The COBOL program is a typical conversion program in that it reads in
the line sequential file and then writes to an indexed file. The key
is the whole record as I don't want duplicates remember. Any WRITE
giving an invalid key is written off to a duplicates file for later
investigation. The ok records are then written off to another indexed
sequential file to form a valid BCP input file that I can bulk copy
into the database.

This works fine but I notice the count is getting slower as the index
gets bigger which is as I expected. After I started it off, I thought
of a few other ways of achieving the same goal but I'm not sure if I
they'd be quicker. For instance, I could sort the file first then just
go through it checking for matching lines next to each other. No index
needed. Any other ideas? File handling like this isn't really my
forte.

Program is as below. It's running at the moment and I'd guess from
it's status it'll take approximately 14 hours. Add in 2 hours to BCP
the data back and It'll beat Delphi and SQL by about 12 hours I'd
think ;-)

       ENVIRONMENT DIVISION.
       FILE-CONTROL.
       select In-File   ASSIGN "infile.dat"
           organization is line sequential.

       select Out-File  assign "outfile.dat"
           organization is indexed
           access mode is random
           record key is out-rec
           file status is out-status.

       select in-File-seq  assign "outfile.dat"
           organization is indexed
           access mode is sequential
           record key is ifs-rec
           file status is out-status.

       select dup-file assign "dupfile.dat"
           organization is line sequential.

       select flat-file assign "flatfile.dat"
           organization is line sequential.

       Data Division.
       File Section.

       FD In-File.
       01  In-Rec          pic x(101).

       FD Out-File.
       01  Out-rec         pic x(101).

       FD in-File-seq.
       01  ifs-rec         pic x(101).

       FD Dup-File.
       01  Dup-Rec         pic x(101).

       FD Flat-File.
       01  flat-rec        pic x(101).

       working-storage section.
       01 rec-count        pic 9(8) comp value 0.
       01 dup-count        pic 9(8) comp value 0.
       01 ok-count         pic 9(8) comp value 0.
       01 stat-count       pic 9(4) comp value 0.
       01 end-of-file      pic 9 comp value 0.
           88 eof          value 1 false 0.
       01 out-status.
           03  os1         pic 9.
           03  os2         pic 9.

       procedure division.
           display space upon crt
           display "Duplicate strip" at 0101
           perform find-dups
           perform update-stats
           display "Completed duplicate search" at 0601
           perform create-bcp
           stop run.


       find-dups section.
           open input in-file
           open output out-file
           open output dup-file

           perform until eof
               read in-file
                   at end
                       set eof to true
                   not at end
                       move in-rec to out-rec

                       write out-rec
                           invalid key
                               perform write-dup
                           not invalid key
                               add 1 to ok-count
                       end-write

                       add 1 to rec-count
                       add 1 to stat-count
                       if stat-count = 1000
                           perform update-stats
                           move 0 to stat-count
                       end-if
               end-read
           end-perform
           close in-file
           close out-file
           close dup-file
           .

       write-dup section.
           add 1 to dup-count
           move in-rec to dup-rec
           write dup-rec
           .

       update-stats section.
           display "Records read = " at 0201
           display rec-count at 0216
           display "Records ok   = " at 0301
           display ok-count at 0316
           display "Duplicates   = " at 0401
           display dup-count at 0416
           .

       create-bcp section.
           set eof to false
           move 0 to rec-count
           move 0 to stat-count
           open input in-file-seq
           open output flat-file

           perform until eof
               read in-file-seq
                   at end
                       set eof to true
                   not at end
                       move ifs-rec to flat-rec
                       write flat-rec
                       add 1 to rec-count
                       add 1 to stat-count
                       if stat-count = 1000
                           display "BCP records written = " at 0801
                           display rec-count at 0823
                           move 0 to stat-count
                       end-if
               end-read
           end-perform

           display "BCP records written = " at 0801
           display rec-count at 0823
           close flat-file
           close in-file-seq
           .



The third test I'm running is for those 'use a text processing
language' types. I know someone would say use awk so I'm doing just
that.

I'm running the following on  a Pentium II 300 with 96Mb ram, NT
server but this doesn't have a SCSI raid array as it's my PC, not the
real server. 

# remdup.awk 
{    
    if (data[$0]++ == 0)
        lines[++count] = $0
}

END {
    for (i = 1; i <= count; i++)
        print lines[i]
}

Which does the trick, certainly on small files, but chews up memory
and CPU cycles like theres no tommorrow. Incidently, the COBOL program
runs in less than 400K as an EXE compiled with Micro Focus COBOL 4.0
(32bit), the Delphi 3 compiled program uses 6Mb and the AWK script
(using GNU Awk 3.something) is using anything it can get it's grubby
mitts on.
```

#### ↳ Re: Removing duplicates [LONG]

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1999-02-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<79ioma$qpt$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<36bcb34a.983999396@news.freeserve.net>`

```
If you are looking for real speed, there are two fast changes
I would make that I bet will speed it up significantly.

First, the validation file.  Instead of writing the whole record,
write a record consisting of key only.  That is all you need for
a duplicate check.

Then, use the INVALID status for two streams.  One
line sequential output to send back, and one line sequential
output of duplicates. That way you do not have to unload
it at the end.

Shaun C. Murray wrote in message <36bcb34a.983999396@news.freeserve.net>...

…[211 more quoted lines elided]…
>(using GNU Awk 3.s
omething) is using anything it can get it's grubby
>mitts on.
>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…

```

#### ↳ Re: Removing duplicates [LONG]

- **From:** Robin Rosenberg <robin.rosenberg@telia.nospam.com>
- **Date:** 1999-02-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36BCF38D.F32435AB@telia.nospam.com>`
- **References:** `<36bcb34a.983999396@news.freeserve.net>`

```
"Shaun C. Murray" wrote:

> I'm having a cross language contest at the moment with a file/data
> handling problem and I'm wondering if any of the locals can come up
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> which are duplicates. The task is to weed out the duplicates.

Method A uses moderate amounts of memory
Use the unix utilities:
    sort <rawdata.dat|uniq >uniquedata.dat

Method B uses lots of memory, but *could* be fast or slow like h depending
on whether you have enough virtual memory.
Use perl instead of awk. The following script weeds out duplicates without
changing the order,

#!/usr/local/bin/perl
%lines=();
while(<>) {
  if ($lines{$_} != 1) {
    $lines{$_} = 1;
    print;
  }
}

Method C:
Some databases allow bulk load to proceed without error when duplicate keys
are encountered, i.e the duplicates are ignored.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Removing duplicates [LONG]

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Removing duplicates [LONG]

- **From:** cwestbury@giant.intranet.com (Chris Westbury)
- **Date:** 1999-02-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1999Feb8.002159.19517@giant>`
- **References:** `<36bcb34a.983999396@news.freeserve.net> <36bdb90e.1051010593@news.freeserve.net>`

```
In article <36bdb90e.1051010593@news.freeserve.net>,
scm@bolo.freeserve.co.uk (Shaun C. Murray) wrote:
>
> In article <36BCF38D.F32435AB@telia.nospam.com>,
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> Hmm, I can see that taking a looong time.

As in thermodynamics, for every problem there is a minimum amount of work
below which you cannot go.  _Any_ solution to this problem will take a
long time.  The question is, _how_ long (in relation to the other
solutions).  You know without even trying it that AWK is not competitive;
not because AWK is a bad language, but because an interpreted program has
more overhead than a compiled program.  You knew without even trying it
that a SQL solution is not competitive; not because SQL is a bad language
but because a database program has far more overhead than a procedural
program.

Sorts are pretty highly evolved procedural programs.  Your CoBOL program
is essentially using side effects; a lot of the hidden processing is
wasted.  I'm not saying I know where the comparison would come out, but
the sort is definitely what I would try first, because it requires no
programing.  I know I could beat the sort in CoBOL if I had to, but it
would take some time to tune, time that I don't need to spend if the sort
comes out in the ballpark.

There is of course the hardware effect.  A few years back, I had a lovely
benchmark consisting of a day's installment loan transactions from a
midsize retail bank.  It took four and a half hours to sort on a Dell
486.  The bank bought a shiny new RS/6000 from their friendly
neighborhood IBM salesman.  Based on the hardware specs in the glossy
brochure, they were expecting this machine to be many times faster than a
486, and were surprised to find that it took three and a half hours to
sort, though they shouldn't have been.  The same data set took almost
ninety seconds to sort on the development 3090.
```

#### ↳ Re: Removing duplicates [LONG]

- **From:** denis548@aol.com (Denis548)
- **Date:** 1999-02-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19990207101123.17882.00001090@ng-fw1.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<36bcb34a.983999396@news.freeserve.net>`

```
I did something like this years ago on a smaller (but not small) file.  I
suggest you sort the file (in place or to another sequential file).  Then make
another pass through the file culling duplicates.  

This sounds clunky BUT... this task will run as fast as I-O to the disk can be
done.  The fewer block  I-O's, the faster the run.  The I-O requirements of
locating a single record in an indexed file will take 3 to 10+ block I-O's.
Because of the huge number of records the index tree will also be huge,
magnifying the amount of physical I-O needed to navigate the tree in order.  

3 to 10+ I-O's on  a sequential file will get more done than 3-10 I-O's on an
indexed file.   The sort is also more efficient than navigating the index tree
of an index file.  I strenuously avoided this conclusion until I was faced with
actually applying it.  My sort-then-cull method was far faster than my initial
indexed file method.  That's why they do it that way on mainframes.

If you must do this in a database environment then I suggest dropping all the
indexes, create an index of just the fields (and the ROWID if possible) that
are to be checked.   When the index is created then fetch it one record at a
time and use the ROWID in the DELETE statement to remove the duplicate records.
 When you're done, recreate the indexes.  The goal again is to reduce I-O to
the minimum level.

Denis Dew
```

#### ↳ Re: Removing duplicates [LONG]

- **From:** "Jeff" <a@a.com>
- **Date:** 1999-02-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<lZuv2.3578$fz.12040977@storm.twcol.com>`
- **References:** `<36bcb34a.983999396@news.freeserve.net>`

```
If you are extracting from one table and inserting into another why not let
the SQL tell you if it is a duplicate, then simply move on to another?

Define the output table as having unique keys, read through one record at a
time, inserting them one at a time. If you get a duplicate record status
code back from your SQL server, then drop the record. This is assuming that
if a table is defined with unique keys, that it will not insert a duplicate
record, physically allowing only one record with that particular key in the
output table.

Another solution would be to use a new fanlgled process, called parallel
processing. Have several programs perform what you have already written, but
only for a group of keys. Say program 1 processes keys A-C, PGM2 D-F, PGM3
G-I ... AND SO ON. Easily done by passing each program a parm and using SQL
to select those particular keys. You may want to figure out the distibution
of the keys to better determine the key groupings. Either way it should
reduce processing time. Make sure you use different jobnames for each
grouping, else they will still run in sequence.
```

<a id="t-c4085be28b"></a>
## Thread: Utility to convert DISPLAY to PACKed — 3 msgs

_Topics: [`Migration and conversion`](topics.md#migration)_

### Utility to convert DISPLAY to PACKed

- **From:** "Darius Cooper" <Darius_cooper@Bigfoot.com>
- **Date:** 1999-02-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<79it35$dh5$1@news.megsinet.net>`

```
I have a file (this is on MVS) that contains a numeric field S9(9) SIGN
LEADING SEPARATE.
I want to sort this file. But, in the output I want to reformat this file to
get a S9(9) COMP-3 field.
We use SYNCSORT and it does Packed -to-Edited conversion but not the other
way around (at least my manual doesn't say how).

I check IEBGENR and found that it has some syntax to convert fields. I tried
the "ZD" (zoned-to-decimal??) option. It does not convert the signed field
properly.

Any utility I can use? It must be something that is always available on the
mainframe, because this will be part of a product that we market.

Ofcourse, I can always write a trivial COBOL program, but have my reasons
for trying to avoid that route if possible.

Any help will be much appreciated,

- Darius Cooper
```

#### ↳ Re: Utility to convert DISPLAY to PACKed

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-02-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<79iue3$32s@sjx-ixn6.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<79it35$dh5$1@news.megsinet.net>`

```
As you are on MVS (and I assume a relatively current COBOL compiler), why
not just do a simple internal SORT with the input file having the USAGE
DISPLAY field and the output file having Packed-Decimal.  Given the FASTSRT
compiler option, this should use the "external" sort utility to the extent
possible and work quite quickly and easily.
```

#### ↳ Re: Utility to convert DISPLAY to PACKed

- **From:** cwestbury@giant.intranet.com (Chris Westbury)
- **Date:** 1999-02-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1999Feb8.000638.19515@giant>`
- **References:** `<79it35$dh5$1@news.megsinet.net>`

```
In article <79it35$dh5$1@news.megsinet.net>,
"Darius Cooper" <Darius_cooper@Bigfoot.com> wrote:
>
> I have a file (this is on MVS) that contains a numeric field S9(9) SIGN
> LEADING SEPARATE.

That's ten bytes, and each comparison takes several instructions.


> I want to sort this file.  But, in the output I want to reformat this
> file to get a S9(9) COMP-3 field.

That's five bytes, and each comparison takes only one instruction. 

Therefore, reformat in the input, before releasing the record to the
sort, not in the output!


> We use SYNCSORT and it does Packed-to-Edited conversion but not the
> other way around (at least my manual doesn't say how).

You're looking at the output side.  If it exists at all, it's on the
input side.


> I check IEBGENR and found that it has some syntax to convert fields.  I
> tried the "ZD" (zoned-to-decimal??) option.  It does not convert the
> signed field properly.

That's correct.  SIGN LEADING SEPARATE is not the same as ZONED DECIMAL,
which has the sign overpunched on the rightmost digit.


> Any utility I can use?  It must be something that is always available
> on the mainframe, because this will be part of a product that we market.
>
> Of course, I can always write a trivial COBOL program, but have my
> reasons for trying to avoid that route if possible.

Too bad, because that's the way to go.

When you use a reformat utility, you must manually analyze your copy
books to prepare the reformat control cards, and you must somehow ensure
that you modify the control cards every time you change the copy books. 
When you use CoBOL, the compiler analyzes the copy books for you, and all
you have to do is recompile when they change. 

When you use a reformat utility, you must make two passes over the entire
input file: one for the reformating and one for the sort.  When you use
CoBOL, you make only one pass over the input file and reformat in memory
on the fly before you release the record to SYNCSORT.
```

<a id="t-0c58ec9513"></a>
## Thread: Fujitsu Cobol85 v 3: setting dll folder — 2 this month / 3 total

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers) · [`COBOL standards (ANS, ISO, 74/85/2002/2014)`](topics.md#standards)_

### Fujitsu Cobol85 v 3: setting dll folder

- **From:** dave <dbailey2@home.com>
- **Date:** 1999-02-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36BBB915.7C67CE44@home.com>`

```
	I recently downloaded the free version of Fujitsu's COBOL85 based on a
recommendation in one of the postings of this NG, now gone from my news
server.

	The immediate problem I have is in trying to compile, link and run a
couple of the sample programs provided in the download.  The compile and
link portions work fine but when I come to run the WINEXEC from the
PROGRAMMING-STAFF window it gives me an "Error Starting Program" Dialog
Box reflecting the message "A required .DLL file, F3BIPRCT.DLL, was not
found."  Moving the F3BIPRCT.DLL to the folder in which the source file
is located resulted in subsequent similar errors for other F3BI*.DLLs.
The only resolution I have found is to move all the .dll files into the
folder which contains the source file.

	Is there an Option that I can set so that I can tell WINEXEC where to
look for the required .dll files?  Or, alternatively, is there another
solution to the problem?

	Thanks in advance,

	Dave
```

#### ↳ Re: Fujitsu Cobol85 v 3: setting dll folder

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1999-02-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<79hm47$8mq$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<36BBB915.7C67CE44@home.com>`

```
When you do the standard install, Fujitsu modifies your
default path.  It sounds like you bypassed that step. Check
that your autoexec has "C:\FSC\COBOL97" in the path(or
wherever you installed it which could also be the problem)

You should also have the environment variable for "TMP"
set to a nice big area, and the environment variable "LIB"
set to \FCS\COBOL97 and \FSC\PCOBOL32.

dave wrote in message <36BBB915.7C67CE44@home.com>...
> I recently downloaded the free version of Fujitsu's COBOL85 based on a
>recommendation in one of the postings of this NG, now gone from my news
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
> Dave
```

## Thread: Retrieve DSN in COBOL — 3 msgs

_Topics: [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe)_

### Re: Retrieve DSN in COBOL

- **From:** cityboy@marz.com
- **Date:** 1999-02-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<z5Ru2.1450$m5.16755361@typhoon.stlnet.com>`
- **References:** `<79a3iu$ovm$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <PRuu2.1409$m5.15974805@typhoon.stlnet.com> <36BB052B.DA236BF@NOSPAMhome.com>`

```
>Please tell people what environment that will work in, and any other
>variables (JCL, etc) required to run it.

The original poster clearly asked the question in an MVS environment.
Therefore the subroutine is for MVS (OS/390). Why should I have
restate what's already been answered? The comments in the program also
clearly state how to call the program from cobol and what JCL is
required ie. you pass the subroutine the ddname of the file for which
you want the information.

However one thing to note is if you compile the cobol program with
dynam you should also use the data(24) option so the call parameters
are in the correct addressing mode otherwise 0C4 may result.
```

#### ↳ Re: Retrieve DSN in COBOL

- **From:** Gilbert Saint-flour <gsf@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 1999-02-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36bc5d72$14$tfs$mr2ice@news>`
- **References:** `<79a3iu$ovm$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <PRuu2.1409$m5.15974805@typhoon.stlnet.com> <36BB052B.DA236BF@NOSPAMhome.com> <z5Ru2.1450$m5.16755361@typhoon.stlnet.com>`

```
In <z5Ru2.1450$m5.16755361@typhoon.stlnet.com>, on 06 Feb 1999 at 06:14,
cityboy@marz.com said:

>However one thing to note is if you compile the cobol program with dynam
>you should also use the data(24) option so the call parameters are in the
>correct addressing mode otherwise 0C4 may result.

You can avoid having to specify DATA(24) by making your assembler module
bimodal, simply add:

FILEINFO AMODE ANY

Also, SSREGS is not a standard IBM macro; you should replace it with YREGS
which is a standard macro in SYS1.MACLIB.

Gilbert Saint-flour <gsf@ibm.net>
```

- **Date:** 1999-02-07T00:00:00

### Re: Need a sanity check...

- **From:** "Jose Ferreira Neto" <rdd35921@mail.telepac.pt>
- **Date:** 1999-02-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<79kn1m$ih5@brown.telepac.pt>`
- **References:** `<79dg27$eit$1@fir.prod.itd.earthlink.net>`

```
Hi,

Some employers are investing in training and contract people to work with
COBOL in mainframes. This approach consist in:

? Course from one to two months in mainframe COBOL (include JCL, CICS and
DB2)
? Celebrate a contract from 4 to 5 years in low salary basis, beginning with
a media from a six hundred dollars (in general, each year is incremented in
more six hundred)
? After 3 years (minimum time to consider a medium level COBOL programmer)
you are ready to trying another JOB

In time:

Y2K project is not unique use to a COBOL programmer. Today, new systems are
developed in this language. After this, maintenance is necessary in thousand
systems.

Mainframe is the future and COBOL is most used language in this environment
(MVS is the best system created to computers).

�Mainframe is back, Long Live to Mainframes�

Good luck
```

<a id="t-917e78eef5"></a>
## Thread: Shorten the COBOL program run time — 1 msgs

### Re: Shorten the COBOL program run time

- **From:** William Lynch <wblynch@att.net>
- **Date:** 1999-02-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36BBDEE5.452BB606@att.net>`
- **References:** `<79cedt$771@news.asiaonline.net> <36BAD3BA.5951337F@zip.com.au>`

```
Ken Foskey wrote:
> 
(snip)
> 
> In MVS:
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> statically linked.

Minor nit, this is true only if you are using the NODYNAM compile option
(required for CICS pgms). If you're using DYNAM (recommended for batch)
COBOL will generate a dynamic CALL regardless of the format of the CALL,
i.e., the called module will not be a part of the calling program's load
module. CALLing a data name forces a dynamic call even if you're using
the NODYNAM option.

Bill Lynch
```

```
Roger Weill wrote:
> 
> I know this is a simple question but there seems to be some confusion here
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> position as printer control characters without having to specify RECFM?

Always use FBA (or FA) as the printers still need to know that the ASI
characters are there when you print it.

Ken
```

## Thread: A STUPID nebie question...I'm BOGGING for help!!! — 11 msgs

### A STUPID nebie question...I'm BOGGING for help!!!

- **From:** kmckenna@q-com.com
- **Date:** 1999-02-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<79kgvf$5is$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
Hi,	Thanks for even looking at this post. I am currently in 2 different
programming courses. C (Which I am picking up really fast) and COBOL 1. We
are reading Stern/Stern v.8. The instructor went right through the first 3
divisions and now, 3 weeks later we are in the procedure division, whic is
also making sense to me, because it's just functions, and I get those.	   I
understand the ID DIV...I'm not THAT stupid, but I'm lost in the environment
and data Div's.  IF, anyone of you folks would be so kind as to explain to me
how these divisions work, like I'm a 3 year old....maybe I'll get through
this class with my sanity. I have bought the "Cobol for Dummies" book, and it
doesn't help. The author goes right past these two divisions as well.	  
The instructor says I'll "get it" because he is also my C instructor, and he
figures I'm making it harder than it is, but I can't figure out what you are
supposed to write in these divisions...exactly. I'm HOPING that someone out
there has been where I am, and got through it, and figured it out, and would
be kind enough to help me. I KNOW, it's something stupid, and then it will
make sense to me.	  I get confused with the selects and assigns and
then in data division, I get confused in the FD's and then in Working
Storage, and HOW you actually set them up.	  I would be very grateful to
anyone who would care to help in whatever way I can. Thanks, Kevin
kmckenna@q-com.com

http://www.dejanews.com/       Search, Read, Discuss, or Start Your Own

#### ↳ Re: A STUPID nebie question...I'm BOGGING for help!!!

- **From:** denis548@aol.com (Denis548)
- **Date:** 1999-02-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19990207132134.19637.00001098@ng-fu1.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<79kgvf$5is$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
As briefly as possible:

The environment division is where cobol compilers interface to files that are
outside the direct control of the compiler.  Files can be accessed but fully
controlled like you would in an operating system.  There's more to it than that
but that's the main use.  

The data division declares and "subdivides" storage.   It's like c struct's. 
Any field can be declared in such a way that it has a structure.  At program
startup, memory is allocated and initialized as shown in the data division.  If
you do not specify otherwise, all data division entries are global in the
program.  

All paragraphs in cobol are "visible" to all other paragraphs in a program as
well.  Think of memory as the playing field and paragraphs as spectators. 
Stadium seating...

Hope that helps
```

#### ↳ Re: A STUPID nebie question...I'm BOGGING for help!!!

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1999-02-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<79knch$3gd$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<79kgvf$5is$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
The two divisions are quite simple, really.  The environment
says where everything is.  What computer you are going to
run on, where the files are located on that computer
(or network), etc.  A lot of it is documentary, but the selects
in particular link file descriptions that you plan to use to specific
files on the computer.  The type of file, the access method, and
information on sharing is also included.

The data division is where you describe your data.  File descriptions
are included, but only that portion that describes the data, not
how it is accessed because that could vary.  For example,
you could have a customer file that existed on disk and was
accessed as an ISAM file in one program, and the same data
spooled off onto tape and accessed as a line sequential tape
file in another program.  The FD's could both be copied from
the same library into two programs, but with each having
different select clauses.

Cobol is highly typed: in other words, every data name must
be described before it can be used in the program. The data
division also includes working-storage where you describe
all the variables that you want to use in your program. It is
pretty well the same as declaring variables in C, except that
it is all done in one place, and the data types that you can
set up are several thousand times as detailed.  C exists on
half a dozen basic types.  Cobol allows you to create your
own types with records and pictures.

it

he

would

#### ↳ Re: A STUPID nebie question...I'm BOGGING for help!!!

- **From:** NoLonger@ix.netcom.com (OldUncleMe)
- **Date:** 1999-02-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36be6391.11134994@news>`
- **References:** `<79kgvf$5is$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
It was: Sun, 07 Feb 1999 17:02:43 GMT and with a STARTLING amount of insight,
"kmckenna@q-com.com" posted "A STUPID nebie question...I'm BOGGING for help!!!" to
"comp.lang.cobol" :

-->Hi,	Thanks for even looking at this post. I am currently in 2 different
-->programming courses. C (Which I am picking up really fast)

<!...wow, I hope I can pick up c really fast....>

-->understand the ID DIV...I'm not THAT stupid, but I'm lost in the environment
-->and data Div's.  IF, anyone of you folks would be so kind as to explain to me

The environment division is the place to name and tell the location of the files you
will be using in your program.  (Nearly everything else normally done here is quite
specific to the machine you're working on, and usually optional.)  Say what kind of
file(s) they are, the path, explicitly, if it is not in the same place as the
program, with some compilers, and with others, always say the full path.  Describe
the type of file it is.  How it will be accessed.  Special features of the file(s).  

            Select no-name assign to 'c:\somedir\some.file'  
              organization is indexed
              access mode is dynamic
              record key is no-acct
              alternate record key is no-cust with duplicates
              file status is ww09-no-status.

no-name is the file name you will use in your program,
         the name has been assigned to the given file for
         internal use only.
path and filename:  'c:\somedir\some.file'  
how it's organized: the program must know explicitly
                                    (line sequential ??) (indexed ??)
how it's accessed:  dynamic?  random? some other way??
some advanced features follow the above, you'll get there if you're around for a
while.






DATA DIVISION:

       fd  idxn-mast.
       01  idxn-rec.
           02 idxn-acct       pic 9(5).
           02 idxn-cust       pic x(20).
           02 idxn-type       pic x.
           02 idxn-begbal     pic S9(4)v99.
           02                 pic x(48).

This describes the organization of a file, broken into records.  Each record is
considered to be 80 columns (bytes) long.  This one is broken into 
5 bytes=they are numeric data
20bytes=they are alpha-numeric
1 that is alpha-num
4 + (implied decimal point) + 2 = all numeric
48 bytes that are unused, but enumerated to fill out the record
__
80

Think of these records as lines on a page, each line is broken into described
'fields'; together all the lines make up a file, each line is 80 characters long (in
this case.)  The fields are sized as above, in order, from left to right, and contain
either data of the type specified.  If empty though, for numeric fields, zeros;  for
alphanumeric fields, spaces.  (Hopefully; though testing to assure that all of this
is true has been the goal of a lot of the programming I've encountered...)

When a record (line) of a file is read into memory by the program, the definitions
given in DD not only place the data into known memory locations but allow the program
to understand what the data is and therefore to work with the data and, perchance, to
find and request of the operating system the correct data and amount of data from
storage of some kind--disk, tape, punch card, memory, sundial.  An FD entry is often
made to describe data that will be output after processing by the program, say, to a
printer, a file, a tape drive, a card puncher, a subprogram, a servo motor
controller?

FD stands for File Description.  The WORKING STORAGE section of the data division,
OTOH, defines memory allocated for the use of the running program, not in association
with reading into ram some file's record, but for intermediate processing steps.  It
is by type of data also.  Each field is normally named something that can be referred
to.  You can move a chunk of data to a location, modify it, copy it, add to it, move
it to a described record, write it to a printer or a file.  You can compound w/s
entries to create tables or arrays that hold multiple data items in memory in an
organized, indexed even, fashion.  Then you read it, massage it, oil it, put it to
bed and it's very nice to you.  TS




"Come to think of it, there are already a million monkeys on a million
 typewriters, and Usenet is NOTHING like Shakespeare." Blair Houghton
***********entia non sunt multiplicanda praeter necessitate***********
++++++****tauceti****@****abraxis****.****com****for***email****++++++
```

#### ↳ Re: A STUPID nebie question...I'm BOGGING for help!!!

- **From:** cwestbury@giant.intranet.com (Chris Westbury)
- **Date:** 1999-02-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1999Feb8.002502.19518@giant>`
- **References:** `<79kgvf$5is$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
In article <79kgvf$5is$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>,
kmckenna@q-com.com wrote:
>
> I am currently in 2 different programming courses.  C (which I am
> picking up really fast) and COBOL 1.

No offense, but this is not a good idea.  It's like trying to study flute
and clarinet at the same time.  There's no problem with studying them
separately (in either order), but if you try to learn them at the same
time, the clarinet embouchure interferes with the flute embouchure, and
it plays hob with your ear because the clarinet is in B-flat and the
flute is in concert.


> We are reading Stern/Stern v.8.

A good choice.


[...]
> I have bought the "Cobol for Dummies" book, and it doesn't help.

"Helen" will be along presently to recommend another book.  :-)

But seriously, and again no offense, what do you think I could possibly
write off the top of my head in this tiny 80 x 24 space that would help
you more than the two professionally-written, -edited, and -printed books
that you already have ??  Usenet is an extremely limited medium.

What you need is to find someone you can talk to in person, perhaps
someone who took the course last year.  It doesn't take more than a few
hours to "get it".

Pay particular attention to the DATA DIVISION, which is, if anything,
more important than the PROCEDURE DIVISION.  Above all, don't stint on
the exercises!  Exercises give you the prerequisites for understanding
even though it may not seem like it while you are doing them.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: A STUPID nebie question...I'm BOGGING for help!!!

- **From:** kjmckenna@q-com.com (BlindWolf)
- **Date:** 1999-02-09T00:00:00

- **Message-ID:** `<36c04e56.1121789@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<79kgvf$5is$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <1999Feb8.002502.19518@giant>`

```
Chris,
   Thanks for the input, and I agree, taking these two courses at the
same time is difficult, but it is what the college I attend suggested,
based on my background.
   I have gotten the answers I needed from some very kind people in
this group. The answer wasn't all that difficult now that I see it for
what it was. I couldn't figure out the select and assigns for the PC I
am on. I missed a couple of quotes and periods, and now that I see the
syntax and the order in which it is used, it makes sense to me...as
does the data division.
   I've put the COBOL for dummies on the shelf, and have gotten fully
into Stern. 
   I do have one opinion though, I'm actually finding that one class
helps the other. I am finding that although the two languages are
completely different, they do reinforce the basic ideas of each other.
For example, the "If" statements and how one uses them becomes
clearer, to me anyway, when looking at the different ways they are
used in the two languages.
   So, with all that said, maybe I can get into the procedure division
and get my but caught up. I understand how things are being done, at
least, in the procedure division, and now that the first 3 divisions
make sense, maybe I can get an A in this course...who knows?
Thanks,
Kevin

## Thread: Negative #'s as Input — 4 msgs

### Re: Negative #'s as Input

- **From:** Barticus@att.spam.net (Randall Bart)
- **Date:** 1999-02-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36bd3f21.21793196@netnews>`
- **References:** `<79asol$n25$1@ash.prod.itd.earthlink.net> <36B9AFF7.5C6F8D9A@NOSPAMhome.com> <1999Feb4.200252.19507@giant>`

```
'Twas 4 Feb 99 20:02:52 EST, when cwestbury@giant.intranet.com (Chris
Westbury) illuminated comp.lang.cobol thusly:

> The
>Ryad-2, the Univac 9000 series, and the RCA Spectra 70 series were among
>the first knockoffs of the IBM System/360 architecture.

The RCA Spectra 70 wasn't a knockoff of the S/360.  The Spectra 70 and
S/360 were identical computers introduced by two different companies on
the same day.  Each company sued the other saying the design was stolen.
The courts determined that both companies had spies in each other's
plants, knew the other side had spies in their plant, and therefore it
would never be decidable just who robbed who.
```

- **Message-ID:** `<1999Feb8.000914.19516@giant>`
- **References:** `<79asol$n25$1@ash.prod.itd.earthlink.net> <36bd3f21.21793196@netnews>`

```

…[7 more quoted lines elided]…

the S/370, not the S/360.

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Negative #'s as Input

- **From:** Barticus@att.spam.net (Randall Bart)

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36bf66f3.41833131@netnews>`
- **References:** `<79asol$n25$1@ash.prod.itd.earthlink.net> <36bd3f21.21793196@netnews> <1999Feb8.000914.19516@giant>`

```
'Twas 8 Feb 99 00:09:14 EST, when cwestbury@giant.intranet.com (Chris
Westbury) illuminated comp.lang.cobol thusly:

>In article <36bd3f21.21793196@netnews>,
>Barticus@att.spam.net (Randall Bart) wrote:

>> The RCA Spectra 70 wasn't a knockoff of the S/360.  The Spectra 70 and
>> S/360 were identical computers introduced by two different companies on
>> the same day.

>Small but important correction: the Spectra 70 was plug-compatible with
>the S/370, not the S/360.
 
According to http://www.islandnet.com/~ianc/dm/0/006.html, the first
Spectra 70 shipped in Oct 1965, the first 370 shipped "Aft 1971" (that's
what it says).  It is amazing that RCA could ship SIX YEARS before IBM and
then still lose the market.  According to the same page the first 360 was
May 1965.  (ISTM that these dates are all a few months late, but doing a
net search I keep finding documents with identical dates, which reference
ona another.)
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Negative #'s as Input

- **From:** "Warren Simmons" <w.g.simmons@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 1999-02-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<79nd73$3fp@bgtnsc01.worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<79asol$n25$1@ash.prod.itd.earthlink.net> <36bd3f21.21793196@netnews> <1999Feb8.000914.19516@giant>`

```
RCA "3xx" computers were not hardware compatible with the IBM
computers.  Ask Exxon!
RCA computers did something better in hardware than the IBM systems, and
programs compiled on one system could not run without re-compiling on the
other.  Exxon put one of each in their shop, and found out the hard way.

At least that's the way I remember it.

Warren Simmons

blind_wolf@hotmail.com (BlindWolf) wrote:

>I've just started COBOL 1, and the damned instructor ordered the wrong
>book. Stern/Stern without the compiler. So, now we are all scrambling
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>blind_wolf@hotmail.com

I've been a Micro Focus programmer for many years now.  Nothing has
filled this need better than The Semware Editor (TSE).  It will do
eveything I need it to do and plus some!

You probably won't need this capability, but it will suck in files
like you read about.   Seems to me it was suck in a 10meg ASCII
file before you do anything else.

Because MF COBOL is such a robust language using a whole bunch
of words and verbs, I have found this text editor (TSE) to be
excellent for that purpose.  Just do a search on Semware or
"Semware Editor" and it will do an excellent job of it.

The instruction manuals are very, very simple to read.  Finding
an

##### ↳ ↳ Re: I'm Looking for a good editor/compiler/debugger pkg for Win95

- **From:** Warren Porter <warrenp@ASPMnetdoor.com>
- **Date:** 1999-02-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36BF13DB.351715@ASPMnetdoor.com>`

```
I have also been a user of TSE for several years, but in our unix shop the
files it writes out all have lower case names, not our shop standard.
After learning a bit about vi's bells and whistles, I don't go back to TSE
unless I have to do wholesale changes to prompt numbers or line numbers.
The macros a user can write in TSE can be invaluable when adding a new
field to a screen and it is necessary to move everything down and renumber
prompts.

Of course, it the user can configure TSE to kick off their compiler when
needed, TSE is priceless.

## Thread: Multiple RELEASE in SORTs - Newbie question... — 3 msgs

_Topics: [`Tutorials, books, learning`](topics.md#learning) · [`Help requests and how-to`](topics.md#help)_

### Re: Multiple RELEASE in SORTs - Newbie question...

- **From:** "PAULO CRUZ" <scruz_paulo@mail.teleweb.pt>
- **Date:** 1999-02-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<79kd2m$bsg$1@fenix.maxitel.pt>`
- **References:** `<79d6am$c9t$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <36BAFEEF.234B96C2@ASPMnetdoor.com>`

```
It surprises me a bit the general trend against COBOL SORTING.
Contrary to this anti-COBOL SORT tide, I very often take advantage of
such a tool, a quick and reliable one - without the burden of further file
description and handling. Of course, the main way of retrieving data
from files should always be by means of their keys (primary or alternates -
the subject of another thread in this n.g.). But sometimes (very frequently
in
my case) you need different ways of sorting your records and COBOL SORT
is up to the job as far as I modestly know.

Someone (another poster) somewhere else (another thread) looking for
anti-COBOL input should peep into here (this thread).

As for the question in the first post, it looks a little messy the author's
aproach and
it wouldn't certainly work. Personally, I would imply a class-code for every
record and
follow something like this:

Main.
   move start-your-file-key-value to your-file-key
   start your-file
   if ok-status 'I rather use declaratives'
      perform sequential (next or previous) read of your-file
      if ok-status
____________'added field'____________
         sort s-sort-file                                          |
         on ascending key s-location-code, s-class-code, s-id-number,
s-last-name
         with duplicates in order 'why not ?'
         input procedure sec01-input
         output procedure sec02-output
     end-if
   end-if

sec0-input.
   perform until eof-status or your-file-key not =
start-your-file-key-value
      initialize s-sort-record
      move your-file-location-field to s-sort-location
      if s-sort-location = 15
         move your-file-class-code to s-class-code
      else
         move low-values to s-class-code 'for instance'
      end-if
      make the other moves
      release s-sort-record
      perform sequential (next or previous) read of your-file
   end-perform

sec0-output.
    in the return paragraph you'll get the 15 location type sorted by
    their class-codes and thus you'll be able to handle/report them
    accordingly.

would this be of any help...?



Warren Porter wrote... <36BAFEEF.234B96C2@ASPMnetdoor.com>...
>Adroit evasion of COBOL SORT may also be a solution.  In what would be your
input
>procedure, build (randomly) an indexed file.  The "output procedure" would
first
>close the file, then open it for sequential reading.
>
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>> location, id number, then name, for example.  However, now if the
location is
>> 15, they want these records to be sorted by a new field(class code).
>> <snip>
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…

## Thread: ACCEPT / Y2K — 6 msgs

_Topics: [`Y2K and Year-2000 remediation`](topics.md#y2k) · [`Language features and syntax`](topics.md#syntax)_

### Re: ACCEPT / Y2K

- **From:** Barticus@att.spam.net (Randall Bart)
- **Date:** 1999-02-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36bd43a9.22952443@netnews>`
- **References:** `<36B9F4CE.1E4@club-internet.fr> <79d2v3$h6v@dfw-ixnews11.ix.netcom.com>`

```
'Twas Thu, 4 Feb 1999 15:19:58 -0600, when "William M. Klein"
<wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> illuminated comp.lang.cobol thusly:

>The current ANSI/ISO Standard is definitely NOT Y2K compatible. It
>absolutely positively MUST return a 2-digit year. 

Checking the draft standard, Bill is correct.  Page 403, 14.10.1.3 GR 7
and 9 clearly specify "The two low order digits of the year in the
Gregorian calendar."  I don't have a copy of X3.23-1985; is it as
rigorous?

But don't trust the documentation.  I would try this program:

 <the usual>
 77  THE-DAY      PIC 9(8).
 77  THE-DATE     PIC 9(8).
 PROCEDURE DIVISION.
 A.
     ACCEPT THE-DATE FROM DATE.
     ACCEPT THE-DAY  FROM DAY.
     DISPLAY THE-DATE " " THE-DAY.
     STOP RUN.

Run this in a time machine.  Eight digits is overkill to check for weird
effects.  Judson, run this with task attribute  DATEOFFSET=330.  My guess
is A Series displays "00000101 00100001", possibly "01000101 00100001" 
```

```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: ACCEPT / Y2K

- **From:** Barticus@att.spam.net (Randall Bart)
- **Date:** 1999-02-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36be9d60.1752052@netnews>`
- **References:** `<36B9F4CE.1E4@club-internet.fr> <79d2v3$h6v@dfw-ixnews11.ix.netcom.com> <36bd43a9.22952443@netnews> <Vwjv2.4188$Xl5.6058347@news1.mia>`

```
'Twas Sun, 07 Feb 1999 16:52:05 GMT, when "Judson McClendon"
<judmc123@bellsouth.net> illuminated comp.lang.cobol thusly:

>Randall Bart wrote:
>>William M. Klein wrote:
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>It uses the phrase "year of century".

Not as rigorous.  That means next year will be 100, and the year after is
1.  Either that or this year is 98 and next year is 99. I wonder how many
compiler writers implemented it that way.

>>[snip] Judson, run this with task attribute  DATEOFFSET=330.

>Sorry, I'm not familiar with that particular task attribute.

The DATEOFFSET attribute causes any current date call (including FUNCTION
CURRENT-DATE and ACCEPT FROM DAY/DATE) run on that stack to get a date
that many days in the future.  The attribute is inherited and may be
modified or interrogated anytime.  The range of the attribute is 0 to
8191.  Too bad it won't go negative; it would be useful for reposts.

This attribute was added recently.  It is not in the base release of the
44.1 or 44.2 MCP, but it's in an early patch release.
```

#### ↳ Re: ACCEPT / Y2K

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 1999-02-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<79n5f5$n0s$1@mail.pl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<36B9F4CE.1E4@club-internet.fr> <79d2v3$h6v@dfw-ixnews11.ix.netcom.com> <36bd43a9.22952443@netnews>`

```
For users of Unisys A Series COBOL74, the extensions toACCEPT FROM DATE and
ACCEPT FROM DAY described in the draft standard are available in SSR 45.1.
A parallel feature for the Unisys extension TODAYS-DATE is being introduced
in SSR 46.1.  And the current Interim Correction versions for all supported
releases of A Series COBOL74 contain these features.

For users of Unisys A Series COBOL85, all three of these features are being
introduced in SSR 46.1 and are also available in the Interim Correction
versions for all supported releases of A Series COBOL85.

The syntax for these three features looks like:
    ACCEPT <numeric-item-1> FROM DATE YYYYMMDD.
    ACCEPT <numeric-item-2> FROM DAY YYYYDDD.
    ACCEPT <numeric-item-3> FROM TODAYS-DATE MMDDYYYY.
The intent of each of these, as well as the capacity requirements of the
three destinations, should be obvious from the syntax.

    -Chuck Stevens

…[34 more quoted lines elided]…

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-02-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<79neci$ohb@dfw-ixnews8.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<36B9F4CE.1E4@club-internet.fr> <79d2v3$h6v@dfw-ixnews11.ix.netcom.com> <36bd43a9.22952443@netnews> <79n5f5$n0s$1@mail.pl.unisys.com>`

```
Chuck Stevens wrote in message <79n5f5$n0s$1@mail.pl.unisys.com>...

>
>For users of Unisys A Series COBOL85, all three of these features are being
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>

For those who might be confused, the first 2 of those ARE in the draft
Standard but the last (using TODAYS-DATE and MMDDYYYY) is *not* in the
draft - and my guess is that as the draft is (primarily) an ISO rather than
ANSI Standard, this "American date format" date seems pretty unlikely to
ever make it into the Standard.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: ACCEPT / Y2K

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 1999-02-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<79ngcu$9fp$1@mail.pl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<36B9F4CE.1E4@club-internet.fr> <79d2v3$h6v@dfw-ixnews11.ix.netcom.com> <36bd43a9.22952443@netnews> <79n5f5$n0s$1@mail.pl.unisys.com> <79neci$ohb@dfw-ixnews8.ix.netcom.com>`

```
I tried to make that clear in the first paragraph, when I described the
first two as part of the draft and the third as being "a parallel feature
for the Unisys extension TODAYS-DATE".  Sorry if anybody was confused by the
fact that I didn't repeat that detail in the second paragraph.   TODAYS-DATE
is indeed a Unisys extension, not part of any standard or draft of which I
am aware.

The feature is essentially a descendant of the Unisys A Series COBOL(68)
special register TODAYS-DATE, which provided direct access to a system date
function that produced the requested information in MMDDYY format.

    -Chuck Stevens

William M. Klein wrote in message <79neci$ohb@dfw-ixnews8.ix.netcom.com>...

being

>>versions for all supported releases of A Series COBOL85.
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…

<a id="t-c7b268b2ec"></a>
## Thread: Need good Cobol book for experienced programmer — 2 msgs

_Topics: [`Tutorials, books, learning`](topics.md#learning)_

### Re: Need good Cobol book for experienced programmer

- **From:** Barticus@att.spam.net (Randall Bart)
- **Date:** 1999-02-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36bd05db.7146786@netnews>`
- **References:** `<79i1go$q6t$1@news101.telia.com> <1X0v2.5345$OS5.5644522@news3.mia>`

```
'Twas Sat, 06 Feb 1999 19:42:53 GMT, when "Judson McClendon"
<judmc123@bellsouth.net> illuminated alt.cobol thusly:

>Paul Schwartzberg wrote:
>>Would someone know of a book for Cobol corresponding to what,
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>Try the ANSI COBOL 85 standards manual.

Or X3.23-1985 as it's know to its friends.  Or ISO 1989:1985.

But Jud, he asked for *succinct*.  The COBOL standard has an organization
which leads to redundancy, and it is also verbose and sesquipedalian.
Anyone programmer who can hack his way thru X3.23-1985 will be better for
it, but he'll also profit to read the mainframe programmer reference
manuals, which are far more accessible to the common reader.  I think the
best one I read was IBM COBOL for OS/VS Release 2.  Release 2 was
Cobol-74, release 1 was Cobol-68, and there was a world of difference in
the writing of those manuals.  IBM discovered tech writing in between.  I
liked all the IBM manuals from the mid to late 1970s.

The draft of COBOL-200x is better than X3.23-1985 in nearly every respect,
but what it lost in redundancy, it has gained and then some in features.
Unfortunately, the language standards (especially COBOL) are not written
for the programmer; they are written for the implementor.  This is just an
inherent part of the ANSI/ISO process.  It makes sense for everything but
computers.  The screw-thread standard is written for the people who make
the nuts and bolts, not the people who use the nuts and bolts.  To milk an
old analogy, a race car driver may know every bolt in his car, but does he
know the thread angles?  (A good race driver knows a machinist who knows
that answer.)

But with a language standard this isn't the case.  Absolutely every point
that is relevant to the compiler writer is also relevant to the compiler
user.  
```

#### ↳ Who is the Standard for (was Re: Need good Cobol book for experienced programmer

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-02-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol

- **References:** `<79i1go$q6t$1@news101.telia.com> <1X0v2.5345$OS5.5644522@news3.mia> <36bd05db.7146786@netnews>`

<ricksmith@aiservices.com> illuminated comp.lang.cobol thusly:

>"The subscript into that table would be equivalent to a pointer."

>This is case where semantics is important. For example, given

No this is a case where semantics is *not* important.  The original point
was that when someone knows one language and you are teaching a second,
you start with what in analogous or similar, and get into the sematics
later.

Randall Bart wrote in message <36bd16f8.11517717@netnews>...
>'Twas Thu, 4 Feb 1999 10:28:38 -0500, when "Rick Smith"
><ricksmith@aiservices.com> illuminated comp.lang.cobol thusly:
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>
The quoted sentences were within the context of an informal proof
(by reductio ad absurdum) of (IMO) the inaccuracy of the sentence
in the following restoration. As part of that proof, the meaning
("semantics") of subscript was required to demonstrate the
inaccuracy.

[The following was restored from the previous post.]
>>>  Remember, we're talking low-level assembly-type pointers or
>>>vectors here (pure addresses), not C-type pointers, where a length and
>>>data type are associated.
[End of restoration]

The context of the restored text was as an additional comment to
Ken Foskey and not as part of the "pointers are like subscripts"
analogy.

Therefore, in that case (the proof), semantics was important and
I did not disagree with the analogy or with "teaching by analogy."
-------------------------------
Rick Smith
e-mail: < ricksmith@aiservices.com >

## Thread: The Day — 1 msgs

### The Day

<a id="t-26ccc7627a"></a>
## Thread: Fujistsu 4.0 — 2 msgs

### Fujistsu 4.0

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1999-02-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<79kf82$rju$1@news.igs.net>`

```
I have been heavily involved with both PowerCobol and
Fujitsu Standard Cobol 4 over the last few months, and am
interested in hearing how others (if any) are using these
new tools.

One very interesting technique that I have just figured out
is to include an entire heritage program, complete with
screen section, in a PowerCobol program as a custom
method, then invoke it from other programs.  The only
caveat is that the program must be an infinite loop, which
took a bit of getting use to.

Typical code is a master file update that accepts a customer
number then goes into an add, change, delete routine depending
on what was entered.  The main loop accepts the customer
number on the background screen, and never exits from
that loop.  To exit, the user *must* click the upper-right-hand
close window box ... anything else gets into trouble.  In effect,
the entire old program, complete with screen, becomes an
icon on the PowerCobol Toolbar with single entry point
and an exit under control of the GUI.

Once that has been done, tested, and working, the program
can be devolved into several subroutines internally, and additional
methods added that call those subroutines.  The end result is
a "customer handling tool" that can look up customers, add
delete, and change customers, etc., for other programs. The
entire process can be done regardless of whether you choose
to use the GUI or not.  It can be left as character mode only
and still use the complete OOPS methodology without a GUI.
Personally, I have been using the GUI to get rid of single
keystroke handling.  It is kind of disconcerting to be running
a character mode screen, and have a typical "OK" box jump
up with a message, but it is really kind of neat how it works.

I am having lots of problems trying to come up with uniform
naming conventions, librarying methods, etc., but it is starting
to fall into place, even if my disk looks like a dog's breakfast.

I would be interested in hearing how other are doing.
```

#### ↳ Re: Fujistsu 4.0

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-02-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<79l887$m62@dfw-ixnews12.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<79kf82$rju$1@news.igs.net>`

```
Anyone interested in "porting" heritage COBOL applications to OO, might want
to contact Micro Focus <Merant> and ask them about the status of their
"COBOL as a Class" functionality.

About the time that I left MF, they introduced a facility that would let you
use ANY "old" COBOL program and treat each SECTION as a "method".  I don't
remember all the details, but it did seem to have some interesting aspects.
```

<a id="t-af89c91fd5"></a>
## Thread: ACCEPT / Y2K — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`Y2K and Year-2000 remediation`](topics.md#y2k) · [`Language features and syntax`](topics.md#syntax)_

### Re: ACCEPT / Y2K

- **From:** "Joel C. Ewing" <jcewing@acm.org>
- **Date:** 1999-02-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36BE4605.8BEAD7D4@acm.org>`
- **References:** `<36B9F4CE.1E4@club-internet.fr> <19990205013208.28378.00000993@ng-fb1.aol.com>`

```
Theo DP wrote:
> 
> >Subject: ACCEPT / Y2K
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
> will get you an 8 digit date.

And I believe this function definition is part of the formal ISO/ANSI
COBOL Extensions, so the complete current COBOL standard does include
4-digit year support if you are running on a compiler that is current
enough to support the functional extensions.  Although there are some
ACCEPT syntax extensions implemented by some compilers, the old ACCEPT
syntax should not have its semantics arbitrarily changed for 4-digit
years, as this would break existing programs (including those programs
that are Y2K compliant despite the 2-digit year from ACCEPT because they
use windowing or some other technique as required to interpret the
date)  
```

program.  My program compiles until it hits the procedure division and
encounters my MOVE commands...please help... Thanks in advance  ERIC

       IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
       PROGRAM-ID. Prog1.
       AUTHOR.   ERIC.

       ENVIRONMENT DIVISION.
       INPUT-OUTPUT SECTION.
       FILE-CONTROL. SELECT CARD-FILE  ASSIGN TO SYS016-UR-2501-S.
                     SELECT PRINT-FILE ASSIGN TO SYS018-UR-1403-S.

       DATA DIVISION.
       FILE SECTION.
       FD  CARD-FILE  LABEL RECORDS ARE OMITTED
                      RECORDING MODE IS F.
       01  CARD-RECORD.
           05  ESTABLISHMENT-NAME               PIC X(20).
           05  ESTABLISHMENT-ADDRESS.
               10  STREET-NUMBER                PIC 9(04).
               10  STREET-NAME                  PIC X(15).
               10  ZIP-CODE                     PIC 9(05).
           05  PHONE-NUMBER.
               10  AREA-CODE                    PIC 9(03).
               10  PREFIX                       PIC 9(03).
               10  EXCHANGE                     PIC 9(04).
           05  SERVICE-DATE.
               10  S-YEAR                       PIC 9(04).
               10  S-MONTH                      PIC 9(02).
               10  S-WEEK-OF-MONTH              PIC 9(01).
           05  DELIVERY-PERSON-NUMBER           PIC 9(02).
           05  BOTTLES-DELIVERED                PIC 9(05).
           05  BOTTLES-RETURNED                 PIC 9(05).
           05  BOTTLES-ORDERED-FOR-NEXT-WEEK    PIC 9(05).
       FD  PRINT-FILE LABEL RECORDS ARE OMITTED
                      RECORDING MODE IS F.
       01  PRINT-RECORD.
           05  PRINT-CC                   PIC X.
           05  PRINT-AREA                 PIC X(131).

       WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
       01  REPORT-LAYOUT.
           05  HDR-1.
               10  FILLER        PIC X(58) VALUE SPACES.
               10  FILLER        PIC X(15) VALUE 'CUSTOMER REPORT'.
               10  FILLER        PIC X(58) VALUE SPACES.
           05  HDR-2.
               10  FILLER        PIC X(55) VALUE SPACES.
               10  FILLER        PIC X(22) VALUE
                                           'PROGRAMMER: ERIC'.
               10  FILLER        PIC X(54) VALUE SPACES.
           05  HDR-3.
               10  FILLER        PIC X(32) VALUE SPACES.
               10  FILLER        PIC X(23) VALUE 'ESTABLISHMENT NAME'.
               10  FILLER        PIC X(13) VALUE 'PHONE NUMBER'.
               10  FILLER        PIC X(3) VALUE SPACES.
               10  FILLER        PIC X(9) VALUE 'DATE'.
               10  FILLER        PIC X(3) VALUE SPACES.
               10  FILLER        PIC X(17) VALUE 'BOTTLES DELIVERED'.
               10  FILLER        PIC X(31) VALUE SPACES.

           05  DETAIL-RECORD.

               10  DR-E-NAME     PIC X(20).
               10  FILLER        PIC X(3) VALUE SPACES.
               10  DR-FILLER     PIC X VALUE '('.
               10  DR-PHONE      PIC X(13).
               10  DR-AREA       PIC 9(3).
               10  FR-FILLER     PIC X VALUE ')'.
               10  DR-PRE        PIC 9(3).
               10  DR-FILLER     PIC X VALUE '-'.
               10  DR-EXCH       PIC 9(4).
               10  FILLER        PIC X(3) VALUE SPACES.
               10  DR-DATE       PIC X(9).
               10  DR-YEAR       PIC 9(4).
               10  DR-FILLER     PIC X VALUE '/'.
               10  DR-MONTH      PIC 9(2).
               10  DR-FILLER     PIC X VALUE '/'.
               10  DR-WEEK       PIC 9.
               10  FILLER        PIC X(3) VALUE SPACES.
               10  DR-BD         PIC 9(5).
       01  WORKING-VARIABLES.
           05  EOF                        PIC 9 VALUE 0.

       PROCEDURE DIVISION.
       0000-DRIVER.
            PERFORM 1000-INIT THRU 1000-EXIT.
            PERFORM 2000-WORK THRU 2000-EXIT UNTIL EOF = 1.
            PERFORM 3000-WRAP THRU 3000-EXIT.
            GOBACK.

       1000-INIT.
            OPEN INPUT     CARD-FILE
                 OUTPUT    PRINT-FILE.
            MOVE HDR1 TO PRINT-AREA.
            MOVE 1 TO PRINT-AREA.
            MOVE HDR2 TO PRINT-AREA.
            MOVE ' ' TO PRINT-AREA.
            MOVE HDR3 TO PRINT-AREA.
            MOVE 0 TO PRINT-AREA.
            MOVE ' ' TO PRINT-CC.
            READ CARD-FILE AT END MOVE 1 TO EOF.

       1000-EXIT.
            EXIT.

       2000-WORK.
            MOVE ESTABLISHMENT-NAME TO DR-E-NAME.

>
>Or my favorite, EP = Execute Programmer :)
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Well, not really political correct - 
	JSO: Jump on Sexy Operator
as opposed to
	JAO: Jump on Any Operator

But more useful, of course,
	EIO	Execute Invalid Opcode
	PPSW	PACK PSW
	CRN	Convert to Roman Numeral (see recent thread)
	BPF	Branch on Power Off
	BL	Branch left
	BR	Branch Right
and, not to forget
	BBW	Branch Both Ways

And I realize, this is all vastly off topic...........

with kind regards

Volker Bandke
(BSP GmbH)
```

#### ↳ Re: Newbie needs help with move

- **From:** John Piggott <John_Piggott@compuserve.com>
- **Date:** 1999-02-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36C07FA7.182B@compuserve.com>`
- **References:** `<79n41a$6ud$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
It's inhuman to have to code this way!! How long would it take you to 
code a really complex printout?! Try doing it like this (and remember the 
Report Section is completely STANDARD COBOL):

       IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
       PROGRAM-ID. Prog1.
       AUTHOR.   ERIC AND JOHN.
       ENVIRONMENT DIVISION.
       INPUT-OUTPUT SECTION.
       FILE-CONTROL. SELECT CARD-FILE  ASSIGN TO SYS016-UR-2501-S.
                     SELECT PRINT-FILE ASSIGN TO SYS018-UR-1403-S.
       DATA DIVISION.
       FILE SECTION.
       FD  CARD-FILE  LABEL RECORDS ARE OMITTED
                      RECORDING MODE IS F.
       01  CARD-RECORD.
           05  ESTABLISHMENT-NAME               PIC X(20).
           05  ESTABLISHMENT-ADDRESS.
               10  STREET-NUMBER                PIC 9(04).
               10  STREET-NAME                  PIC X(15).
               10  ZIP-CODE                     PIC 9(05).
           05  PHONE-NUMBER.
               10  AREA-CODE                    PIC 9(03).
               10  PREFIX                       PIC 9(03).
               10  EXCHANGE                     PIC 9(04).
           05  SERVICE-DATE.
               10  S-YEAR                       PIC 9(04).
               10  S-MONTH                      PIC 9(02).
               10  S-WEEK-OF-MONTH              PIC 9(01).
           05  DELIVERY-PERSON-NUMBER           PIC 9(02).
           05  BOTTLES-DELIVERED                PIC 9(05).
           05  BOTTLES-RETURNED                 PIC 9(05).
           05  BOTTLES-ORDERED-FOR-NEXT-WEEK    PIC 9(05).
       FD  PRINT-FILE LABEL RECORDS ARE OMITTED
                      RECORDING MODE IS F
                      REPORT IS REPORT-LAYOUT.
       WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
       01  WORKING-VARIABLES.
           05  EOF                        PIC 9 VALUE 0.
       REPORT SECTION.
       RD  REPORT-LAYOUT
           PAGE LIMITS 60 LINES
                FIRST DETAIL 4.
       01  TYPE PAGE HEADING.
           05  LINE 1.
               10  COLUMN 59     PIC X(15)  VALUE 'CUSTOMER REPORT'.
           05  LINE 2.
               10  COLUMN 56     PIC X(22)  VALUE
                                            'PROGRAMMER: ERIC'.
           05  LINE 3.
               10  COLUMN 33     PIC X(23)  VALUE 'ESTABLISHMENT NAME'.
               10  COLUMN 56     PIC X(13)  VALUE 'PHONE NUMBER'.
               10  COLUMN 72     PIC X(9)   VALUE 'DATE'.
               10  COLUMN 84     PIC X(17)  VALUE 'BOTTLES DELIVERED'.
       01  DETAIL-RECORD TYPE IS DETAIL.
           05  LINE + 1.
               10  COLUMN 33     PIC X(20)  SOURCE ESTABLISHMENT-NAME.
               10  COLUMN 56     PIC X VALUE '('.
               10  COLUMN 57     PIC 9(3)   SOURCE AREA-CODE.
               10  COLUMN 60     PIC X VALUE ')'.
               10  COLUMN 61     PIC 9(3)   SOURCE PREFIX.
               10  COLUMN 64     PIC X VALUE '-'.
               10  COLUMN 65     PIC 9(4)   SOURCE EXCHANGE.
               10  COLUMN 72     PIC 9(4)   SOURCE S-YEAR.
               10  COLUMN 76     PIC X VALUE '/'.
               10  COLUMN 77     PIC 9(2)   SOURCE S-MONTH.
               10  COLUMN 79     PIC X VALUE '/'.
               10  COLUMN 80     PIC 9      SOURCE S-WEEK-OF-MONTH.
               10  COLUMN 84     PIC 9(5)   SOURCE BOTTLES-DELIVERED.
       PROCEDURE DIVISION.
       0000-DRIVER.
            PERFORM 1000-INIT THRU 1000-EXIT.
            PERFORM 2000-WORK THRU 2000-EXIT UNTIL EOF = 1.
            PERFORM 3000-WRAP THRU 3000-EXIT.
            GOBACK.
      *
       1000-INIT.
            OPEN INPUT     CARD-FILE
                 OUTPUT    PRINT-FILE.
            INITIATE REPORT-LAYOUT
            READ CARD-FILE AT END MOVE 1 TO EOF.
       1000-EXIT.
            EXIT.
      *
       2000-WORK.
            GENERATE DETAIL-RECORD
            READ CARD-FILE AT END MOVE 1 TO EOF.
       2000-EXIT.
            EXIT.
      *
       3000-WRAP.
            TERMINATE REPORT-LAYOUT
            CLOSE CARD-FILE
                  PRINT-FILE.
       3000-EXIT.
            EXIT.

I fixed a few little problems in the layout and filled in some missing 
bits. This is easy when you're not bogged down in FILLERs and MOVEs.

##### ↳ ↳ Report Writer

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com>
- **Date:** 1999-02-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36C044D5.C847DC2D@NOSPAMhome.com>`
- **References:** `<79n41a$6ud$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <36C07FA7.182B@compuserve.com>`

```
John Piggott wrote:
> 
> It's inhuman to have to code this way!! How long would it take you to
> code a really complex printout?! Try doing it like this (and remember the
> Report Section is completely STANDARD COBOL):

It is incompletely STANDARD COBOL.  Some ANSI standards have it, others
don't.  

I never code new programs using REPORT WRITER unless it is a shop
standard.  All COBOL programmers know how to handle I-O without REPORT
WRITER.  Lots don't know REPORT WRITER, so lets keep it simple for
maintenance programmers.  Your "inhuman" coding quickly becomes second
nature.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Report Writer

- **From:** John Piggott <John_Piggott@compuserve.com>
- **Date:** 1999-02-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36C0F39C.BBE@compuserve.com>`
- **References:** `<79n41a$6ud$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <36C07FA7.182B@compuserve.com> <36C044D5.C847DC2D@NOSPAMhome.com>`

```
Of course all COBOL programmers *know* how to do I-O without Report Writer just 
as they *know* how to wash clothes by hand. On the projects I've monitored, we 
found that maintaining RW code is also quicker and cheaper, even if it's 
initally not known by the programmer. But we had the advantage of a good 
tutorial text. It makes a hell of a difference.  RW was in all the ANSI 
standards that I know of (including COBOL 200x).

Howard Brazee wrote:
> 
> John Piggott wrote:
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> nature.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Report Writer

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1999-02-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<WF0w2.5006$Xl5.7345533@news1.mia>`
- **References:** `<79n41a$6ud$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <36C07FA7.182B@compuserve.com> <36C044D5.C847DC2D@NOSPAMhome.com> <36C0F39C.BBE@compuserve.com>`

```
John Piggott wrote:
>RW was in all the ANSI standards that I know of (including COBOL 200x).

It is in the standard.  But it is an optional module, not part of the
nucleus.  Not all compilers implement it (though I'm sure most do), and
some installations may not have purchased the module when it is.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Report Writer

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** stever@sjrnet.u-net.com (Steve R)
- **Date:** 1999-02-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36c1eedd.6761744@news.u-net.com>`
- **References:** `<79n41a$6ud$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <36C07FA7.182B@compuserve.com> <36C044D5.C847DC2D@NOSPAMhome.com> <36C0F39C.BBE@compuserve.com> <WF0w2.5006$Xl5.7345533@news1.mia>`

```
Unfortunately IBM decided that it was "optional" and although it was implemented in ANS4
OS/VS Cobol it was removed from VS II !!!  So much for upwards compatability.

I remember we were a pilot project for using it at my shop and most of our reports were
written using it.  Come migration to VS II and we had to strip it all out again and go
back to counting all them spaces to pad out the print lines. 
RW was quite good for simple reports but became a pain when dealing with grouped
printlines as it always threw a page in the wrong place.


SteveR

COBOL compiler since) there has been an ADD-ON product that fully supports
both OS/VS COBOL compatible and ANSI conforming Report Writer code.

The key being "optional".   A barebones ANSI compliant compiler for Wang VS
systems
did not include Report Writer.  DOS/VSE ANSI 74 compliant compiler also omits
it.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Report Writer

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@inospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-02-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7atj17$o7g@dfw-ixnews8.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<79n41a$6ud$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <36C07FA7.182B@compuserve.com> <36C044D5.C847DC2D@NOSPAMhome.com> <36C0F39C.BBE@compuserve.com> <WF0w2.5006$Xl5.7345533@news1.mia> <36D08F28.D5F8B17D@norad.net> <8rrA2.15044$OS5.15018371@news3.mia>`

```
James King wrote in message <8rrA2.15044$OS5.15018371@news3.mia>...
>WRONG.  Report Writer is OWNED BY IBM and ONLY IBM COBOL
>HAS REPORT WRITER.  They only write it for HIGH-END COMPILERS.
>MVS, OS/VS, CMS, OS/390
>

Over the years, I have heard lots of mis-information about Report Writer -
but usually I could at least figure out how the "rumor" began.  This is one,
I can't even imagine.  It has absolutely no basis in truth and no
relationship to any fact that I can imagine.

Just so it is clear
    - Report Writer has been a part of the ANSI (and when relevant) ISO
Standards for about 3 decades (although it is an OPTIONAL module in the past
Standards - but required in the next)
    - There are full feature implementations of Report Writer in many COBOL
compilers - on mainframes, mid-range, and PC/UNIX platforms. A variety of
"proprietary" mainframe COBOL compilers support it.
    -  IBM doesn't even "own" the report writer technology that it currently
sells for its own mainframe compilers.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Report Writer

```

report writer and I've been using it for years.

Tony M. Mina
