```
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>Bill Lynch

I am a strong believer in Report Writer.  When I was doing (IBM) mainframe
contracting, I had a standard skeleton that I "took with me" from contract
to contract that had an internal sort (commented out input procedure) and
Report Writer in the Output Procedure.  It may not have "beat out" some of
those 4GLs, but it sure could handle most of what they did just as quickly
and easily.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Report Writer

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** John Piggott <John_Piggott@compuserve.com>
- **Date:** 1999-02-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36C26F1E.5E4E@compuserve.com>`
- **References:** `<79n41a$6ud$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <36C07FA7.182B@compuserve.com> <36C044D5.C847DC2D@NOSPAMhome.com> <36C0F39C.BBE@compuserve.com> <36C100BD.9B95F279@att.net> <36C19845.9A954463@NOSPAMhome.com>`

```
Around 20 years ago Report Writer was unpopular with some of our contract 
programmers because they were paid by number of lines of code. When print 
program specs were doled out, the managers always worked on the 
assumption that COBOL was strictly a third generation language, so there 
was a fairly generous expectation for print programs. Now a programmer 
using Report Writer was in a fix. He loses one line per "variable" in the 
printout because RW does it in one line with a SOURCE (cutting out the 
MOVE). Then he loses almost half the fields used for print-line 
definitions because RW doesn't need FILLERs. So the ones that were 
enterprising enough to teach themselves the RW syntax and used it had 
their programs retrospecively re-assessed. This affected not only the RW 
users but also the ones that didn't use it, because the effect was 
averaged over the whole project.

My most successful project had a timescale that couldn't have been met 
with just "vanilla" COBOL, and for some good reason the whole thing had 
to be written in COBOL. Furthermore the project was top-heavy with 
printouts. So a taskgroup was formed to learn the RW syntax (not an easy 
matter as there were no texts other than the Language Reference - ugh!) 
and teach it to the others. To cap all this there was a commandment "Thou 
Shalt Use Report Writer" for every program that wrote anything to the 
printer. This forced everyone to get to know it. The opinion most people 
took away was that it was soooo much easier than it looks in the Language 
Reference. I'd recommend Steve Eckol's book for those who don't know it. 
There are some good courses now (just do a websearch on "COBOL Report 
Writer").

Bill Lynch wrote:
> Can we have a quick show of hands (with no prejudice) of those who 
> don't know Report Writer?
> Count me as the first.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Report Writer

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com>
- **Date:** 1999-02-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36C2EC95.F0710D4A@NOSPAMhome.com>`
- **References:** `<79n41a$6ud$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <36C07FA7.182B@compuserve.com> <36C044D5.C847DC2D@NOSPAMhome.com> <36C0F39C.BBE@compuserve.com> <36C100BD.9B95F279@att.net> <36C19845.9A954463@NOSPAMhome.com> <36C26F1E.5E4E@compuserve.com>`

```
John Piggott wrote:
> 
> Around 20 years ago Report Writer was unpopular with some of our contract
> programmers because they were paid by number of lines of code. 

About the only place where I have seen people use Report Writer
consistently is with contracting companies.  These companies come in, do
a job for a fixed price, and then leave.  If you know Report Writer, it
is quicker to write.  Of course the programmers left behind have to
maintain it, and they know regular COBOL I-O.
```

```
learning curve!].
```

- **References:** `<79n41a$6ud$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <36C07FA7.182B@compuserve.com> <36C044D5.C847DC2D@NOSPAMhome.com> <36C0F39C.BBE@compuserve.com> <36C100BD.9B95F279@att.net>`

```
It was: 10 Feb 1999 03:44:04 GMT and with STARTLING insight,  "William Lynch
<wblynch@att.net>" posted "Re: Report Writer" to "comp.lang.cobol" :

-->John Piggott wrote:
-->> 
-->> Of course all COBOL programmers *know* how to do I-O without Report Writer just
-->> as they *know* how to wash clothes by hand. On the projects I've monitored, we
-->> found that maintaining RW code is also quicker and cheaper, even if it's
-->> initally not known by the programmer. 

-->Can we have a quick show of hands (with no prejudice) of those who don't
-->know Report Writer?

-->Count me as the first.

-->Bill Lynch

Student report:  we did a 3 level control break program in Q-2 of the course.  It
took weeks to do all the editing, figure out all the processing steps, format the
report, which had summaries and other analysis pages as separate items.

In Q-3 we redid the whole thing with the requirement of using Report Writer.  It took
me about 4 hours to complete the whole program.  It went from ~500 lines of code to
about 75.  Some of those lines were comments to explain what was going on.  Of course
the report section was long, 75 to 100 lines.  But it was so easy.

The difference was not short of miraculous.   TS


"Come to think of it, there are already a million monkeys on a million
 typewriters, and Usenet is NOTHING like Shakespeare." Blair Houghton
***********entia non sunt multiplicanda praeter necessitate***********
++++++****tauceti****@****abraxis****.****com****for***email****++++++
```

```
I did not intend my comments to be an evaluation of RW in any way, just
that it's not widely known (to say the least). I don't know if anyone in
my dept. knows RW (which has nothing to do with whether or not anyone
*should* know it).

BTW, the difference between coding and recoding any program I can think
of is virtually always miraculously shorter, and produces a better
product. How do you think you'd have done if you'd had a different
problem to solve using RW? Also, how many input files did you have to
deal with & did you do any file updating? In the real world a 500 line
program is not usually a very big deal, especially if those 500 lines
include the Data Division.

Bill Lynch

PS: "TS" - Tough ???

PPS: Not intended as a flame.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Report Writer

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1999-02-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<x4Ww2.7794$Fs.8593441@news4.mia>`
- **References:** `<79n41a$6ud$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <36C07FA7.182B@compuserve.com> <36C044D5.C847DC2D@NOSPAMhome.com> <36C0F39C.BBE@compuserve.com> <36C100BD.9B95F279@att.net> <36c37985.1325319@news> <36C3BC70.40274D12@att.net>`

```
William Lynch wrote:
>
>OldUncleMe wrote:
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
>*should* know it).

Actually, I think the fact that only a very small percentage of COBOL
programmers use Report Writer does have a bearing on whether or not
anyone 'should' know it.  I have looked at Report Writer but decided that
it did not offer any real advantages to me.  My evaluation was relatively
brief, but my impression was that what RW gave in brevity it took away in
flexibility.  I have never seen a single 4GL tool which did not have this
characteristic.  (I believe I know the theoretical reasons behind this,
but that's another thread. :)  We have seen in this thread a number of
posts by programmers who used it for a while, then dropped it.  To me,
that fact alone is very damning.  I cannot imagine a truly good and
productive tool which many programmers would simply abandon *after* using
it in practice.  We might assume that the programmers who have not tried
Report Writer are simply ignorant of its advantages.  But we can't assume
that about the programmers who abandoned it after using it.  If Report
Writer were truly a wondrously better tool, it would imply that those
programmers who abandoned it were obtuse.  How many programmers abandoned
structured programming after using it for a while?  I don't know any.
What would be the implications if the same low percentage of programmers
used structured programming as use Report Writer?

>BTW, the difference between coding and re-coding any program I can think
>of is virtually always miraculously shorter, and produces a better
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>include the Data Division.

Those are valid points, here are a few more.  Experienced programmers
develop a repertoire of methods and techniques for common programming
problems.  Years and years ago I spent perhaps two years experimenting
with different ways of writing report programs.  During that time I
wrote hundreds of report programs using different methods.  I picked
what I considered the best methods of handling page overflow, control
breaks, spacing the print line, etc.  When I need to write a particular
type of report, I already know just how I am going to do it.

It is unusual in practice for a COBOL programmer to create a program by
starting from scratch.  We select an existing program which is close in
structure to what we want, then modify it.  We almost never code the
entire program, only the changes.  For any specific program, especially
report programs, the number of lines of code can be a poor indication of
how much work it took to create.  A largely copied program means that
much of the code is already debugged, reducing debugging work.

Considering all the above points, my assessment of COBOL Report Writer
is that it definitely does provide for shorter code.  But in practice,
for experienced programmers, it does not provide dramatically faster
or more convenient development over rolling your own.  If it did, the
many programmers who have abandoned it would be dummies, and I don't
believe that.  Okay, why then do some programmers swear by it?  I
believe it is for the same reason that some programmers will go to any
lengths to minimize their keystrokes.  They find typing laborious, or
they put a very high priority on that issue for whatever reasons.  From
my experience, this is a personality trait.  Those programmers simply
view less code as less work, period.  And the fact that RW makes for
less code makes it better ('easier') for them.

How about it Report Writer advocates?  Any comments? :-)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Report Writer

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** bwspoor@fridaycs.win-uk.net (Brian W Spoor)
- **Date:** 1999-02-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<94@fridaycs.win-uk.net>`
- **References:** `<79n41a$6ud$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <36C07FA7.182B@compuserve.com> <36C044D5.C847DC2D@NOSPAMhome.com> <36C0F39C.BBE@compuserve.com> <36C100BD.9B95F279@att.net> <36c37985.1325319@news> <36C3BC70.40274D12@att.net> <x4Ww2.7794$Fs.8593441@news4.mia>`

```
 
In article <x4Ww2.7794$Fs.8593441@news4.mia>, "Judson McClendon" (judmc123@bellsouth.net) writes:
>
[SNIP]
>
>
…[23 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Personally, I like it and use it in all systems that I program.

I have been programming (mainly COBOL) for over 25 years on a
variety of systems from mainframes down, currently for PCs on
Novell networks writing custom software for small businesses.

I agree that most programs are 'variations' on existing code and
you get faster (more correct) results by re-using code.

This does not IMHO obviate the use of RW, but RW can ehance this
process.  I have always hated counting FILLER bytes to get things in
the correct position, with RW you just specify the line/column and
only have entries for what you are actually printing - one of the
major things that will change when re-using code.

Before learning RW, I wrote using standard IO, with the usual mass
of lines in working-storage that could become muddled and
meaningless with several generations of enhancement.

I find RW is more concise in it's definitions, which, to me, means
more readable and more easily modified. I don't get paid by lines
of code, but by the job. The quicker I can complete a job the
better, naturaly with the proviso of getting it right.

I got to grips with RW after learning the Screen Section (MF
extention on PCs/Unix?) - this made screen handling far easier,
again no fillers to count and less 'garbage' lines in the
definitions.

When I trained, concise, accurate, efficient, robust and
maintainable code was what was taught - with the mainframes of the
day code had to be efficient.

My own belief is that RW (and Screen Section) meet those
requirements - the generated code may not be quite as efficient,
but it is more maintainable (less programmer time - faster
response to the end-user) and execution efficiency (in most systems)
is not as important as it once was - although there always will be
exceptions to this.

RW does not cover every situation, but my normal report using the
SORT verb with an input procedure to select the data and an output
procedure with RW to generate the report with sub-totals covers
most things that I come across.

Brian


-----------------------------------------------------------
Brian W Spoor MBCS
Chartered Information Systems Practitioner
Friday Computer Services          Phone: +44-(0)1803 852625
bwspoor@fridaycs.co.uk            Fax:   +44-(0)1803 854926
-----------------------------------------------------------
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Report Writer

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "Warren Simmons" <w.g.simmons@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 1999-02-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7a2703$hce@bgtnsc03.worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<79n41a$6ud$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <36C07FA7.182B@compuserve.com> <36C044D5.C847DC2D@NOSPAMhome.com> <36C0F39C.BBE@compuserve.com> <36C100BD.9B95F279@att.net> <36c37985.1325319@news> <36C3BC70.40274D12@att.net>`

```
Report Writer, memory lane.....

I was a member of the COBOL Committee during the definition of the first RW
spec.

Most of the people involved on the user side were experienced with the
univac 1.  However, one user was an IBM Business System user, and as such
had much experience with a Report Writer that was an extension (as I recall)
to the assembler they used.

This user member came onboard with the specific agenda of adding a report
writer feature in support of his employer's participation to the COBOL
development.  Few of the members at that time had ever heard of a report
writer, and this person had an uphill battle to convence everyone that this
was a worthwhile extension to the already "rich" features of the language.

I can't seem to recall Tom's last name, but shortly after he manged to get
the report writer feature into COBOL he went on to other things.  Since this
feature came into the language as a optional feature, little pressure was
placed upon vendors by users except in the IBM Business Systems arena.  It
is the efforts of other vendors to support this feature as a competitive
thing that gave it enough momentum to reach the state it now enjoys.

I've never personally seen the spec, nor used it.  However, we had a Meta
COBOL procedure that worked quite well so I can appreciate the loyalty that
it enjoys from many who have used it.

Warren Simmons
w.g.simmons@worldnet.att.net
'
```

```
identity consistently, but not tell the world my name and address....  This could be
argued at great length.  One reason for my use is that I have looked up my address
and phone #, gotten a map to my house, and don't really care to have people who might
disagree with me doing more than sending me an email flood.  I like to sense the
freedom to express my opinion with no more consequence than debate==that is the
purpose of forums.  (Paranoid?  You bet!)  I don't always express the popular
opinion, nor do I think that the people who might disagree with what I have to say
will necessarily limit the expression of their feelings to debate.  This is the real
world.  

Using the Report Writer did not involve much reuse of code.  In fact, verbatim, I
only copied the date section (to put date in header line of report.)  I'll post the
entire program in two sections as responses to this post, 
(IDENTIFICATION-DATA divisions) & (PROCEDURE DIVISION).  The features of RW did more
than allow making the report's layout simple to produce, simple the same way that a
screen-section is simple to produce in MFcobol compared to accept/display statements
scattered (based on use/occurrance) in code throughout the procedure division.  By
having everything in one layout section and having the utility counting lines, making
page breaks, summing totals, producing multiple levels of control breaks, the logic
is extremely easy to follow (given a simple understanding of RW) and quite easy to
produce.

I'm not arguing against learning cobol, doing all the same methods by hand at any
time, nor especially while learning to program cobol.  It's just that this was such a
useful tool and such a good experience, it's amazing it doesn't get more coverage!

Hey, I'm not here to debate why others don't use it.  That's a matter of affinity,
experience, inertia, pride?, utility? and familiarity?  Perhaps many have not been
exposed to RW.  It's not a shop standard?  It is something else to learn (ain't
broke, don't fix it.)  It would really not be useful?  There is too large a body of
existing code emplaced to begin to do something totally differently?  it would
therefore lead to confusion, difficulty in maintaining two methods?  "I like the way
I do things well enough already!"  This is not an attack, nor, really, a criticism.
RW was the ticket for this program I wrote, may not be great in many other
situations, I don't have the experience to say.  TS
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Report Writer

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** NoLonger@ix.netcom.com (OldUncleMe)
- **Date:** 1999-02-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36c700f5.6393648@news>`
- **References:** `<79n41a$6ud$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <36C07FA7.182B@compuserve.com> <36C044D5.C847DC2D@NOSPAMhome.com> <36C0F39C.BBE@compuserve.com> <36C100BD.9B95F279@att.net> <36c37985.1325319@news> <36C3BC70.40274D12@att.net>`

```
       IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
       PROGRAM-ID.  Pr4d.
      *  Make Report with multilevel control breaks using
      *  Report-Writer
       AUTHOR. T S  CIS-215 Prog-#4.
      *....................

       ENVIRONMENT DIVISION.
       INPUT-OUTPUT SECTION.
       FILE-CONTROL.
           select sale-tran assign to '/u/unix/path/cis114/saletran'
              organization is line sequential.
           select rpt-file assign to printer 'pr4d'.
      *        organization is line sequential.
           select srt-file assign to 'sort-file'.
      *....................

       DATA DIVISION.
       FILE SECTION.
       fd  sale-tran.
       01  sale-rec.
           02  sale-store     pic 99.
           02  sale-dept      pic 99.
           02  sale-salesman  pic 99.
           02  sale-date.
              04 sale-mm      pic 9(2).
              04 sale-dd      pic 9(2).
              04 sale-yy      pic 9(2).
           02  sale-amt       pic 9(8)v99.
           02  sale-commcode  pic 9.

       fd rpt-file
           report is rpt-rec.

       sd  srt-file.
       01  srt-rec.
           02  srt-store      pic 99.
           02  srt-dept       pic 99.
           02  srt-salesman   pic 99.
           02  srt-date.
              04 srt-mm       pic 9(2).
              04 srt-dd       pic 9(2).
              04 srt-yy       pic 9(2).
           02  srt-amt        pic 9(8)v99.
           02  srt-commcode   pic 9.
      *....................

       WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
       01  wk01-comm-rates.
           02 wk01-commissions.
              04              pic 99    value 10.
              04              pic 99    value 20.
              04              pic 99    value 25.
              04              pic 99    value 30.
              04              pic 99    value 35.
           02 wk01-comms    redefines wk01-commissions
                              pic 99    occurs 5 times.
       01  ws01-eof-sw        pic x     value 'n'.
       01  wt01-stores-depts.
           02 wt01-name-list.
              04              pic x(20)
                 value 'Albany    Appliances'.
              04              pic x(20)
                 value 'Bremen    BeesWax   '.
              04              pic x(20)
                 value 'ChatsworthCords     '.
              04              pic x(20)
                 value 'Dalton    Dragons   '.
              04              pic x(20)
                 value 'Elberton  Elbows    '.
           02 wt01-names    redefines wt01-name-list occurs 5 times.
              04 wt01-stores  pic x(10).
              04 wt01-depts   pic x(10).
       01  ww01-curr-date     pic x(21) value spaces.
       01  ww01-date          pic x(10) value spaces.
       01  ww02-comm-amt      pic 9(10)v99 value zeros.
       01  ww03-sale-date     pic x(10) value spaces.
      *....................
       REPORT SECTION.
      * 
       rd rpt-rec
           controls are final srt-store srt-dept srt-salesman
           page limit is 66 lines
           heading 4 first detail 9 last detail 58.
           
       01 type is report heading.
           02 line 4.
              04 column 1  pic x(9)  value 'Tim Smith'.
              04 column 25 pic x(21) value 'Sales Analysis Report'.
              04 column 47 pic x(6)  value 'Date: '.
              04 column 54 pic x(10) source ww01-date.
      
       01 type is page heading.
      *     02 line 4.
      *        04 column 54 pic x(6)  value 'Page: '.
      *        04 column 59 pic zzz9  source page-counter.
           02 line 6.
              04 column 1  pic x(5)  value 'Store'.
              04 column 10 pic x(4)  value 'Dept'.
              04 column 27 pic x(8)  value 'Salesman'.
              04 column 37 pic x(10) value 'Comm. Code'.
              04 column 55 pic x(12) value 'Date of Sale'.
              04 column 79 pic x(12) value 'Sales Amount'.
              04 column 92 pic x(17) value 'Commission Amount'.
              04 column 113 pic x(6)  value 'Page: '.
              04 column 119 pic zzz9  source page-counter.
       01 rpt-head1 type is control heading srt-store.
           02 line plus 2.
              04 column 1  pic x(6)  value 'Store'.
              04 column 7  pic z9    source srt-store.
              04 column 9  pic x     value ':'.
              04 column 10 pic x(10) source wt01-stores(sale-store).
       01 rpt-foot1 type is control footing srt-store.
           02 line plus 2.
              04 column 10 pic x(16) value 'Total for Store '.
              04 column 28 pic z9    source srt-store.
              04 column 30 pic x     value ':'.
              04 column 32 pic x(10) source wt01-stores(sale-store).
              04 column 75 pic z,zzz,zzz,zz9.99 sum srt-amt.
              04 column 92 pic z,zzz,zzz,zz9.99 sum ww02-comm-amt.
           02 line plus 2.
              04 column 1  pic x     value space.
       01 rpt-foot2 type is control footing srt-dept.
           02 line plus 2.
              04 column 10 pic x(22) value 'Total for Department: '.
              04 column 30 pic z9    source srt-dept.
              04 column 34 pic x(10) source wt01-depts(sale-dept).
              04 column 75 pic z,zzz,zzz,zz9.99 sum srt-amt.
              04 column 92 pic z,zzz,zzz,zz9.99 sum ww02-comm-amt.
           02 line plus 1.
              04 column 1  pic x     value space.
       01 rpt-foot3 type is control footing srt-salesman.
           02 line plus 2.
              04 column 10 pic x(20) value 'Total for Salesman: '.
              04 column 30 pic z9    source srt-salesman.
              04 column 75 pic z,zzz,zzz,zz9.99 sum srt-amt.
              04 column 92 pic z,zzz,zzz,zz9.99 sum ww02-comm-amt.
           02 line plus 1.
              04 column 1  pic x     value space.
       01 rpt-det-line type is detail.
           02 line plus 1.
              04 column 1  pic z9    source srt-store.
              04 column 10 pic z9    source srt-dept.
              04 column 30 pic z9    source srt-salesman.
              04 column 55 pic x(10) source ww03-sale-date.
              04 column 75 pic z,zzz,zzz,zz9.99 source srt-amt.
              04 column 92 pic z,zzz,zzz,zz9.99 source ww02-comm-amt.
       01 rpt-footing-final type is control footing final.
           02 line plus 3.
              04 column 10 pic x(27)
                 value 'Final Total for All Stores:'.
              04 column 75 pic z,zzz,zzz,zz9.99 sum srt-amt.
              04 column 92 pic z,zzz,zzz,zz9.99 sum ww02-comm-amt.
      *....................
```

```
       PROCEDURE DIVISION.
       1000-main section.
       1100-report-date.
           move function current-date to ww01-curr-date.
           string ww01-curr-date (5:2) '/' ww01-curr-date (7:2) '/'
              ww01-curr-date (1:4) delimited by size into ww01-date
           end-string.
       1300-main.

project.  It would be unethical to put the full code here.  Write me if you you'd
like to see it.  It is short and efficient and works like a charm!   TS
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Report Writer

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-02-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<79qfr0$ao7@dfw-ixnews4.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<79n41a$6ud$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <36C07FA7.182B@compuserve.com> <36C044D5.C847DC2D@NOSPAMhome.com>`

```
Every ANSI Standard since 1968 has had Report Writer.  Are you really
working in a world before that?  It is (has always been - and looks like it
always will be) Standard.  What you may have MEANT TO SAY was that it was
"optional" in older ANSI Standards.  Luckily, this is getting fixed with the
next Standard where it will be required.  Therefore, if you are using a
compiler that supports it - and developing code that is intended to last for
the future, it is perfectly safe to use (and any programmers who aren't
familiar with it - need to "brush up" on their ANSI/ISO skill-set).
```

## Thread: Dumb COPY issue... — 43 msgs

_Topics: [`Language features and syntax`](topics.md#syntax)_

### Dumb COPY issue...

- **From:** paulr@bix.com (paulr)
- **Date:** 1999-02-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<79mn3c$3ij@lotho.delphi.com>`

```
I have several copybooks I want to share between BATCH programs,
where they are define FD statements, and a CICS program, where
the record definitions become working storage records. 

So how can I use COPY replacing to replace ' FD' with '*FD'?
The COPY pragma does some strang things with this in all the variants
I used, but mostly added spaces.       

Examples:

  COPY FDLIB01 REPLACING == FD == BY ==*FD ==

Turns:
        FD Some-File-Name
        01 Some-File-Name-Record

Into:
               *FD Some-File-Name
        01 Some-File-Name-Record

Oh - this is in COBOL for MVS & VM under MVS.

-Paul
```

- **From:** Barticus@att.spam.net (Randall Bart)
- **Date:** 1999-02-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36bf7351.44994395@netnews>`
- **References:** `<79mn3c$3ij@lotho.delphi.com>`

```
'Twas 8 Feb 1999 12:59:24 GMT, when paulr@bix.com (paulr) illuminated
comp.lang.cobol thusly:

>I have several copybooks I want to share between BATCH programs,
>where they are define FD statements, and a CICS program, where
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>I used, but mostly added spaces.       

Records in the copybook are comments or not comments based on the
unmodified text.  The replacing clause cannot change a line into a
comment.  I don't know of a way to do what you want on IBM with a single
copybook.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Dumb COPY issue...

- **From:** kownby@humboldt1.com (Kindrick Ownby)
- **Date:** 1999-02-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36c082bc.21050775@news2.humboldt1.com>`
- **References:** `<79mn3c$3ij@lotho.delphi.com> <36bf7351.44994395@netnews>`

```
'Twas 8 Feb 1999 12:59:24 GMT, when paulr@bix.com (paulr) illuminated
comp.lang.cobol thusly:

>I have several copybooks I want to share between BATCH programs,
>where they are define FD statements, and a CICS program, where
>the record definitions become working storage records. 
[...]

I have a similar requirement. In my case the record layouts reside in
LINKAGE in dynamically called subprograms.  For example, TR1CPY
contains the FD statement and TR2CPY contains the record layout. The
main program has TR1CPY immediately followed by TR2CPY and the
subprograms have  TR2CPY only in LINKAGE.

Kindrick
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Dumb COPY issue...

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>

```
Actually, the way I usually do it not only has two copies,
but the 01 level is left out of both, like so:
      COPY "FILE.FD".
 01 RECORD-NAME.
      COPY "RECORD.LIB".
That way, it can also be copied to working storage as a
temporary record (or several).  If you need to make it
GLOBAL or EXTERNAL, then it is also easy to do so
in one place, and not another.

paulr wrote in message <79o63s$75u@lotho.delphi.com>...

>What happens when you have 40 or so files?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Dumb COPY issue...

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Warren Porter <warrenp@ASPMnetdoor.com>
- **Date:** 1999-02-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36C08683.D4065B8@ASPMnetdoor.com>`
- **References:** `<79mn3c$3ij@lotho.delphi.com> <36bf7351.44994395@netnews> <36c082bc.21050775@news2.humboldt1.com> <79o63s$75u@lotho.delphi.com> <79prii$6r9$1@news.igs.net>`

```
Our shop requires 4 copybooks for each file *.SL, *.FD *.WS, and *.DS for
select, file description, working-storage entry (dsn and file status), and
declarative section entry.  The naming conventions are also fairly strict:
filename-FILE filename-RECORD and
filename-FILE-STATUS.  This comes in handy when converting a copy of a
program to process a different file.

Another shop (uses DPS-6) came with a number of system copybooks with the 01
missing and that became a defacto standard.  Where a record layout needs to
be replicated in working-storage in this shop we need to have a session on
an editor, but that is the trade off.

Donald Tees wrote:

> Actually, the way I usually do it not only has two copies,
> but the 01 level is left out of both, like so:
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> >What happens when you have 40 or so files?
```

```
That is interesting ... the declaratives part.  It is only
recently that I have stated using them.  My normal way
would be to also have a copy library for all the file I/O
that would be copied in at the end of the procedure
division.  Then the programs all do performs of standardised
names.

That makes five files per file ... select. FD, record, Ws, and
procedures.  Too complex.  That is why I am starting to
think in terms of subroutines or oo-objects with methods.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Dumb COPY issue...

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** swiegand@neo.rr.com (SkippyPB)
- **Date:** 1999-02-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<4712E0C89D3A1940.7D66B7DAC9BD0089.2D2EB699234A9473@library-proxy.airnews.net>`
- **References:** `<79mn3c$3ij@lotho.delphi.com> <36bf7351.44994395@netnews> <36c082bc.21050775@news2.humboldt1.com> <79o63s$75u@lotho.delphi.com> <79prii$6r9$1@news.igs.net> <36C08683.D4065B8@ASPMnetdoor.com> <79qjnq$jbo$1@news.igs.net>`

```
Your approach is a good one.  The company I work for writes software
that must run on MVS and VSE.  Therefore, since I/O is the biggest
difference between the two OS's, we have absolutely no Select's or
FD's in our Cobol programs.  All file descriptions are in Working
Storage or Linkage Section and standard copybooks are used for the
descriptions and I/O access.  I/O itself is handled by a standard set
of assembler subroutines.  The names are the same for VSE or MVS, only
the code generated by macros we wrote is different. 

This takes the burden of writing selects, FD's reads, writes etc. out
of the programmers hands and give us a lot more flexibility with our
record descriptions and OS's differences.

Regards,


On Tue, 9 Feb 1999 19:32:18 -0500, "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>
enlightened us:

>That is interesting ... the declaratives part.  It is only
>recently that I have stated using them.  My normal way
…[44 more quoted lines elided]…
>

          ////
         (o o)
-oOO--(_)--OOo-

Middle age is when you choose your cereal for the
fiber, not the toy.

 Steve
```

```
Actually, you write a blasted REXX filter to take ':::TAG:::' 
where the FD ought to be, and create two new members, one with 
'FD' wher it belongs and one with '*FD' where it belongs. 
<grin>  I couldn't stand the cluttered look with all those
baby include files in there.  Heck, I even do that to my CICS
BMS include files, though I admit, I maintain those by hand. 
It is much more reasonable to me to have decent variable names 
than automated creation.

-Paul
```

```
Randall Bart (Barticus@att.spam.net) wrote:
: 'Twas 8 Feb 1999 12:59:24 GMT, when paulr@bix.com (paulr) illuminated
: comp.lang.cobol thusly:

: >I have several copybooks I want to share between BATCH programs,
: >where they are define FD statements, and a CICS program, where
: >the record definitions become working storage records. 
: >
: >So how can I use COPY replacing to replace ' FD' with '*FD'?
: >The COPY pragma does some strang things with this in all the variants
: >I used, but mostly added spaces.       

: Records in the copybook are comments or not comments based on the
: unmodified text.  The replacing clause cannot change a line into a
: comment.  I don't know of a way to do what you want on IBM with a single
: copybook.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Dumb COPY issue...

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** John Piggott <John_Piggott@compuserve.com>

```
You can't change FD to *FD with the * in column 7 but you could replace 
the FD with null and code a comment in the REPLACING text (which most 
compilers will show on the listing e.g.

 COPY member replacing ==FD  xyz LABEL RECORDS STANDARD.== BY
 ==
*the FD entry has been suppressed here
 ==.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Dumb COPY issue...

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** cwestbury@giant.intranet.com (Chris Westbury)
- **Date:** 1999-02-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1999Feb10.010609.19530@giant>`
- **References:** `<79mn3c$3ij@lotho.delphi.com> <36bf7351.44994395@netnews> <36C1121A.2C77@compuserve.com>`

```
In article <36C1121A.2C77@compuserve.com>,
John Piggott <John_Piggott@compuserve.com> wrote:
>
> You can't change FD to *FD with the * in column 7 but you could replace 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>     COPY member replacing ==FD  xyz LABEL RECORDS STANDARD.== BY

This won't work without at least one space between the full stop and the
pseudo-text delimiter, as in my previous followup.

In the copybook, STANDARD. is two text-words, STANDARD and a separator
period.  In the REPLACING phrase, STANDARD.== is only one text-word.  The
full stop is part of the character string STANDARD. because a space does
not follow it, so there will be no match.

However, STANDARD. == is two text-words, STANDARD and a separator period,
that can match the two text-words in the copybook.
```

#### ↳ Re: Dumb COPY issue...

- **From:** cwestbury@giant.intranet.com (Chris Westbury)
- **Date:** 1999-02-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1999Feb8.232026.19526@giant>`
- **References:** `<79mn3c$3ij@lotho.delphi.com>`

```
In article <79mn3c$3ij@lotho.delphi.com>,
paulr@bix.com (paulr) wrote:
>
> I have several copybooks I want to share between BATCH programs, where
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> but mostly added spaces.

I don't think it's possible for REPLACING to convert a statement to a
comment.  Certainly my compilers can't do it; I strip comments in the
tokenizer, long before REPLACING becomes active.

This is not actually a problem with COPY REPLACING; the real problem
comes earlier, namely, it's a misunderstanding of the COPY feature to put
an 01-level in a copybook.

CoBOL is the original database.  Copybooks are the DDL.  You should
describe the data exactly once, then copy it in at whatever level you
need it under whatever name you need it, whether it be in the FILE
SECTION, the WORKING-STORAGE SECTION, the LINKAGE SECTION, or whatever. 

Putting the name in the copybook is an example of binding too early,
which reduces your flexibility.  So the best way to solve this problem is
to take both the FD and the 01-level out of the copybooks.

If it's politically impossible to fix the copybooks, you can use the
REPLACING to convert the FD to a blank line instead of to a comment.  I
don't have access to your platform today, but I just tested this on VAX
CoBOL:

  COPY "FDLIB01.LIB" REPLACING ==FD Some-File-Name. == BY == ==.
```

```
I disagree with this on matters of style and encapsulation Bill.
I want the user to add two COPY lines to batch programs, one 
to CICS programs and have access to all the data formats & files. 

Part of this is because the system is a bit distributed in design,
and part because when a change is made to the file structures, I 
want to force a full system recompile - in short, a new version of
the application. (That means it has to be tested before it is 
released.) 

The other part is because part of the system is batch, and
part is CICS, and putting the two together is a tough
proposition at this point. EXCI looks like the only possible
way to do it safely, and finding an example of it is - err - 
difficult. :) 

-Paul
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Dumb COPY issue...

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-02-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7a4ui4$fvo@sjx-ixn8.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<79mn3c$3ij@lotho.delphi.com> <36C0FF86.B0C427CE@att.net> <79s29d$ok9@lotho.delphi.com> <36C51664.E177D351@att.net> <7a4aos$og4@lotho.delphi.com>`

```
For those who are not aware of it, the next COBOL Standard "officially" adds
support for nested COPY statements (happily WITHOUT the REPLACING phrase).
I know that the "attached" discussion relates to IBM mainframe environments
where this facility has been supported for over a decade (as an extension).
However, I think this will provide a portable/standard way of keeping
multiple file descriptions in separate copy members - but bringing them into
a program via a single COPY statement.

Obviously, there are matainance PROs and CONs of this functionality, but I
think that this will prove to be a useful enhancement to the language.

>spot in my professional experience. I have a database background,
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
>: Bill Lynch
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Dumb COPY issue...

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** Barticus@att.spam.net (Randall Bart)
- **Date:** 1999-02-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36ccdb90.3736712@netnews>`
- **References:** `<79mn3c$3ij@lotho.delphi.com> <36C0FF86.B0C427CE@att.net> <79s29d$ok9@lotho.delphi.com> <36C51664.E177D351@att.net> <7a4aos$og4@lotho.delphi.com> <7a4ui4$fvo@sjx-ixn8.ix.netcom.com>`

```
'Twas Sat, 13 Feb 1999 14:30:05 -0800, when "William M. Klein"
<wmklein@ix.netcom.com> illuminated comp.lang.cobol thusly:

>For those who are not aware of it, the next COBOL Standard "officially" adds
>support for nested COPY statements (happily WITHOUT the REPLACING phrase).

Well I'm certainly not happy about it.  I should be allowed to use
REPLACING on any COPY.  I think the committe was worried about the
interaction of REPLACING phrases at different levels.  I figure one vendor
will allow REPLACING only at the top leve, and another will only allow it
at the bottom level, and then someone will do both, then someone else will
do both, and then in COBOL 202X it will be a feature, but then they'll
find the two vendors who already did it have different semantics, and the
semantics specified in the standard are self contradictory.  It will all
be ironed out in time for COBOL 21XX.

And there's a nesting depth of five.  What if I want 10?  Why can't I nest
COPY statements 10 deep?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Dumb COPY issue...

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@inospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-02-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7aj48c$kqn@sjx-ixn5.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<79mn3c$3ij@lotho.delphi.com> <36C0FF86.B0C427CE@att.net> <79s29d$ok9@lotho.delphi.com> <36C51664.E177D351@att.net> <7a4aos$og4@lotho.delphi.com> <7a4ui4$fvo@sjx-ixn8.ix.netcom.com> <36ccdb90.3736712@netnews>`

```

Randall Bart wrote in message <36ccdb90.3736712@netnews>...
  <snip>
>
>And there's a nesting depth of five.  What if I want 10?  Why can't I nest
>COPY statements 10 deep?
>
>--


The nesting level limit goes back to when the committee thought they could
specify "portable minimums" for all the "ellipsis" features of the Standard.
For example, how big of an alphanumeric item MUST an implementor support?
There was never any intention (I think) that one would be forced to LIMIT
the nesting level to 5 - only that every vendor MUST support at least 5
levels.

Having said that, when they figured out that they couldn't develop portable
limits for everything else I *think* (but am not positive) that there was
direction to remove the 5 level limit for nested COPY statements.  I suggest
that you contact the committee to see if this is or is not included in the
current draft direction.

P.S.  The problems with REPLACING on a COPY statement include:

A) Interaction between REPLACING and REPLACE scope

B) Can a REPLACING phrase impact a nested COPY statement? How about the
actual pseudo-text in a nested COPY REPLACING statement?

C) Error prone problems with REPLACING impacting a COPY member that the
coder of the COPY statement can't even see or get to (or know about).

***

At least one implementation DOES currently allow REPLACING on the
bottom-level of a set of nested COPY statements. Personally, I don't know of
any problems with this - but I think the committee felt it was "cleaner" to
just prohibit it completely - and have people use the enhanced REPLACE
statement (now with "cumulative" options) should they need that
functionality.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Dumb COPY issue...

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1999-02-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8qfz2.7536$Xl5.12784331@news1.mia>`
- **References:** `<79mn3c$3ij@lotho.delphi.com> <36C0FF86.B0C427CE@att.net> <79s29d$ok9@lotho.delphi.com> <36C51664.E177D351@att.net> <7a4aos$og4@lotho.delphi.com> <7a4ui4$fvo@sjx-ixn8.ix.netcom.com> <36ccdb90.3736712@netnews> <7aj48c$kqn@sjx-ixn5.ix.netcom.com>`

```
William M. Klein wrote:
>
>P.S.  The problems with REPLACING on a COPY statement include:
>
>B) Can a REPLACING phrase impact a nested COPY statement? How about the
>actual pseudo-text in a nested COPY REPLACING statement?

This seems simple.  REPLACING is a preprocessor directive, it acts on
the copied file before the copied file is processed.  Then the already
changed copied file works in it's modified state.  Upper level REPLACING
phrases would NOT affect nested files directly.  I can't see any other
interpretation making sense in this context, or any ambiguity.  I also
don't see that this would present implementation problems, or be more
bug prone than REPLACING is now.  Programmers aren't children, if we
shoot ourselves in the foot, we shoot ourselves in the foot.  On the
other hand, I don't expect to need REPLACING on embedded COPY files, so
it's no big deal to me personally. :-)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Dumb COPY issue...

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** "Karl Wagner" <kewagner@home.com>
- **Date:** 1999-02-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<lUfz2.367$fp.2658@news1.rdc1.on.wave.home.com>`
- **References:** `<79mn3c$3ij@lotho.delphi.com> <36C0FF86.B0C427CE@att.net> <79s29d$ok9@lotho.delphi.com> <36C51664.E177D351@att.net> <7a4aos$og4@lotho.delphi.com> <7a4ui4$fvo@sjx-ixn8.ix.netcom.com> <36ccdb90.3736712@netnews> <7aj48c$kqn@sjx-ixn5.ix.netcom.com> <8qfz2.7536$Xl5.12784331@news1.mia>`

```

Judson McClendon wrote in message <8qfz2.7536$Xl5.12784331@news1.mia>...
>William M. Klein wrote:
>>
…[22 more quoted lines elided]…
>

I don't believe it is as simple and straightforward as you believe it to be.
For the past 15 years my company has been selling a product that, among
other things, implements a form of hierarchical COPY REPLACING.

There is a significant issue of scoping and precedence of replacement which
can lead to completely unpredictable results if not implemented correctly.

Given a data structure composed of a number of nested COPYs something like
this.:

 A
    provides some stuff
    copies B
    provides some more stuff

You write a program like this :

-- program ---

     COPY A REPLACING ==:PREFIX:== BY ==B==

-- A.CPY --
  01 :PREFIX:-VAR1 ...
      COPY B REPLACING ==:PREFIX:== BY ==A==
  01 :PREFIX:-VAR2

-- B.CPY --
  :PREFIX:-VAR1 ...

Do you end up with everything prefixed by B, or everything from A.CPY
prefixed by B and everything from B.CPY prefixed by A? What about A-VAR2? Is
it A-VAR2 or B-VAR2?

You could argue that each copybook be constructed in such a way that the
cascading replacement needs to be explicitly handled in each child copybook
to avoid ambiguity, but that can lead to different usage rules depending on
the context in which a copybook is used which is problematic for
programmers.

And then there are procedural copybooks... Imagine the merry mixups that can
occur if you're replacing variable names inconsistently.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Dumb COPY issue...

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1999-02-19T00:00:00

```
Karl Wagner wrote:
>
>I don't believe it is as simple and straightforward as you believe it to be.
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>can lead to completely unpredictable results if not implemented correctly.


The key is not the implementation, it is the use.  Gosh, are programmers
so obtuse that we have to treat them like morons? ;-)  Just explain that
REPLACING will operate on the copied file, *everything* in the copied
file, but not directly on nested COPY files.  Anybody that can't figure
that out should be in another profession.  Are we going to suffer that
annoying 'medicine bottle cap' mentality in professional circles too?
Let professional people take responsibility for themselves. :-)

>You could argue that each copybook be constructed in such a way that the
>cascading replacement needs to be explicitly handled in each child copybook
>to avoid ambiguity, but that can lead to different usage rules depending on
>the context in which a copybook is used which is problematic for
>programmers.

No more 'problematic' than being unable to do it at all, no? ;-)
```

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** Barticus@att.spam.net (Randall Bart)

- **Message-ID:** `<36cdb0fc.35301615@netnews>`

```
'Twas Fri, 19 Feb 1999 16:00:17 GMT, when "Karl Wagner"
<kewagner@home.com> illuminated comp.lang.cobol thusly:

>-- program ---
>
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>it A-VAR2 or B-VAR2?

I understand there are circumstances where the semantics are hard to
determine, but this isn't one of them.  The first one is A-VAR1 because
the second replacing isn't in effect yet, the second is B-VAR1 because the
more local replacing takes precedence, and the thrid on is A-VAR2 because
the scope of the inner replacing has ended.

I know there are issues to decide, but those issues need to be decided.
Most programmers will only use REPLACING with unique tokens.  If the
programmer writes something weird and it does something weirder, so what?
Tools are dangerous.


'Twas Fri, 19 Feb 1999 10:11:09 -0800, when "William M. Klein"
<wmklein@inospam.netcom.com> illuminated comp.lang.cobol thusly:

>As I said in the earlier part of this thread, the REPLACE statement has been
>enhanced to get rid of some of its problems, so I think that anything that
>you WANT to do, can be done - just not with the REPLACING phase.

I don't think so.  Consider this example:

Outer Program

     REPLACE == REP-A == BY == REP-AA ==.
     COPY BOOK-A.
     REPLACE OFF.

In BOOK-A

     REPLACE ALSO == REP-B == BY == REP-BB ==.
     COPY BOOK-B.
     REPLACE LAST OFF.

After COPY BOOK-B I need to turn off the inner REPLACE, but the REPLACE
LAST OFF turns off both REPLACE directives.  If I remove the ALSO on the
inner REPLACE, the outer REPLACE will apply after REPLACE LAST OFF, but it
won't apply to BOOK-B.

Unless I'm misreading the semantics of REPLACE [LAST] OFF.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Dumb COPY issue...

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@inospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-02-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7aluc7$9dn@dfw-ixnews5.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<79mn3c$3ij@lotho.delphi.com> <36C0FF86.B0C427CE@att.net> <79s29d$ok9@lotho.delphi.com> <36C51664.E177D351@att.net> <7a4aos$og4@lotho.delphi.com> <7a4ui4$fvo@sjx-ixn8.ix.netcom.com> <36ccdb90.3736712@netnews> <7aj48c$kqn@sjx-ixn5.ix.netcom.com> <8qfz2.7536$Xl5.12784331@news1.mia> <lUfz2.367$fp.2658@news1.rdc1.on.wave.home.com> <36cdb0fc.35301615@netnews>`

```
Randall Bart wrote in message <36cdb0fc.35301615@netnews>...
  <much snippage>
>>As I said in the earlier part of this thread, the REPLACE statement has
been
>>enhanced to get rid of some of its problems, so I think that anything that
>>you WANT to do, can be done - just not with the REPLACING phase.
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
>


Randall,
  I think this is WAY more than the average C.L.C. reader wants to hear
about, but I think you have missed a rule.  If you look at GR9b (in CD 1.4 -
page 47),

When you do your REPLACE ALSO statement, processing continues "as if" the
active replace statement included both replacements - BUT there is also the
original REPLACE statement (before the ALSO) which is placed on the "stack"
as an inactive REPLACE statement.  Then when the REPLACE LAST OFF statement
is encountered, the replacement with both strings is "cancelled" and the top
of the stack (inactive) REPLACE becomes ACTIVE again - and this has only the
original REPLACE string going.

At least that is how I read the rules (and is what I would expect).  If you
think this is wrong, you might want to submit an I-doc to the committee - or
at least send a note out with your concern.

Note: I have *not* checked the "combined change proposal" to see if any of
these rules have been changed since CD 1.4 - so I could be "out of date".
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Dumb COPY issue...

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** William Lynch <wblynch@att.net>
- **Date:** 1999-02-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36C66BFF.C2261ECF@att.net>`
- **References:** `<79mn3c$3ij@lotho.delphi.com> <36C0FF86.B0C427CE@att.net> <79s29d$ok9@lotho.delphi.com> <36C51664.E177D351@att.net> <7a4aos$og4@lotho.delphi.com>`

```
paulr wrote:
> 
> Actually, I am kind of curious why, this is probably a blind
> spot in my professional experience. I have a database background,
> and tend to want to keep the schema or dictionary in one place.

No argument there, but I see "one place" as the COPYLIB, not a single
data structure. 

> <grin>
> 
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> Where else can such really bite me at?

Assume a CICS environment and a copy member which contains 5 data
structures. The program I'm writing needs 4 of them in my W-S and the
5th in the Linkage Section (as my COMMAREA). Yet that 5th member should
be in W-S for those who link to me. Also, some complex file update
programs may need 15 data structures in their W-S, yet most inquiry
programs require only 6 of them. IMHO this sort of flexibility leads to
better software, i.e., fewer workarounds for the mongo copy member which
contains all the data structures. Would you actually put all the copy
code for an entire project into one member?

Bill Lynch
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Dumb COPY issue...

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** paulr@bix.com (paulr)

- **Message-ID:** `<7a6pf0$i4@lotho.delphi.com>`

```
Not procedural code, no. But in the instance you brought up about
needing a structure in WS and in the LINKAGE section, I sure wish 
we had a SIZEOF operator.   

Workstation CICS often requires a rotten LENGTH parameter that 
MF COBOL provides for you, and it would be handy there too. 

I see, in the case you point out, that using separate copybooks is
a really smart idea. I do tend to separate out the sections; as in 
Working Storage vs. FD's vs. Selects vs things like DFHAID.

I must rethink this. :) 

-Paul
```

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1999-02-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7aac6k$1b0$1@news.igs.net>`

```
Cobol does have a SIZEOF operator.  It is called LENGTH.
In fact, I have taken to putting one of those debug statements
into my DLL's that checks the length of records against what
they are supposed to be, as PowerCobol likes 8K arguments
only.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Dumb COPY issue...

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** William Lynch <wblynch@att.net>
- **Date:** 1999-02-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36C90AF1.14F2051B@att.net>`
- **References:** `<79mn3c$3ij@lotho.delphi.com> <36C0FF86.B0C427CE@att.net> <79s29d$ok9@lotho.delphi.com> <36C51664.E177D351@att.net> <7a4aos$og4@lotho.delphi.com> <36C66BFF.C2261ECF@att.net> <7a6pf0$i4@lotho.delphi.com> <36C80BFA.44972C2@zip.com.au> <7aa2sq$fkv@lotho.delphi.com>`

```
paulr wrote:
> 
> Missed it I suppose. I was thinking of length as a parameter in some
> CICS calls.... Thank you for the pointer, I will check the manual.

Are you aware that the translator will insert most of the LENGTH(LENGTH
OF ...) statements for you (COBOL II and up, of course)? Just omit the
LENGTH parameter altogether. The only exception I can think of off the
top is RETURN with COMMAREA, you must supply the LENGTH. In case the
LENGTH OF special register is new to you, it applies to any field, W-S
or L-S, and any level (unlike the ADDRESS OF special register).

Bill Lynch
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Dumb COPY issue...

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** paulr@bix.com (paulr)
- **Date:** 1999-02-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7ab5d3$d3g@lotho.delphi.com>`
- **References:** `<79mn3c$3ij@lotho.delphi.com> <36C0FF86.B0C427CE@att.net> <79s29d$ok9@lotho.delphi.com> <36C51664.E177D351@att.net> <7a4aos$og4@lotho.delphi.com> <36C66BFF.C2261ECF@att.net> <7a6pf0$i4@lotho.delphi.com> <36C80BFA.44972C2@zip.com.au> <7aa2sq$fkv@lotho.delphi.com> <36C90AF1.14F2051B@att.net>`

```
Yeah, in MF COBOL. Unfortunately, as soon s you look at Open Systems
CICS / Transaction Server, you loose a LOT. Like full BMS support
and the compilers complain loudly about missing LENGTH parameters. 

Annoying, I assure you... :)
```

#### ↳ Re: object homework need help!!

- **From:** dxmixxer@netdirect.net (Doug Miller)
- **Date:** 1999-02-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<79nvbb$32k_001@news.netdirect.net>`
- **References:** `<36BF3F21.779087C2@kc-primary.net>`

```
In article <36BF3F21.779087C2@kc-primary.net>,
   steve garrett <steve1@kc-primary.net> wrote:
+This is a multi-part message in MIME format.
+
+could some1 plz take a look @ these 2 programs
+that are suppose 2 work together 4 a class project
+and tell me what i'm doing wrong and make any
+changes if u can thanks
+
The first thing you're doing wrong is expecting us to do your
debugging for you. The second thing you're doing wrong is expecting
us to do it without any information to go on. What are the programs
supposed to do? What do they do, that they should not? What do 
they not do, that they should?
```

#### ↳ Re: object homework need help!!

- **From:** "Chris Osborne" <chris_n_osborne@yahoo.com>
- **Date:** 1999-02-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36c064a1.0@news3.uswest.net>`
- **References:** `<36BF3F21.779087C2@kc-primary.net>`

```
steve garrett wrote in message <36BF3F21.779087C2@kc-primary.net>...
>could some1 plz take a look @ these 2 programs
>that are suppose 2 work together 4 a class project
>and tell me what i'm doing wrong and make any
>changes if u can thanks
>

Steve,

First, I usually don't open the unidentifiable (to me) attachments found in
messages on newsgroups.  So your code remains unknown to me.  (I have no
idea if other people feel the same way about downloading attachments from
usenet.)

Second, it is a fair standard to post your code in the actual message (lurk
around for a while and see), and provide a description of what you're
program does, and what has gone wrong.  If you're program is too big to
post, post only the appropriate portions of the Environment, Data, and
Procedure divisions.  Don't forget the error messages your compiler is
giving you, or highly accurate descriptions of what is happening to the data
if the error is a logical one.

Third, if you had lurked around for a while you would have seen that
chatroom abbreviations are not normal.  In fact, I've seen people be
attacked from time to time for having used them.  This newsgroup is
monitored by many professional programmers from many areas of industry, and
you may want to put your best "face" on when you, virtually speaking, walk
in and make a first impression.


Please take the time to find and read the newsgroup FAQ.  If it isn't
currently listed in your message download queue, you can go to
www.dejanews.com and do a search on comp.lang.cobol in the newsgroup field
and FAQ in the subject field.
If you do not have a browser available (as many don't), you can monitor the
messages for a while and wait until the FAQ is posted.
```

<a id="t-428a4a919e"></a>
## Thread: Retrieve DSN in COBOL — 2 msgs

_Topics: [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe)_

### Re: Retrieve DSN in COBOL

- **From:** Peter W. Smith <Peter_W._Smith@LNOTES3.bankofny.com>
- **Date:** 1999-02-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<79nj5t$jqi$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`
- **References:** `<79a3iu$ovm$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <79ar84$507$1@uranium.btinternet.com> <79djnf$no7$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
In article <79djnf$no7$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>,
  brixford@my-dejanews.com wrote:
> I forwarded, E-Mail to Peter my COBOL solution to fetching DSNAME from DDNAME.
>
> -----------== Posted via Deja News, The Discussion Network ==----------
> http://www.dejanews.com/       Search, Read, Discuss, or Start Your Own

Thank you very much, I tried it but it's off some where, Everything seem to be
right up to the QDB, but then things don't match up. Probably running on a
different version of OS/390(nobody can tell me the exact version were using).
So far I have this .

The address of the TCB is at offset 21C. The JSCB is at offset B4 into the
TCB. I found the TIOT at offset 10 into the TCB. The TIOT has a 24 byte
prefix which contains the jobname and the stepname, followed by 4 bytes of
filler, the DDNAME, pointer to the TTR( whatever that is), and a pointer to
the UCB, which occurs depending on the number of DD statements. The UCB
contains the VOL-SER and a lot of hex numbers. I've been searching IBM's web
site for documentation on the format of these tables, but haven't found
anything. I'm getting close. I've been trying to figure this out for over a
year. It seems that either nobody knows how to do this or their just not
telling me.

-----------== Posted via Deja News, The Discussion Network ==----------
http://www.dejanews.com/       Search, Read, Discuss, or Start Your Own
```

#### ↳ Re: Retrieve DSN in COBOL

- **From:** Gilbert Saint-flour <gsf@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 1999-02-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36bf6026$2$tfs$mr2ice@news>`
- **References:** `<79a3iu$ovm$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <79ar84$507$1@uranium.btinternet.com> <79djnf$no7$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <79nj5t$jqi$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
In <79nj5t$jqi$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>, on 08 Feb 1999 at 20:58, Peter W.
Smith <Peter_W._Smith@LNOTES3.bankofny.com> said:

>Thank you very much, I tried it but it's off some where, Everything seem
>to be right up to the QDB, but then things don't match up. Probably
>running on a different version of OS/390 (nobody can tell me the exact
>version were using).

The TCB, JSCB, TIOT, UCB and JFCB control block haven't changed much in 30
years, and very little since the early 1990s, so that's NOT the problem. 
I haven't seen the code you're trying to get working, but if you pull the
address of the JFCB from the TIOT and get garbage instead of the expected
dsname, it's probably because your job runs with SWA=ABOVE and you need a
SWAREQ macro to convert the JFCB "TTR" to a 31-bit address.

Gilbert Saint-flour <gsf@ibm.net>
```

_Topics: [`Jobs, careers, recruiting, salary`](topics.md#jobs)_

### Average Starting salary

- **From:** just4u2@my-dejanews.com
- **Date:** 1999-02-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<79lm61$2tg$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
My brother will be graduating HS this year. He wants to pursue a career in
COBOL. He will attend a two-year program and wants to work in the DC area. Can
anyone tell us what the average starting salary is? Any information is
appreciated.
```

#### ↳ Re: Average Starting salary

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com>
- **Date:** 1999-02-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36BF5FE8.5DB54D2@NOSPAMhome.com>`
- **References:** `<79lm61$2tg$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
> My brother will be graduating HS this year. He wants to pursue a career in
> COBOL. He will attend a two-year program and wants to work in the DC area. Can
> anyone tell us what the average starting salary is? Any information is
> appreciated.

That's a tough one.  It is pretty hard to get a starting job, so most
programmers start off with some type of trial position.  If they have
the right type of mind, their salaries go up quite rapidly, but that
first job is quite often a fudge.
```

#### ↳ Re: Part III - This Is Your Brain On SAS...This Is Your Brain On COBOL...Any Questions?

- **From:** theodp@aol.com (Theo DP)
- **Date:** 1999-02-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

```
>Subject: Re: Part III - This Is Your Brain On SAS...This Is Your Brain On
>COBOL...Any Questions?
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Just repeating what was posted...

Careful readers will note that COBOL solution (2) below is in fact only a
subroutine that performs no IO and will require an additional (main) program.
Unfair comparison? I don't think so...

If "fair" means a fully executable program that displays the results, it's as
easy as 1-2-3 (statements):

1. DATA;
2. FIELD=COMPRESS("The rain in spain falls mainly in the  
plain!","~!@#$%^&*()_+`1234567890-={}|[]\:"";'<>?,./");
3. PUT FIELD=;

Don't have access to SAS at the moment to run  the above, but hopefully it's
"accuracte" enough to get the point across that COBOL could be made easier
without a real lot of work if people would open their eyes to other
languages... 

>>*** SAS Solution Follows ***
>>
…[159 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

<a id="t-aa54d41026"></a>
## Thread: COBOL74 HP MPE/ix — 4 msgs

_Topics: [`COBOL standards (ANS, ISO, 74/85/2002/2014)`](topics.md#standards)_

### COBOL74 HP MPE/ix

- **From:** mary plath <mplath@accmail.umd.edu>
- **Date:** 1999-02-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36BF04D1.25B83FC4@accmail.umd.edu>`

```
Does anyone know whether COBOL 74 is Y2K compliant  ?
Is there a website on this anywhere?
I've been to www.cobol.org and they do not have the 74 standard there.

Thanks
```

#### ↳ Re: COBOL74 HP MPE/ix

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1999-02-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<V0Ev2.2840$wj1.5698043@news2.mia>`
- **References:** `<36BF04D1.25B83FC4@accmail.umd.edu>`

```
mary plath wrote:
>Does anyone know whether COBOL 74 is Y2K compliant  ?
>Is there a website on this anywhere?
>I've been to www.cobol.org and they do not have the 74 standard there.

Standard COBOL 74 is not Y2K compliant, in that there is no way to get
a four digit current year.  However, most vendors who have 'current'
COBOL 74 compilers (e.g. Unisys), have provided extensions which allow
you to get the full year.  Unisys has a system call which returns the
exact structure returned by the COBOL 85 FUNCTION CURRENT-DATE:

      *
      *  ** CURRENT DATE/TIME (MUST BE 01) **
      *
       01  DW-CURR-DATE-TIME       WITH LOWER-BOUNDS.
           03  DW-CURR-CCYYMMDD        PIC  9(08).
           03  DW-CURR-HOUR            PIC  9(02).
           03  DW-CURR-MINUTE          PIC  9(02).
           03  DW-CURR-SECOND          PIC  9(02)V9(02).
           03  DW-CURR-DIFF-GMT        PIC  X(01).
      *                            '+' DIFF IS AHEAD OF GREENWICH
      *                            '-' DIFF IS BEHIND GREENWICH
      *
           03  DW-CURR-DIFF-HOURS      PIC  9(02).
           03  DW-CURR-DIFF-MINUTES    PIC  9(02).

           CHANGE ATTRIBUTE LIBACCESS OF "GENERALSUPPORT" TO BYFUNCTION.
           CALL "CURRENT_DATE OF GENERALSUPPORT"
               USING DW-CURR-DATE-TIME.

In practical terms, it is not that big a deal.  Assuming your COBOL 74
compiler will correctly return yymmdd after 1/1/2000, you can get by
for the next 100 years using this code:

           03  ccyymmdd      PIC  9(08).
           03  ccyymmdd-r    REDEFINES ccyymmdd.
               05  cc            PIC  9(02).
               05  yymmdd        PIC  9(06).
               05  yymmdd-r      REDEFINES yymmdd.
                   07  YY            PIC  9(02).
                   07  mm            PIC  9(02).
                   07  dd            PIC  9(02).

           ACCEPT yymmdd FROM DATE.
           IF yy < 99
               MOVE 20 TO cc
           ELSE
               MOVE 19 TO cc.

This will produce a correct ccyymmdd for all dates from now through
December 31, 2099.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL74 HP MPE/ix

- **From:** "Mark Undrill" <marku@affirm.co.uk>
- **Date:** 1999-02-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<918561052.5155.0.nnrp-09.9e987e6e@news.demon.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<36BF04D1.25B83FC4@accmail.umd.edu> <V0Ev2.2840$wj1.5698043@news2.mia>`

```

Judson McClendon <judmc123@bellsouth.net> wrote in message
news:V0Ev2.2840$wj1.5698043@news2.mia...
>mary plath wrote:
>>Does anyone know whether COBOL 74 is Y2K compliant  ?
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>exact structure returned by the COBOL 85 FUNCTION CURRENT-DATE:

There is no way to do this using the COBOL74 entry point. Of course if you
use the COBOL85 entry point and the POST85 compiler directive than the COBOL
FUNCTIONS including CURRENT-DATE are available.

The rest of Judson's post which I have sniped is, of course, pure gold :)
<SNIP>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL74 HP MPE/ix

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1999-02-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<itWv2.3421$wj1.6234839@news2.mia>`
- **References:** `<36BF04D1.25B83FC4@accmail.umd.edu> <V0Ev2.2840$wj1.5698043@news2.mia> <918561052.5155.0.nnrp-09.9e987e6e@news.demon.co.uk>`

```
Mark Undrill wrote
>
>Judson McClendon wrote:
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>FUNCTIONS including CURRENT-DATE are available.

Mark, I think you have misunderstood what I was trying to say, or I am
misunderstanding what you are saying. :-).  No, you can't actually code
"FUNCTION CURRENT-DATE" in A Series COBOL 74 and get it to work. :-)
But there is a system call which returns the exact same structure as the
COBOL 85 intrinsic FUNCTION CURRENT-DATE.  To illustrate, below are the
compile listing and CANDE screens from an A Series COBOL 74 test program.

-------------------- COMPILE LISTING -----------------------------------------
VERSION 44.289.8013  (02/04/1998)   Unisys ClearPath HMP NX Series COBOL74 COMPILER         1999 FEBRUARY 09    06:56

                                     (CANDE)CANDE/CODE2490 ON PROGPK

000200 IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.                                                              0000:0000:0
                                                                                    START OF SEGMENT AT (01,002)
000300 PROGRAM-ID.                      JUDTEST.                                             0002:0000:0
000400 ENVIRONMENT DIVISION.                                                                 0002:0000:0
000500 CONFIGURATION SECTION.                                                                0002:0000:0
000600 SOURCE-COMPUTER.                 A-SERIES.                                            0002:0000:0
000700 OBJECT-COMPUTER.                 A-SERIES.                                            0002:0000:0
000800 DATA DIVISION.                                                                        0002:0000:0
000900 WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.                                                              0002:0000:0
001000*                                                                                      0002:0000:0
001100*  ** CURRENT DATE/TIME (MUST BE 01) **                                                0002:0000:0
001200*                                                                                      0002:0000:0
001300 01  DW-CURR-DATE-TIME       WITH LOWER-BOUNDS.                                        0002:0000:0
001400     03  DW-CURR-CCYY            PIC  9(04).                                           0002:0000:0
001500     03  DW-CURR-CCYY-R          REDEFINES DW-CURR-CCYY.                               0002:0000:0
001600         05  DW-CURR-CC              PIC  9(02).                                       0002:0000:0
001700         05  DW-CURR-YY              PIC  9(02).                                       0002:0000:0
001800     03  DW-CURR-MM              PIC  9(02).                                           0002:0000:0
001900     03  DW-CURR-DD              PIC  9(02).                                           0002:0000:0
002000     03  DW-CURR-HOUR            PIC  9(02).                                           0002:0000:0
002100     03  DW-CURR-MINUTE          PIC  9(02).                                           0002:0000:0
002200     03  DW-CURR-SECOND          PIC  9(02)V9(02).                                     0002:0000:0
002300     03  DW-CURR-DIFF-GMT        PIC  X(01).                                           0002:0000:0
002400*                                '+' DIFF IS AHEAD OF GREENWICH                        0002:0000:0
002500*                                '-' DIFF IS BEHIND GREENWICH                          0002:0000:0
002600*                                                                                      0002:0000:0
002700     03  DW-CURR-DIFF-HOURS      PIC  9(02).                                           0002:0000:0
002800     03  DW-CURR-DIFF-MINUTES    PIC  9(02).                                           0002:0000:0
002900 PROCEDURE DIVISION.                                                                   0002:0000:0
003000 00000-CONTROL.                                                                        0002:0000:0
                                                                                  DATA SEGMENT 0004 IS 0002A LON
003100     CHANGE ATTRIBUTE LIBACCESS OF "GENERALSUPPORT" TO BYFUNCTION.                     0002:0000:1
003200     CALL "CURRENT_DATE OF GENERALSUPPORT"                                             0002:0002:3
003300         USING DW-CURR-DATE-TIME.                                                      0002:0002:3
003400     DISPLAY DW-CURR-DATE-TIME.                                                        0002:0004:1
003500     STOP RUN.                                                                         0002:0004:1
                                                                                       SEGMENT 0002 IS 000B LONG
                                                                                  DATA SEGMENT 000A IS 00032 LON
                                                                                    START OF SEGMENT AT (01,006)
                                                                                       SEGMENT 0006 IS 001C LONG
                                                                                    START OF SEGMENT AT (01,00B)
                                                                                  DATA SEGMENT 0001 IS 0000D LON
                                                                                       SEGMENT 000B IS 001F LONG
COMPILE O.K.
TOTAL CARD COUNT: 34
D[01] STACK SIZE: 0016(010) WORDS
D[02] STACK SIZE: 0019(013) WORDS
CORE ESTIMATE:    903 WORDS
STACK ESTIMATE:   512 WORDS
CODE FILE SIZE:   13 RECORDS
PROGRAM SIZE:     3 CODE SEGMENTS, 70 TOTAL WORDS
SUBROUTINE NAME:  CODE2490, LEVEL 02
COMPILED ON THE NX4800 FOR THE LEVEL5 SERIES
COMPILER COMPILED WITH THE FOLLOWING OPTIONS:
    NONE.
COMPILE TIMES:    ELAPSED    CPU      I-O    RPM
                 0002.338 0000.299 0000.131 06826

----------- CANDE SCREEN -----------------------------------------------------
C;SAVE
#COMPILING 6718
#ET=2.8 PT=0.4 IO=0.2
#WORKOBJECT JUD/TEST SAVED; OLD OBJECT REMOVED
RUN
#RUNNING 6720
#6720 DISPLAY:1999020906575397-0500.
#ET=0.1 PT=0.0 IO=0.0
------------------------------------------------------------------------------

If you check the display output above, you will see that it indeed
does match the output from FUNCTION CURRENT-DATE. :-)
```

```
	I'm  writing a database program for a class which allows
adding, updating, and deleting records.  This is all interactive
involving screens, screens, and more screens.  The question I have to
you folks is Does anyone have a sample code of forcing upper-case in
all user input.  I am having trouble finding this in any on-line
library.  This seems like a more practical solution then hard coding
every possible input.  Thank You in advance.
		
	Nick
```
