<a id="t-b1cbd05109"></a>
## Thread: Multiple RELEASE in SORTs - Newbie question... — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`Tutorials, books, learning`](topics.md#learning) · [`Help requests and how-to`](topics.md#help)_

### Re: Multiple RELEASE in SORTs - Newbie question...

- **From:** "Jeff" <a@a.com>
- **Date:** 1999-02-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Mwuv2.3572$fz.12027829@storm.twcol.com>`
- **References:** `<79d6am$c9t$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
>Here's the problem. I am working on a report that sorts all records by
>location, id number, then name, for example.  However, now if the location
is
>15, they want these records to be sorted by a new field(class code).


Although I belive this could be better done using an external sort in JCL, I
would do the following if my life depended on it:

Filter off all records into 3 files, with location code <15 =15 >15.

Sort files <15 and >15 as normal, keeping files separate.

Sort =15 file as needed.

Then either concatenate them in order internally or use the output in JCL as
a concatenated DD statement:

//SORTIN   DD DSN=SOTIN.FILE,DISP=SHR
//SORTLT15 DD DSN=&&LT15,DISP=(NEW,PASS,KEEP)
//SORTEQ15 DD DSN=&&EQ15,DISP=(NEW,PASS,KEEP)
//SORTGT15 DD DSN=&&GT15,DISP=(NEW,PASS,KEEP)
......THEN WHEN YOU NEED TO INPUT INTO NEXT STEP....
//INFILE   DD DSN=&&LT15,DISP=(SHR,PASS,KEEP)
//         DD DSN=&&EQ15,DISP=(SHR,PASS,KEEP)
//         DD DSN=&&GT15,DISP=(SHR,PASS,KEEP)

or after the 3 files have been created, simply read them in one at a time
writing them all out to the same file.


Excuse me if my clarity is insulting, but I just wanted to make sure you
understood.
```

---

<a id="t-8f31393cb3"></a>
## Thread: NOADV compile parameter — 1 msgs

### Re: NOADV compile parameter

- **From:** "Jeff" <a@a.com>
- **Date:** 1999-02-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<yDuv2.3573$fz.12030792@storm.twcol.com>`
- **References:** `<01be5114$d2bc3ca0$9e8489d0@rweill>`

```
If you do not specify FBA it is possible the file will be created without
them. Whatever parameters are not specified will be filled in by COBOL. Even
if you use the ADV/NOADV if you do not use AFTER/BEFORE ADVANCING the file
will not have carriage control characters in it. People sometimes write
reports without them.

Assuming I may be wrong, create a simple test program to verify this.
Compile and ruun. Only by trial and error can you be 100% sure. Create 4
programs. 2 w/ ADV specified 1 with ADVANCING clause, one without. 2 w/
NOADV clause 1 with ADVANCING clause, one without. Run all of them and check
attributes of output files. That is one way to be sure. The manual may not
tell you exactly what you are looking for.
```

---

<a id="t-c76e7c73e1"></a>
## Thread: Perform "Standards" question — 74 msgs

_Topics: [`Language features and syntax`](topics.md#syntax) · [`COBOL standards (ANS, ISO, 74/85/2002/2014)`](topics.md#standards)_

### Perform "Standards" question

- **From:** Bogy@texas.net (Peter)
- **Date:** 1999-02-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<MPG.112a77f51ef0dd60989682@news.texas.net>`

```
Hope you can answer a question to resolve a disagreement at work ...

Does anyone know the "Industry standard" for the following. I feel the 
following code is hogwash ...  Whats the "accepted standard".

NOTE: The use of the "thru" on the perform.  It really does nothing, it 
seems to be a practice I certainly was never taught to do.  Also note the 
use of the "go to" thats in use here that I was shot if I used it.

   procedure division.
   000-start.
	....
	perform 100-test thru 100-exit until its-the-end = 'y'.
	....
	stop run.

   100-test.
	move 'y' to its-the-end.
	go to 100-exit.
	.....

   100-exit.
	exit.
```

#### ↳ Re: Perform "Standards" question

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com>
- **Date:** 1999-02-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36C0C93C.A449580E@NOSPAMhome.com>`
- **References:** `<MPG.112a77f51ef0dd60989682@news.texas.net>`

```
That's a common debate here and elsewhere.  I would abolish sections and
perform thru, if I had my choice, but it is a popular standard.  Some of
the newer COBOL features such as in-line performs, end-if's and continue
are converting people away from liking the GO TO EXIT (which is the only
reason people want perform thru).

Your example doesn't make any sense though.  The GO TO EXIT only makes
sense as a conditional.  Since your example follows a period, it will
perform every time.

Peter wrote:
> 
> Hope you can answer a question to resolve a disagreement at work ...
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
>         exit.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Is this possible?

- **From:** "R. Hayes" <rmch@cadvision.com>
- **Date:** 1999-02-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36c10ca7.0@news.cadvision.com>`
- **References:** `<MPG.112a77f51ef0dd60989682@news.texas.net> <36C0C93C.A449580E@NOSPAMhome.com>`

```
Maybe someone can debunk this, but in '92-'95 I was working on a COBOL74
compiler in a VM environment that appeared to have a bug.  At the time I was
NOT using PERFORM-THRUs since I had just graduated from college where we
used COBOL85 without them.

However in one program I continually got bombs when I ran it... for no
apparent reason (no compile or link errors).  I finally had the crazy idea
that MAYBE the computer was not returning from a paragraph that just
happened to be at the end of the code (I was PERFORMING it in the middle of
the program).  From Assembler programs I knew that leaving out a return
operand was disasterous, and thought that maybe the compiler screwed up (fat
chance).

When I stuck an exit paragraph at the end of the culprit paragraph and then
did a PERFORM-THRU, it worked.

Is this possible or is it my imagination?!?!

Since then I've had a habit of doing PERFORM-THRUs.


Howard Brazee wrote in message <36C0C93C.A449580E@NOSPAMhome.com>...
>That's a common debate here and elsewhere.  I would abolish sections and
>perform thru, if I had my choice, but it is a popular standard.  Some of
…[32 more quoted lines elided]…
>>         exit.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Is this possible?

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-02-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<79r2q7$7va@dfw-ixnews8.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<MPG.112a77f51ef0dd60989682@news.texas.net> <36C0C93C.A449580E@NOSPAMhome.com> <36c10ca7.0@news.cadvision.com>`

```
I am almost positive that there was exactly this bug in the OS/VS COBOL
compiler at some maintenance level.  (I assume that the "VM" that you are
talking about is the IBM one.)

It was a bug,
   IBM provided a fix for it,
     I sure wouldn't have changed my coding technique because of such a bug.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Is this possible?

- **From:** scm@bolo.freeserve.co.uk (Shaun C. Murray)
- **Date:** 1999-02-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36c1e364.114545257@news.enterprise.net>`
- **References:** `<MPG.112a77f51ef0dd60989682@news.texas.net> <36C0C93C.A449580E@NOSPAMhome.com> <36c10ca7.0@news.cadvision.com>`

```
On Tue, 9 Feb 1999 21:36:56 -0700, "R. Hayes" <rmch@cadvision.com>
wrote:

>
>When I stuck an exit paragraph at the end of the culprit paragraph and then
>did a PERFORM-THRU, it worked.
>
>Is this possible or is it my imagination?!?!

Perfectly possible and it's for that reason I don't use paragraphs and
I've never used a perfrom...thru. It's a dangerous practice which can
accidently get code ran by accident.

Say you have a program as below...


       perform a thru d
       stop run.

    a.
       display "hello".
    b.
       display " ".
    d.
       display "world".


and then a maintenance programmer comes along and sticks in a c
paragraph between b and d.

   c.
      display "Disney".


They've now completely altered the intention of your perform a thru d.


>
>Since then I've had a habit of doing PERFORM-THRUs.

I hope you're more careful than the average programmer above.
Personally, I only perform sections and never use paragraphs. Others
will argue the case for paragraphs but I think they are up high on my
list of things I'd rather not see.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Is this possible?

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com>
- **Date:** 1999-02-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36C1E930.9758C044@NOSPAMhome.com>`
- **References:** `<MPG.112a77f51ef0dd60989682@news.texas.net> <36C0C93C.A449580E@NOSPAMhome.com> <36c10ca7.0@news.cadvision.com> <36c1e364.114545257@news.enterprise.net>`

```
> >Since then I've had a habit of doing PERFORM-THRUs.
> 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> list of things I'd rather not see.

It doesn't matter how careful HE is.  It's how careful the maintenance
programmer will be sometime in the future.  Cobol is a great language
for programming for some future entry level programmer to maintain, so
remember that Cobol programs last forever and make your code as easy to
maintain without mistakes as possible.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Is this possible?

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Richard S. Coldiron" <trc2984@unixmail.ks.boeing.com>
- **Date:** 1999-02-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36C21161.601F@unixmail.ks.boeing.com>`
- **References:** `<MPG.112a77f51ef0dd60989682@news.texas.net> <36C0C93C.A449580E@NOSPAMhome.com> <36c10ca7.0@news.cadvision.com> <36c1e364.114545257@news.enterprise.net> <36C1E930.9758C044@NOSPAMhome.com>`

```
Performs...Perform thrus...Perform "paragraphs"...Perform "sections...

> Howard Brazee wrote:
> > 
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> > maintain without mistakes as possible.

Let's face it folks...we can beat this horse so many times that there
won't even be enough left to make glue!  The chances of persuading any
COBOL programmer with time under his belt to switch his/her methodology
is slim at best, so the most obvious thing to preach and hope for is
"consistency," regardless of the method used.  Personally I've had 20+
years of good luck using perform thrus where the thru is strictly an
exit paragraph, but whenever confronted with other pieces of code that
don't employee that method, then I at least attempt to adhere to it's
coding style.  And regardless of the coding methods used, I've always
encountered "well" and "poorly" written code, so instead of preaching
and promoting specific syntax structures, why don't we concentrate on
more important issues like clarity and consistency.

Ok...stepping down from the soapbox now.


RC in ICT
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Is this possible?

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<HEzw2.7399$Fs.7931429@news4.mia>`
- **References:** `<MPG.112a77f51ef0dd60989682@news.texas.net> <36C0C93C.A449580E@NOSPAMhome.com> <36c10ca7.0@news.cadvision.com> <36c1e364.114545257@news.enterprise.net> <36C1E930.9758C044@NOSPAMhome.com> <36C21161.601F@unixmail.ks.boeing.com>`

```
Richard S. Coldiron wrote:
>Performs...Perform thrus...Perform "paragraphs"...Perform "sections...
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>"consistency," regardless of the method used.

I completely agree with the above, Richard.  However, there are a large
number of newbies who read these newsgroups.  I believe these exchanges
provide useful input to those newbies, who are now in the process of
developing their own coding style.  It is for them, not us old timers,
that I articulate my position and reasoning in threads like this.  I
would have dearly loved such input when I was a newbie. :-)

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Is this possible?

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com>
- **Date:** 1999-02-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36C2EA17.6A34F636@NOSPAMhome.com>`
- **References:** `<MPG.112a77f51ef0dd60989682@news.texas.net> <36C0C93C.A449580E@NOSPAMhome.com> <36c10ca7.0@news.cadvision.com> <36c1e364.114545257@news.enterprise.net> <36C1E930.9758C044@NOSPAMhome.com> <36C21161.601F@unixmail.ks.boeing.com>`

```
I've switched my methodology often.  I go by my employer's standards
when they exist.

But my point here is that it is not sufficient to measure the
effectiveness of a standard by looking at how hard or easy it is to
write the program.  It is necessary to look at the life cycle of the
program.  Which standard causes the fewest problems down the road? 
Which take less time to maintain?  Which are easiest to read and
understand?


> > > It doesn't matter how careful HE is.  It's how careful the maintenance
> > > programmer will be sometime in the future.  Cobol is a great language
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
> RC in ICT
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Is this possible?

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1999-02-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3CCw2.7457$Fs.8013470@news4.mia>`
- **References:** `<MPG.112a77f51ef0dd60989682@news.texas.net> <36C0C93C.A449580E@NOSPAMhome.com> <36c10ca7.0@news.cadvision.com> <36c1e364.114545257@news.enterprise.net> <36C1E930.9758C044@NOSPAMhome.com> <36C21161.601F@unixmail.ks.boeing.com> <36C2EA17.6A34F636@NOSPAMhome.com>`

```
Howard Brazee wrote:
>
>But my point here is that it is not sufficient to measure the
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>understand?

Absolutely!  It is not what you think about a standard as you hear it
described, and particularly it isn't how well it matches what a college
teacher who never wrote 1000 lines of production code in their life told
you, or what you read in a book.  It is seeing how it well *works* for
development and maintenance over many years which is the telling point.
I've been really trying to get this point across. ;-)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ No way!

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "R. Hayes" <rmch@cadvision.com>
- **Date:** 1999-02-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36c2129b.0@news.cadvision.com>`
- **References:** `<MPG.112a77f51ef0dd60989682@news.texas.net> <36C0C93C.A449580E@NOSPAMhome.com> <36c10ca7.0@news.cadvision.com> <36c1e364.114545257@news.enterprise.net>`

```
In the thousands of programs I've looked at, I've never seen anyone do what
you did in your example.  And I never would.

When using the wonky OS/VS COBOL74 compiler it was more like:


       perform a thru a-exit.
       perform b thru b-exit.
       perform d thru d-exit.
       stop run.

    a.
       display "hello".
    a-exit.
       exit.

    b.
       display " ".
    b-exit.
       exit.

    d.
       display "world".
    d-exit.
       exit.


So, only an idiot would stick another paragraph before an exit paragraph.

As a side note, I seem to recall someone else mentioning that they saw this
more often in CICS code.  That was my experience too after 4 years in a CICS
shop.

Shaun C. Murray wrote in message <36c1e364.114545257@news.enterprise.net>...
>On Tue, 9 Feb 1999 21:36:56 -0700, "R. Hayes" <rmch@cadvision.com>
>wrote:
>
>>
>>When I stuck an exit paragraph at the end of the culprit paragraph and
then
>>did a PERFORM-THRU, it worked.
>>
…[43 more quoted lines elided]…
>GoFar MTB mag (issue 0.9 now available)      http://www.gofar.demon.co.uk/
```

PERFORM's of SECTIONS were (and probably still are) used often. There can be
any number of labels within the SECTION that are branched to via a GO TO.
You'll eventually hit the EXIT for the SECTION as you transcend downward or GO
TO it explicitly.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: No way!

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com>
- **Date:** 1999-02-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36C43EA4.132A837C@NOSPAMhome.com>`
- **References:** `<MPG.112a77f51ef0dd60989682@news.texas.net> <36C0C93C.A449580E@NOSPAMhome.com> <36c10ca7.0@news.cadvision.com> <36c1e364.114545257@news.enterprise.net> <36c2129b.0@news.cadvision.com> <79takt$l7t$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <36c2506e.0@news.cadvision.com> <36c2d796.177059127@news.enterprise.net> <36C2F014.E944E412@NOSPAMhome.com> <36c30bab.0@news.cadvision.com>`

```
No, you don't code for idiots.  But they are an element which you should
consider while you make your other programming choices.

If you have a choice of two methods which produce comparable results -
do the one less likely to be messed up by idiots.

In fact, give up a bit of effeciency to make maintenance easier.  Most
structured programming does this.  Keep maintenance and beginning
programmers in mind when you create standards and when you code
solution.

Then do what needs doing.

R. Hayes wrote:
> 
> Ok... so I code for idiots?  Forget about exit-paragraphs then, what about
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
> >Some of them have done maintenance on programs I have written.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: No way!

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** cwestbury@giant.intranet.com (Chris Westbury)
- **Date:** 1999-02-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1999Feb13.020807.19537@giant>`
- **References:** `<MPG.112a77f51ef0dd60989682@news.texas.net> <36C43EA4.132A837C@NOSPAMhome.com>`

```
In article <36C43EA4.132A837C@NOSPAMhome.com>,
Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com> wrote:
>
> In fact, give up a bit of efficiency to make maintenance easier.  Most
> structured programming does this.  Keep maintenance and beginning
> programmers in mind when you create standards and when you code

The sad truth in this statement shows just how far software "engineering"
is from engineering:

"I'm sorry, we can't use high-strength steel in your bridge because a
beginning engineer might not understand it."

This is a double canard.  First, even the greenest engineer knows a lot
more than a beginning programer.  The odds are that he does understand
it.  Second, if an engineer, green or otherwise, discovers that he
doesn't understand something, it is his _job_ to learn it on his own time
before Monday morning.  And he does.

"Software enginering" is never going to be anything but a pot of gold at
the end of the rainbow until we can confidently recommend the use of best
practice instead of what we do recommend: dumbing down to the level of
beginning programers.

The other half of this folie a deux is the computer "scientist":

"Through extensive research, we have discovered that most fatal auto
accidents occur in third gear.  Therefore, effective immediately, use of
third gear is prohibited."

Never mind that you don't get very good gas mileage in second ("give up a
bit of efficiency...").  Never mind that the number of fatalities is
still the same, they just happen in second now instead of third (because
everyone still has to drive).

Computer "science" is never going to be a science until it can expain
gotos instead of banning them.

(Let me repeat, in case anyone missed it, that I agree with Mr. Brazee's
advice.)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: No way!

_(thread depth: 12)_

- **From:** paulr@bix.com (paulr)
- **Date:** 1999-02-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7a4bb8$og4@lotho.delphi.com>`
- **References:** `<MPG.112a77f51ef0dd60989682@news.texas.net> <36C43EA4.132A837C@NOSPAMhome.com> <1999Feb13.020807.19537@giant>`

```
Not sure that analogy is at all reflective of reality. 
It would be more like:

Bridge Engineer: 
  I'm sorry sir, it would be a bad idea for us to optimize this 
usage of high end steel in this bridge based soley upon cost criteria.
Besides the thing not being able to cope with unforseen circumstances,
some poor engineer that has to repair or enhance it 10 years from now
might not have access to the design desicisions that were made, and
will have to figure out why we didn't just do this according to 
standards. 
  Not to mention sir, that if we cut corners to 'make the bridge more
efficient' and it fails, who will be responsible for the failure? No 
sir, it is much better to use the accumulated design standards we have
here, and pay a little more to ensure that the thing does what it is 
supposed to do, and is maintainable in the future. 
  After all sir, the cost of steel has come down dramatically in the 
past 20 years, and all indiciations predict that it will contiue to 
do so, as well as improve in quality.

...

  In short, there is little sense to shave cycles, in most cases,
when doing so may save you a few dollars on the average, but cost
you a LOT more when you go to maintain it, or enhance it, or
even fix it when it invarably breaks. 

  Which brings up another point, those highly optimized programs
are jus as subject to programming errors, perhaps *more* subject 
to programming errors, than those programs that are not so highly 
hand coded. Even when done by the best. 

  The beancounters got *one* thing right- the program is useless,
not matter how efficient it is, if it does not operate correctly.

-Paul


Chris Westbury (cwestbury@giant.intranet.com) wrote:
: In article <36C43EA4.132A837C@NOSPAMhome.com>,
: Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com> wrote:

: doesn't understand something, it is his _job_ to learn it on his own time
: before Monday morning.  And he does.

: "Software enginering" is never going to be anything but a pot of gold at
: the end of the rainbow until we can confidently recommend the use of best
: practice instead of what we do recommend: dumbing down to the level of
: beginning programers.

: The other half of this folie a deux is the computer "scientist":

: accidents occur in third gear.  Therefore, effective immediately, use of

: Never mind that you don't get very good gas mileage in second ("give up a

: Computer "science" is never going to be a science until it can expain

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: No way!

_(thread depth: 13)_

- **From:** cwestbury@giant.intranet.com (Chris Westbury)
- **Date:** 1999-02-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1999Feb14.234726.19541@giant>`
- **References:** `<MPG.112a77f51ef0dd60989682@news.texas.net> <7a4bb8$og4@lotho.delphi.com>`

```
In article <7a4bb8$og4@lotho.delphi.com>,
paulr@bix.com (paulr) wrote:
>
> In article <1999Feb13.020807.19537@giant>,
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> Not sure that analogy is at all reflective of reality. 

No, it's reflective of engineering!  You can disagree with my choice of
examples, but my point remains: no profession determines best practice by
whether or not a beginner can do it.  Not medicine, not law, certainly
not engineering!

We don't even rank high among the crafts on this measure.  Carpenters
don't have to keep beginning carpenters in mind when they create
standards and when they build houses.  They make their decisions, then
teach their apprentices whatever is needed.

We have grown too comfortable with the monstrous anomaly on which our
field is now based.  Beginning surgeons don't know advanced surgical
techniques yet?  We don't start them on heart transplants!  We start them
by assisting at appendectomies and bring them along gradually.

Beginning programers don't know advanced programing techniques yet?  We
ban those techniques!  God forbid that beginning programers should have
to know anything before cutting into a program.  It would be bad for
their self-esteem.

As long as we cannot count on any practitioner at any level having any
particular skill, we cannot realize the full potential of software.
                                        

[...]
> In short, there is little sense to shave cycles, in most cases, when
> doing so may save you a few dollars on the average, but cost you a LOT
> more when you go to maintain it, or enhance it, or even fix it when it
> invariably breaks. 

Sad but true.  Did you think I was disagreeing?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: No way!

_(thread depth: 14)_

- **From:** paulr@bix.com (paulr)
- **Date:** 1999-02-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7a8c9l$35o@lotho.delphi.com>`
- **References:** `<MPG.112a77f51ef0dd60989682@news.texas.net> <7a4bb8$og4@lotho.delphi.com> <1999Feb14.234726.19541@giant>`

```
: We have grown too comfortable with the monstrous anomaly on which our
: field is now based.  Beginning surgeons don't know advanced surgical
: techniques yet?  We don't start them on heart transplants!  We start them
: by assisting at appendectomies and bring them along gradually.

This is true. If we are a craft then we need some sort of apprenticeship
program. I f we are an engineering discipline, then we need mentoring,
and qualification exams. :) 


: Beginning programers don't know advanced programing techniques yet?  We
: ban those techniques!  God forbid that beginning programers should have
: to know anything before cutting into a program.  It would be bad for
: their self-esteem.

Mostly I find that it takes two or three years to get a beginner truely 
productive. First you have to disabuse them of the notion that they 
already know everything, and then you have to instill a work ethic
in them, and then you hav eto teach them that rewards are earned, not
awarded for looking good. :) 


: As long as we cannot count on any practitioner at any level having any
: particular skill, we cannot realize the full potential of software.
:                                         

: [...]
: > In short, there is little sense to shave cycles, in most cases, when
: > doing so may save you a few dollars on the average, but cost you a LOT
: > more when you go to maintain it, or enhance it, or even fix it when it
: > invariably breaks. 

: Sad but true.  Did you think I was disagreeing?

Yep, but I was wrong. We thing very much alike.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: No way!

- **From:** docdwarf@clark.net ()

```
In article <7a8c9l$35o@lotho.delphi.com>, paulr <paulr@bix.com> wrote:
>: We have grown too comfortable with the monstrous anomaly on which our
>: field is now based.  Beginning surgeons don't know advanced surgical
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>and qualification exams. :) 

But what we deal with is neither... it is programming!  *Everyone's* a
programmer, didn't you know that?... especially when you deal with COBOL,
it looks just like English, you know, except for all those capital letters
and dashes and well, I'm not technical, mind you, but I say it would go a
*great* deal more easily if you listened to *me*... and since *I* sign
your timesheets you'd better listen!... and all ya gotta do is...

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: No way!

_(thread depth: 12)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com>
- **Date:** 1999-02-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36C83D67.4526A246@NOSPAMhome.com>`
- **References:** `<MPG.112a77f51ef0dd60989682@news.texas.net> <36C43EA4.132A837C@NOSPAMhome.com> <1999Feb13.020807.19537@giant>`

```
In Denver we I-25 is known as The Valley Highway.  There's not much of a
valley, but I-25 through Denver is built low with lots of bridges over
it.  The new governor got elected with a pledge to widen this highway. 
But the original builders did not plan on such maintenance and expansion
of the original specs, so it will be expensive.

I bet there's lots of engineering where the best engineers design their
products so that future "maintenance" engineers don't have to be as
knowledgeable as they are, so that epansion of the original specs can be
done cheaply, and so that their product adapts easily to future needs.  

Of course, they could be modular too.  Replace chip A with chip B.  Plug
car into computer etc.

Chris Westbury wrote:
> 
> In article <36C43EA4.132A837C@NOSPAMhome.com>,
…[41 more quoted lines elided]…
> Christopher Westbury, Midtown Associates, 15 Fallon Place, Cambridge, MA 02138
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: No way!

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** scm@bolo.freeserve.co.uk (Shaun C. Murray)
- **Date:** 1999-02-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36c9979a.97202670@news.enterprise.net>`
- **References:** `<MPG.112a77f51ef0dd60989682@news.texas.net> <36C0C93C.A449580E@NOSPAMhome.com> <36c10ca7.0@news.cadvision.com> <36c1e364.114545257@news.enterprise.net> <36c2129b.0@news.cadvision.com> <79takt$l7t$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <36c2506e.0@news.cadvision.com> <36c2d796.177059127@news.enterprise.net> <36C2F014.E944E412@NOSPAMhome.com> <36c30bab.0@news.cadvision.com>`

```
On Thu, 11 Feb 1999 09:57:16 -0700, "R. Hayes" <rmch@cadvision.com>
wrote:

>Ok... so I code for idiots?

Nope, but you don't leave the front door open for them ;-)

> Forget about exit-paragraphs then, 

Easy - don't use paragraphs at all.

>what about
>COMP-3 fields

Never used them myself other than in test programs or in importing
data from a mainframe. You may have a use for them on a mainframe but
they are of little use on anything else.

>, redefined fields, reference modification or even PERFORM
>UNTIL?  If an idiot can't figure out exit-paragraphs then forget about these
>others.  Do I leave them out too?

You're talking about specific elements whereas I meant that you should
code tighter than a lawyers letter so that no alternative meaning can
be inferred. That way any code that doesn't belong there looks out of
place and can be spotted easily.
```

```
Actually, I use NOT a lot myself. In some architectures is a much more 
efficient (ack! Me on efficiency?!) because the NOT condition will
check a processor flag. The opposite has to check a register value.

_(thread depth: 12)_

- **From:** cwestbury@giant.intranet.com (Chris Westbury)
- **Date:** 1999-02-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1999Feb15.232330.19544@giant>`

Ed.Stevens@nmm.nissan-usa.com wrote:
>
> My personal favorite was the system I took over from a guy whose
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>    do some work.

I'll see your "NOT" and raise you a pair of parentheses:

    If NOT(temp-amt = amt of Rec)
        then
            ...
        else
            ...
    end-if.

No kidding.  Hundreds and hundreds of lines of this stuff.  In production
today.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: No way!

_(thread depth: 13)_

- **From:** Ed.Stevens@nmm.nissan-usa.com
- **Date:** 1999-02-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7abu9b$mkg$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`
- **References:** `<MPG.112a77f51ef0dd60989682@news.texas.net> <7a9d8f$imc$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <1999Feb15.232330.19544@giant>`

```
In article <1999Feb15.232330.19544@giant>,
  cwestbury@giant.intranet.com (Chris Westbury) wrote:
> In article <7a9d8f$imc$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>,
> Ed.Stevens@nmm.nissan-usa.com wrote:
…[23 more quoted lines elided]…
>

I believe it -- and I'll see your pair of parentheses and raise you a compound
nested IF

IF FIELD-A NOT > LOW-VALUES AND KEY-FIELD NOT = PREV-KEY
   IF FIELD-B NOT = FIELD-C OR FIELD-D NOT = LOW-VALUES
      NEXT SENTENCE
   ELSE
     IF ...
ELSE
  IF ....


But that's not nearly as bizzare as the guy who thought white-space made for
larger load modules and slower run-times -- so he wrote "wall-to-wall"

IF MASTER-KEY > TRAN-KEY READ TRAN-REC INTO WS-TRAN-REC AT END GO TO
READ-NEXT-EXIT ELSE IF MASTER-KEY = TRAN-KEY PERFORM UPDATE-MASTER ELSE
READ MASTER-REC INTO WS-MASTER-REC AT END GO TO PROCESS-EXIT.

Ed Stevens

-----------== Posted via Deja News, The Discussion Network ==----------
http://www.dejanews.com/       Search, Read, Discuss, or Start Your Own
```

- **References:** `<MPG.112a77f51ef0dd60989682@news.texas.net> <7a9d8f$imc$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <1999Feb15.232330.19544@giant> <7abu9b$mkg$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <36C994B2.C1CA650@NOSPAMhome.com>`

```
'Twas Tue, 16 Feb 1999 08:54:26 -0700, when Howard Brazee
<brazee@NOSPAMhome.com> illuminated comp.lang.cobol thusly:

>> But that's not nearly as bizzare as the guy who thought white-space made for
>> larger load modules and slower run-times -- so he wrote "wall-to-wall"
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>Best laugh I had all week - or was I crying?

My friend was called in by the boss's secretary because of a word
processing problem.  The secretary opened up a document and all he saw was
a bunch of dots.  He started to comment that he'd never seen a problem
like that but she cut him off and said that wasn't the problem.  She
changed the font to 10 point and the text appeared.

It turns out the secretaries in the executive suite were conserving disk
space by saving all their word processing documents in 1 point type.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: No way!

_(thread depth: 13)_

- **From:** iscdavid@aol.com (ISCDavid)
- **Date:** 1999-02-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19990223101016.29130.00005684@ng114.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<1999Feb15.232330.19544@giant>`

```
Sorta reminds me of some code I found back in the 70's<G>. 

The stupid thing had an IF statement than ran on forever just to avoid a
"getouttahere" forward GOTO. 

I told my staff that if I ever found a thing like that in any code they wrote,
I'd make them eat the entire listing I found it in<G>.

I've done enough of the 2 AM number to appreciate real simple code...especially
in I-O routines (Murphy lives there doesn't he?)

David Olson
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: No way!

_(thread depth: 12)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com>
- **Date:** 1999-02-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36C87AD8.878505CE@NOSPAMhome.com>`
- **References:** `<MPG.112a77f51ef0dd60989682@news.texas.net> <36C0C93C.A449580E@NOSPAMhome.com> <36c10ca7.0@news.cadvision.com> <36c1e364.114545257@news.enterprise.net> <36c2129b.0@news.cadvision.com> <79takt$l7t$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <36c2506e.0@news.cadvision.com> <36c2d796.177059127@news.enterprise.net> <36C2F014.E944E412@NOSPAMhome.com> <7a1bo6$433$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <36C49AEB.428FBD90@NOSPAMhome.com> <7a9d8f$imc$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
> My personal favorite was the system I took over from a guy whose favorite
> reserved word was "NOT".  He really loved things like
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>    do some work.

Funny.  I will have NEXT SENTENCE to avoid having NOT in my logic.  Now
if you replace the = with a >, then I can see it a bit - there's
something to be said in not re-writing user spec when they choose either
"not greater than" vs "less than or equal to".

Now where a case isn't binary, the NOT is more valuable.  Check for
ERROR-STATUS NOT = DB-STATUS-OK can catch more than ERROR-STATUS =
DB-END-OF-SET.
```

- **From:** Ed.Stevens@nmm.nissan-usa.com

In article <36C87AD8.878505CE@NOSPAMhome.com>,
  Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com> wrote:
> > My personal favorite was the system I took over from a guy whose favorite
> > reserved word was "NOT".  He really loved things like
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Oh, I have no problem with NOT if it helps clarify the logic.  But for me,it
seldom does.  But then, whether it does or not may be partially dependant on
how the particular programmer's brain is wired.  Another of his favorites was

IF FIELD-A NOT > LOW-VALUES ...

Start using it in compound conditions (which my predecessor did) and end up
with double negatives and it gets really fun . . . . NOT!


(Opinions are mine and not necessarily those of my employer.)

Ed Stevens

http://www.dejanews.com/       Search, Read, Discuss, or Start Your Own
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ What happened

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "R. Hayes" <rmch@cadvision.com>
- **Date:** 1999-02-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36c2142f.0@news.cadvision.com>`

```
By the way, I forgot to mention what happened with the compiler...

When I ran the following code, the computer would not return from the d
paragraph and hit the stop run.  It bombed (I'm guessing) by falling through
the d paragraph to whatever garbage was there.  When I put the
exit-paragraph on the end of the d paragraph and did a perform-thru, it
worked.

       perform a.
       perform b.
       perform d.
       stop run.

    a.
       display "hello".

    b.
       display " ".

    d.
       display "world".
Shaun C. Murray wrote in message <36c1e364.114545257@news.enterprise.net>...
>On Tue, 9 Feb 1999 21:36:56 -0700, "R. Hayes" <rmch@cadvision.com>
>wrote:
>
>>
>>When I stuck an exit paragraph at the end of the culprit paragraph and
then
>>did a PERFORM-THRU, it worked.
>>
…[43 more quoted lines elided]…
>GoFar MTB mag (issue 0.9 now available)      http://www.gofar.demon.co.uk/
```

- **From:** Ed.Stevens@nmm.nissan-usa.com

```
I observed similar behavior, but didn't know it was a recognized bug.  Like
Bill, I wouldn't change my coding style for it.  I just stuck a dummy
paragraph at the end of the program, like this:

  perform last-real-paragraph
  .
  .
last-real-paragraph.
  do some work.

zzzzzz-end-of-pgm-dummy.

then
>> did a PERFORM-THRU, it worked.

…[22 more quoted lines elided]…

it

the

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Is this possible?

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com>
- **Date:** 1999-02-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36C19283.AB626301@NOSPAMhome.com>`
- **References:** `<MPG.112a77f51ef0dd60989682@news.texas.net> <36C0C93C.A449580E@NOSPAMhome.com> <36c10ca7.0@news.cadvision.com>`

```
Did you try putting a dummy paragraph at the end?  That would seem like
an easier solution which wouldn't change a programming style.

I've used dummy Sections to get rid of error messages caused by our IDMS
error routine copied in.  I've also used a dummy section
ALL-OTHER-PARAGRAPHS SECTION, when compilers required sort routines to
be sections.

R. Hayes wrote:
> 
> Maybe someone can debunk this, but in '92-'95 I was working on a COBOL74
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> chance).

what you want to know ... I do not think there is a hogwash
standard that is really official ...

#### ↳ Re: Perform "Standards" question

- **From:** "WOB" <wobconsult@sprynet.com>
- **Date:** 1999-02-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<79qq9r$il7$1@juliana.sprynet.com>`
- **References:** `<MPG.112a77f51ef0dd60989682@news.texas.net>`

```
"THRU" condones some type of early exit label from the performed routine
(such as a go to). You can get rid of the THRU, EXIT and GO TO and just
PERFORM 100-TEST UNTIL ITS-THE-END = 'Y'. However, if you find that you're
having to maintain a program that uses either PERFORM THRU or PERFORM
Section Name, verify with management whether this is the method that should
be continued (or maybe it's their standard) or you have their blessing to
use standard PERFORM's.

HTH....

WOB

Peter wrote in message ...
>Hope you can answer a question to resolve a disagreement at work ...
>
…[20 more quoted lines elided]…
> exit.
```

#### ↳ Re: Perform "Standards" question

- **From:** "Don Weigend" <dweigend@iswi.net>
- **Date:** 1999-02-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<a6lw2.187$h3.43111@homer.alpha.net>`
- **References:** `<MPG.112a77f51ef0dd60989682@news.texas.net>`

```
The only place I have seen THRU paragraphs used frequently is in on-line
programs (CICS or IMS/DC). It seems to me that some programmers use the EXIT
associated with the THRU as a quick escape route when an edit fails or some
program condition requires getting out. This practice leads to very
unstructured programming that can get harder to maintain and test in the
future. I believe that it is poor programming and should not be done.

Sometimes you really don't have a choice and it is necessary to maintain
code like this because there isn't the time or the risk tolerance to remove
it. The best thing to do is not allow this type of programming by requiring
code reviews and enforcing standards. This takes some time and management
doesn't want us spending any more time than necessary but it will save much
more time supporting and updating the program after it is deployed.
_______________________________________
Don Weigend | Technical Consultant
SPR, Inc.
TEL | 414.319.2000
FAX | 414.319.2020

Peter wrote in message ...
>Hope you can answer a question to resolve a disagreement at work ...
>
…[20 more quoted lines elided]…
> exit.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Perform "Standards" question

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1999-02-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<dPew2.5167$Xl5.7739287@news1.mia>`
- **References:** `<MPG.112a77f51ef0dd60989682@news.texas.net> <36c1099d.518827443@news1.ibm.net>`

```
Thane Hubbell wrote:
>Peter wrote:
>
…[24 more quoted lines elided]…
>Depends on who you ask.  Judson? <G>

I was thinking of staying out of this one. :-)

Peter, I know of only one good reason for using PERFORM THRU, and
that is to permit an early exit from a paragraph.  All the block
structured languages I am familiar with provide some sort of block
EXIT statement.  The designers of all those languages surely thought
it worthwhile to have such a construct.  Were they all fools?  In
my opinion, after years and years seriously studying the issue, it
results in more code, more complex and obfuscated code, when you must
program around the lack of a block exit statement.  Unfortunately,
current COBOL standards do not provide such a construct.  Fortunately,
the proposed new standard does have one: EXIT PARAGRAPH.  When EXIT
PARAGRAPH is available for all my clients, I will happily abandon
PERFORM THRU and GOTO.  Until then, there are many programs which are
simpler, clearer, and more efficient when programmed with PERFORM
THRU and GOTO exit.  An exacerbating factor for me is that my two best
clients still use the Unisys A Series COBOL 74 compiler, so END-xxxx
constructs are not available.  Both have the COBOL 85 compiler.  But
it is much slower than the COBOL 74 compiler, so they rarely use it.

The only disadvantage to PERFORM THRU and GO TO exit (besides irking
GO TO-less purists :), is that it creates opportunity to PERFORM THRU
a mismatched exit, or GO TO out of the paragraph.  These are serious
issues.  To avoid those problems, I use a paragraph numbering scheme
and a simple code checker which flags those errors, plus a few other
coding errors like a missing period or a period in the middle of a
sentence.  The result, with the checker, is cleaner code than I would
produce otherwise. :-)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Perform "Standards" question

- **From:** swiegand@neo.rr.com (SkippyPB)
- **Date:** 1999-02-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

- **References:** `<MPG.112a77f51ef0dd60989682@news.texas.net> <36c1099d.518827443@news1.ibm.net> <dPew2.5167$Xl5.7739287@news1.mia>`

See my comments within Judson's message.



On Wed, 10 Feb 1999 12:19:21 GMT, "Judson McClendon"
<judmc123@bellsouth.net> enlightened us:

>Thane Hubbell wrote:
>>Peter wrote:
…[31 more quoted lines elided]…
> All the block

Well there is another reason.  If it is good or not is the subject for
debate.  You might have a structure that contains more than one
paragraph name and you want to perform all paragraphs.  E.G.,

100-start.
.
.
.
.
110-do-something.
.
.
.
.
999-exit-this-thing.
      Exit.

So a PERFORM 100-START THRU 999-EXIT-THIS-THING would execute all code
in all of the paragraphs where as PERFORM 100-START would only execute
code between 100-start and 110-do-something.

I don't believe there is such a thing as industry standards.  There
are shop standards, for sure and there are programmer standards.
What you use depends on your shop and your own methodology.




>structured languages I am familiar with provide some sort of block
>EXIT statement.  The designers of all those languages surely thought
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
>produce otherwise. :-)

          ////
         (o o)
-oOO--(_)--OOo-

Middle age is when you choose your cereal for the
fiber, not the toy.

 Steve

#### ↳ Re: Perform "Standards" question

- **From:** "John Hicks (Remove \\"nospam\\" from address before replying)" <johnhicks@nospam.ibm.net>
- **Date:** 1999-02-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36C34D40.8A8F6343@nospam.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<MPG.112a77f51ef0dd60989682@news.texas.net>`

```
Peter:

There is no single industry standard for identifying and performing
subroutines in COBOL. The best you can hope for, at present, is a "shop
standard". There are at least three different such standards which can
be reasonably defended. Here's a quick synopsis:

1. OUTLAW GO-TOs. Each subroutine is contained in a single paragraph and
performed with a simple PERFORM. GO-TOs are outlawed. 

(Advantage: Subroutines are clearly identified. Disadvantages:
Distracting devices (flags, excessive nesting) become necessary to avoid
GO-TOs. Breaks down easily (when a heretical programmer inserts an
exceptional GO-TO).

2. USE COBOL SECTIONS. Each subroutines is contained in a single COBOL
section and performed with a simple PERFORM of that section. GO-TOs, if
used, must be to paragraphs with the current section. 

(Advantages: Subroutines are clearly identified. Doesn't break down with
the use of GO-TOs. 
Disadvantage: Most COBOL programmers are afraid of using sections.)

3. OUTLAW SIMPLE PERFORMS. Each subroutine is contained in a range of
paragraphs and performed with a PERFORM-THRU of that range. The last
paragraph in the range must be named the same as the first with an
"-EXIT" tacked on.  GO-TOs may transfer control within a single
subroutine. (Optional to the standard:) GO-TOs may transfer out of a
subroutine only in exceptional (terminal error) conditions. 

(Advantage: Identifies subroutines, but not as clearly as 1 & 2. Doesn't
break with GO-TOs. Doesn't require sections. 
Disadvantages: Perform-thrus are less readable than simple performs.
Breaks easily when the naming convention is violated. You can't identify
a subroutine without first searching for an exit paragraph. PERFORM-THRU
allows for overlapping ranges, a very messy "structure".)

As you can see, the example you cite follows the third standard. Both
you and your co-workers are correct: It is hogwash. It is also the most
prevalent of the three standards!

There have been numerous threads on this subject in this newsgroup. It
is a religious subject (and we may be embarking on another long
thread...)

(There is consensus on one thing: You do not want to mix these standards
in a single program!)

John Hicks
johnhicks at ibm dot net

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Perform "Standards" question

- **From:** Barticus@att.spam.net (Randall Bart)
- **Date:** 1999-02-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36c38066.9968722@netnews>`
- **References:** `<MPG.112a77f51ef0dd60989682@news.texas.net> <36C34D40.8A8F6343@nospam.ibm.net>`

```
'Twas Thu, 11 Feb 1999 16:36:00 -0500, when "John Hicks" illuminated
comp.lang.cobol thusly:

>1. OUTLAW GO-TOs. Each subroutine is contained in a single paragraph and
>performed with a simple PERFORM. GO-TOs are outlawed. 
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>exceptional GO-TO).

A big disadvantage of absolute abolition of GO TO is what I call false
recursion.  Suppose we have a paragraph (could be a section) PERFORMed
from all over the program to retrieve the next open record.  It frequently
gets coded this way:

GET-NEXT-OPEN-RECORD.
    MOVE 0 TO END-INPUT-FLAG
    READ INPUT-FILE
        AT END 
            MOVE 1 TO END-INPUT-FLAG  
        NOT AT END
            IF  IN-OPEN-FLAG = 0
                PERFORM GET-NEXT-OPEN-RECORD
            END-IF
    END-READ.

What's wrong with this program is that it works.  It may work correctly
every day for months, perhaps years, and then one day it hits 87
consecutive closed records and blows the perform stack.  By changing
PERFORM to GO, the routine is functionally the same, except for not
failing.

If you were called in at 2:00 when this program blew up, how would you fix
it?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Perform "Standards" question

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Arnold" <arnoldh@home.com>
- **Date:** 1999-02-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<AjEx2.361$0A4.1070@news.rdc1.mi.home.com>`
- **References:** `<MPG.112a77f51ef0dd60989682@news.texas.net> <36C34D40.8A8F6343@nospam.ibm.net> <36c38066.9968722@netnews> <36C40B16.317D569B@zip.com.au>`

```
Write it using an in-line perform instead.

Asumming IN-OPEN-FLAG value is 1 for an open record:

GET-NEXT-OPEN-RECORD.
      MOVE 0 TO END-INPUT-FLAG
      PERFORM UNTIL END-INPUT-FLAG = 1 OR IN-OPEN-FLAG = 1
          READ INPUT-FILE
             AT END
                   MOVE 1 TO END-INPUT-FLAG
          END-READ
      END-PERFORM.

Performing the above from wherever will get you the next "open" record and
handle end-fo-file conditions without the use of a GOTO.

I agree that the inline perform is the way to go, but why not the much more
readable form:

get-next-open-record.
    set not-end-of-input to true
    perform until end-of-input on record-type-in-open
      READ INPUT-FILE
        AT END
          set end-of-input to true
      END-READ
    END-PERFORM.

>how would you fix it....
(1) Find the person that coded it and strangle them; 

(2) Code it like this in the first place:

 GET-NEXT-OPEN-RECORD.
    MOVE 0 TO END-INPUT-FLAG
    perform while end-input-flag not 1
      READ INPUT-FILE
        AT END 
          MOVE 1 TO END-INPUT-FLAG  
        NOT AT END
          perform some-work-on-this-record
        END-READ
      end-perform
      .

#### ↳ Re: looking for utility to show file layouts from copybooks

- **From:** "John Hicks (Remove \\"nospam\\" from address before replying)" <johnhicks@nospam.ibm.net>
- **Date:** 1999-02-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36C86362.11A81D9@nospam.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<79qsti$ftc$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
I have a utility I wrote that creates a database table from a cobol data
copybook: one row per data element and with columns for:

-Data element key (unique sequential number)
-COBOL level number
-Dataname
-Group/elementary indicator
-Redefines indicator
-Maximum occurrences
-Size (elementary and group, aware of redefines and occurs)
-Usage
-Picture
-Sign indicator
-Value
-Record name (i.e. the current 01 level dataname)
-Pointer to group-level parent 
-Copybook name

It would be simple enough to patch it to give a starting point based on
a running sum of the size column.

It's written in Realia COBOL and is DOS-based. The output is a
comma-delimited file which can be imported into most any database.

If you're interested, let me know!

--John Hicks
johnhicks at ibm dot net

## Thread: COMP/COMP-3 Conversion Problems — 23 msgs

_Topics: [`Language features and syntax`](topics.md#syntax)_

### COMP/COMP-3 Conversion Problems

- **From:** gregs@security-life.com
- **Date:** 1999-02-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<79q0rr$mcd$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
I am trying to convert a file containing COMP/COMP-3 fields to remove the
packed data.  I wrote a somple Cobol program in the IBM environment to do
this process.  I am moving PIC s9(4) COMP  to PIC 9(4).  When I execute the
program, I get a data conversion error (0C7).  I have not had this type of
problem working on other platforms and was wondering if there was something
special about the IBM world?

Thanks
Greg Stromath

-----------== Posted via Deja News, The Discussion Network ==----------
http://www.dejanews.com/       Search, Read, Discuss, or Start Your Own
```

#### ↳ Re: COMP/COMP-3 Conversion Problems

- **From:** WOB@my-dejanews.com
- **Date:** 1999-02-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<79q9pp$ur5$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`
- **References:** `<79q0rr$mcd$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
In article <79q0rr$mcd$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>,
  gregs@security-life.com wrote:
> I am trying to convert a file containing COMP/COMP-3 fields to remove the
> packed data.  I wrote a somple Cobol program in the IBM environment to do
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>

What is the source of your data? Is it ASCII? You'll never bomb moving BINARY
(COMP) to DISPLAY-NUMERIC, regardless whether it was created as ASCII or
EBCDIC. Your BINARY-DATA will be loaded into a register, an instruction
converts it to COMP-3 and it is then Unpacked. I suspect the culprit is the
PACKED-DECIMAL (COMP-3) Data, in particular, the sign-nibble in the last byte.
Having said that, what puzzles me is that moving PACKED-DECIMAL to DISPLAY-
NUMERIC should not cause a S0C7 (this can't happen with an UNPK instruction),
unless your sending or receiving picture clause specifies an implied decimal-
point. In which case, a decimal shift instruction could very well bomb if the
data is not a valid PACKED-DECIMAL value.

Could you post the basics of your code so we can have look see and maybe a
snapshot of some of the data?

Cheers,

WOB

-----------== Posted via Deja News, The Discussion Network ==----------
http://www.dejanews.com/       Search, Read, Discuss, or Start Your Own
```

#### ↳ Re: COMP/COMP-3 Conversion Problems

- **From:** DOUG-PARTCH@WORLDNET.ATT.NET
- **Date:** 1999-02-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<79qb46$87$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`
- **References:** `<79q0rr$mcd$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
In article <79q0rr$mcd$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>,
  gregs@security-life.com wrote:
> I am trying to convert a file containing COMP/COMP-3 fields to remove the
> packed data.  I wrote a somple Cobol program in the IBM environment to do
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Here is your problem, a "S9(4) COMP" field can handle numbers from -32768 to
32767 and "S9(9) COMP" field can handle numbers from -2147483648 to
2147483647.

Hope this helps.

Above info taken directly from IBM manuals.

-----------== Posted via Deja News, The Discussion Network ==----------
http://www.dejanews.com/       Search, Read, Discuss, or Start Your Own
```

#### ↳ Re: COMP/COMP-3 Conversion Problems

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1999-02-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36c108f0.518654505@news1.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<79q0rr$mcd$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
On Tue, 09 Feb 1999 19:04:35 GMT, gregs@security-life.com wrote:

>I am trying to convert a file containing COMP/COMP-3 fields to remove the
>packed data.  I wrote a somple Cobol program in the IBM environment to do
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>special about the IBM world?

PIC S9(4) COMP can hold up to 32767 (or there abouts) - depending on
your TRUNC options - try moving it to a PIC S9(5) field and see where
that gets you.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: COMP/COMP-3 Conversion Problems

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-02-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<79r2gk$7oc@dfw-ixnews8.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<79q0rr$mcd$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <36c108f0.518654505@news1.ibm.net>`

```
Trunc/Notrunc (or with newer compilers) TRUNC (OPT), (BIN), (STD) is an
interesting and worthwhile discussion. HOWEVER, there is no way that any
TRUNC setting has (to the best of my knowledge in the history of IBM
mainframe compilers) had anything to do with a S0C7 ABEND (the originally
reported problem).

You can, however, get an S0C7 if you work REAL hard at it and specify the
wrong NUMPROC compiler option.  (The more likely symptom of making the wrong
choice here is to NOT get an S0C7 - when you would expect it.)

For the original question:

1)  The original problem stated that you were moving a signed field to an
unsigned field. Was this intentional?  Are you certain that your input DOES
include a valid sign-nibble (i.e. X'C' or X'D' - and not X'F' which
indicates UNsigned) - this only applies if your sending item is "packed"
(see below for my confusion on what you are actually trying to do)

2) Did you (by any chance) happen to upload the data to your mainframe from
a PC specifying ASCII to EBCDIC conversion for ALL fields (including those
defined as COMP)?  This will cause "nasty" data in your comp fields (but
again, I can't imagine this causing a S0C7 on a simple MOVE statement).
This could mess up COMP-3 fields no end (and could easily cause S0C7
symptoms).

3) Your original report of the problem says
  "remove the packed data" but references COMP/COMP-3 fields (and then you
talk about moving a COMP field to a DISPLAY field).  Are you trying to
convert binary data? packed data? or both?  (and do you have your input
fields defined correctly to match both what you want to do and what the data
has).
```

#### ↳ Re: COMP/COMP-3 Conversion Problems

- **From:** "Tom Eisenman" <Tom_Eisenman@NOJUNKbigfoot.com>
- **Date:** 1999-02-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Etgw2.59$j3.36@testbox>`
- **References:** `<79q0rr$mcd$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
OC7 can also mean that there is non-numeric data in that numeric PIC S9(4)
COMP. field.  You might have spaces or high-values.

Another thing to watch out for is that you are moving a signed field to an
unsigned field.


gregs@security-life.com wrote in message
<79q0rr$mcd$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>...
>I am trying to convert a file containing COMP/COMP-3 fields to remove the
>packed data.  I wrote a somple Cobol program in the IBM environment to do
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>http://www.dejanews.com/       Search, Read, Discuss, or Start Your Own
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: COMP/COMP-3 Conversion Problems

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-02-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<79slsl$5dg@dfw-ixnews12.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<79q0rr$mcd$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <Etgw2.59$j3.36@testbox>`

```


Tom Eisenman wrote in message ...
>OC7 can also mean that there is non-numeric data in that numeric PIC S9(4)
>COMP. field.  You might have spaces or high-values.
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>

As pointed out in another post, you can't get INVALID data in a COMP field.
(You can get data "out of range" - but that isn't the same thing and doesn't
cause S0C7's).  If your PIC S9(04) COMP field has "spaces" in it, then
X'4040' will be evaluated as a number and treated accrodingly.  Similarly,
if you have high-values X'FFFF" will give you a numeric value.  Finally, you
can't even get an invalid sign in a binary field (IBM mainframe - other
systems might do this - but I doubt it).  The high-order bit is going to be
on or it is going to be off - and both are valid signs.  (Everything stated
above is NOT true if the field is really Packed-Decimal or COMP-3 - on IBM -
but it is true for Binary or COMP)

NOTE (for those following the draft of the next Standard),  they have
defined the extended "IF NUMERIC" test so that binary fields with values
outside the range of the PICTURE clause *will fail* the IF NUMERIC test (for
the traditional binary data types - not the new C-like "true binary" data
types).
```

> 
> NOTE (for those following the draft of the next Standard),  they have
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…

C like binary values???    I assume that s9(04) BINARY is not what you

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COMP/COMP-3 Conversion Problems

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-02-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7a4u99$dm3@dfw-ixnews12.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<79q0rr$mcd$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <Etgw2.59$j3.36@testbox> <79slsl$5dg@dfw-ixnews12.ix.netcom.com> <36C40DA2.38EC1840@zip.com.au>`

```

Ken Foskey wrote in message <36C40DA2.38EC1840@zip.com.au>...
>William M. Klein wrote:
>>
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>

COMP, BINARY, and all the existing USAGEs keep their existing meanings.
However, there are new USAGEs (which are like USAGE INDEX where you don't
specify a PICTURE clause) with names like

   BINARY-CHAR
   BINARY-LONG
   BINARY-LONG

These also default to SIGNED but have a possible "clause" of UNSIGNED.

I don't like the fact that they are like C datatypes that are not guaranteed
to be portable (because not all operating systems have 8-bit bytes) - but I
will be happy to see a way to get source code that is not dependent upon
"TRUNC" compiler options.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: COMP/COMP-3 Conversion Problems

- **From:** Binyamin_Dissen@theoffice.net (Binyamin Dissen)
- **Date:** 1999-02-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36c4bcd8.84447728@news.netvision.net.il>`
- **References:** `<79q0rr$mcd$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <Etgw2.59$j3.36@testbox>`

```
On Wed, 10 Feb 1999 09:13:52 -0500 "Tom Eisenman"
<Tom_Eisenman@NOJUNKbigfoot.com> wrote:

:>OC7 can also mean that there is non-numeric data in that numeric PIC S9(4)
:>COMP. field.  You might have spaces or high-values.

There is not such thing as a non-numeric comp field.

Spaces = x'4040' = 4*1024+4*16 = 4160

A move from a COMP field to a display field cannot generate an 0C7 (except if
there is a compiler bug).

:>Another thing to watch out for is that you are moving a signed field to an
:>unsigned field.

Would not cause an 0C7.

:>gregs@security-life.com wrote in message
:><79q0rr$mcd$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>...
:>>I am trying to convert a file containing COMP/COMP-3 fields to remove the
:>>packed data.  I wrote a somple Cobol program in the IBM environment to do
:>>this process.  I am moving PIC s9(4) COMP  to PIC 9(4).  When I execute the
:>>program, I get a data conversion error (0C7).  I have not had this type of
:>>problem working on other platforms and was wondering if there was something
:>>special about the IBM world?

Find out exactly where the 0C7 is occurring.

It is not occurring on the move from COMP to display.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COMP/COMP-3 Conversion Problems

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com>
- **Date:** 1999-02-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36C1F889.DBE8E598@NOSPAMhome.com>`
- **References:** `<79q0rr$mcd$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <Etgw2.59$j3.36@testbox> <36c4bcd8.84447728@news.netvision.net.il>`

```
> A move from a COMP field to a display field cannot generate an 0C7 (except if
> there is a compiler bug).
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> Would not cause an 0C7.

I just thought of a way to get an OC7 (or many other errors). 
Overwriting code with tables can do this.  If the program has tables,
double check its boundary logic.  Maybe even populate a filler after the
table and check it to see that it hasn't been overwritten.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COMP/COMP-3 Conversion Problems

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** JD <beta1@bigfoot.com>
- **Date:** 1999-02-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36C216EF.8C210A7C@bigfoot.com>`

```

decimal.  This would then cause the "data exception" this person is getting.

> > A move from a COMP field to a display field cannot generate an 0C7 (except if
> > there is a compiler bug).
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> table and check it to see that it hasn't been overwritten.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COMP/COMP-3 Conversion Problems

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-02-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<79trpu$2uu@dfw-ixnews10.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<79q0rr$mcd$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <Etgw2.59$j3.36@testbox> <36c4bcd8.84447728@news.netvision.net.il> <36C1F889.DBE8E598@NOSPAMhome.com> <36C216EF.8C210A7C@bigfoot.com>`

```
No compiler with the concept of "S0C7" also has a way of treating COMP as
Packed-Decimal.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COMP/COMP-3 Conversion Problems

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** Barticus@att.spam.net (Randall Bart)
- **Date:** 1999-02-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36c37d50.9181372@netnews>`
- **References:** `<79q0rr$mcd$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <Etgw2.59$j3.36@testbox> <36c4bcd8.84447728@news.netvision.net.il> <36C1F889.DBE8E598@NOSPAMhome.com> <36C216EF.8C210A7C@bigfoot.com> <79trpu$2uu@dfw-ixnews10.ix.netcom.com>`

```
'Twas Thu, 11 Feb 1999 00:03:31 -0600, when "William M. Klein"
<wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> illuminated comp.lang.cobol thusly:

>No compiler with the concept of "S0C7" also has a way of treating COMP as
>Packed-Decimal.

First, AIUI no compiler has a concept of "SOC7", period.  That's an
operating system concept.

Second, he said "OC7" not "SOC7".  I don't know if there's any
distinction, but if there is you missed it.

Third, on AS/400 COMP means packed decimal and it can get data exceptions.
Does AS/400 call that "OC7"?  I have no idea.  I know nothing about
AS/400.

Fourth, since AS/400 uses COMP for packed decimal, it would make a lot of
sense for IBM to provide an AS/400 compatibility option in VSE and MVS.  I
thought there was such an option, but if you say there isn't then that's
just one more example of IBM bumbling.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: COMP/COMP-3 Conversion Problems

- **From:** William Lynch <wblynch@att.net>
- **Date:** 1999-02-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36C25EA6.F9098B65@att.net>`
- **References:** `<79q0rr$mcd$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <Etgw2.59$j3.36@testbox>`

```
Tom Eisenman wrote:
> 
> OC7 can also mean that there is non-numeric data in that numeric PIC S9(4)
> COMP. field.  You might have spaces or high-values.

I don't see this as a problem with a COMP (or BINARY) field (restricting
my comments, of course, to the IBM h/w & s/w with which I'm familiar).
There is no invalid bit pattern in a binary field, e.g., if you get
SPACES in a PIC S9(9) field it's interpreted as one billion plus. 

Bill Lynch
```

- **From:** John Trifon <jtrifon@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-02-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36C2A5EC.1B305770@ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<79q0rr$mcd$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <79tanj$4ak3@cns.compuware.com>`

```
The only requirement is the sending field  of a MOVE be in the proper format.
The receiving field can contain any anything. As noted earlier, there are no
invalid bit patterns for a COMP field.

gee wrote:

> r u initialising the receiving fields prior to the move statement?
>
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> >http://www.dejanews.com/       Search, Read, Discuss, or Start Your Own
```

- **Date:** 1999-02-11T00:00:00

> The only requirement is the sending field  of a MOVE be in the proper format.
> The receiving field can contain any anything. As noted earlier, there are no
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> > >special about the IBM world?

It could be his compiler defines COMP as packed decimal and the User's Guide for
his machine would be the best source for that.  I have seen COMP-1 defined as
either short floating-point OR as a binary field on different compilers and
wouldn't be surprised if COMP changes its meaning as well.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COMP/COMP-3 Conversion Problems

- **From:** "Rick Price" <rick@hpdi.demon.co.uk>
- **Date:** 1999-02-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<918821029.17233.0.nnrp-01.9e98b131@news.demon.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<79q0rr$mcd$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <79tanj$4ak3@cns.compuware.com> <36C2A5EC.1B305770@ix.netcom.com>`

```
I always thought that COMP was implementation dependant and  meant  the
'native' arithmetic type.

On Wang VS and IBM AS400 COMP means packed decimal but on the unix  (MF
COBOL) it means binary.  I can't remember about IBM mainframes, as I mainly
used BAL and PL/I but I think it meant packed decimal there as well.

So possibly your treating a binary field as a packed decimal field which
would certainly give conversion errors.

Rick

"certificate" I look at when I am considering an employee is the resume.
That should tell me the experience of the individual.

I think the MSCD/MSCE is just an advertising plug for MS products.  I wonder
if Grace Hopper was a certified Cobol programmer?  If not, would she be
denied a job over someone who was certified in Cobol?


Bill

<a id="t-665156fc47"></a>
## Thread: Question on path calls with IMS databases — 3 msgs

_Topics: [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe) · [`Databases and SQL`](topics.md#databases)_

### Question on path calls with IMS databases

- **From:** Anonymous <nobody@base.xs4all.nl>
- **Date:** 1999-02-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<a3906e407b9f0ba3b711bbb23e888d90@base.xs4all.nl>`

```
Here's a question for all you fossils out there: path calls with sequential
processing of an IMS hiearchial database.

I've an IMS database named BILLS with the following segments, layed out in
hiearchial order:
A001
  A002
    A004
      A011

Each segment can have multiple occurances.
Each segment has one field as a unique key. The name of the key field
in segment A011 is A011-REFDES .

Suppose I wish to sequentially process the database for every occurance of
every A011 segment with a key value of 'RRR' and *only* those occurances. This
I could easily do with coding an qualified SSA in my DLI call:

	CALL 'CBLTDLI' USING DLI-GN
	                     BILLS-PCB
	                     A011-IO
	                     SSA-A011.

	            where SSA-A011 is declared as 'A011    (A011-REFDES= RRR)'
                    and RRR is the value of the key field I want.

BUT suppose I also want to grab information from other fields in the segments
above A011 in the hiearchy.  I could do this by making the DLI call a "path call":
	
	CALL 'CBLTDLI' USING DLI-GN
	                     BILLS-PCB
	                     A011-IO
	                     SSA-A001-P
	                     SSA-A002-P 
	                     SSA-A004-P 
	                     SSA-A011.

My problem is I can't figure out how to code an SSA to use the D command code
(for path call)  for sequential processing  i.e. withOUT coding a search argument
in the SSA.

The path-call SSAs examples I've seen all look like this:
	'A001    *D(A001-ITEMI1= ZZZ)'
	'A002    *D(A002-ITEMI2= AAA)'
	'A004    *D(A001-ITEMI4= BBB)'
  that is, they're set up for GU calls, not sequential GN calls. 

So my criteria are:

-sequential stepping through database, processing only lowest-segment occurances with a 
 specific key-field value
-load IO areas of higher-level segment occurances at the same time.

Can this be done? Anybody have an idea on how to set this up? Examples?



Please reply to the following address after removing "antispam" and
reversing "tom" in the following address:
antip23spam610@email.tom.com

or simply reply to this article in the ng, I'll see it.


Thanks,

Mike Striefel
Motorola SSTG
```

#### ↳ Re: Question on path calls with IMS databases

- **From:** "Simon Cordingley" <simonc@casegen.co.uk>
- **Date:** 1999-02-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36c0b443.0@mercury.nildram.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<a3906e407b9f0ba3b711bbb23e888d90@base.xs4all.nl>`

```
Just leave a space after the "D" command code. This is an unqualified SSA.

Simon Cordingley
Casegen Systems Ltd

simon@casegen.co.uk
www.casegen.co.uk


Anonymous wrote in message ...
>Here's a question for all you fossils out there: path calls with sequential
>processing of an IMS hiearchial database.
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
>every A011 segment with a key value of 'RRR' and *only* those occurances.
This
>I could easily do with coding an qualified SSA in my DLI call:
>
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>BUT suppose I also want to grab information from other fields in the
segments
>above A011 in the hiearchy.  I could do this by making the DLI call a "path
call":
>
> CALL 'CBLTDLI' USING DLI-GN
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>My problem is I can't figure out how to code an SSA to use the D command
code
>(for path call)  for sequential processing  i.e. withOUT coding a search
argument
>in the SSA.
>
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>-sequential stepping through database, processing only lowest-segment
occurances with a
> specific key-field value
>-load IO areas of higher-level segment occurances at the same time.
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

- **From:** Anonymous <nobody@base.xs4all.nl>

<a id="t-3860cf4c6e"></a>
## Thread: Micro Focus: A Better Way of Programming ? — 4 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### Micro Focus: A Better Way of Programming ?

- **From:** "Camp David" <dvdwaaij@advantage.nl>
- **Date:** 1999-02-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<79pr92$n3e@news3.euro.net>`

```
Lately I bought an "Animator Version 2" of Micro Focus
running on W95

But I can't even make *.exe files with it.

Is there somebody who can tell me what to do?
Is there somebody who programmed a program making *.exe files himself ?

Please help me.

D.
```

#### ↳ Re: Micro Focus: A Better Way of Programming ?

- **From:** scm@bolo.freeserve.co.uk (Shaun C. Murray)
- **Date:** 1999-02-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36c1e0fd.113930523@news.enterprise.net>`
- **References:** `<79pr92$n3e@news3.euro.net>`

```
On 9 Feb 1999 17:29:06 GMT, "Camp David" <dvdwaaij@advantage.nl>
wrote:

>Lately I bought an "Animator Version 2" of Micro Focus
>running on W95

[snip]

Memory Lane cogs whirring again. 

Just remembered my Micro Focus pre-history. The 'A Better Way of
Programming' slogan started me thinking.

Animator Version 2 *was* sold as a seperate product a long, long time
ago. It was an add on to Level II COBOL circa 1984 and ran on DOS and
CP/M plus a few others of that era. I even have the rather fetching
wipe clean blue plastic manual here in the office library.

David, if it's character mode only and not GUI and dates from the
80's, it's not worth bothering with at all. It's not a complete
compiler without Level II COBOL and that's out of date.
```

#### ↳ Re: Micro Focus: A Better Way of Programming ?

- **From:** scm@bolo.freeserve.co.uk (Shaun C. Murray)
- **Date:** 1999-02-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36c186eb.90872908@news.enterprise.net>`
- **References:** `<79pr92$n3e@news3.euro.net>`

```
On 9 Feb 1999 17:29:06 GMT, "Camp David" <dvdwaaij@advantage.nl>
wrote:

>Lately I bought an "Animator Version 2" of Micro Focus
>running on W95

Usually it comes with a compiler too.

>
>But I can't even make *.exe files with it.

Animator Version 2 is the source level debugger and editor. It's not a
linker.

>
>Is there somebody who can tell me what to do?
>Is there somebody who programmed a program making *.exe files himself ?

Indeed there is but I suggest the manuals are a better option. Try the
System Reference 'Linking/Library' chapter. Depending on what version
of compiler you have, you may find a demo program with a batch file
called TTDEMO.BAT which will go through most of the stages of
compiling and linking a program for you.
```

#### ↳ Re: Micro Focus: A Better Way of Programming ?

- **From:** Ed.Stevens@nmm.nissan-usa.com
- **Date:** 1999-02-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<79s3pe$g91$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`
- **References:** `<79pr92$n3e@news3.euro.net>`

```
You bought Animator?  By itself?  If so, that's really odd, since Animator is
simply one piece of the MF product package and, to my knowledge, is not sold
as a separate product.

In any event, I'm not surprised you can't get an .EXE out of Animator. 
That's not what it's for.  Animator is an interactive source code level
debugger.  To get an .EXE file you need a compiler (to produce object files)
and a linker (to link the object files into EXEcutables).

Opinions are mine and not necessarily those of my employer.

- Ed Stevens

In article <79pr92$n3e@news3.euro.net>,
  "Camp David" <dvdwaaij@advantage.nl> wrote:
> Lately I bought an "Animator Version 2" of Micro Focus
> running on W95
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Ed Stevens
Nissan Motor Mfg. Corp., USA

```

<a id="t-7510bbd892"></a>
## Thread: COBOL on a IBM SYSTEM36 — 4 msgs

### COBOL on a IBM SYSTEM36

- **From:** bobek <bobek@swbell.net>
- **Date:** 1999-02-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36C0082F.517E@swbell.net>`

```
Someone is telling me that cobol on a System36 ( they are calling it
System36 COBOL) is a diffrent language then COBOL74/COBOL85. Can someone
give me a little feed back on this.
				Thanks;
				Bob
```

#### ↳ Re: COBOL on a IBM SYSTEM36

- **From:** kownby@humboldt1.com (Kindrick Ownby)
- **Date:** 1999-02-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36c09dc4.8374249@news2.humboldt1.com>`
- **References:** `<36C0082F.517E@swbell.net>`

```
On Tue, 09 Feb 1999 02:04:31 -0800, bobek <bobek@swbell.net> wrote:

>Someone is telling me that cobol on a System36 ( they are calling it
>System36 COBOL) is a diffrent language then COBOL74/COBOL85. Can someone
>give me a little feed back on this.
>				Thanks;
>				Bob

From the IBM System/36 COBOL manual:

... is designed according to the following industry standards as
understood and interpreted by IBM, as of April 1983:

- The ANS COBOL, X3.23-1974 standard.

- The December 1975 Federal Information Processing Standard (FIPSPUB
21-1) low-intermediate level.

And there are a few IBM extensions in the compiler.

Kindrick
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL on a IBM SYSTEM36

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-02-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<79qf00$a8v@dfw-ixnews4.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<36C0082F.517E@swbell.net> <36c09dc4.8374249@news2.humboldt1.com>`

```
And just to make it perfectly clear the System/36 pre-dates IBM even
thinking about supporting ANSI'85.  It also isn't really clear what you mean
by "COBOL74".  There have been some vendors who have actually called their
products that  (Unisys - I think is one).  In which case, the fact that the
System/36 compiler met the ANSI'74 Standard does NOT mean that it is "equal
to" the Unisys implementation.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL on a IBM SYSTEM36

- **From:** Barticus@att.spam.net (Randall Bart)
- **Date:** 1999-02-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36c1000a.67384461@netnews>`
- **References:** `<36C0082F.517E@swbell.net> <36c09dc4.8374249@news2.humboldt1.com> <79qf00$a8v@dfw-ixnews4.ix.netcom.com>`

```
'Twas Tue, 9 Feb 1999 17:06:34 -0600, when "William M. Klein"
<wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> illuminated comp.lang.cobol thusly:

>In which case, the fact that the
>System/36 compiler met the ANSI'74 Standard does NOT mean that it is "equal
>to" the Unisys implementation.

Especially since

>Kindrick Ownby wrote in message <36c09dc4.8374249@news2.humboldt1.com>...

>>From the IBM System/36 COBOL manual:

>>- The December 1975 Federal Information Processing Standard (FIPSPUB
>>21-1) low-intermediate level.

Unisys COBOL74 is high-level.  BTW, which levels of X3.23-1974 and
X3.23-1985 contained Report Writer?  Which levels contained COPY?
```

- **From:** "Jim" <jimyt@REMOVEMEyahoo.com>
