- **Message-ID:** `<%%_v2.1121$QK4.1234@news2.giganews.com>`

```
Compiler : Microfocus Personal Cobol for Windows
OS : Win98

I have a problem with a Sort Routine in my program. All the literature I
have on Cobol doesn't say much about sorting variable length records, could
this be where the problem is? It will not Compile. It keeps coming up with
the message:

"C:\Coding\Mt25p2.cbl 49: JMN2896I-S  SORT-MERGE FILE CAN ONLY SPECIFY
FILE-IDENTIFIER IN ASSIGN CLAUSE."

Here are the relevant sections of code :
**********************************************************************
IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
 PROGRAM-ID. MT25P2.
 ENVIRONMENT DIVISION.
 INPUT-OUTPUT SECTION.
 FILE-CONTROL.
     SELECT VALID-FILE ASSIGN TO "MT25VF.DAT"
         ORGANIZATION IS LINE SEQUENTIAL.
     SELECT PRINT-FILE ASSIGN TO PRINTER.
     SELECT SORTED-FILE ASSIGN TO "MT25SD.DAT"
         ORGANIZATION IS LINE SEQUENTIAL.
     SELECT WORK-FILE ASSIGN TO "WORKFILE.DAT".

 DATA DIVISION.
 FILE SECTION.

FD  VALID-FILE.
 01  VAL-IR-REC.
     03  V-IR-REC-TYPE         PIC X.
     03  V-IR-CUST-CODE       PIC X(5).
     03  V-IR-PART-NUM          PIC X(6).
     03  V-IR-QUANTITY           PIC 9(4).

01  VAL-D-REC.
     03  V-D-REC-TYPE             PIC X.
     03  V-D-CUST-CODE          PIC X(5).

01  VAL-C-REC.
     03  V-C-REC-TYPE             PIC X.
     03  V-C-CUST-CODE           PIC X(5).
     03  V-C-CUST-NAME           PIC X(20).
     03  V-C-CUST-ADDRESS     PIC X(60).
     03  V-C-CUST-BALANCE      PIC 9(7)V99.
     03  V-C-CRED-LIMIT             PIC 9(7).

 FD  SORTED-FILE.
 01  SORT-IR-REC.
     03  S-IR-REC-TYPE           PIC X.
     03  S-IR-CUST-CODE         PIC X(5).
     03  S-IR-PART-NUM            PIC X(6).
     03  S-IR-QUANTITY             PIC 9(4).

01  SORT-D-REC.
     03  S-D-REC-TYPE              PIC X.
     03  S-D-CUST-CODE            PIC X(5).

 01  SORT-C-REC.
     03  S-C-REC-TYPE               PIC X.
     03  S-C-CUST-CODE             PIC X(5).
     03  S-C-CUST-NAME             PIC X(20).
     03  S-C-CUST-ADDRESS       PIC X(60).
     03  S-C-CUST-BALANCE        PIC 9(7)V99.
     03  S-C-CRED-LIMIT               PIC 9(7).

 SD  WORK-FILE.
 01  WORK-REC.
     03     REC-TYPE                PIC X.
     03     CUST-CODE-KEY      PIC X(5).
     03                                     PIC X(96).
**************************************************************************
SORT-ROUTINE.
     SORT WORK-FILE
     ON ASCENDING KEY CUST-CODE-KEY
     USING VALID-FILE
     GIVING SORTED-FILE.
**************************************************************************
```

#### ↳ Re: Problem with a Sort Routine

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-02-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<79qg9g$b0k@dfw-ixnews4.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<%%_v2.1121$QK4.1234@news2.giganews.com>`

```
You may THINK you are using a Micro Focus compiler - but you aren't.  You
are using the Fujitsu one - where this problem is reported about once a
month. Change the Select/Assign clause for the file specified in your SD
from a literal to an identifier and the message will go away (as it has
every other time this STUPID error on Fujitsu's part was reported).

NOTE: As I also state monthly, this means that the Fujitsu compiler is NOT
ANSI conforming and you should demand a "refund" from their false
advertising on this - until they fix it.
```

#### ↳ Re: Problem with a Sort Routine

- **From:** "Leif Svalgaard" <lsvalg@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 1999-02-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36c18423@news3.us.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<%%_v2.1121$QK4.1234@news2.giganews.com>`

```
as the message clearly states, you cannot use a filename (such as
workfile.dat) in the
assign clause. Only an identifier (no quotes) is allowed. Try to use simply
SORTFILE.
The assign clause is for documentation only anyway.

Jim wrote in message
>Compiler : Microfocus Personal Cobol for Windows
>OS : Win98
…[79 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

<a id="t-78dad1ed75"></a>
## Thread: "EXIT Immediate" from the middle of a Procedure — 22 this month / 27 total

### "EXIT Immediate" from the middle of a Procedure

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-02-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<79sni8$6a0@dfw-ixnews12.ix.netcom.com>`

```
I have checked my "saved" and "sent" folders as well as DejaNews and can't
find the summary that I did a few months ago on ways to get out of the
middle of  a procedure "ASAP".  Did anyone else either save it or can you
find it in DejaNews?  This seems to be coming up often enough that I thought
I would repost it.  A semi-short summary of it (without all the PROs and
CONs) is:

A) It is quite common that you are able to detect a situation "early" in a
Procedure (Paragraph, Section, whatever) that causes you to want to "get
out" and not do anything else in that procedure.

B) There are a NUMBER of ways of doing this; which one you use will depend
on your "religious" beliefs and/or shop Standards.  They all work; they are
all maintainable; and any attempt to "mix" them leads to difficult to
maintain code.  (Everyone seems to think that there method is LESS
error-prone that the others - but when "pressed" this is only true if you
try to mix methods or coding techniques)

C)  The (most?) common methods are:

1) Use the EXIT PARAGRAPH/SECTION syntax which is an extension in many
compilers today and will be in the next COBOL Standard.

2) Use "native" COBOL syntax to keep nesting levels of conditionals, e.g.

    Read ...
        At End
            do at end processing - but nothing else
       Not at End
           do normal processing
           If condition-1
                 do fairly common stuff
           Else
                 do less common stuff
           End-IF
           Do more normal processing
    End-Read

3) (Particularly with pre-ANS'85 compilers), break out your code into
separate Paragraphs whenever you hit a new condition, e.g.  In pre-ANS'85
you can't have

   If cond-1
      If Cond-2
            Do X
      Else
            Do Y
      End-IF
     Display  "how do I get here?"
  Else
     Do Z
  End-IF

Therefore, you need to move the "If Cond-2" thru its End-IF to a separate
paragraph and PERFORM it.

4) The ever controversial PERFORM THRU with a GO TO exit-paragraph,  In
other words, for every paragraph (and you need either manual or automated
tools/standards to enforce this) you code an EXIT-Paragraph.  You never
allow any other paragraph labels between an "opening" and "exit" paragraph
label - but you do allow GO TO exit-paragraph syntax when you want to "get
out quick".  (no other GO TOs or "crossing" GO TOs are allowed)

5) Variation on 4 (seemingly most popular in England), you don't ever
PERFORM paragraph thru paragraph-exit, but do PERFORM SECTIONs.  Then within
a section you have 1 paragraph and its exit, so you can again go to that
paragraph-exit.


   *****

OK, regulars, have I forgotten anything (again?)
```

#### ↳ Re: "EXIT Immediate" from the middle of a Procedure

- **From:** "Simon Cordingley" <simonc@casegen.co.uk>
- **Date:** 1999-02-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36c204c6.0@mercury.nildram.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<79sni8$6a0@dfw-ixnews12.ix.netcom.com>`

```
Seems pretty complete to me - didn't know about EXIT PARAGRAPH though, only
thought you could EXIT SECTION - look forward to it being in the standard so
we can use it in Casegen (we pretty much stick to very simple ANSI '85
syntax).

Strange you should mention "England" in your last possibility - that's what
we do in Casegen and we are definitly from the European side of the pond.

Thanks for your informative posting.

Simon Cordingley
Casegen Systems Ltd
www.casegen.co.uk


William M. Klein wrote in message <79sni8$6a0@dfw-ixnews12.ix.netcom.com>...
>I have checked my "saved" and "sent" folders as well as DejaNews and can't
>find the summary that I did a few months ago on ways to get out of the
>middle of  a procedure "ASAP".  Did anyone else either save it or can you
>find it in DejaNews?  This seems to be coming up often enough that I
thought
>I would repost it.  A semi-short summary of it (without all the PROs and
>CONs) is:
…[58 more quoted lines elided]…
>PERFORM paragraph thru paragraph-exit, but do PERFORM SECTIONs.  Then
within
>a section you have 1 paragraph and its exit, so you can again go to that
>paragraph-exit.
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: "EXIT Immediate" from the middle of a Procedure

- **From:** Ian Glen <glenirxxx@xxerols.com>
- **Date:** 1999-02-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36C3AC60.B6D6A7A7@xxerols.com>`
- **References:** `<79sni8$6a0@dfw-ixnews12.ix.netcom.com> <36c204c6.0@mercury.nildram.co.uk>`

```
Rexx provides 2 ways out of the middle of a loop  - 'leave' which exits
the loop to the next instruction, and 'iterate', which resumes from the
beginning of the loop. 

The only cobol equivalent of 'iterate' that comes close would be a 'GO
TO start-paragraph' - I don't think there is anything equivalent for an
inline PERFORM - does anyone else think that they would have a use for
the 'iterate' instruction?

IG

Simon Cordingley wrote:
> 
> Seems pretty complete to me - didn't know about EXIT PARAGRAPH though, only
…[93 more quoted lines elided]…
> >
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: "EXIT Immediate" from the middle of a Procedure

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-02-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7a2qdp$s4n@dfw-ixnews11.ix.netcom.com>`

```
This is the exact difference between EXIT PERFORM with and without the
"CYCLE" keyword.

>Rexx provides 2 ways out of the middle of a loop  - 'leave' which exits
>the loop to the next instruction, and 'iterate', which resumes from the
```

- **Message-ID:** `<79t079$258$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<79sni8$6a0@dfw-ixnews12.ix.netcom.com>`

```
looks to me like you have them covered ...

William M. Klein wrote in message <79sni8$6a0@dfw-ixnews12.ix.netcom.com>...
>I have checked my "saved" and "sent" folders as well as DejaNews and can't
>find the summary that I did a few months ago on ways to get out of the
>middle of  a procedure "ASAP".  Did anyone else either save it or can you
>find it in DejaNews?  This seems to be coming up often enough that I
thought
>I would repost it.  A semi-short summary of it (without all the PROs and
>CONs) is:
…[58 more quoted lines elided]…
>PERFORM paragraph thru paragraph-exit, but do PERFORM SECTIONs.  Then
within
>a section you have 1 paragraph and its exit, so you can again go to that
>paragraph-exit.
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

#### ↳ Re: "EXIT Immediate" from the middle of a Procedure

- **From:** denis548@aol.com (Denis548)
- **Date:** 1999-02-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19990214090348.09998.00001408@ng29.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<79sni8$6a0@dfw-ixnews12.ix.netcom.com>`

```
>I have checked my "saved" and "sent" folders as well as DejaNews and can't
>find the summary that I did a few months ago on ways to get out of the
>middle of  a procedure "ASAP".  Did anyone else either save it or can you
>find it in DejaNews?  This seems to be coming up often enough that I thought
>I would repost it.  

I worked for a software development company for years and here's what we
settled over time:  PERFORM through an exit if you need to "bail out in the
middle".   This is the rough equivalent of the "return" in some languages.  

Also we allowed a "GO TO" to only go to the top of the paragraph.  This is the
equivalent of "continue" in a while loop in some languages.

Denis
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: "EXIT Immediate" from the middle of a Procedure

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com>
- **Date:** 1999-02-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36C842A8.FBB26F70@NOSPAMhome.com>`
- **References:** `<79sni8$6a0@dfw-ixnews12.ix.netcom.com> <19990214090348.09998.00001408@ng29.aol.com>`

```
> Also we allowed a "GO TO" to only go to the top of the paragraph.  This is the
> equivalent of "continue" in a while loop in some languages.
> 
> Denis

I would be very reluctant to GO TO the top of a performed paragraph.  I
don't have confidence that any increments and checks would be done
correctly.  Testing would mean that it would work correctly for this
particular compile, but maybe not when we upgrade compilers.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: "EXIT Immediate" from the middle of a Procedure

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** rkrayhawk@aol.com (RKRayhawk)
- **Date:** 1999-02-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19990215172820.07685.00001563@ng156.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<36C88E7B.6186E498@NOSPAMhome.com>`

```
Now, class, we have a pop quiz.

It would appear that we have had here in this innocent thread a clear
recordation of the concerns, felt mutually, of two very informed
prognosticators to the effect that should there be a mixture of GOTO and
PERFORM of the particular variety of a GOTO to the top paragraph name that
there could be a breakage, as it were, betwixt releases of the compiler.

In particular, the iterative functionality latent in PERFORM may be
dysfunctionalized by such a jump.  There was humorously a disagreement as to
whether this would be a break of the GOTO or a break of the PERFORM, (surely
that is expertise instantiated).

Now it must be agreed that the iterator is actaully a transparent piece of code
that is in there somewhere between the header and whatever might be considered
the end, and the august postors are concerned that this iterator might, oh
heaven forbid, be skipped by a precociously executed GO-of-some-sort. Oh,
anything but that!

For 'tis true as the great commentors have so implied that an implementor, be
they the great blue or the littlest language deployer, is free to implement
that iterator in the way they choose and in a manner fitting to the device
where implemented, as long as they stick to the language syntax rules.

So, ..., class, for extra extra super extra credit, beyond the holding capacity
of the largest numeric field, I mean in outright overflow of the shear capacity
to calculate, please write an entry suitable for posting on the internet
itself, commenting as to whether you feel that it  is not by these very same
tokens of admonitions that we mighten not take cognizance of the fact that the
exact locus of an iterator in a structure coded as the object of a PERFORM 1-a
THRU 1-exit is syntactically undefined.

Comment to the effect, think you not, that a GOTO the 1-exit is just as 'risky'
since we have no language standard stating the location of the iterator.  

And since, in most of the interesting discussions the 1-exit paragraph is, of
course, recommended to contain nothing except the EXIT statement, which
statement is guaranteed to do nothing as a matter of language standard, then
could not the implementor place the iterator just nigh of the 1-exit label?  

And could not the arguements presented to dissuade coders from expressing the
GOTO top paragraph construct be, perforce, presented in opposition to the GOTO
1-exit, when in the same context these same sources insist that  1-exit be void
of code? 

So, class, the point might be that the advocacy of GOTO 1-exit depends utterly
upon an experts knowledge of implementation details, and that implementors are
now limitted by the practices in the field, not by the approved or documented
language standard.

In contradistinction to the foregoing comments in this thread the proposition
is that a GOTO begining, can not be more offensive than GOTO ending. If the
expert tells you that the iterator, about which only the greatest minds could
be informed, might be damaged by a non-linear jump, then it must be true no
matter which direction you jump.


Best Wishes for those who jump not at all,
 

Robert Rayhawk
RKRayhawk@aol.com
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: "EXIT Immediate" from the middle of a Procedure

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Barticus@att.spam.net (Randall Bart)
- **Date:** 1999-02-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36cc96c3.76825519@netnews>`
- **References:** `<79sni8$6a0@dfw-ixnews12.ix.netcom.com> <19990214090348.09998.00001408@ng29.aol.com> <36C842A8.FBB26F70@NOSPAMhome.com> <vP%x2.6593$wj1.9765393@news2.mia> <36C88E7B.6186E498@NOSPAMhome.com>`

```
'Twas Mon, 15 Feb 1999 14:15:39 -0700, when Howard Brazee
<brazee@NOSPAMhome.com> illuminated comp.lang.cobol thusly:

>Judson McClendon wrote:
>> 
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
>be more likely to break if it has a GOTO within it.

Howard, I have used COBOL and read about COBOL for over 25 years and
AFAICR this is the first time that anyone has suggested that GO TO top of
paragraph might have any effect on the PERFORM.  Increments and checks
happen when you fall off the bottom of your PERFORM range, not when you GO
anywhere.  This includes GO TO EXIT-PAR.  The PERFORM exit logic kicks in
when you fall out of the EXIT paragraph, and only if it's the end of your
PERFORM range.

For the record, I too abhor the reckless use of GO TO.  I was raised with
the opposite.  My first language was Basic, where the only thing IF could
do was GOTO.  I then learned Fortran IV, with the choice between
artihmetic IF or a logical IF which took only a single statement.  In the
mid-70s I had a COBOL teacher who said, "Never use ELSE.  Use GO TO to
show what you really mean."  At the time I considered this advice
assinine, but my code was GO-heavy until I learned the wisdom of
"structured coding" a few years later.

Let me enumerate the situations where I still use GO TO:

1)  GO TO top, as an alternative to recursive PERFORM.  (For the poor
fellow whose compiler limits PERFORM recursion to 2, I feel very sad.
With Burroughs you always had room for a PERFORM stack of at least 10 and
if you asked for more space it was hundreds.  I remember using
non-recursive compilers in the 1970s, but people don't still use those, do
they?)
2)  GO TO EXIT.
3)  GO TO abort procedure.  Actually PERFORM abort procedure is better
form, but if the risk of getting stack overflow on that PERFORM is
credible, GO TO becomes inherently superior.  On Burroughs Medium System
(Unisys V Series) there were cases where this mattered.  I haven't
actually used this in 15 years, and the arguments for it grow only weaker.
4)  GO TO next paragraph.  Before there were scope terminators this was
often the easiest way to write a routine.  Scope terminators mostly
eliminate the need for this construct, but not completely.  Sometimes the
code gets so nested that it is useful to break it up just to reduce the
nesting.  Usually this is best done by moving a logical chunk to another
location and PERFORMing.  But this has the unfortunate effect of putting
code logically out of sequence.  By using GO TO next paragraph a large but
relatively simple routine can be kept in logical sequence.

The reason GO TO is (appropriately) shunned is that it is the most bug
prone command.  Toward this end, any style checker should flag any use of
GO TO, and attempt to categorize it as one of these 4 (and #3 is pretty
iffy).  Any GO TO which doesn't fit one of these should be banned.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: "EXIT Immediate" from the middle of a Procedure

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com>
- **Date:** 1999-02-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36CD8194.E42B109A@NOSPAMhome.com>`
- **References:** `<79sni8$6a0@dfw-ixnews12.ix.netcom.com> <19990214090348.09998.00001408@ng29.aol.com> <36C842A8.FBB26F70@NOSPAMhome.com> <vP%x2.6593$wj1.9765393@news2.mia> <36C88E7B.6186E498@NOSPAMhome.com> <36cc96c3.76825519@netnews>`

```
> Let me enumerate the situations where I still use GO TO:

> 3)  GO TO abort procedure.  Actually PERFORM abort procedure is better
> form, but if the risk of getting stack overflow on that PERFORM is
> credible, GO TO becomes inherently superior.  On Burroughs Medium System
> (Unisys V Series) there were cases where this mattered.  I haven't
> actually used this in 15 years, and the arguments for it grow only weaker.

This is the only place I still use GO TO.  Mainly because I disagree
that PERFORM is better form.  If there's no way I want to ever come back
and coming back will mess me up, I will be honest and write GO TO. 
PERFORM is a obsfucation.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: "EXIT Immediate" from the middle of a Procedure

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** JD <beta1@bigfoot.com>
- **Date:** 1999-02-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36D5ECB3.E5F2A589@bigfoot.com>`
- **References:** `<79sni8$6a0@dfw-ixnews12.ix.netcom.com> <19990214090348.09998.00001408@ng29.aol.com> <36C842A8.FBB26F70@NOSPAMhome.com> <vP%x2.6593$wj1.9765393@news2.mia> <36C88E7B.6186E498@NOSPAMhome.com> <36cc96c3.76825519@netnews> <36CD8194.E42B109A@NOSPAMhome.com>`

```
I agree.  A case in point is CICS transactions.  I've been in shops where ALL
routines were performed, even though it would be impossible to return from the
routine (e.g. the routine does a map send and returns control to CICS).  This is
actually a DUMB practice, and makes for much more difficulty in maintenance of
the program.  Perform should ONLY be used if the code will come back after the
performed routine completes, otherwise it simply "obfuscates" the code's
intended function..

Howard Brazee wrote:

> > Let me enumerate the situations where I still use GO TO:
>
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> PERFORM is a obsfucation.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: "EXIT Immediate" from the middle of a Procedure

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1999-02-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36d60cac.88876140@news1.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<79sni8$6a0@dfw-ixnews12.ix.netcom.com> <19990214090348.09998.00001408@ng29.aol.com> <36C842A8.FBB26F70@NOSPAMhome.com> <vP%x2.6593$wj1.9765393@news2.mia> <36C88E7B.6186E498@NOSPAMhome.com> <36cc96c3.76825519@netnews> <36CD8194.E42B109A@NOSPAMhome.com> <36D5ECB3.E5F2A589@bigfoot.com>`

```
On Thu, 25 Feb 1999 16:37:07 -0800, JD <beta1@bigfoot.com> wrote:

>I agree.  A case in point is CICS transactions.  I've been in shops where ALL
>routines were performed, even though it would be impossible to return from the
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>intended function..

Actually control does not return to CICS until an EXEC CICS RETURN is
executed - unless you are conversational - in which case your program
just stays in control.  At any rate, the point to this is - performing
the "return to CICS" routine is complete and utterly appropriate -
although I would prefer to "fall through" it instead.

I'm not saying "perform" so you don't have "go to".  The reality as I
have seen it work itself in ME is this:

Trying first just to eliminate go to:  Leads to the necessity to
"think differently" about the creation of the code.

This "different" way of thinking leads to more structured code, fewer
bugs and faster development.

Using this "different" way of thinking automatically creates code
without GO TO.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: "EXIT Immediate" from the middle of a Procedure

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** William Lynch <wblynch@att.net>
- **Date:** 1999-02-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36D63DF4.A5E689DE@att.net>`
- **References:** `<79sni8$6a0@dfw-ixnews12.ix.netcom.com> <19990214090348.09998.00001408@ng29.aol.com> <36C842A8.FBB26F70@NOSPAMhome.com> <vP%x2.6593$wj1.9765393@news2.mia> <36C88E7B.6186E498@NOSPAMhome.com> <36cc96c3.76825519@netnews> <36CD8194.E42B109A@NOSPAMhome.com> <36D5ECB3.E5F2A589@bigfoot.com>`

```
JD wrote:
> 
> This is actually a DUMB practice, and makes for much more difficulty in maintenance of
> the program.

Really? If you're looking at a program which performs a paragraph
containing a GOBACK or EXEC CICS RETURN END-EXEC and you're confused,
who's the DUMB one? I've worked with IBM mainframes more than thirty
years and God knows how many COBOL programmers. They weren't all A-list
programmers, but I can't recall anyone finding the above confusing.

> Perform should ONLY be used if the code will come back after the
> performed routine completes, otherwise it simply "obfuscates" the code's
> intended function..

I don't agree - color me DUMB (or is this a troll?). It seems all the
pontificating doesn't come from Rome.

Bill Lynch
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: "EXIT Immediate" from the middle of a Procedure

- **From:** veronem@aol.com (Veronem)
- **Date:** 1999-02-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19990226160215.15438.00001234@ng-fi1.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<19990214090348.09998.00001408@ng29.aol.com>`

```
I've been reading this thread for a couple of days now,  I find it long winded
and unrealistic.  Basically,  whatever your compiler or shop wants is what you
are going to do.

My preference is to use  xxx-EXIT, only because it shows a definite beginning
and ending of a paragraph.  And because I have worked on several different
compiler, some required the exit others didn't.  

As far as GOTO, everyone uses them.  If your a good programmer you know how to
use them properly and if your not, well nothing you do is going to help.

Just my 2 cents,
Mary Veronesi


>Subject: Re: "EXIT Immediate" from the middle of a Procedure
>From: denis548@aol.com (Denis548)
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

<a id="t-58faaff7c0"></a>
## Thread: Quick ? about Fujitsu COBOL 85... — 3 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers) · [`COBOL standards (ANS, ISO, 74/85/2002/2014)`](topics.md#standards)_

### Quick ? about Fujitsu COBOL 85...

- **From:** "Tom Montana" <tommontana@REMOVEME.earthlink.net>
- **Date:** 1999-02-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<79t5ch$g9o$1@birch.prod.itd.earthlink.net>`

```
Hey all...

I have a little problem...

I've been using version 3 of the Fujitsu COBOL 85 compiler and I can't seem
to convert display numeric values (-ZZZ,ZZZ.ZZ) to editable numeric values
(S999999V99). I've tried using different types of accepts...

ACCEPT ENTRY-FIELD WITH CONVERSION.
ACCEPT ENTRY-FIELD CONVERT.
ACCEPT ENTRY-FIELD.

Nothing seems to work. Meanwhile, I'll start reading the Fujitsu COBOL 85
standard book. I was just hoping someone out in the audience had the answer
off the top of their heads.

Thanks..

Tom.
```

#### ↳ Re: Quick ? about Fujitsu COBOL 85...

- **From:** "John Hicks (Remove \\"nospam\\" from address before replying)" <johnhicks@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 1999-02-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36CA508F.44042E4@ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<79t5ch$g9o$1@birch.prod.itd.earthlink.net>`

```
Tom--

I'm not familiar with Fujitsu and with the "with conversion" or
"convert" options, but if you're in a hurry, you can always do it the
old fashioned way: accept into an alphanumeric display field and then
parse the field, character by character, checking for signs, decimal
point, and digits, accumulating the result in a numeric field (which you
multiply by ten before adding each digit).

--John

Tom Montana wrote:
> 
> Hey all...
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
> Tom.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Quick ? about Fujitsu COBOL 85...

- **From:** "don ferrario" <don@ferrario.com>
- **Date:** 1999-02-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7afmm5$c4c$1@news1.epix.net>`
- **References:** `<79t5ch$g9o$1@birch.prod.itd.earthlink.net> <36CA508F.44042E4@ibm.net>`

```
01 accept-in      pic s9(7)v99.
01 accept-out   pic zzz,zz9.99-.

accept accept-in.
move accept-in to accept-out.
display accept-out.

Display the output in the same position as the input,
and the user will not know the conversion was done.
Make sure the accept-out uses at least as many character
positions as the accept-in, or it may not over-write all the
original input.
```

#### ↳ Re: Importing Cobol

- **From:** "John Hicks (Remove \\"nospam\\" from address before replying)" <johnhicks@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 1999-02-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36CA4DED.E9F1DFDB@ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<79so38$6f3$1@news3.infoave.net>`

```
Gary--

Check first to see if perchance the files are sequential and: 
--are fixed format 
--contain no binary data 
--have a carriage-return/line-feed after each record
(You can tell by displaying the data on your screen with the DOS TYPE
command. If all the data lines up from line to line, you're probably
OK.)

If so, you can import the files directly into MS Access. It lets you
tell it where each field begins. 

--John

> the programs?
```

<a id="t-3714d58086"></a>
## Thread: Moving fields - any Quicker Way?\` — 15 msgs

### Moving fields - any Quicker Way?\`

- **From:** "George" <DELETEGeoCon7@concentric.net>
- **Date:** 1999-02-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<79tioe$6fm@chronicle.concentric.net>`

```
College student writing a sequential file update program.  Master File +
validated Transaction file gives Audit Report (printer) and New Master file.
I am wondering if I can avoid using so many MOVE statements. About 7 fields
in Master file, same 7 in Transaction file with a Transaction code at the
beginning of each record.  Output is to printer and Master file (that is, of
course) has same fields as the original Master file.

Is there a way to move one record to another without field to field moves
for every field?  If I write the MASTER-RECORD to the NEW-MASTER-RECORD,
since they are the same, I can WRITE
NEW-MASTER-RECORD FROM MASTER-RECORD. That's easy enough.

Other moves are going into records which contain the same data items (same
PIC's and all) but in different order, and sometimes with an added blank PIC
X field.

Tks to any ideas,
George
```

#### ↳ Re: Moving fields - any Quicker Way?\`

- **From:** "George" <DELETEGeoCon7@concentric.net>
- **Date:** 1999-02-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<79tir5$6ii@chronicle.concentric.net>`
- **References:** `<79tioe$6fm@chronicle.concentric.net>`

```
Sorry, I'm writing in COBOL/400 based on COBOL 85.  COBOL/400 is for the
IBM's AS/400, but is probably highly compatible with vanilla COBOL 85.

-George
```

#### ↳ Re: Moving fields - any Quicker Way?\`

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1999-02-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9tzw2.7396$Fs.7926941@news4.mia>`
- **References:** `<79tioe$6fm@chronicle.concentric.net>`

```
George wrote:
>College student writing a sequential file update program.  Master File +
>validated Transaction file gives Audit Report (printer) and New Master file.
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>X field.

As Bill Klein has pointed out, you can use MOVE CORRESPONDING.  But I
believe that is a bad idea, and not a good way to address this issue,
especially with today's programmer's editors.  Here's my approach:

1. Always use a consistent data naming scheme.  This is an *extremely*
   important thing.  I have found it expedient and profitable to follow
   these rules:

   A. Always append a short (2-5 character) prefix to each data name.
      This prefix is unique to each 01 record within the program.  An
      acronym derived from the 01 data name works well.  e.g. If you
      have an 01 named DATE-WORK-AREA, all the subordinate data names
      should start with 'DW-' or 'DWA-'. (I use 'WS-' for all 77's)

   B. The remainder of a given data name is consistent throughout the
      entire application, not just a single program.  The only exception
      is when a given data field actually changes it's essence when moved
      from one place to another.  For example:
      MOVE DW-CURRENT-DATE TO DW-PREVIOUS-DATE.

   C. Use consistent abbreviations.  e.g. Don't abbreviate 'NUMBER' as
      'NBR' in some data names and 'NO' in other data names.  When writing
      computer systems, I often create an 'abbreviation dictionary' for
      consistency.  One primary difference between the amateur and the real
      pro in any field is consistency.  The amateur can produce good ones
      on occasion, but the real pro turns out good ones consistently, like
      a cookie cutter.

2. Using your nifty-gee-whiz programmer's editor create a line with just
   'MOVE' in the appropriate column, then duplicate it at least as many times
   as you have fields to be inserted (see next step).  The result will look
   like this:

           MOVE
           MOVE
           MOVE
           ...
           MOVE

3. Use your editor's 'column copy' command to copy the names of the 'from'
   fields from the record description (01) immediately to the right of the
   MOVE's.  After you insert all the field names, you may need to delete any
   names which aren't needed.  The result will look like this:

           MOVE TRAN-FIELD-ONE
           MOVE TRAN-FIELD-TWO
           MOVE TRAN-FIELD-THREE
           ...
           MOVE TRAN-FIELD-NNN

4. Use a 'column insert' or macro to place 'TO XXXX-' after the field names,
   where 'XXXX-' is the prefix of the receiving fields.  You now have this:

           MOVE TRAN-FIELD-ONE   TO MAST-
           MOVE TRAN-FIELD-TWO   TO MAST-
           MOVE TRAN-FIELD-THREE TO MAST-
           ...
           MOVE TRAN-FIELD-NNN   TO MAST-

5. Now use your editor's 'column copy' to copy the part of the data names
   after the prefix from the left to the right.  The result should look like
   this:

           MOVE TRAN-FIELD-ONE   TO MAST-FIELD-ONE
           MOVE TRAN-FIELD-TWO   TO MAST-FIELD-TWO
           MOVE TRAN-FIELD-THREE TO MAST-FIELD-THREE
           ...
           MOVE TRAN-FIELD-NNN   TO MAST-FIELD-NNN

6. When I want periods after each move, I make sure to copy a space at the
   end of each data name, so each line will end with at least one space.
   I now do a search and replace on those lines, replacing all ending spaces
   with a period.  In Brief that sequence would be: replace ' +>' with '.'.
   You now have:

           MOVE TRAN-FIELD-ONE   TO MAST-FIELD-ONE.
           MOVE TRAN-FIELD-TWO   TO MAST-FIELD-TWO.
           MOVE TRAN-FIELD-THREE TO MAST-FIELD-THREE.
           ...
           MOVE TRAN-FIELD-NNN   TO MAST-FIELD-NNN.

Though this took a while to describe, with practice you can do it in just a
few seconds.  The above sequence on 50 moves would take me perhaps five to
ten seconds.  Plus, because you use the editor to copy the data names from
the record itself, the result is unlikely to contain typing errors.

If you don't have a programmer's editor that can do the above kind of thing,
then get one! :-)
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Moving fields - any Quicker Way?\`

- **From:** paulr@bix.com (paulr)
- **Date:** 1999-02-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<79uqvk$3d9@lotho.delphi.com>`
- **References:** `<79tioe$6fm@chronicle.concentric.net> <9tzw2.7396$Fs.7926941@news4.mia>`

```
So... I tend to use MOVE CORRESPONDING a lot, especially with dates. 
My 'template' always has something like this in it:

        01 WS-DATE                     PIC 9(8).
        01 WS-DATE-FIELDS REDEFINES WS-DATE.
           15  THE-YEAR.               Pic 9(4).            
           15  THE-MONTH               PIC 9(2).
           15  THE-DAY                 PIC 9(2).


 Almost all the 01 and other fields that have dates are then:

        01 WS-DISPLAY-DATE.
           15 THE-MONTH                PIC 9(2).
           15 FILLER                   PIC X VALUE '/'.
           15 THE-DAY                  PIC 9. 
           15 FILLER                   PIC X VALUE '/'.
           15 THE-YEAR                 PIC 9.
  
TO display the date, for example, them becomes something like
     ....
     .....
            accept ws-date from date YYYYMMDD
            move correspong ws-date to ws-display-date
            display ws-display-date
     .....
     ....

It works just as well when you split up the year in CC and YY,
and allows you to always store dates in the system native fashion. ;) 
It does trip up people because they can not do a MOVE CORRESPONDING
from WS-DISPLAY-DATE to ws-date, at least in most compilers. 
The filler fields cause some kind of consternation in the compiler.


-Paul
```

```
paulr wrote:
> 
> So... I tend to use MOVE CORRESPONDING a lot, especially with dates.
…[22 more quoted lines elided]…
>             display ws-display-date

I have recently started going for 100% warning free code.  You would be
surprised how many errors I have picked up.  In Cobol for MVS this
generates a warning so I dislike this construct.

I must sit down and see if I can disable this specific warning then all
would be well.  Does anyone know how to remove this and the RECORDING
MODE warning message?

Tip:  Enable all warnings your compiler can give you and READ them!

Ken
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Moving fields - any Quicker Way?\`

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** paulr@bix.com (paulr)
- **Date:** 1999-02-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7a4biq$og4@lotho.delphi.com>`
- **References:** `<79tioe$6fm@chronicle.concentric.net> <9tzw2.7396$Fs.7926941@news4.mia> <79uqvk$3d9@lotho.delphi.com> <36C42DD7.3C32DF0E@zip.com.au>`

```
Perhaps I copied this wrong, but it came off an MVS system, with 
COBOL for MVS and VM as the compiler. No warnings... (?) Can 
you specify the warning tht it gives please?

I do agree with you about getting the warnings out of code though. 
The want to get the recording mode out of a compile is to specify it.
(In the FD). 

THe one warning I have yet to get rid of is when you specify a 
numeric key in a VSAM file. It *really* wants that key to be 
alphanumeric. The only way to get rid of the warnings I have 
found is to define the key as PIC X(xx), and then REDEFINE 
it as PIC9(xx).

Annoying.
Yours,
-Paul

: >            15 THE-MONTH                PIC 9(2).
: >            15 FILLER                   PIC X VALUE '/'.
: >            15 THE-DAY                  PIC 9.
: >            15 FILLER                   PIC X VALUE '/'.
: >            15 THE-YEAR                 PIC 9.
: > 
: > TO display the date, for example, them becomes something like
: >      ....
: >      .....
: >             accept ws-date from date YYYYMMDD
: >             move correspong ws-date to ws-display-date
: >             display ws-display-date

: I have recently started going for 100% warning free code.  You would be
: surprised how many errors I have picked up.  In Cobol for MVS this
: generates a warning so I dislike this construct.

: I must sit down and see if I can disable this specific warning then all
: would be well.  Does anyone know how to remove this and the RECORDING
: MODE warning message?

: Tip:  Enable all warnings your compiler can give you and READ them!

: Ken
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Moving fields - any Quicker Way?\`

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com>
- **Date:** 1999-02-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36C2EE69.30F8AF04@NOSPAMhome.com>`
- **References:** `<79tioe$6fm@chronicle.concentric.net> <9tzw2.7396$Fs.7926941@news4.mia>`

```
The only time I have used MOVE CORRESPONDING in the last decade was to
write a program to unload data from the production IDMS database and
reload it to the test IDMS database.  The test dictionary had 4
character year fields.

I compiled the program using the test dictionary and cut and pasted the
copy members into the program.  Now I could read the database record,
move spaces to the new record, move corresponding, fix the year fields,
then write to a file.

For a quick & dirty move corresponding seemed to be a very nice way to
do things.
```

#### ↳ Re: Moving fields - any Quicker Way?\`

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1999-02-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<79uib0$11q$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<79tioe$6fm@chronicle.concentric.net>`

```
There is no reason that you cannot move an entire
record at a time if the records are identical. In the case
of similar fields but a different order, take a look at
the move corresponding method.

George wrote in message <79tioe$6fm@chronicle.concentric.net>...
>College student writing a sequential file update program.  Master File +
>validated Transaction file gives Audit Report (printer) and New Master
file.
>I am wondering if I can avoid using so many MOVE statements. About 7 fields
>in Master file, same 7 in Transaction file with a Transaction code at the
>beginning of each record.  Output is to printer and Master file (that is,
of
>course) has same fields as the original Master file.
>
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>PIC's and all) but in different order, and sometimes with an added blank
PIC
>X field.
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Moving fields - any Quicker Way?\`

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com>
- **Date:** 1999-02-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36C2EF54.6994086E@NOSPAMhome.com>`
- **References:** `<79tioe$6fm@chronicle.concentric.net> <79uib0$11q$1@news.igs.net>`

```
Remember, you can have multiple levels in a record.

01  my-record.
    05  my-field1
    05  my-field2
    05  my-field3
    05  my-field4

If you can't move my-record because my-field4 has a different format, do
the following:

01  my-record.
    05   my-move-fields 
      10  my-field1
      10  my-field2
      10  my-field3
    05  my-field4

Now, you can move my-move-fields and move my-field4.  See if this
strategy makes sense for your data.
```

#### ↳ Re: Moving fields - any Quicker Way?\`

- **From:** nan_forrant@bigfoot.com
- **Date:** 1999-02-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<79uoog$rru$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`
- **References:** `<79tioe$6fm@chronicle.concentric.net>`

```
In article <79tioe$6fm@chronicle.concentric.net>,
  "George" <DELETEGeoCon7@concentric.net> wrote:
> College student writing a sequential file update program.  Master File +
> validated Transaction file gives Audit Report (printer) and New Master file.
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> Tks to any ideas,


you can use the same data names in both files and use the Move corresponding

I.e. 01 xx.  01  yy.  05 bb pic x(5).  05  dd pic x(3).  05 cc pic x(3).  05 
cc pic x(3).  05 dd pic x(3).  05  filler pic x(1).  05  bb pic x(5). move
corresponding xx to yy. (will move all same named fields (accept for filler
area --- the only "drawback" to this is if you then reference bb or cc etc
you need to use ---i.e. If "aa of yy"--- so the program knows which field i
you are using.

-----------== Posted via Deja News, The Discussion Network ==----------
http://www.dejanews.com/       Search, Read, Discuss, or Start Your Own
```

#### ↳ Re: Moving fields - any Quicker Way?\`

- **From:** docdwarf@clark.net ()
- **Date:** 1999-02-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<79ur1m$e0c$1@callisto.clark.net>`
- **References:** `<79tioe$6fm@chronicle.concentric.net>`

```
In article <79tioe$6fm@chronicle.concentric.net>,
George <DELETEGeoCon7@concentric.net> wrote:

[snippage]

>Is there a way to move one record to another without field to field moves
>for every field?  If I write the MASTER-RECORD to the NEW-MASTER-RECORD,
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>X field.

Research the syntax of MOVE CORR... now do your own homework, please.

DD
```

#### ↳ Re: Moving fields - any Quicker Way?\`

- **From:** Ed.Stevens@nmm.nissan-usa.com
- **Date:** 1999-02-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<79ur4e$tsa$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`
- **References:** `<79tioe$6fm@chronicle.concentric.net>`

```
If you have 2 *identical* records, like this:

01  record-1.
    05  fld-a  pic x(5).
    05  fld-b  pic 9(5).
    05  fld-c  pic s9(7) comp-3.

01  record-2.
    05  fld-1  pic x(5).
    05  fld-2  pic 9(5).
    05  fld-3  pic s9(7) comp-3.

You can MOVE RECORD-1 TO RECORD-2.  Notice that field names do not have to be
the same, but field descriptions do.


If you have 2 dissimilar records, but with identical field names, like this:

01  record-1.
    05  fld-1  pic x(5).
    05  fld-2  pic 9(5).
    05  fld-3  pic s9(7) comp-3.

01  record-2.
    05  fld-2  pic 9(5).
    05  fld-x  pic x.
    05  fld-3  pic s9(7) comp-3.
    05  filler pic x.
    05  fld-1  pic x(5).

then you can MOVE CORRESPONDING RECORD-1 TO RECORD-2.  A lot of people,
myself included, really don't like MOVE CORRESPONDING, for a variety of
reasons.


In article <79tioe$6fm@chronicle.concentric.net>,
  "George" <DELETEGeoCon7@concentric.net> wrote:
> College student writing a sequential file update program.  Master File +
> validated Transaction file gives Audit Report (printer) and New Master file.
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Ed Stevens
Nissan Motor Mfg. Corp., USA

-----------== Posted via Deja News, The Discussion Network ==----------
http://www.dejanews.com/       Search, Read, Discuss, or Start Your Own
```

```
and how error prone it is in real world maintenance.
```

#### ↳ Re: Moving fields - any Quicker Way?\`

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1999-02-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36c26568.607875642@news1.ibm.net>`

```
wrote:

>College student writing a sequential file update program.  Master File +
>validated Transaction file gives Audit Report (printer) and New Master file.
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…

Yes.  I demonstrate this example in my book "Teach Yourself COBOL in
24 Hours".  You can name the fields the SAME under each record -
input, output and detail, then use MOVE with CORRESPONDING.  If you
have a bookstore nearby go check my book - chapter 19.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Moving fields - any Quicker Way?\`

- **From:** "Arnold" <arnoldh@home.com>
- **Date:** 1999-02-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<v7Ex2.360$0A4.970@news.rdc1.mi.home.com>`
- **References:** `<79tioe$6fm@chronicle.concentric.net> <36c26568.607875642@news1.ibm.net>`

```
You might also try multiple receiving fields on a single move.

WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.

01  TEST-1            PIC X(12).
01  TEST-2            PIC X(12).
01  TEST-3            PIC X(12).

PROCEDURE DIVISION.

...

MOVE 'TEST MESSAGE"            TO            TEST-1

TEST-2

TEST-3.

This does  several things.  It allows the use of more rational field names
(personal bias here, I like prefixes denoting a WS definition of an FD file,
which tends to eradicate the CORRESPONDING), sets up a visual cue that the
same value is moved to multiple places, and also tends to respond well to
copying field names for insertion into code.  (I hate retyping when I can
copy after, before or over.)

>>College student writing a sequential file update program.  Master File +
>>validated Transaction file gives Audit Report (printer) and New Master
file.
>>I am wondering if I can avoid using so many MOVE statements. About 7
fields
>>in Master file, same 7 in Transaction file with a Transaction code at the
>>beginning of each record.  Output is to printer and Master file (that is,
of
>>course) has same fields as the original Master file.
>>
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>>PIC's and all) but in different order, and sometimes with an added blank
PIC
>>X field.
```

## Thread: AITP — 1 msgs

### AITP

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com>
- **Date:** 1999-02-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36C1C37D.57B2F034@NOSPAMhome.com>`

```
I have been a member of DPMA/AITP since around 1981, with a 5 year
hiatus.  I notice that its membership has dropped steadily, while
specialty clubs (Oracle, NT, Powerbuilder, etc) have had increased
membership.

Of course, society as a whole is specializing.  Social clubs have been
largely replaced by specialty clubs.  But I think I am better off
spending one evening per week learning about areas in DP outside of my
specialty than learning more about my specialty.

Is generalism within a technical field such as programming dead?  How
does that effect our productivity?
```

```
> I am a college student, currently playing around with an old version of
> rm cobol 85 for dos, however I don't have any documentation to tell me
…[20 more quoted lines elided]…
>  I will try to help you.

Compiler: O= to bring the path and name of the object program  A=Generate de
Allocation Map	Z=Generate de object in diferent RmCobol Versions  L=Generate
a Compilation list in disk Runtime: A=To pass parameters to de main program 
B=To modify the buffersize of indexed files (not sure)	X=To use an extended
configuration files (you have to use some keys)  S=To turn on or off switches
that the program evaluate in execution	time  K=To hide de RmCobol message
(at execution time)

Please send me any questions by E-Mail.

Best Regards


H.O.
```

<a id="t-cdfd03d0c7"></a>
## Thread: Search all — 5 msgs

### Search all

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com>
- **Date:** 1999-02-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36C34EEA.CAE3A2F0@NOSPAMhome.com>`

```
When I create a binary table of unknown size which I want to search a
bunch of times in a program, I fill it with high values (or something to
make it sort right) before I populate it.  This works, but I am
wondering if there's a more efficient way.
```

#### ↳ Re: Search all

- **From:** "Alan Russell" <RUSSELAH@apci.com>
- **Date:** 1999-02-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<79vjnp$13o@netnews1.apci.com>`
- **References:** `<36C34EEA.CAE3A2F0@NOSPAMhome.com>`

```
The purpose of filling the table with high-values is so that a "SEARCH ALL"
will find the entire contents of the table ascending.  However, one can
instruct the SEARCH verb to only search that portion of the table which you
have populated by adding a OCCURS ... DEPENDING ON ... clause to the table
definition.  Note however, that since a SEARCH ALL is binary a 1000 entry
table only takes 10 compares to find any entry.  If you fill only half (500)
of the table, then DEPENDING ON clause will only reduce the number of
compares to 9, so there is not as much savings as you might think.

Alan Russell, PhD, CCP
Work - Russelah@apci.com
Home - AHRussell@computer.org
Home page - http://members.aol.com/ahrussell
-------------------------------------------------------
The views expressed are mine alone and do not necessarily reflect those of
my employer

Howard Brazee wrote in message <36C34EEA.CAE3A2F0@NOSPAMhome.com>...
>When I create a binary table of unknown size which I want to search a
>bunch of times in a program, I fill it with high values (or something to
>make it sort right) before I populate it.  This works, but I am
>wondering if there's a more efficient way.
```

#### ↳ Re: Search all

- **From:** rkrayhawk@aol.com (RKRayhawk)
- **Date:** 1999-02-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19990212062500.03163.00000518@ng18.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<36C34EEA.CAE3A2F0@NOSPAMhome.com>`

```
Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com>
On Thu, 11 Feb 1999 14:43:06 -0700
posted 

<< When I create a binary table of unknown size which I want to search a
bunch of times in a program, I fill it with high values (or something to
make it sort right) before I populate it.  This works, but I am
wondering if there's a more efficient way.
>>

In a few cases there is a special trick available.

If your search tokens are comming thru in random order, then this does not
apply. But if you can sort the input on the search argument, then the following
nifty trick applies.

Assume the table is in ascending order. Then sort the input in descending
order.  Use an ODO as suggested by other excellent posters.  

Initially set the table-max value to the actual last valid entry. As you get
hits, process them as you like but also add a trickster adjustment to the
ongoing ODO table-max  - set it to the cell you just hit. By definition no
later record can be in a higher position on the table.

Upon each hit you establish a little less work for the following  searches. You
cannot adjust the ODO object upon misses, but all misses and all hits benefit
from any preceding successful adjustments.

A previous poster concisely explained why you will have a hard time squeezing
much out of a binary search. But you did say that you want to search 'a bunch
of times'.

The actual benefit of the ascending-descending-adjustment trick will depend
upon your data distributions.  In the manner explained, it would be best if you
had few tokens with high keys, and many tokens with low keys.

With this in mind optimizing data conditions can be designed into the system
... but surely this begins to assume much more flexibility then you have yet
indicated.

Keep in mind that the ascending table with descending data sequence, could as
easily be descending table with ascending data sequence. These two just need to
be converse The ODO object, table-max, would decrement in either case.

(As an aside, use of the ODO technique can in cases of a very large table
actually reduce paging. A previous poster rightly suggested that cutting a
table in half only reduces the max iteration count by one.  But the portion of
the table left out constitutes, in some cases, much virtual memory that can be
placed safely out of the way by the OS.  Conversely, always hitting that top
high-values record will demand that page needlessly. ((but not necessarily all
pages from the midpoint to the max point)). ) (((This is all about locality of
reference))).


Further, again assuming a situation in which you can sort the input upon the
search key.  In some applications the search key may be repeated, and such
duplicates or runs might occur many times. When the current key is equal to the
previous key, you can just use the previous results - saving the CPU time to
enter into the search logic, all for the cost of a mere IF statement (and the
overhead of preseving enough from the previous matching hit). 

But note that that IF statement costs a little each time, not just when it
finds a duplicate.  So, here too, data occurence patterns will motivate your
judgement.

One other note. If for any reason you do use an ODO object as a table-max
mechanism to reduce the search, then you really do not need to fill the rest of
the table with high-values.

You are not likely to find a person who likes to see data elements initialized
more than I do. But on large tables there is an issue.  

For in some applications that would search a table a bunch of times, there is
actually a pattern of loading the table quite a few times for different phases.
In such cases the time taken to move the high-values is significant. This is
particularly true in cases where many phases have relatively few real records
and therefore many dummy high-value records.

All of that having been said, still in truth almost all COBOL applications are
I/O bound. It is rare, though possible, that CPU time is your critical factor. 
Storage media spins so slowly compared to the speed of modern chips that you
will have to be a real genius or else be dealing with one very gigantic number
of searches in order to get a return on the effort you have sketched.

Hope that is close enough to your actual situation to be useful.

Best Wishes,

Robert Rayhawk
RKRayhawk@aol.com
```

#### ↳ Re: Search all

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1999-02-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36c39333.685146352@news1.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<36C34EEA.CAE3A2F0@NOSPAMhome.com>`

```
On Thu, 11 Feb 1999 14:43:06 -0700, Howard Brazee
<brazee@NOSPAMhome.com> wrote:

>When I create a binary table of unknown size which I want to search a
>bunch of times in a program, I fill it with high values (or something to
>make it sort right) before I populate it.  This works, but I am
>wondering if there's a more efficient way.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Search all

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com>
- **Date:** 1999-02-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36C83685.20B452DF@NOSPAMhome.com>`
- **References:** `<36C34EEA.CAE3A2F0@NOSPAMhome.com> <36c39333.685146352@news1.ibm.net>`

```
or if you have Liant's Relational Databridge product, any ODBC
compliant report writer for Windows will work       - www.liant.com

I'm sure there are others.

Hope it helps!

Chris Loy
Maxom Holdings, Inc.
http://www.maxom.com
```

<a id="t-9d08773cf7"></a>
## Thread: How are negative values stored in COMP? — 6 msgs

### How are negative values stored in COMP?

- **From:** tgala9760@aol.com (TGala9760)
- **Date:** 1999-02-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19990211102252.05470.00000032@ng-ce1.aol.com>`

```
I am a tech writer documenting some programs and I came across a question I
hoped someone would be able to help with here...


If a variable is binary, i.e., S9(4) COMP, how is a negative value stored?  I
know it is a two byte field, and I've heard everything from "they use the
parity bit" to "they subtract the number from high values" as explanations to
how negative numbers are stored.

Anyone know for sure?

Thanks a lot,

Terri

TGala9760@aol.com

(please e-mail any responses directly to me if possible!)
```

#### ↳ Re: How are negative values stored in COMP?

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com>
- **Date:** 1999-02-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36C35FFF.57C9EAD5@NOSPAMhome.com>`
- **References:** `<19990211102252.05470.00000032@ng-ce1.aol.com>`

```
TGala9760 wrote:
> 
> I am a tech writer documenting some programs and I came across a question I
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> how negative numbers are stored.

On some systems 2's complement, on others 1's complement.  What system
do you use?
```

#### ↳ Re: How are negative values stored in COMP?

- **From:** Warren Porter <warrenp@ASPMnetdoor.com>
- **Date:** 1999-02-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36C30943.EE9C906F@ASPMnetdoor.com>`
- **References:** `<19990211102252.05470.00000032@ng-ce1.aol.com>`

```
The negative of a binary is often stored in 2's complement notation.  Each bit is
inverted (1's complement at this point) and 1 is added to the result to get 2's
complement.  Also, the leading bit of the number indicates the sign.  This limits
the highest signed binary two byte field to the value of 32767.  It works like
the old adding machines where if you subtracted a large value from a small one,
you got a lot of leading 9's.

+4 = 0000 0000 0000 0100
-4 = 1111 1111 1111 1100

+7 0000 0000 0000 0111
-4 1111 1111 1111 1100
------------------------------
+3 0000 0000 0000 0011

Note when the 1's get added in the 3rd from the right position, the carry gets
propagated all the way to the left end.  HTH

TGala9760 wrote:

> I am a tech writer documenting some programs and I came across a question I
> hoped someone would be able to help with here...
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> (please e-mail any responses directly to me if possible!)
```

#### ↳ Re: How are negative values stored in COMP?

- **From:** vbandke@ibm.net (Volker Bandke)
- **Date:** 1999-02-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36c2fa0f.122554584@news3.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<19990211102252.05470.00000032@ng-ce1.aol.com>`

```
On 11 Feb 1999 15:22:52 GMT, tgala9760@aol.com (TGala9760) wrote:

>I am a tech writer documenting some programs and I came across a question I
>hoped someone would be able to help with here...
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Okay, 

I am taking a shot at this, but keep in mind that everything I say is only true in respect
to MVS etc:

Also, all the examples are for 2 byte hex data only, appropriate changes have to be made
for 4 or 8 byte fields...

An UNSIGNED BINARY number is stored as its direct hexadecimal representation, i.e. it can
have a range of 0 to 65535 (X'0000' TO X'FFFF')

77 VAR-1  PIC 9(4) BINARY VALUE '37212'.

would store as X'915C' (as long as the TRUNC compiler option allows it)

A SIGNED BINARY number has the sign in its first bit position.  If it is 0, the number is
considered positive (or zero), if is one the number is considered negative.  The range
therefore is -32768 to + 32767 (X'8000' to X'7FFF')

A positive signed number will just use the standard hex representation, just as the
unsigned number. 

A  NEGATIVE signed number uses the 2-complement format:
As an example, let us use the number -28324

Take the absolute value of the number in question (28324)
Convert to hexadecimal representation X'6EA4'
Flip all the bits ( i.e. 1 --> 0, and 0 --> 1)
This is an XOR operation with X'FFFF' and yields X'915B'
now add 1, giving X'915C'

This is the requested negative number -28324
Proof:

-28324 + 28324 = X'915C' + X'6EA4' = X'0000' + carry = 0

HTH



with kind regards

Volker Bandke
(BSP GmbH)
```

#### ↳ Re: How are negative values stored in COMP?

- **From:** Angela Schwarz <angela.schwarz@sdm.de>
- **Date:** 1999-02-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36C30CEE.41BE@sdm.de>`
- **References:** `<19990211102252.05470.00000032@ng-ce1.aol.com>`

```
My book about COBOL II (ANSI'85) says:

01 V1 PIC S9(4) VALUE 14 COMP.     -> 00 0E
01 V2 PIC S9(4) VALUE -4 COMP.     -> FF FC

So the value is stored in 2's complement notation.

	Angela
```

#### ↳ Re: How are negative values stored in COMP?

- **From:** Binyamin_Dissen@theoffice.net (Binyamin Dissen)
- **Date:** 1999-02-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36c55265.4291880@news.netvision.net.il>`
- **References:** `<19990211102252.05470.00000032@ng-ce1.aol.com>`

```
On 11 Feb 1999 15:22:52 GMT tgala9760@aol.com (TGala9760) wrote:

[Posted and mailed]

:>I am a tech writer documenting some programs and I came across a question I
:>hoped someone would be able to help with here...


:>If a variable is binary, i.e., S9(4) COMP, how is a negative value stored?  I
:>know it is a two byte field, and I've heard everything from "they use the
:>parity bit" to "they subtract the number from high values" as explanations to
:>how negative numbers are stored.

:>Anyone know for sure?

Depends on the architecture (hardware). The two systems that I am aware of are
twos-complement and complement. 

In the complement system one flips every bit. There is both a positive and
negative zero.

In the twos-complement one flips every bit and adds one. The highest negative
number is one more than the highest positive number.

In both systems a number is negative if the first bit is on.
```

<a id="t-9b4c9118cd"></a>
## Thread: SPECIAL-NAMES/Y2K — 5 msgs

_Topics: [`Y2K and Year-2000 remediation`](topics.md#y2k)_

### SPECIAL-NAMES/Y2K

- **From:** Angela Schwarz <angela.schwarz@sdm.de>
- **Date:** 1999-02-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36C2D3E2.3A76@sdm.de>`

```
Hi,

I want to get the system date with a 4-digit-year. I'm working with
BS2000 (V110) and COBOL (V02.2C31). I know this solution:

ENVIRONMENT DIVISION.
...
SPECIAL-NAMES.
    DATE-ISO4 IS DATE-ISO4.
...
PROCEDURE-DIVISION.
...
    ACCEPT DATE-VAR FROM DATE-ISO4.

My problem:

1. Today I'm using ACCEPT DATE-VAR FROM DATE and I don't have to use
SPECIAL-NAMES. I have all my section working with date in a seperate
copy code. If I use this solution I have to modify a lot of programs.
This would be terrible and the programms won't look nice. How is it
possible to not use this SPECIAL-NAMES (i.e. compiler option)? 

2. What else can I do to get a 4-digit-year? I don't have CENTURY-DAY,
CENTURY-DATE, DATE YYYYMMDD, CURRENT-DATE.

Thanks,

        Angela
```

#### ↳ Re: SPECIAL-NAMES/Y2K

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1999-02-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<owBw2.7440$Fs.7980538@news4.mia>`
- **References:** `<36C2D3E2.3A76@sdm.de>`

```
Angela Schwarz wrote:
>
>2. What else can I do to get a 4-digit-year? I don't have CENTURY-DAY,
>CENTURY-DATE, DATE YYYYMMDD, CURRENT-DATE.

Assuming your COBOL compiler will correctly return yymmdd after 1/1/2000,
you can get by for the next 100 years using this code:

           03  ccyymmdd      PIC  9(08).
           03  ccyymmdd-r    REDEFINES ccyymmdd.
               05  cc            PIC  9(02).
               05  yymmdd        PIC  9(06).
               05  yymmdd-r      REDEFINES yymmdd.
                   07  YY            PIC  9(02).
                   07  mm            PIC  9(02).
                   07  dd            PIC  9(02).

           ACCEPT yymmdd FROM DATE.
           IF yy < 99
               MOVE 20 TO cc
           ELSE
               MOVE 19 TO cc.

This will produce a correct ccyymmdd for all dates from now through
December 31, 2099.
```

#### ↳ Re: SPECIAL-NAMES/Y2K

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1999-02-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<79uv5g$8io$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<36C2D3E2.3A76@sdm.de>`

```
You could go to a window scheme ... that way you
get the two digit date, then convert it in a way that
will work for the application.  For example, all years
before "50" are considered 2000-2049, and all dates
after 1950-1999. Windowing works fine when the range
of dates are somewhat controllable (like invoicing).  It
is no good for birthdates, etc.

Angela Schwarz wrote in message <36C2D3E2.3A76@sdm.de>...
>Hi,
>
…[25 more quoted lines elided]…
>        Angela
```

```
Hi Angela


> 1. Today I'm using ACCEPT DATE-VAR FROM DATE and I don't have to use
> SPECIAL-NAMES. I have all my section working with date in a seperate
> copy code. If I use this solution I have to modify a lot of programs.
> This would be terrible and the programms won't look nice. How is it

Unfortunally the BS2000 (german operating system) is not in common use, I
also know
only MVS/OS/390/VSE/VM but I have a customer who run both and is in
migration
phase. Please drop me an email at Roland.Schiradin@t-online.de and I'll
forward your question to our "BS2000" people


Roland
```

#### ↳ Re: SPECIAL-NAMES/Y2K

- **From:** Karl Kiesel <karl.kiesel@mch6.siemens.de>
- **Date:** 1999-02-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36C425AB.520A816E@mch6.siemens.de>`
- **References:** `<36C2D3E2.3A76@sdm.de>`

```
Angela Schwarz schrieb:
> 2. What else can I do to get a 4-digit-year? I don't have CENTURY-DAY,
> CENTURY-DATE, DATE YYYYMMDD, CURRENT-DATE.

Mrs Schwarz,
Your COBOL Compiler COBOL85 V2.3C31 already supports intrinsic functions
    DATE-TO-YYYMMDD, DAY-TO-YYYDDD and YEAR-TO-YYYY
that will be part of the next COBOL Standard. If you have a 2-digit
year, these functions provide convertions it to a 4-digit year based on
a sliding window (documentation can be found in the Freigabemitteilung
for V2.2C or in the language manual for version V2.3A).

regards,
 K. Kiesel,   SIEMENS AG, Munich
```

<a id="t-52310598e1"></a>
## Thread: an ASCII all-upper to mixed-lower conversion subprogram — 4 msgs

### an ASCII all-upper to mixed-lower conversion subprogram

- **From:** casten@usfca.edu (John Casten)
- **Date:** 1999-02-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<casten-1002991850510001@138.202.137.124>`

```
have at it gentlemen, make it better:

IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
PROGRAM-ID. UPPERTOLOWER.
AUTHOR. MOI.
*
** translation subroutine all ASCII upper-case to mixed upper/lower
** this will handle such oddball cases as "12 8th Ave NE", "Kwon, Su (Lily)",
** "Jungle, George of the, III" etc.
*
DATA DIVISION.
WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
*
** it works!
*
01  ASCII-VAL                  PIC 9(02)  COMP.
01  FILLER               REDEFINES ASCII-VAL.
    03  ASCII-CHAR             PIC X(01).
01  VALUE-32                   PIC 9(02)  COMP SYNC VALUE 32.

01  PAREN-SW                   PIC X(01)  VALUE 'N'.

01  FIELD-LENGTH               PIC 9(09)  COMP.

01  TEST-FIELD                 PIC X(64).
01  TEST-POS                   PIC 9(09)  COMP.
01  TEST-LEN                   PIC 9(09)  COMP.

01  OUTPUT-FIELD               PIC X(64).
01  OUTPUT-POS                 PIC 9(09)  COMP.
*
** these tables will inevitably grow over time
*
01  ALL-UPPER-TBL.
    03  ALL-UPPER-DATA.
        05                     PIC X(04)  VALUE 'II'.
        05                     PIC X(04)  VALUE 'III'.
        05                     PIC X(04)  VALUE 'IV'.
        05                     PIC X(04)  VALUE 'IIII'.
        05                     PIC X(04)  VALUE 'NE'.
        05                     PIC X(04)  VALUE 'PO'.
        05                     PIC X(04)  VALUE 'SW'.
        05                     PIC X(04)  VALUE 'SE'.
        05                     PIC X(04)  VALUE 'NW'.
        05                     PIC X(04)  VALUE 'FPO'.
        05                     PIC X(04)  VALUE 'APO'.
        05                     PIC X(04)  VALUE 'RR'.
        05                     PIC X(04)  VALUE 'RSC'.
        05                     PIC X(04)  VALUE 'PSC'.
        05                     PIC X(04)  VALUE 'HC'.
        05                     PIC X(04)  VALUE 'CSJ'.
    03  ALL-UPPER-ENTRIES REDEFINES ALL-UPPER-DATA
                            OCCURS 16
                           INDEXED AUX
                               PIC X(04).
*
** these will be converted to all lower, except when they are at the left edge
*
01  ALL-LOWER-TBL.
    03  ALL-LOWER-DATA.
        05                     PIC X(04)  VALUE 'THE'.
        05                     PIC X(04)  VALUE 'OF'.
        05                     PIC X(04)  VALUE 'AND'.
        05                     PIC X(04)  VALUE 'FOR'.
        05                     PIC X(04)  VALUE 'DE'.
        05                     PIC X(04)  VALUE 'DEL'.
        05                     PIC X(04)  VALUE 'LOS'.
        05                     PIC X(04)  VALUE 'AT'.
    03  ALL-LOWER-ENTRIES REDEFINES ALL-LOWER-DATA
                            OCCURS 8
                           INDEXED ALX
                               PIC X(04).
*
** there have to be many more out there, but these are the most common in 
** our database.  We lookup these names since there are a great many more
** exceptions, such as Macy, Macias, Machen, etc.
*
01  MAC-ARRAY.
    03  MAC-DATA.
        05                     PIC X(12)  VALUE 'MACALLISTER'.
        05                     PIC X(12)  VALUE 'MACARTHER'.
        05                     PIC X(12)  VALUE 'MACARTHUR'.
        05                     PIC X(12)  VALUE 'MACAULAY'.
        05                     PIC X(12)  VALUE 'MACAULEY'.
        05                     PIC X(12)  VALUE 'MACBETH'.
        05                     PIC X(12)  VALUE 'MACBRIDE'.
        05                     PIC X(12)  VALUE 'MACCALLA'.
        05                     PIC X(12)  VALUE 'MACCONNELL'.
        05                     PIC X(12)  VALUE 'MACCORMICK'.
        05                     PIC X(12)  VALUE 'MACCUAIG'.
        05                     PIC X(12)  VALUE 'MACCULLOUGH'.
        05                     PIC X(12)  VALUE 'MACDERMID'.
        05                     PIC X(12)  VALUE 'MACDONALD'.
        05                     PIC X(12)  VALUE 'MACDONELL'.
        05                     PIC X(12)  VALUE 'MACDOUGALL'.
        05                     PIC X(12)  VALUE 'MACEWAN'.
        05                     PIC X(12)  VALUE 'MACEWEN'.
        05                     PIC X(12)  VALUE 'MACFADDEN'.
        05                     PIC X(12)  VALUE 'MACFARLAND'.
        05                     PIC X(12)  VALUE 'MACFARLANE'.
        05                     PIC X(12)  VALUE 'MACFEE'.
        05                     PIC X(12)  VALUE 'MACGILLIVRAY'.
        05                     PIC X(12)  VALUE 'MACGINLEY'.
        05                     PIC X(12)  VALUE 'MACGLAUN'.
        05                     PIC X(12)  VALUE 'MACGLOAUN'.
        05                     PIC X(12)  VALUE 'MACGOWAN'.
        05                     PIC X(12)  VALUE 'MACGRATH'.
        05                     PIC X(12)  VALUE 'MACGREGOR'.
        05                     PIC X(12)  VALUE 'MACGUIRE'.
        05                     PIC X(12)  VALUE 'MACHALE'.
        05                     PIC X(12)  VALUE 'MACINNES'.
        05                     PIC X(12)  VALUE 'MACINNIS'.
        05                     PIC X(12)  VALUE 'MACINTOSH'.
        05                     PIC X(12)  VALUE 'MACINTYRE'.
        05                     PIC X(12)  VALUE 'MACISAAC'.
        05                     PIC X(12)  VALUE 'MACIVER'.
        05                     PIC X(12)  VALUE 'MACKEAN'.
        05                     PIC X(12)  VALUE 'MACKENZIE'.
        05                     PIC X(12)  VALUE 'MACKINNON'.
        05                     PIC X(12)  VALUE 'MACKNIGHT'.
        05                     PIC X(12)  VALUE 'MACLACHLAN'.
        05                     PIC X(12)  VALUE 'MACLAREN'.
        05                     PIC X(12)  VALUE 'MACLAUGHLIN'.
        05                     PIC X(12)  VALUE 'MACLEAN'.
        05                     PIC X(12)  VALUE 'MACLELLAN'.
        05                     PIC X(12)  VALUE 'MACLENNAN'.
        05                     PIC X(12)  VALUE 'MACLEOD'.
        05                     PIC X(12)  VALUE 'MACMAHON'.
        05                     PIC X(12)  VALUE 'MACMANUS'.
        05                     PIC X(12)  VALUE 'MACMASTER'.
        05                     PIC X(12)  VALUE 'MACMICHAEL'.
        05                     PIC X(12)  VALUE 'MACMILLAN'.
        05                     PIC X(12)  VALUE 'MACMULLAN'.
        05                     PIC X(12)  VALUE 'MACMULLEN'.
        05                     PIC X(12)  VALUE 'MACMURRAY'.
        05                     PIC X(12)  VALUE 'MACNAMARA'.
        05                     PIC X(12)  VALUE 'MACNAUGHTON'.
        05                     PIC X(12)  VALUE 'MACNEIL'.
        05                     PIC X(12)  VALUE 'MACNEILL'.
        05                     PIC X(12)  VALUE 'MACPHAIL'.
        05                     PIC X(12)  VALUE 'MACPHEE'.
        05                     PIC X(12)  VALUE 'MACPHERSON'.
    03  MAC-ENTRIES      REDEFINES MAC-DATA
                            OCCURS 63
                         ASCENDING MAC-ENTRY
                           INDEXED MACX.
        05  MAC-ENTRY          PIC X(12).
/
LINKAGE SECTION.
01  CALLED-FIELD.
    03  CF-BYTE             OCCURS 64
                           INDEXED CFX
                               PIC X(01).

01  CALLED-LENGTH              PIC 9(09)  COMP SYNC.
/
PROCEDURE DIVISION           USING CALLED-FIELD
                                   CALLED-LENGTH.
MAINLINE
    .
** guard against overflow
*
    IF CALLED-LENGTH > 64
       EXIT PROGRAM
    END-IF
*
** get our actual field length
*
    SET CFX TO CALLED-LENGTH
    PERFORM UNTIL CF-BYTE (CFX) > SPACE
               OR CFX < 1
        SET CFX DOWN BY 1
    END-PERFORM

    IF CFX < 1
       EXIT PROGRAM
    END-IF

    SET FIELD-LENGTH TO CFX
*
** look for a lowercase character - if found, exit
*
    SET CFX TO 1
    PERFORM UNTIL CFX > FIELD-LENGTH
       IF CF-BYTE (CFX) >= 'a' and <= 'z'
          EXIT PROGRAM
       END-IF
       SET CFX UP BY 1
    END-PERFORM
*
** initialize
*
    MOVE SPACES                TO TEST-FIELD
                                  OUTPUT-FIELD
    MOVE 1                     TO OUTPUT-POS
*
** leading spaces?
*
    SET CFX TO 1

    PERFORM UNTIL CF-BYTE (CFX) > SPACE
               OR CFX > FIELD-LENGTH
       SET CFX UP BY 1
    END-PERFORM
*
** convert the field, piece by piece
*
    PERFORM UNTIL CFX > FIELD-LENGTH
       PERFORM CONVERT-FRAGMENT
    END-PERFORM
*
** we done, baby
*
    MOVE OUTPUT-FIELD            TO CALLED-FIELD

    EXIT PROGRAM
    .
/
CONVERT-FRAGMENT
    .
    SET TEST-POS TO CFX
*
** find the length of the next chunk
*
    PERFORM UNTIL CF-BYTE (CFX) = SPACE OR ',' OR '-'
               OR CFX > FIELD-LENGTH
       SET CFX UP BY 1
    END-PERFORM

    SET TEST-LEN TO CFX
    SUBTRACT TEST-POS FROM TEST-LEN
*
** test-field is where we do the actual work
*
    MOVE CALLED-FIELD (TEST-POS:TEST-LEN) TO TEST-FIELD

    PERFORM CONVERT-TEST-FIELD
*
** output-field is our finished product
*
    MOVE TEST-FIELD (1:TEST-LEN) TO OUTPUT-FIELD (OUTPUT-POS:TEST-LEN)

    ADD TEST-LEN TO OUTPUT-POS
*
** our next field is either a space, comma, period or dash
*
    MOVE CF-BYTE (CFX)  TO OUTPUT-FIELD (OUTPUT-POS:1)
    IF OUTPUT-FIELD (OUTPUT-POS:1) = ','
       ADD 2 TO OUTPUT-POS
    ELSE
       ADD 1 TO OUTPUT-POS
    END-IF
*
** this will solve the multiple space problem
*
    SET CFX UP BY 1
    PERFORM UNTIL CF-BYTE (CFX) > SPACE
               OR CFX > FIELD-LENGTH
       SET CFX UP BY 1
    END-PERFORM
    .
/
CONVERT-TEST-FIELD
    .
** lots of special cases
*
    IF TEST-FIELD (1:1) = '(' OR '"' OR ''''
       MOVE TEST-FIELD (1:1)   TO OUTPUT-FIELD (OUTPUT-POS:1)
       ADD 1 TO OUTPUT-POS
       MOVE TEST-FIELD (2:TEST-LEN) TO TEST-FIELD
       SUBTRACT 2 FROM TEST-LEN
       MOVE 'Y'                TO PAREN-SW
    END-IF

    PERFORM CHECK-ALL-UPPER

    IF OUTPUT-POS > 1
       PERFORM CHECK-ALL-LOWER
    END-IF

    PERFORM CHECK-MC-MAC

    IF TEST-FIELD (1:1) NUMERIC
       PERFORM NUMBER-RTN
    END-IF
*
** special case for addresses like "King's Rd"; otherwise it's "O'Reilly" and
** the like.  For everything else we add 32 to the ASCII value
*
    PERFORM UNTIL TEST-FIELD (TEST-POS:1) = SPACE
               OR TEST-POS > 64
       IF TEST-FIELD (TEST-POS:1) >= 'A' AND <= 'Z'
          MOVE TEST-FIELD (TEST-POS:1) TO ASCII-CHAR
          ADD VALUE-32 TO ASCII-VAL
          MOVE ASCII-CHAR      TO TEST-FIELD (TEST-POS:1)
          ADD 1 TO TEST-POS
       ELSE
       IF TEST-FIELD (TEST-POS:3) = '''S'
          ADD 1 TO TEST-POS
       ELSE
          ADD 2 TO TEST-POS
       END-IF END-IF
    END-PERFORM

    IF PAREN-SW = 'Y'
       MOVE 'N'                TO PAREN-SW
       ADD 1 TO TEST-LEN
    END-IF
    .
/
CHECK-ALL-UPPER
    .
    SET AUX TO 1
    SEARCH ALL-UPPER-ENTRIES
       END
          CONTINUE
       WHEN ALL-UPPER-ENTRIES (AUX) = TEST-FIELD
          MOVE 65              TO TEST-POS
    END-SEARCH
    .
/
CHECK-ALL-LOWER
    .
    SET ALX TO 1
    SEARCH ALL-LOWER-ENTRIES
       END
          CONTINUE
       WHEN ALL-LOWER-ENTRIES (ALX) = TEST-FIELD
          MOVE 1               TO TEST-POS
    END-SEARCH
    .
/
CHECK-MC-MAC
    .
    IF TEST-FIELD (1:2) = 'MC'
       MOVE 'Mc'               TO TEST-FIELD (1:2)
       ADD 2 TO TEST-POS
    ELSE
    IF TEST-FIELD (1:3) = 'MAC'
       SEARCH ALL MAC-ENTRIES
          END
             CONTINUE
          WHEN MAC-ENTRY (MACX) = TEST-FIELD (1:TEST-LEN)
             MOVE 'Mac'        TO TEST-FIELD (1:3)
             ADD 3 TO TEST-POS
       END-SEARCH
    END-IF END-IF
    .
/
NUMBER-RTN
    .
    PERFORM UNTIL TEST-FIELD (TEST-POS:1) NOT NUMERIC
               OR TEST-POS > 64
       ADD 1 TO TEST-POS
    END-PERFORM
*
** once we are past the numbers then we must convert 21ST to 21st and the like
*
    IF TEST-FIELD (TEST-POS:3) = 'ST'
       MOVE 'st'      TO TEST-FIELD (TEST-POS:2)
       ADD 2 TO TEST-POS
    ELSE
    IF TEST-FIELD (TEST-POS:3) = 'ND'
       MOVE 'nd'      TO TEST-FIELD (TEST-POS:2)
       ADD 2 TO TEST-POS
    ELSE
    IF TEST-FIELD (TEST-POS:3) = 'RD'
       MOVE 'rd'      TO TEST-FIELD (TEST-POS:2)
       ADD 2 TO TEST-POS
    ELSE
    IF TEST-FIELD (TEST-POS:3) = 'TH'
       MOVE 'th'      TO TEST-FIELD (TEST-POS:2)
       ADD 2 TO TEST-POS
    ELSE
       ADD 1 TO TEST-POS
    END-IF END-IF END-IF END-IF
    .
```

```
well, in testing the program I ran it against the database to see what
differences popped up, and here are a few (not including the McNames or
the MacNames):
DeAsis
```
