```
LeMoss

MaGatz
VanDyke
VanSlyke

I mean you can only do so much (sniff)...

Seriously tho' way back when I had to struggle to make ends meet I took a
job at a savings and loan (remember savings and loans?), and one of our
favorite games was to collect oddball names.  Probably the wierdest was a
very kindly old black gentleman name of Flavors Maybon.  My personal
favorite was Mimi Wighals, which until you saw it spelled out would be
Mimi Wiggles.  And one day she actually came in and sat at my desk!  I was
thrilled.  I excused myself, went into a back room and dialed the manager,
and breathlessly told her "Margaret, I'm so excited! Mimi Wighals is
sitting at my desk!"  Gales of laughter.
```

<a id="t-7c74e064db"></a>
## Thread: Question on path calls with IMS databases — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe) · [`Databases and SQL`](topics.md#databases)_

### Re: Question on path calls with IMS databases

- **From:** Anonymous <nobody@base.xs4all.nl>
- **Date:** 1999-02-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<98a9475b7f9f0872db13c1feb052fdd6@base.xs4all.nl>`

```
>> Just leave a space after the "D" command code. This is an
>> unqualified SSA.
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> Damn syntax. Thx Simon and to all who answered privately.

Two steps forward, one step back - hit another snag.


setting SSA-A001-D     PIC(11) VALUE 'A001    *D '.   	
        SSA-A002-D     PIC(11) VALUE 'A002    *D '.   	
        SSA-A004-D     PIC(11) VALUE 'A004    *D '.   	
        SSA-A011       PIC(25) VALUE 'A011    *-(A011-KEY= YYY)'.
                                          where A011-KEY is the key field 
                                          for the A001 segment

and executing the following call repetitively

CALL 'CBLTDLI' USING DLI-GN
	             BILLS-PCB
	             A011-IO
		     SSA-A001-D
		     SSA-A002-D
		     SSA-A004-D
		     SSA-A011.

does step through the database sequentially but gives a GP error
code each time and does *not* fill in the ioareas for A001,2 and 4
as expected. 

My book says a GP error code is almost always a case of issuing
a GNP call without first establishing parentage. Fair enough, but
I'm issuing GN calls so parentage should not be an issue. And since
I issue a qualified SSA against the lowest-level segment I should
get only A011 occurances with the path-call SSAs to fill up the other
ioareas.

So, any ideas on what's going on and how to make it work like I
want?

Any/all help is appreciated - I am WAY too rusty at this stuff.


Please reply to the following address after removing "antispam" and
reversing "tom" in the following address:
antip23spam610@email.tom.com


Thanks,

Mike Striefel
Motorola SSTG
```

### Re: ACCEPT / Y2K

- **From:** Angela Schwarz <angela.schwarz@sdm.de>
- **Date:** 1999-02-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36C2D075.5F68@sdm.de>`
- **References:** `<36B9F4CE.1E4@club-internet.fr>`

```
Frï¿½dï¿½ric Joliot wrote:
> 
> Hello,
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> Thank you!

I'm working with BS2000 V110 and COBOL V02.2C31. My solution:

ENVIRONMENT DIVISION.
...
SPECIAL-NAMES.
    DATE-ISO4 IS DATE-ISO4.
...
PROCEDURE-DIVISION.
...
    ACCEPT DATUM FROM DATE-ISO4.

You will get 'YYYY-MM-DDNNN '. NNN= number of the day in this year.

	Angela
```

```
environment.  Familiarity with JLC, IBM utilities and testing and debugging
tools needed.  If you have the skills, we have the salary to match!!!

- provide technical analysis and production support for manufacturing
planning systems

- interface with customers to define problems and/or enhancement requests.

- analyze reported problems and provide timely solutions

- ensure that SEI requirements are met

Top flight company. Great working environment.  US CITIZENSHIP is a
requirement.

(preferred) or ASCII format for immediate consideration.

Larry Maucher
FSI
```

<a id="t-48fdc2eb2e"></a>
## Thread: Security Key Compatability — 1 msgs

### Security Key Compatability

- **From:** "Justin Roberts" <JustinRoberts@btinternet.com>
- **Date:** 1999-02-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<79t7dk$q6s$1@uranium.btinternet.com>`

```
I have several versions of Microfocus Cobol which require the use of a
security key.
Unfortunately, it is rather old and grey and has the following raised
markings :

    SOFTWARE
    SENTINEL-C TM
    RAINBOW
    TECHNOLOGIES, INC.
    IRVINE CA

I have tried to get this key to work using various Key enablers supplied by
Rainbow without success.  This may be due to the key being broken, but I
suspect that it is the key enabling software which no longer supports keys
of this type.  When I used keys like these in the good 'ole days, I remember
using a command called INSTKEY to install it.  Does anybody on the planet
have an old disk or a functioning version of MF Cobol which uses this type
of key and knows anything about how it the key was installed ?

This is my last effort to get it to work before I throw the lot in the bin
and get to grips with the Fujitsu alternative that everybody keeps
mentioning.

Please send replies / attachments to justinroberts@btinternet.com

Thank you.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: GO TO EXIT

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com>
- **Date:** 1999-02-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36C83605.6038258A@NOSPAMhome.com>`
- **References:** `<36C49A5F.F642DAE6@NOSPAMhome.com> <36C49B67.2CDEFEC1@anywhere.com> <36c4c3d1.2073303@news1.ibm.net>`

```
I'm really looking foreward to exit-paragraph.  

I've seen people mention how GO TO EXIT can make it harder for dumb
beginners to do maintenance, implying that experienced programmers won't
have this problem.  But experienced programmers often have cut & paste
errors.

This particular error can be eliminated by not having GO TO's in the
code.  

Thane Hubbell wrote:
> 
> On Fri, 12 Feb 1999 15:21:43 -0600, Mike <nospam@anywhere.com> wrote:
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> >
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: GO TO EXIT

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** JD <beta1@bigfoot.com>

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

```
What makes you think you can do a better job using the EXIT-PARAGRAPH any better
than you can use the GO TO EXIT?  There is NO syntax which can be developed to
make bad or careless programmers any better.

Howard Brazee wrote:

> I'm really looking foreward to exit-paragraph.
>

```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: GO TO EXIT

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com>
- **Date:** 1999-02-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36D6ABC8.A46B48C7@NOSPAMhome.com>`
- **References:** `<36C49A5F.F642DAE6@NOSPAMhome.com> <36C49B67.2CDEFEC1@anywhere.com> <36c4c3d1.2073303@news1.ibm.net> <36C83605.6038258A@NOSPAMhome.com> <36D5EA25.CA353875@bigfoot.com>`

```
JD wrote:
> 
> What makes you think you can do a better job using the EXIT-PARAGRAPH any better
> than you can use the GO TO EXIT?  There is NO syntax which can be developed to
> make bad or careless programmers any better.

Because there's no cutting and pasting necessary.  EXIT-PARAGRAPH always
exits the paragraph it is in.  Also, with an EXIT-PARAGRAPH clause,
there's no controversy about a standard which proscribed GO TO's.  And
it is easier for maintenance programmers to debug when they don't have
to double check the labels.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: GO TO EXIT

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@inospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-02-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7b76h6$2ls@dfw-ixnews11.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<36C49A5F.F642DAE6@NOSPAMhome.com> <36C49B67.2CDEFEC1@anywhere.com> <36c4c3d1.2073303@news1.ibm.net> <36C83605.6038258A@NOSPAMhome.com> <36D5EA25.CA353875@bigfoot.com> <36D6ABC8.A46B48C7@NOSPAMhome.com>`

```
As the person who wrote the proposal adding Exit Paragraph/Section to the
draft Standard, let me show you how bad coding can still cause "strange"
(unpredictable?  nasty?) results.

Just think of the following "syntactically" correct code.

Procedure Division.
  A Section.
     B.
        Perform D
        Stop Run.
  C Section.
    D.
       Display "In Paragraph D"
        Exit Section.
    E.
       Display "Certainly never reached".
   F Section.
       G.
            Display "About to fall out of program".

   ****

Anyone want to tell me what happens when you EXIT SECTION in a performed
(from another Section) paragraph?   The Standard sure doesn't make this
clear (and J4 was unwilling to even add a rule making it illegal).  There
are BUNCHES of other types of "bad"  code using EXIT Paragraph/Section where
the "flow" of the program is "fuzzy" at best.  Basically, any problem that
you can run into with a GO TO can be recreated with an EXIT
Paragraph/Section.  This doesn't mean that I am against the new syntax -
just that I want to make it clear that it doesn't "automatically solve" bad
coding practices.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: GO TO EXIT

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 1999-02-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<90kC2.4$iZ5.350@news19.ispnews.com>`
- **References:** `<36C49A5F.F642DAE6@NOSPAMhome.com> <36C49B67.2CDEFEC1@anywhere.com> <36c4c3d1.2073303@news1.ibm.net> <36C83605.6038258A@NOSPAMhome.com> <36D5EA25.CA353875@bigfoot.com> <36D6ABC8.A46B48C7@NOSPAMhome.com> <7b76h6$2ls@dfw-ixnews11.ix.netcom.com>`

```
From CD 1.2,
"14.10.13 EXIT statement

    ... The EXIT PARAGRAPH and EXIT SECTION statements provide
    a means of exiting a structured procedure without executing any of
    the following statements within the procedure."

The draft seems to be missing the description of what constitutes
a "structured procedure." I would suggest that it is reasonable for
the standard to define this term and to provide a translator option
that would allow for diagnostic messages should the translator
find violations of the rules supporting the definition.

The rules for implementing "structured procedure(s)" required by
EXIT SECTION could include:

1. Sections may only be PERFORMed.
2. PERFORM section-name may only refer to a single section.
3. PERFORM may not refer to a paragraph-name in a different
    section.
4. GO TO may not refer to a paragraph-name in a different
    section.

Under these rules, EXIT SECTION would work as expected; that is,
it would return control to the executable statement following the
PERFORM that referred to the section. Without these rules, EXIT
SECTION should not be permitted.
-------------------------------
Rick Smith
e-mail: < ricksmith@aiservices.com >


William M. Klein wrote in message <7b76h6$2ls@dfw-ixnews11.ix.netcom.com>...
>As the person who wrote the proposal adding Exit Paragraph/Section to the
>draft Standard, let me show you how bad coding can still cause "strange"
…[24 more quoted lines elided]…
>are BUNCHES of other types of "bad"  code using EXIT Paragraph/Section
where
>the "flow" of the program is "fuzzy" at best.  Basically, any problem that
>you can run into with a GO TO can be recreated with an EXIT
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>
[...]
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: GO TO EXIT

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** Barticus@att.spam.net (Randall Bart)
- **Date:** 1999-02-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36d90612.43156110@netnews>`
- **References:** `<36C49A5F.F642DAE6@NOSPAMhome.com> <36C49B67.2CDEFEC1@anywhere.com> <36c4c3d1.2073303@news1.ibm.net> <36C83605.6038258A@NOSPAMhome.com> <36D5EA25.CA353875@bigfoot.com> <36D6ABC8.A46B48C7@NOSPAMhome.com> <7b76h6$2ls@dfw-ixnews11.ix.netcom.com>`

```
'Twas Fri, 26 Feb 1999 14:14:22 -0800, when "William M. Klein"
<wmklein@inospam.netcom.com> illuminated comp.lang.cobol thusly:

>Procedure Division.
>  A Section.
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>            Display "About to fall out of program".

>Anyone want to tell me what happens when you EXIT SECTION in a performed
>(from another Section) paragraph?   The Standard sure doesn't make this
>clear (and J4 was unwilling to even add a rule making it illegal).  

There is a perfectly worthless rule at p 437, 14.10.13.2 SR 11:

"The EXIT statement with the SECTION phrase shall be specified only in a
section."

IOW, EXIT SECTION can't be used in a program without sections.  If a
program has any sections, all statements are in sections.

P 438, 14.10.13.3 GR 12 is the relevant rule:

"If an EXIT statement with the SECTION phrase is executed, the exit
statement causes control to be passed to an implicit CONTINUE statement
immediately following the last statement in the last paragraph in the
section."

Clearly this is a branch a point outside paragraph D, the end of the
perform range is never reached, and the program falls into F.  Since this
condition can't be detected at compile time, I can think of two other ways
this could work:  Exit the perform, or raise an exception
(EC-FLOW-something).  If this exits the perform it must exit any perform
whose range ends between here and the end of this section, which
complicates the compiler's job immensely.  Raising the exception would
also be hard to implement.  This is an unfortunate case where we just
can't engineer a safety into the tool, and programmers will be able to
write stupid code.

The wording of GR12 clearly makes the "implicit CONTINUE statement" part
of paragraph E.  Thus an EXIT SECTION exits the perform range iff the end
of the perform range is:

1) The section where the EXIT SECTION occurs.
2) The last paragraph of the section where the EXIT SECTION occurs.

The problem is that what people really want is EXIT PERFORM, but J4 has
decided that EXIT PERFORM should only work for inline PERFORM.  Don't
worry, it will be in COBOL 21XX.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ FYI: The EXIT Statement (was: Re: GO TO EXIT)

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 1999-02-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<wXyB2.2$gY4.52@news19.ispnews.com>`
- **References:** `<36C49A5F.F642DAE6@NOSPAMhome.com> <36C49B67.2CDEFEC1@anywhere.com> <36c4c3d1.2073303@news1.ibm.net> <36C83605.6038258A@NOSPAMhome.com> <36D5EA25.CA353875@bigfoot.com> <36D6ABC8.A46B48C7@NOSPAMhome.com>`

```

Several posters in this thread have put hyphens between the
EXIT and its scope, as in EXIT-PARAGRAPH and EXIT-SECTION.

The hyphen should not be present. The correct forms are:
    EXIT PARAGRAPH and
    EXIT SECTION.

I think it better to learn the correct form now than to unlearn the
incorrect form later.
-------------------------------
Rick Smith
e-mail: < ricksmith@aiservices.com >
```

#### ↳ Re: GO TO EXIT

- **From:** cwestbury@giant.intranet.com (Chris Westbury)
- **Date:** 1999-02-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1999Feb13.020915.19538@giant>`
- **References:** `<36C49A5F.F642DAE6@NOSPAMhome.com>`

```
In article <36C49A5F.F642DAE6@NOSPAMhome.com>,
Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com> wrote:
>
> A few minutes ago, I watched an experienced programmer discover the
> reason his program didn't get all the results he was expecting.  He had
> a few GO TO 0800-EXIT statements instead of 0850-EXIT statements.

For this style of programing to work properly, you need the function name
in the exit paragraph name as well as in the paragraph name.  The numbers
and the function names are independent; the numbers act as an index to
help you locate the paragraph and the function name tells you what it
does.  The function names should be long enough to be "grabbed" by the
editor's global replace.  You can move a routine by global replace on the
number, and you can clone a routine by global replace on the function
name.  To write 0800-EXIT instead of 0800-FOO-BAR-EXIT is like calling
your variables A1, A2, A3, etc., instead of using meaningful variable
names.  It may save you typing, but it does not make for a good program.
```

```
This is the style I used until I started using a code checker.  The
code checker also tests for missing or extra periods, paragraph
numbers out of sequence, PERFORM THRU the wrong exit, etc., so it's
worthwhile for other reasons as well.
```

#### ↳ Re: GO TO EXIT

- **From:** "Sven" <informatica@freemail.nl>
- **Date:** 1999-02-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7a6h0u$rpi$1@nickel.uunet.be>`
- **References:** `<36C49A5F.F642DAE6@NOSPAMhome.com>`

```
I'm rather more an analyst then a programmer right now, but let me give you
this advise : never, ever use a goto statement.

Perform statements are the method for structural programming, and following
the structure an analyst has spent many hours working on...

I'm not saying this problem occurred because of the goto (I don't know
enough about COBOL to say that), but I'm sure that many other problems can
be avoided when you use performs instead of goto's...

Sven.

Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com> wrote in message
news:36C49A5F.F642DAE6@NOSPAMhome.com...
>A few minutes ago, I watched an experienced programmer discover the
>reason his program didn't get all the results he was expecting.  He had
>a few GOTO 0800-EXIT statements instead of 0850-EXIT statements.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: GO TO EXIT

- **From:** "Arnold" <arnoldh@home.com>
- **Date:** 1999-02-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7UDx2.358$0A4.941@news.rdc1.mi.home.com>`

```
As an advocate of structured programming, due to my long years of
maintaining and debugging non-structured code, I have come to the conclusion
that the PERFORM THRU form virtually guarantees the use of GOTO.  I know
there are times when you can't get around the GOTO, but the less it is used,
the more structure you gain, and the more maintainable the code will be.

John Trifon wrote in message <36C6CD11.89D74BF3@ix.netcom.com>...
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: GO TO EXIT

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** zberger@ldl.net

```
headache of gotos can make some of the most tenacious compilers give up and
fall back on the default, e.g. no optimizitions, way of doing things.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: GO TO EXIT

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Barticus@att.spam.net (Randall Bart)
- **Date:** 1999-02-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36ccb346.84065239@netnews>`
- **References:** `<36C49A5F.F642DAE6@NOSPAMhome.com> <7a6h0u$rpi$1@nickel.uunet.be> <36C6CD11.89D74BF3@ix.netcom.com> <36cb49d3.25650773@news.netvision.net.il> <7acdv8$5cl$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
'Twas Tue, 16 Feb 1999 18:38:34 GMT, when zberger@ldl.net illuminated
comp.lang.cobol thusly:

>Because "perform thru" is rather hard to optimize (think about it)

Not really.  An early optimization pass checks for overlapping PERFORM
ranges, and if there are, that kills a lot of optimization.  If there are
no overlapping ranges, optimizing shoudl be little effected by a GO TO
within the PERFORM range.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: GO TO EXIT

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "James King" <mangogwr@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1999-02-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<TDqA2.15034$OS5.14992525@news3.mia>`
- **References:** `<36C49A5F.F642DAE6@NOSPAMhome.com> <7a6h0u$rpi$1@nickel.uunet.be> <36C6CD11.89D74BF3@ix.netcom.com> <36cb49d3.25650773@news.netvision.net.il> <7acdv8$5cl$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <36ccb346.84065239@netnews>`

```
The ONLY difference between Perform and perform thru
is (1) you elimenate the possibility of falling thru by specifiying the
EXIT.
whether you code thru makes NO difference if your program is
structured and coded correctly.

perform a
or perform a thru a-b.

a.
 ...
a-b.

b.
..
b-c.
the compiler will still generate the code to exit at a-b.

Optimizations end usually when the compiler has expanded code
by 50% anyways.
```

- **References:** `<36C49A5F.F642DAE6@NOSPAMhome.com> <7a6h0u$rpi$1@nickel.uunet.be> <36C6CD11.89D74BF3@ix.netcom.com> <36cb49d3.25650773@news.netvision.net.il> <7acdv8$5cl$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <36ccb346.84065239@netnews> <TDqA2.15034$OS5.14992525@news3.mia> <0E80A9B58B2CFDA4.3087171EB24082C4.7A5330BE4D3EF3DF@library-proxy.airnews.net>`

```
You missed the obvious.  Parabraph a-b is EMPTY.  It is the equivalent of an
EXIT paragraph.  Therefore, the generated code would be identical as a result
of the two different performs.

SkippyPB wrote:

> On Tue, 23 Feb 1999 05:02:43 GMT, "James King"
> <mangogwr@bellsouth.net> enlightened us:
…[56 more quoted lines elided]…
>  Steve
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: GO TO EXIT

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** Barticus@att.spam.net (Randall Bart)
- **Date:** 1999-02-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36d90f44.45506296@netnews>`
- **References:** `<36C49A5F.F642DAE6@NOSPAMhome.com> <7a6h0u$rpi$1@nickel.uunet.be> <36C6CD11.89D74BF3@ix.netcom.com> <36cb49d3.25650773@news.netvision.net.il> <7acdv8$5cl$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <36ccb346.84065239@netnews> <TDqA2.15034$OS5.14992525@news3.mia>`

```
'Twas Tue, 23 Feb 1999 05:02:43 GMT, when "James King"
<mangogwr@bellsouth.net> illuminated comp.lang.cobol thusly:

>The ONLY difference between Perform and perform thru
>is (1) you elimenate the possibility of falling thru by specifiying the
>EXIT.

THRU provides no such safety feature.  You can still GO TO something
outside your perform range.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: GO TO EXIT

- **From:** vbandke@ibm.net (Volker Bandke)
- **Date:** 1999-02-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

```
On Sun, 14 Feb 1999 13:54:27 +0100, "Sven" <informatica@freemail.nl> wrote:

>I'm rather more an analyst then a programmer right now, but let me give you
>this advise : never, ever use a goto statement.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>
<Tongue_in_cheek>
Never use GO TO, as it gives you spaghetti code.  The following is much more preferable
(and structured, of course)

Main-Control.	
	Perform 01000-Initialization
	Perform 02000-Standard-Processing
	Perform 03000-Termination
	GOBACK
	.
01000-Initialization.
	Perform 01100-Open-Files
	Perform 01200-Build-Headers
	Perform 01300-Initvars
	....

....
01100-Open-Files.
	Perform 01110-Open-Input-files
	Perform 01110-Open-Output-files
.....
01111-OPEN-Input-files.
	Perform 01111a-Open-Required-Input-Files
	Perform 01111b-Open-Optional-Input-Files

etc etc pp

Of course, the experts call this Lasagne code....
</tongue_in_cheek>


with kind regards

Volker Bandke
(BSP GmbH)
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: GO TO EXIT

- **From:** zberger@ldl.net
- **Date:** 1999-02-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7acdlu$57i$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`
- **References:** `<36C49A5F.F642DAE6@NOSPAMhome.com> <7a6h0u$rpi$1@nickel.uunet.be>`

```
I tend to worry when I hear someone say "never-ever use gotos or your computer
will melt"....

Gotos have their place (exceptions come to mind), the problem seems to be the
code style in use, the:

   perform 1234-something thru 5678-something-exit

1234-something.
   ...
   if whatever go to 5678-exit
12345-something.
   ...
   if whatever go to 5678-exit
123456-something.
   ...
   if whatever go to 5678-exit
5678-exit.

which is not at all well structured, is hard to maintain and encourages really
bad code anyway.  To be nicely structured in the "normal" sense it should be
recoded as:

   perform 1234-something.
1234-something.
   if not (whatever )
     perform 12345-something
     if not ( whatever )
       perform 123456-something
     end-if
   end-if
   exit.

In article <7a6h0u$rpi$1@nickel.uunet.be>,
  "Sven" <informatica@freemail.nl> wrote:
> I'm rather more an analyst then a programmer right now, but let me give you
> this advise : never, ever use a goto statement.
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
>

-----------== Posted via Deja News, The Discussion Network ==----------
http://www.dejanews.com/       Search, Read, Discuss, or Start Your Own
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: binary search

- **From:** sff5ky@aol.comxxx123 (Sff5ky)
- **Date:** 1999-02-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19990212183446.04923.00000344@ngol07.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<19990212170915.11548.00000721@ng33.aol.com>`

```

In article <19990212170915.11548.00000721@ng33.aol.com>, dupavoy@aol.combatSPAM
(Dupavoy) writes:

>Subject:	Re: binary search
>From:	dupavoy@aol.combatSPAM (Dupavoy)
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
>>

Using the COBOL SEARCH ALL syntax requires that the table be ordered by the
search key field in ascending order, no duplicates, and the table has to be
completely filled or defined as a variable length with a pointer to the number
of entries in the table.   These are the only criteria I know of for
successfully getting a binary search using the standard COBOL syntax without
having to write your own routine.
As I recall, doing your own routine still involves the above criteria and doing
checks against a LOW, MID, and HIGH point for spltting the table further.
Usually getting an answer within 7 loops.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: binary search

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Arnold" <arnoldh@home.com>
- **Date:** 1999-02-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

- **References:** `<19990212183446.04923.00000344@ngol07.aol.com> <19990212194911.06123.00000805@ng-cc1.aol.com>`

```
Order can be specified in the table definition, default is ascending, and
order doesn't have to be specified in the SEARCH ALL statement, as again a
default is used.  If you don't want ascending order, you must specify
everywhere.


>I believe the table can be in descending or ascending oeder. As long as
your
>search statement specifies the correct order. Can someone please correct
me, if
>I am wrong?
>
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: binary search

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@inospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-02-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7afv4c$q85@dfw-ixnews8.ix.netcom.com>`

```
Somewhere in this thread, there was a statement about not allowing
duplicates in a table that you SEARCH ALL.  I think this is the problem with
all high-values.  On the other hand, I know of NO compiler that does have
such a restriction (and I certainly don't think it is in the Standard) so I
think it is the underlying assumption that is wrong (and causing difficulty
in implementation).
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: binary search

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** sff5ky@aol.comxxx123 (Sff5ky)
- **Date:** 1999-02-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19990219065750.15962.00000051@ngol01.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<7afv4c$q85@dfw-ixnews8.ix.netcom.com>`

```
In article <7afv4c$q85@dfw-ixnews8.ix.netcom.com>, "William M. Klein"
<wmklein@inospam.netcom.com> writes:

>Somewhere in this thread, there was a statement about not allowing
>duplicates in a table that you SEARCH ALL.  I think this is the problem with
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>

I know from past history that the SEARCH ALL works with duplicates on the key,
the problem with duplicates comes when the remaining data for said duplicate
key is not the same.  You are subject to getting an arbitrary hit on the
duplicate key and being certain of the data content.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: binary search

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** Warren Porter <warrenp@ASPMnetdoor.com>
- **Date:** 1999-02-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36D1DBD8.7C8E9ED8@ASPMnetdoor.com>`
- **References:** `<7afv4c$q85@dfw-ixnews8.ix.netcom.com> <19990219065750.15962.00000051@ngol01.aol.com>`

```
Sff5ky wrote:

> In article <7afv4c$q85@dfw-ixnews8.ix.netcom.com>, "William M. Klein"
> <wmklein@inospam.netcom.com> writes:
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> duplicate key and being certain of the data content.

True.  One could fill or initialize an oversize table with high-values to be
loaded with sorted data at runtime.  When the search hit one of these unused
entries it would then turn to the lower end of the table towards "real" data.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: binary search

```
I believe the issue is not the entries with keys of  high values, but the  'real'
duplicate keys with potentially different data. Using  the code segment for
discussion, it is evident that one can never be sure if the SEARCH ALL

01 tab.
    02   ele occurs 20 times
                            indexed ele-x
                           ascending ele-key.
        03  ele-key pic x  value high-values.
         03  ele-data pic x value space.
....
move 'A' to ele-key (1)
                  ele-key (2)
move 'x' to ele-data (2)...
search all ele
       when ele-key = 'A'
            display ele-data.
```

- **References:** `<36c84949.59453721@news1.ibm.net>`

```

In article <36c84949.59453721@news1.ibm.net>, redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
writes:

>>I never thought about the no duplicates requirement.  I checked my
>>manual and it doesn't mention it.  Maybe it's compiler dependent.  Why
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Having duplicates in and of itself is not a bad thing.  Having duplicate keys
with 
different detail data is a bad thing.  I have experienced this situation where 
the input table has many points of entry and rather than search for the
existence of the key a new entry is made with the current values.  
Further, why extend the table size to accommodate multiple entries of the same
data.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: binary search

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)

```
>
>In article <36c84949.59453721@news1.ibm.net>, redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Depends on the application.  One can still take advantage of the
binary search used by (most implementations) search all.  Then
manually search one at a time backward to find the first occurance.  I
used this - same key different detail - in my software that translated
Aviation weather to english.  MDT means Moderate or Middletown -
depending on the context.  The detail information contained context
flags to help determine the context.  Sometimes these were changed "on
the fly" - hence the table.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: binary search

- **From:** sff5ky@aol.comxxx123 (Sff5ky)
- **Date:** 1999-02-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19990213003311.00116.00000439@ngol06.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<36c4c425.0@news3.uswest.net>`

```

In article <36c4c425.0@news3.uswest.net>, "Chris Osborne"
<chris_n_osborne@yahoo.com> writes:

>
>Because there is a binary search command built into COBOL, SEARCH ALL, I
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>

In various implementations, the COBOL code is not that efficient and a manually
developed routine can sometime be significantly faster.  In past development
projects, I had to search 2 to 6 different tables before returning a target
value to a peripheral device within micro seconds.  The COBOL SEARCH ALL syntax
failed to complete in time the service the device properly and when we switched
to a manually coded binary routine, there was time to spare.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: binary search

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1999-02-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<e6hx2.8998$Fs.9219628@news4.mia>`
- **References:** `<36c4c425.0@news3.uswest.net> <19990213003311.00116.00000439@ngol06.aol.com>`

```
Sff5ky wrote:
>
>In various implementations, the COBOL code is not that efficient and a manually
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>to a manually coded binary routine, there was time to spare.

The COBOL 85 standard merely says that if you use SEARCH ALL "a nonserial
type of search operation *may* take place" (emphasis mine).  It does not
say that a nonserial search *will* take place, or what kind of nonserial
search it might be, if it does take place.  Your compiler may not generate
a binary search at all. :-)
```

```

I'd never realized that the compiler (tricky thing, isn't it) wouldn't
actually do a binary search.

However, running VS COBOL II release 4 I have found substantially faster
performance when using SEARCH ALL.  So I guess the times I did use it I was
lucky and the compiler came through.


Sincerely,
     Chris Osborne
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: binary search

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** Barticus@att.spam.net (Randall Bart)
- **Date:** 1999-02-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36ccaf9c.83131239@netnews>`
- **References:** `<36c4c425.0@news3.uswest.net> <19990213003311.00116.00000439@ngol06.aol.com> <e6hx2.8998$Fs.9219628@news4.mia> <36c77da5.0@news2.uswest.net> <3ETx2.6429$wj1.9550367@news2.mia>`

```
'Twas Mon, 15 Feb 1999 11:35:27 GMT, when "Judson McClendon"
<judmc123@bellsouth.net> illuminated comp.lang.cobol thusly:

>Chris Osborne wrote:
>>
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>for SEARCH ALL, but evidently some do not, for whatever 'reasons'. :-)

Not everyone wanted to write a binary search routine so the COBOL
committee made a compromise.  They created SEARCH ALL, and declared it to
have all the necessary restrictions so that it could be a binary search so
that an implementor had a way to offer the efficiency of a binary search.
But they also allowed for an implementor not to implement a binary search.
This way a program which used a binary search could be recompiled on a
system without a binary search and still run correctly.

Pragmatically, the A Series Cobol74 compiler used a sequenctial search for
many years.  Someone had once written a routine for a binary search, but
didn't realize he couldn't use the normal condition parsing routine.  This
never worked.  Rather than fix that routine immediately, the routine was
bypassed by just ignoring the ALL after SEARCH.  This meant we were
allowing any condition in the WHEN clause and allowing multiple WHEN
clauses.  So then we put in deimplementation warning for all those invalid
SEARCH ALLs, and then a couple years after that a binary SEARCH ALL was
implemented.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: binary search

- **From:** "Leif Svalgaard" <lsvalg@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 1999-02-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36c504c2@news3.us.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<7a27j7$4ls$1@clarknet.clark.net> <19990212170425.11548.00000718@ng33.aol.com>`

```

Dupavoy wrote in message <19990212170425.11548.00000718@ng33.aol.com>...
>>Subject: Re: binary search
>>From: docdwarf@clark.net ()
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>those who ask for help here?

Here is another answer (homework or not), so take the trouble to read it.


A sorted table has the same structure as a simple table, with the addition
of some extra indices. These are used when searching and changing the table.
We use a flag (item-found) to improve the search algorithm.

01  VARIOUS-INDICES.
    02  ITEM-NBR                PIC S9(3)  COMP.
    02  COPY-PTR                PIC S9(3)  COMP.
    02  LOWER-LIMIT             PIC S9(3)  COMP.
    02  UPPER-LIMIT             PIC S9(3)  COMP.
    02  MOVE-DIRECTION          PIC S9(3)  COMP.

01  VARIOUS-VALUES.
    02  ITEM-FOUND              PIC X.
        88  ITEM-WAS-FOUND                 VALUE IS "Y".

01  SORTED-TABLE.
    02  SORTED-SIZE             PIC S9(3)  VALUE ZERO COMP.
    02  SORTED-MAX              PIC S9(3)  VALUE +100 COMP.
    02  SORTED-ITEM                        OCCURS 100 TIMES.
        03  SORTED-ITEM-KEY     PIC X(...).
        03  SORTED-ITEM-DATA    PIC X(...).

The Binary Search Procedure

Before anything else, you need a way to search the table. To add an item,
for example, you must first find the place where it should go. You could use
an item-by-item scan, as above for a simple table. Alternatively, you can
take advantage of the fact that the table is sorted, and use a binary search
technique, which is much faster for more than about ten items. For a table
of ten thousand items, we would need on average five thousand comparisons
using a simple scan, but at most 14 comparisons using a binary search.

The binary search is an interesting technique; easy to explain, but much
harder to write correctly because of the variety of end-cases to handle.

Take the entire table, and look at the middle item. If higher than the test
item, look only at the lower part of the table; if less, look only at the
top part of the table; if equal, you have found the item. Repeat this
process, chopping the area of interest into smaller and smaller pieces

Give this problem to any twenty people who say they can write a correct
program, and nineteen will get it wrong the first time. They will forget to
consider what happens if the table is empty, or if the item is not found.
The following code does it correctly:

SCAN-SORTED-TABLE-FOR-ITEM.
    MOVE    "NO"     TO ITEM-FOUND
    MOVE      1      TO LOWER-LIMIT
    MOVE SORTED-SIZE TO UPPER-LIMIT

    PERFORM BINARY-SEARCH-OF-TABLE
      UNTIL ITEM-WAS-FOUND
         OR LOWER-LIMIT > UPPER-LIMIT
    .

BINARY-SEARCH-OF-TABLE.
    COMPUTE ITEM-NBR = (LOWER-LIMIT + UPPER-LIMIT) / 2
    IF SORTED-ITEM-KEY (ITEM-NBR) > ITEM-KEY
        COMPUTE UPPER-LIMIT = ITEM-NBR - 1
    ELSE
    IF SORTED-ITEM-KEY (ITEM-NBR) < ITEM-KEY
        COMPUTE LOWER-LIMIT = ITEM-NBR + 1
    ELSE
        MOVE "YES" TO ITEM-FOUND
    .

After the binary search, item-found is "Y" or "N". If "Y", item-nbr points
to the found item. If "N", lower-limit points to the next higher item. This
is also the place where you insert a new item. The compute statement used in
binary-search-of-table is costly on some systems, and you could use an
alternative:

    ADD LOWER-LIMIT, UPPER-LIMIT GIVING ITEM-NBR
    DIVIDE ITEM-NBR BY 2


On the other hand, do not do this except as an optimization technique for
true inner loops - it is a compromise which sacrifices the readability of
the program for efficiency.

If the table is organized as a heap even the division can be dispensed with
and replaced by an addition. We have here another example of the critical
nature of the right choice of data structure. As far as the primitive
operations you might want to perform on the table (search, add, remove, ...)
its internal structure is really not of interest.

The above code makes up to two comparisons each time through the loop. A
significantly faster method that only makes one comparison is:

SCAN-SORTED-TABLE-FOR-ITEM.
    MOVE "NO" TO ITEM-FOUND
    MOVE ZERO TO LOWER-LIMIT
    COMPUTE UPPER-LIMIT = SORTED-SIZE + 1

    PERFORM BINARY-SEARCH-OF-TABLE
      UNTIL UPPER-LIMIT - LOWER-LIMIT = 1

    IF (UPPER-LIMIT NOT > SORTED-SIZE
    AND SORTED-ITEM-KEY (UPPER-LIMIT) = ITEM-KEY)
        MOVE UPPER-LIMIT TO ITEM-NBR
        MOVE    "YES"    TO ITEM-FOUND
    .

BINARY-SEARCH-OF-TABLE.
    COMPUTE ITEM-NBR = (LOWER-LIMIT + UPPER-LIMIT) / 2
    IF SORTED-ITEM-KEY (ITEM-NBR) < ITEM-KEY
        MOVE ITEM-NBR TO LOWER-LIMIT
    ELSE
        MOVE ITEM-NBR TO UPPER-LIMIT
   .

With this code, upper-limit points to the next higher item, which is also
more natural than having lower-limit pointing to it. The code has a careful
test after the loop, but makes only one comparison per prope, has only one
loop termination test, and does not increment/decrement inside the loop. If
the key is of some length, the improved code is about twice as fast.

For additional speed, the division by two can be replaced by a table-lookup
using the sum of the two limits to index into a table containing half their
value. This table should then be initialized at the beginning of the
program.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: binary search

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Mandrake" <concept_9@yahoo.com>
- **Date:** 1999-02-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7a8i77$e87$1@newshost.cyberramp.net>`
- **References:** `<7a27j7$4ls$1@clarknet.clark.net> <19990212170425.11548.00000718@ng33.aol.com> <36c504c2@news3.us.ibm.net>`

```
JHC! (explative condensed)

How about a simple answer for a simple question.

SOMEWHERE IN THE DATA DIVISION.....

01    BLA-BLA-TABLE.
    05    SORTED-TABLE
    OCCURS 50 TIMES
    INDEXED BY SORTED-TABLE-INDEX
    ASCENDING KEY IS SORT-KEY.
        10    SORT-KEY                    PIC 9.
        10    SORT-STUFF               PIC 99999.

SOMEWHERE IN THE PROCEDURE DIVISION....

SEARCH ALL SORTED-TABLE
    AT END
        DISPLAY 'RECORD NOT FOUND'
    WHEN SORT-KEY (SORTED-TABLE-INDEX) = SEARCH-ITEM
        DISPLAY 'RECORD FOUND'
END-SEARCH.

voila.....of course....you'll have to worry about populating the table, and
you'll probably want more than a display when the record is found......but
that's your problem.

yours truely,
The Mighty Mandrake
```

<a id="t-522d04d2a7"></a>
## Thread: Fujitsu COBOL 3.0 and Y2K — 2 msgs

_Topics: [`Y2K and Year-2000 remediation`](topics.md#y2k) · [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### Re: Fujitsu COBOL 3.0 and Y2K

- **From:** Alistair Maclean <LD50Macca@ld50macca.demon.co.uk>
- **Date:** 1999-02-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<GFVVXCA$ODx2EwY9@ld50macca.demon.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<36B890F2.11B53D4C@worf.netins.net> <918118916.3722.0.nnrp-04.9e98b131@news.demon.co.uk>`

```
The problem with reverting to two digit years , as far as I se it, is
that an increasing proportion of the population is living to 100+ years.
Therefore a birth year of 05 may be 1905 or 2005. Which one do you
choose? Incidentally, I came across this problem in 1995 on a TESSA
system (a peculiarly British tax exempt savings scheme).


In article <918118916.3722.0.nnrp-04.9e98b131@news.demon.co.uk>, Rick
Price <rick@hpdi.demon.co.uk> writes
>
>Floyd Johnson wrote in message <36B890F2.11B53D4C@worf.netins.net>...
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

#### ↳ Re: Fujitsu COBOL 3.0 and Y2K

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com>
- **Date:** 1999-02-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36C83269.C2794AB9@NOSPAMhome.com>`
- **References:** `<36B890F2.11B53D4C@worf.netins.net> <918118916.3722.0.nnrp-04.9e98b131@news.demon.co.uk> <GFVVXCA$ODx2EwY9@ld50macca.demon.co.uk>`

```
Alistair Maclean wrote:
> 
> The problem with reverting to two digit years , as far as I se it, is
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> system (a peculiarly British tax exempt savings scheme).

The problem depends on what you're trying to measure.  And it's not a
Y2K problem.  When you're trying to see how old a school kid is, two
digits are sufficient.  When you're trying to find out how many people
in town are of school age you get the famous cases of the 105 year old
getting truancy notices.

But since there are so many cross referenced databases, it pays to put 4
digit years in everyplace.  

I tried to send my taxes in electronically.  It wouldn't take my taxes
because Social Security somehow has my wrong birth date.  I don't see
why there should be any relationship.  So I paid $20 to a service to
send me a certified birth certificate, and I will take a couple of hours
off work at the local social security office, then wait 3 weeks before I
can send in my tax forms again.

Just because there is no logical reason for a date to be compared
doesn't mean it won't be compared.  Since storage is cheap now, go to
the 4 digit year.
```

<a id="t-f4f3a400c8"></a>
## Thread: Dynamic CALL — 2 msgs

### Re: Dynamic CALL

- **From:** "Jeff" <a@a.com>
- **Date:** 1999-02-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bM%w2.126$_2.911874@storm.twcol.com>`
- **References:** `<A3mu2.10073$3J3.2520@news2.giganews.com>`

```
>I am trying to get dynamic calls approved as a standard in our shop.  I am
>looking for evidence supporting the use of dynamic calls as opposed to
>static.  We develop COBOL/390 on OS/390 under IMS/DC, CICS, batch, etc.
>accessing IMS, DB2, VSAM, flat files, etc.


I worked at a shop that had them Specifically, building the called
subprogram based on a field input from a screen in CICS. It was horrible to
trace down. If you don't know the valid fields, than you don't know what
subprogram will be called. You would have to document all of the valid
choices. Validate all of the choices. And hope no one changes your logic in
the future.

Programs would be much simpler if these were not used. However if the logic
is properly documented and the standard of documenting the logic is
enforced. Then I don't see a big problem with it.

I seem to recall something about some type of overhead in CICS to maintain
dynamic calls. You may want to check into this before moving on. If this is
true, once your standard begins to proliferate it will begin to bog down
CICs.
```

#### ↳ Re: Dynamic CALL

- **From:** William Lynch <wblynch@att.net>
- **Date:** 1999-02-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36C51612.4C91B9D5@att.net>`
- **References:** `<A3mu2.10073$3J3.2520@news2.giganews.com> <bM%w2.126$_2.911874@storm.twcol.com>`

```
Jeff wrote:
> 
(snip)
> 
> I worked at a shop that had them Specifically, building the called
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> the future.

I think this is an argument against such a technique, not against
dynamic CALLs. Do you object to CALLing programs in batch? Following the
program logic concerning CALLs shouldn't be any more difficult than
following the paragraph names you PERFORM. A CALL is simply an external
subroutine (or paragraph, if you prefer). Other languages, e.g., REXX,
don't have separate ways of invoking internal/external routines. You
CALL everything, if it's not defined in your program it must be
external.

I recently found an LE/370 paper (thanks to a URL posted by Bill Klein)
in which the current recommendation is to use dynamic CALLs if you're
concerned about the overhead of CICS LINK commands (search DejaNews if
you're interested). Go figure:-)

Bill Lynch
```

## Thread: TCB-SRB — 2 msgs

### TCB-SRB

- **From:** joshid@nationwide.com
- **Date:** 1999-02-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7a150j$u74$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
Hi all,

   I need some help in interpreting SRB and TCB time for a job on MVS. How are
these related to the elapsed time ? Any help would be appreciated. TIA

-----------== Posted via Deja News, The Discussion Network ==----------
http://www.dejanews.com/       Search, Read, Discuss, or Start Your Own
```

#### ↳ Re: TCB-SRB

- **From:** Gilbert Saint-flour <gsf@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 1999-02-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36c43b97$3$tfs$mr2ice@news>`
- **References:** `<7a150j$u74$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
In <7a150j$u74$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>, on 12 Feb 1999 at 11:58,
joshid@nationwide.com said:

>   I need some help in interpreting SRB and TCB time for a job on MVS.
>How are these related to the elapsed time ?

They have no relationship to the elapsed time.  They represent CPU time
spent executing the instructions in your program (TCB) and CPU time spent
in system routines doing work for your job (SRB).  In most jobs, SRB time
is directly related to I/O activity.

Gilbert Saint-flour <gsf@ibm.net>
```

<a id="t-9e295a8000"></a>
## Thread: Linking — 4 msgs

### Linking

- **From:** "Camp David" <dvdwaaij@advantage.nl>
- **Date:** 1999-02-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7a3jma$5s1@news3.euro.net>`

```
When I want to link my object, I can choose between a filename with *.exe
and a filename with *.dll.
In which situation is *.dll used and in which is *.exe used.
Has this something to do with compiling as 'main' (calling) modules and
compiling as (called) modules???
```

#### ↳ Re: Linking

- **From:** "John Hicks (Remove \\"nospam\\" from address before replying)" <johnhicks@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 1999-02-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36CA569D.F3457BC0@ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<7a3jma$5s1@news3.euro.net>`

```
Yes. DLL stands for Dynamic Link Library. It's a Windows thing.

From the Tech Encyclopedia <http://www.techweb.com/encyclopedia/>:	

              (Dynamic Link Library) An executable
              program module that performs some
              function. DLLs are not launched directly
              by users. When needed, they are called for
              by a running application and loaded to
              perform a specific function. DLLs are
              generally written so that their routines are
              shared by more than one application at the
              same time (see reentrant code).

              In the DOS world, there was never a solid
              way to dynamically link and share
              routines at runtime. ISRs and TSRs were
              created for this purpose, but they were not
              formally sanctioned by Microsoft and
              often caused conflict.

              Windows, on the other hand, uses DLLs as
              a standard way of linking and sharing
              functionality. There are many DLLs that
              come with Windows that applications
              depend on and call for as required (look
              in your \WINDOWS\SYSTEM directory
              for the .DLL extension). Applications may
              also include their own DLLs, which are
              usually stored in the application directory.

--John

Camp David wrote:
> 
> When I want to link my object, I can choose between a filename with *.exe
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> compiling as (called) modules???
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Linking

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1999-02-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7aejnq$q0v$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<7a3jma$5s1@news3.euro.net> <36CA569D.F3457BC0@ibm.net>`

```
One correction ... in the DOS world, most dynamically
linked code was .EXE or .INT.

<36CA569D.F3457BC0@ibm.net>...
>Yes. DLL stands for Dynamic Link Library. It's a Windows thing.
>
…[37 more quoted lines elided]…
```

## Thread: pause execution for screen update — 3 msgs

### pause execution for screen update

- **From:** "don ferrario" <don@ferrario.com>
- **Date:** 1999-02-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7a4c30$h4j$1@news1.epix.net>`

```
PowerCobol again...

Subprogram is in a loop, possibly reading through
thousands of records in a search.  Results are to be
written to screen elements as the search finds them.

PROBLEM:  So long as the subprogram is executing,
the screen is not updated.  The results are not shown
until the search is totally completed.

QUESTION:  Is there something analagous to a PAUSE
statement, that will interrupt the loop, allow the display to
be updated, and then return to the loop?  The PAUSE
could be executed every so-many interations of the loop,
as desired.

I remember from my limited exposure to VisualBasic there was
something like this.  It was important because the program
wouldn't check for any other events until the loop was finished.

A typical example of the need would be to offer the user a "cancel"
button to end the search before completion, if needed.

don ferrario
http://www.ferrario.com/don
```

#### ↳ Re: pause execution for screen update

- **From:** "John Hicks (Remove \\"nospam\\" from address before replying)" <johnhicks@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 1999-02-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36CA5A2A.2C558F78@ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<7a4c30$h4j$1@news1.epix.net>`

```
A don't know about PowerCobol, but the general way is to periodically
call your operating system to check for keyboard input. You probably
don't want to do this with each iteration of your loop since such calls
add a lot of overhead, so you can set up a counter and do a call every
100 or 1000 iterations. The MS-DOS call is Int 21h AH=0Bh. Your compiler
probably has a standard call for all operating systems.

--John

don ferrario wrote:
> 
> PowerCobol again...
…[23 more quoted lines elided]…
> http://www.ferrario.com/don
```

#### ↳ Re: pause execution for screen update

- **From:** "Gary Roush" <gkr@adtools.com>
- **Date:** 1999-02-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7adurf$rmt$1@remarQ.com>`
- **References:** `<7a4c30$h4j$1@news1.epix.net>`

```
Don,

If you're using Version 4 of PowerCOBOL, there is a "timer" feature that you
can incorporate to your form.  You can then tell this timer what you want to
use the time for and when.  Works quite well.   For example, you want the
timer to check something every ten seconds. You install the timer, and then
insert code that you want to be done. Use the code there to do some checking
and then return control back to what you're doing.

Gary Roush
Fujitsu COBOL support

don ferrario wrote in message <7a4c30$h4j$1@news1.epix.net>...
```

## Thread: Brick wall with my Calc feilds — 4 msgs

### Brick wall with my Calc feilds

- **From:** <laurabrook@mindspring.com>
- **Date:** 1999-02-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7a5aup$nr6$1@camel29.mindspring.com>`

```
Thanks in advance,
  First time trying to manipulate data and don`t understand the errors when
trying to run this program. I`ve tried to make this copy syntax error free
but a few might exist ...though thats not the prob. due to the fact that
this is not the compiled copy. When I run this program my calculate lines
have the error : Data decimal error...  I can only assume the working
storage fields are not coded correctly but with my lack of experience with
run-errors despite many hours of book study I cant deduce whats wrong.
       Thanks again
               Brook.

       IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
       PROGRAM-ID. EBF70.
       DATE-WRITTEN. FEBRUARY 9, 1999
       DATE-COMPILED.  .
      *
      * *       *       *       *       *       *       *       *       *
      *
      *  PURPOSE: THIS PROGRAM PRINTS CUSTOMERS CURRENT BALANCE         *
      *
      *  INPUT: INPUT FILE IS RECFILE                                   *
      *
      *  OUTPUT: OUTPUT TO PRINTER                                      *
      *
      *  PROCESSING: CALCULATES NET CURRENT CHARGES AND CURRENT BALANCE *
      *                                                                 *
      * *       *       *       *       *       *       *       *       *
       ENVIRONMENT DIVISION.
       CONFIGURATION SECTION.
       SOURCE-COMPUTER. AS400.
       OBJECT-COMPUTER. AS400.
       INPUT-OUTPUT SECTION.
       FILE-CONTROL.
    SELECT ACCOUNT-FILE
        ASSIGN TO DISK-RECFILE.
    SELECT REPORT-FILE
        ASSIGN TO PRINTER-QPRINT.
      *
       DATA DIVISION.
       FILE SECTION.
       FD  ACCOUNT-FILE.
       01  ACCOUNT-REC                    PIC X(101).
       FD  REPORT-FILE.
       01  REPORT-REC                      PIC X(132).
      *
       WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
       01  WS-EOF-FLAG                     PIC X.
      *
       01  TOT-PAY-CR                       PIC S999999V99.
       01  WS-NET-CHARG                 PIC S999999V99.
      *
       01  ACCOUNT-REC.
    05  CUSTOMER DATA.
        10  CUS-NUM                           PIC X(5).
        10  CUS-NAME                         PIC X(18).
        10  CUS-STREET                      PIC X(16).
        10  CUS-CITY                           PIC X(14).
        10  CUS-STATE                        PIC XX.
        10  CUS-ZIP                             PIC X(5).
        10  CUS-CODE                         PIC X.
    05  SALESPERSON DATA.
        10  EMP-NUM                          PIC XXXX.
        10  EMP-REG                          PIC XX.
    05  ACCOUNT DATA.
        10  PRE-BAL                           PIC S999999V99.
        10  CUR-PUR                          PIC S999999V99.
        10  CUR-PAY                          PIC S999999V99.
        10  CUR-CR                            PIC S999999V99.
    05  PAY-DIS-CODE                     PIC X.
    05  CR-RATE-CODE                    PIC X.
      *
       01  DETAIL-LINE.
    05  FILLER                                 PIC X(3).
    05  RT-CUS-NUM                        PIC X(5).
    05  FILLER                                 PIC X(3).
    05  RT-CUS-NAME                     PIC X(18).
    05  FILLER                                 PIC X(3).
    05  RT-PRE-BAL                         PIC 999,999.99CR.
    05  FILLER                                 PIC X(3).
    05  RT-NET-CHARG                   PIC 999,999.99CR.
    05  FILLER                                 PIC XXX.
    05  RT-CUR-BAL                        PIC 999,999.99CR.
    05  FILLER                                 PIC X(59).
      *
       PROCEDURE DIVISION.
      *
       100-MAIN-ROUTINE.
        PERFORM 200-INIT
        PERFORM 500-MAIN-LOOP
     UNTIL WS-EOF-FLAG = "Y"
        PERFORM 700-EOJ
        STOP RUN.
      *
       200-INIT.
        MOVE "N" TO WS-EOF-FLAG
        PERFORM 300-OPEN
        PERFORM 400-READ.
      *
       300-OPEN.
  OPEN INPUT ACCOUNT-FILE
  OUTPUT REPORT-FILE.
      *
       400-READ.
        READ ACCOUNT-FILE INTO REPORT-REC
     AT END MOVE "Y" TO WS-EOF-FLAG
        END-READ.
      *
       500-MAIN-LOOP.
        PERFORM 900-CALC
        PERFORM 600-WRITE
        PERFORM 400-READ.
      *
       600-WRITE.
        MOVE CUS-NUM TO RT-CUS-NUM
        MOVE CUS-NAME TO RT-CUS-NAME
        MOVE PRE-BAL TO RT-PRE-BAL
        MOVE WS-NET-CHARG TO WS-NET-CHARG
        WRITE REPORT-REC FROM DETAIL-LINE.
      *
       700-EOJ.
        MOVE SPACES TO REPORT-REC
        MOVE "BROOK FULTON" TO REPORT-REC
        WRITE REPORT-REC
        CLOSE ACCOUNT-FILE , REPORT-FILE.
       900-CALC.
        COMPUTE TOT-PAY-CR = CUR-PAY + CUR-CR
        COMPUTE WS-NET-CHARG = CUR-PUR - TOT-PAY-CR
        COMPUTE RT-CUR-BAL = PRE-BAL + WS-NET-CHARG.
```

#### ↳ Re: Brick wall with my Calc feilds

- **From:** cwestbury@giant.intranet.com (Chris Westbury)
- **Date:** 1999-02-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1999Feb13.221816.19540@giant>`
- **References:** `<7a5aup$nr6$1@camel29.mindspring.com>`

```
In article <7a5aup$nr6$1@camel29.mindspring.com>,
<laurabrook@mindspring.com> wrote:
>
>     FD  ACCOUNT-FILE.
>     01  ACCOUNT-REC                    PIC X(101).
[...]
>     01  ACCOUNT-REC.

You've got two ACCOUNT-RECs ...


[...]
>     READ ACCOUNT-FILE INTO REPORT-REC

... and you aren't using either of them, so your data is uninitialized.

One way to fix this is to delete the 01-level after the FD and replace it
with the entire ACCOUNT-REC from WORKING-STORAGE.  Then READ ACCOUNT-FILE
without the INTO phrase.
```

- **References:** `<7a5aup$nr6$1@camel29.mindspring.com> <1999Feb13.221816.19540@giant>`

## Thread: MS-COBOL v3.x manuals? — 3 msgs

### MS-COBOL v3.x manuals?

- **From:** "Ralph Wade Phillips" <ralphp@techie.com>
- **Date:** 1999-02-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7a456b$aqv$1@blackhole.shreve.net>`

```
Does anyone out there have a set of the manuals - or at least the switch
settings - for MS Cobol 3.x?  I'd like to be able to automate some of the
compiling I'll be doing.

I'd also like to know how to run the REBUILD to pull out the data
dictionaries for the ISAM files, and to rebuild the current ones (there's
not been a rebuild for at least 5 years - I'll bet that the main 45meg file
is actually more like 15megs in size ... )

RwP
```

#### ↳ Re: MS-COBOL v3.x manuals?

- **From:** "Leif Svalgaard" <lsvalg@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 1999-02-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36c628f1@news3.us.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<7a456b$aqv$1@blackhole.shreve.net>`

```

Ralph Wade Phillips wrote in message <7a456b$aqv$1@blackhole.shreve.net>...
>Does anyone out there have a set of the manuals - or at least the switch
>settings - for MS Cobol 3.x?  I'd like to be able to automate some of the
>compiling I'll be doing.
>


the bat file below is what I use.
You will have to change directories to fit your setup.

@echo off
if [%1] == [] goto help
if exist %1.cob goto compile
echo %1.cob not found
goto end

:compile
echo Compiling %1...
if exist %1.err del %1.err > nul
debug %mf%\bin\cob.exe %1.cob,%1.obj,nul,nul
use"%etkdir%\d.dir"<%etkdir%\q>nul
if errorlevel 1  goto compile_error
if not exist %1.obj goto compile_error

link %2 %1,,,coblib+cobapi,,; > nul
copy %1.map cobol.map > nul
del %1.map > nul
del %1.obj > nul
if [%1] == [pmenu]  call dosx pmenu
if [%1] == [apgen]  call dosx apgen
if [%1] == [signon] call dosx signon
call $exes
goto end

:help
echo  COBOL program
echo  Compiles and creates an .EXE module from
echo  a source program file.  If there are any
echo  errors a file is left with extension .ERR
goto end

:compile_error
debug %mf%\bin\cob.exe %1.cob,%1.obj,n,n
use"%etkdir%\d.dir"<%etkdir%\q>%1.err
debug %mf%\bin\cob.exe %1.cob,%1.obj,n,n use"%etkdir%\d.dir"<%etkdir%\q
if exist %1.int del %1.int > nul

:end


The d.dir file with my options follows:


01SHUFFLE
ALIGN"4"
NOALTER
NOBOUND
NOCHECKDIV
FASTLINK
FLAG-CHIP
KEYCOMPRESS"5"
IDXFORMAT"4"
REMOVE"PROMPT" "COMP"
OPT"1"
OPTSIZE
OVERRIDE"COMP-5" = "COMP"
NOQUAL
NOQUERY
NOTRICKLE
NOLIST
NOSMALLDD
NOTRUNC
RTNCODE-SIZE"4"
TARGET"386-16"
```

#### ↳ Re: MS-COBOL v3.x manuals?

- **From:** bwspoor@fridaycs.win-uk.net (Brian W Spoor)
- **Date:** 1999-02-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<95@fridaycs.win-uk.net>`
- **References:** `<7a456b$aqv$1@blackhole.shreve.net>`

```
 
In article <7a456b$aqv$1@blackhole.shreve.net>, "Ralph Wade Phillips" (ralphp@techie.com) writes:
>Does anyone out there have a set of the manuals - or at least the switch
>settings - for MS Cobol 3.x?  I'd like to be able to automate some of the
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>
What do you specifically want to know?

I have the manuals and compiler here - still in use for 1 system.

To rebuild a data file (.DAT & .IDX) where both files are in tact,
just type REBUILD filename.DAT - take backup first. 

REBUILD has various switches to recreate/modify the index file, but
if this is in tact will use the key information form the .IDX..  All
rebuild does is to create a new clean index file - it does not tidy
the .DAT portion. 

The easiest way that I find to do this is to write a short program
to read the indexed file sequentially and write all valid records
to a sequential work file, clease and re-open the index file in
output mode and write the records back from the sequential file -
this will clean and tidy both the data and index parts. 

To do this, you do need the file layouts, but I don't know of any
other way to tidy/compress the .DAT part. 

COBOL is supposed to reuse deleted record gaps, rather tham just
keep adding to the end of the file, but I don't know how well this
works in practice. 

Brian


-----------------------------------------------------------
Brian W Spoor MBCS
Chartered Information Systems Practitioner
Friday Computer Services          Phone: +44-(0)1803 852625
bwspoor@fridaycs.co.uk            Fax:   +44-(0)1803 854926
-----------------------------------------------------------
```

<a id="t-d2d24377fd"></a>
## Thread: CEEGMTO Callable Service — 3 msgs

### CEEGMTO Callable Service

- **From:** "WOB" <wobconsult@sprynet.com>
- **Date:** 1999-02-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7a4ftp$3u6$1@juliana.sprynet.com>`

```
According to the LE manual, in order for routine CEEGMT to return the
current GMT Date-Time, the System-Time MUST be set to GMT (A/K/A Standard
Epoch). However, not all shops follow this rule.

Because of this inconsistency, I chose to use CEEGMTO, which will pass back
the GMT Hours/Minutes Offset to Local Time. If you generate the ASSEMBLER
expansion for a FUNCTION CURRENT-DATE, you'll see a BALR to CEEGMTO, which
returns this offset in bytes 18-21, with byte-18 equal to a '-' or a '+',
followed by 'HHMM'.

I can successfully call CEEGMTO, get back a binary-zero (normal)
feedback-list, but the hours and minutes returned are binary-zeros. BTW, I
still get the same results after issuing a CURRENT-DATE, which I would
expect.

Does anyone know if the rule for CEEGMT regarding system time in GMT also
applies to CEEGMTO?

I know that when attempting to use the TIME Macro requesting GMT, a special
PDS member SYS1.PARMLIB(CLOCKxx), must be set to the proper GMT Offset to
Local. I wonder if CEEGMTO uses this same animal?

Any help would be appreciated.

TIA....

Cheers,

WOB,
Atlanta
```

#### ↳ Re: CEEGMTO Callable Service

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-02-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7a4v7p$t67@dfw-ixnews11.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<7a4ftp$3u6$1@juliana.sprynet.com>`

```
Ok,  If the intrinsic function CURRENT-DATE is showing the correct offset
from GMT and the CEEGMTO callable service is NOT, then my suggestion is to
use the intrinsic function to get the information.

I can think of one environment (that may be yours) that would explain the
need to use the callable service.  IBM documents that you can dynamically
call the LE date/time services from VS COBOL II compiled programs - when
running under LE.  Is this your environment?  If so, (and I assume you do
have the COBOL for this-and-that compilers - or otherwise you wouldn't know
that the intrinsic function is giving the correct answer), I would suggest
writing your own MINIMAL subprogram that just gets the offset via the
intrinsic function, compile this with the new compiler, and call it from
your VS COBOL II program.

P.S.  I have sent a note to my "usually reliable" LE source to try and find
out why/when CEEGMTO gets a different answer than FUNCTION CURRENT-DATE -
and I will pass it on, once I get it.
WOB wrote in message <7a4ftp$3u6$1@juliana.sprynet.com>...
>According to the LE manual, in order for routine CEEGMT to return the
>current GMT Date-Time, the System-Time MUST be set to GMT (A/K/A Standard
…[29 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

#### ↳ Re: CEEGMTO Callable Service

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-02-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7a50bn$6dc@sjx-ixn5.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<7a4ftp$3u6$1@juliana.sprynet.com>`

```
From my "usually reliable" LE source,

"Bill,

  The rule about how to set the time on an MVS system is
the same for all LE and host COBOL products:  If you set
the system date/time to local time, then you will get
incorrect results.  If the user gets 0 back from GMTO
then his system is not set correctly, or he is in Greenwich,
England.  He will get the same result from GMTO as for the
offset field in his CURRENT-DATE function results.  It was
not clear to me by the post that he got non-zero from FUNCTION
CURRENT-DATE.  The offset only reports hours and minutes,
not seconds, by the way.  We have had complains about this,
for some reason!"


WOB wrote in message <7a4ftp$3u6$1@juliana.sprynet.com>...

>current GMT Date-Time, the System-Time MUST be set to GMT (A/K/A Standard
```

## Thread: I search a cheap Micro Focus Workbench 4.x — 1 msgs

<a id="t-6409f51714"></a>
## Thread: event driven, execute with no return — 1 msgs

### event driven, execute with no return

- **From:** "don ferrario" <don@ferrario.com>
- **Date:** 1999-02-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7a4bpm$gr0$1@news1.epix.net>`

```
I am working with Fujitsu PowerCobol, which like some others
is an event driven cobol system.

When a program statement (such as changing focus to a control)
generates an event, an event program is begun.

When the event program ends, flow returns to the original point,
as expected.

PROBLEM:  I don't always want the program flow to return.  Depending
on the user's actions, these returns can stack up, and the flow is sometimes
not as desired.

Is there a statement, within a subprogram, which can be issued which
will cancel all previous  waiting subprograms?  This almost sounds
like the old ALTER statement, but not quite.  CANCEL comes to mind,
but so far as I know, you can only CANCEL programs under the current
one, not programs executing *above* the current program.

This is becoming quite a problem for me, because depending on how
the user interacts with the program, it is almost impossible to code
anticipating
all the possible program return-flows.

don ferrario
http://www.ferrario.com/don
```

---

<a id="t-2fea5cc428"></a>
## Thread: * Please read and comment — 94 msgs

### * Please read and comment

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-02-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7a7s8e$34s@dfw-ixnews10.ix.netcom.com>`

```
(Posted to the COBOL Usenet Newsgroups - but with CC's to various
individuals.)

Preface:
  For those who are regular readers of the COBOL newsgroups, you are aware
that I think that there are lots AND LOTS of great new  features in the
draft of the next COBOL Standard.  However, you are also aware that I have a
distinct reputation among the Standards committees for "throwing monkey
wrenches" into the works and never letting something that I disagree with
"drop" - even when all the "experts tell me I am wrong".  Given this
preface, I would like input from the readers (and if appropriate appreciate
these readers sending their comments PRO or CON to the appropriate places).

ISSUE:
  The following is a direct quote from the CD 1.4 draft of the next COBOL
Standard. (page 4)

"3.1.5 Processor-dependent language elements
     ...
The decision of whether to claim support for a processor-dependent component
is within an implementor's discretion.  Factors that may be considered
include, but are not limited to, hardware capability, software capability,
and market positioning of the processor. ..."

Some of the items in this list (which - historically - is a follow-on to the
ANSI'85 Hardware Dependent list) include (but are not limited to):

    - USAGEs BINARY, floating-point, and PACKED-DECIMAL
    - The DELETE and REWRITE statements
    - I-O and EXTEND mode on an OPEN statement
    - BEFORE/AFTER ADVANCING on WRITE
    - READ/START PREVIOUS
    - SCREEN SECTION ACCEPT/DISPLAY statements
    - Record Locking and File Sharing

****

Now, in my opinion,  the one thing that COBOL has going for it over other
popular languages (like C and C++) is its portability.  I am really REALLY
concerned that allowing vendors to decide to include (or EXCLUDE) support
for these old AND NEW features based on "market positioning" or ANY THING
ELSE THEY WANT (hence, the "not limited to" phrase in the definition) is the
worst possible thing to happen to COBOL.  In fact, it is so bad, that I
question (I haven't made up my mind completely on this yet) that having a
Standard with this definition is WORSE than having no Standard at all
(which - after all - is basically what we have at the current time - when
there is no way to get amendments or even interpretations done on the '85
Standard - and there are no  conformance tests to validate implementations).

What do you think?

Please respond in comp.lang.cobol or via private email to me.  However, no
matter which way you feel (agree with me or disagree with me), if you have
strong feelings about this, PLEASE, let the Standards committees know.

If you are in the US (US resident, work for a US company, etc), then you can
send any comments for consideration by the US delegation to the ISO WG4
committee to

     das@microfocus.com

(These can include indications that you think this feature would be bad/good
in an ISO Standard or that the ANSI Standard should be different from the
ISO Standard - if the ISO Standard includes this definition.)

If you are in a country other than the US (and I really hope some other
countries WILL provide their input on this topic),  you can find out HOW to
reach your country's delegation by contacting the WG4 convenor at

    nwallace@us.ibm.com

or you can try to find it via the WG4 home page at

    http://anubis.dkuug.dk/jtc1/sc22/wg4/
```

```
Bill:

standard features in a specific compiler won't necessarily compile
using a different compiler.
```

#### ↳ Re: * Please read and comment

- **From:** "Chris Osborne" <chris_n_osborne@yahoo.com>
- **Date:** 1999-02-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36c84ce6.0@news3.uswest.net>`
- **References:** `<7a7s8e$34s@dfw-ixnews10.ix.netcom.com>`

```
William Klein,

At the risk of sounding like a defeatist, I think the antics of the
Standards committee are going to hurt COBOL. I do not predict the doom of
COBOL by any means, or even a downturn in its use. But instead of getting in
gear and positioning COBOL as an attractive and modern programming language,
the Standards Committee seems to vote in a direction implying language
integrity is not the issue, but rather convenience of vendor implementation
is.

COBOL's huge installed base is and will shield it from this sort of thing.
However, it doesn't take a genius to look at language manual and see '85 on
the last standard. Even with the '89 addendum, that is still older than
Fortran's last standard.

A ton of stuff in, say C++, is vendor-determined, but that doesn't seem to
prevent their standards committee from moving forward and releasing a
standard.

Dragging the next Standard farther and farther looks, well, bad. IMO.

To take C++ as a an example again, it has a number of standard header files
included with the language.  Many are necessary for simply language
functionality, however, a few carry information on the vendor-defined values
for the specific implementation.  Too bad COBOL can't come up with some
standard ways to include vendor-specific information directly in the
language.  It wouldn't even require a compiler re-write, just have a
language mandated copybook library that contains a limited number of
predefined data items.  The names and values would clearly show the
attributes of such things as Bytes-in-Halfword-Binary,
Bytes-in-Fullword-Binary, Max-Unsigned-Fullword-Binary,
Max-Signed-Fullword-Binary, etc.  Simply glancing through the copybook would
tell a prospective programmer everything they needed to know.

COBOL is my language of choice, and I would like to see it supported more
vigorously by the most prominent authority over it, the Standards Committee.
What form do I feel such support should take? Stop arguing over
implementation issues. Start agreeing on language integrity. Publish a
Standard. Now.
```

#### ↳ Re: * Please read and comment

- **From:** paulr@bix.com (paulr)

```
William M. Klein (wmklein@ix.netcom.com) wrote:

:     - USAGEs BINARY, floating-point, and PACKED-DECIMAL
   THis one I don't care about all that much. The compiler should
figure it out, in my opinion, rather than the programmer. It is a move
towards strong typing in the language. 

:     - The DELETE and REWRITE statements
:     - I-O and EXTEND mode on an OPEN statement
   These two seem to be utterly necessary to me, unless you are using 
a database I suppose. What do they propose to replace them?

:     - BEFORE/AFTER ADVANCING on WRITE
:     - READ/START PREVIOUS
   Read previous is handy, and the before /after advancing makes non-cics
programming nice. Again, what do they propose to replace them with? 

:     - SCREEN SECTION ACCEPT/DISPLAY statements
   Oh my, this I could do without. Nothing but trouble in my opinion. 
Every vendor does it differently, on purpose I believe, in an attempt
to lock in customers. Bob Wolfe & crew got it right, and so did IBM
with Visual Age and Visual Age COBOL. 


:     - Record Locking and File Sharing
  Again, what in the world do they plan to replace this with? 


Is there some strong political motion in the background to cripple the 
language, or what?
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: * Please read and comment

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-02-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7a84ru$gq@dfw-ixnews7.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<7a7s8e$34s@dfw-ixnews10.ix.netcom.com> <7a7uth$1ih@lotho.delphi.com>`

```
No one (as far as I can figure out) is saying that some vendors will or will
not "replace" these Standard features with something else. What they are
trying to do (again in my interpretation) is:

A) Make it optional for the vendors to implement them at all

B) Let them implement totally NON-standard "features" if they want - instead
of what the Standard defines.  (For example, IBM could keep COMP-1 and
COMP-2 as floating point and NEVER add the new portable floating-point data
types.)

Note: There is a BIG difference between what the '85 Standard says and what
the draft says about USAGE BINARY (just an example).  The '85 Standard says
that every COBOL implementation needs to include this syntax - but how they
"store" the data is hardware/software dependent.  What the draft Standard
says is that if the vendor doesn't want to even support this USAGE, they
don't even need to include support for the syntax - they can say AT THEIR
OWN DISCRETION that this is not part of the "market position - or something
else" for their processor and simply not support the syntax or semantics.

AGAIN, please do send notes to the committee people if you think this is
"bad" - don't just tell me.


paulr wrote in message <7a7uth$1ih@lotho.delphi.com>...
>William M. Klein (wmklein@ix.netcom.com) wrote:
>
…[29 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```
