```
Binyamin Dissen wrote in message
<36cd138e.13865630@news.netvision.net.il>...
>Error message is quite clear. You can only write a record. You can not
write
>anything else.


It rather astonished me the number of beginners that
will believe a programmer, but will not believe a compiler
when it tells them the same thing.

Compiler: you cannot use edited fields in arithmetic
Student: What is wrong with this program?
Programmer:you cannot use edit fields in arithmetic
Student: Oh! (goes off and fixes it)
Compiler: you can only write records
Student: What is wrong with this program?
Programmer:you can only write records
Student:Oh! (goes off and fixes it)

Can't these kids read?  Maybe they are becoming so
used to Windows95 telling them stupid things six times per
second that they automatically dismiss error messages ...
```

## Thread: coblib problem under Windows NT 4.0 — 2 msgs

### coblib problem under Windows NT 4.0

- **From:** "Sanya" <bluesyst@mail.matav.hu>
- **Date:** 1999-02-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7a9dfm$g5i$1@bolka.matav.net>`

```
We like to run our DOS based cobol applications under Win* eg. Win 95,98,NT.
There were no problems on Win95 and 98. But when we tried it under NT the
program said: Cobol run time library not installed. We sure that coblib.dle
is in the application's directory or in the path and the cobdir variable has
been set previously.
Strange that all of the 16 bits programs can run without any problems just
our applications can't 8-((. If you have any idea, please share me.
I'd like to ask you, that mail to my private mailbox too:
bluesyst@mail.matav.hu


TIA and best regards., Sandor Feher from Hungary
```

#### ↳ Re: coblib problem under Windows NT 4.0

- **From:** "Sven" <informatica@freemail.nl>
- **Date:** 1999-02-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7aa53m$b0k$1@xenon.inbe.net>`
- **References:** `<7a9dfm$g5i$1@bolka.matav.net>`

```
Hi,

I'm wondering where you did put the declaration of set coblib= ??? I hope
not in autoexec.bat, because NT does not use that file.

I hope not in autoexec.nt, because NT usually forgets about it to.

The only correct place to declare them is in Control Panel, System,
Environment.  You then have two options.  You can put it under System
variables, so that every user has them, or only under the current logged on
user.

With kind regards,

Sven.
```

## Thread: event driven, execute with no return — 1 msgs

### Re: event driven, execute with no return

- **From:** paulr@bix.com (paulr)
- **Date:** 1999-02-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7a7ta0$1ih@lotho.delphi.com>`
- **References:** `<7a4bpm$gr0$1@news1.epix.net> <36c75626.0@mercury.nildram.co.uk>`

```

Ah- the concept of OS Semaphores makes it to COBOL at last... :) 

<grin>


Simon Cordingley (simonc@casegen.co.uk) wrote:
: I have done something similar to this in a Casegen program - might be worth
: trying in PowerCobol.

: If I understand you correctly, you want to be able to cancel outstanding
: events from an event in your form.

: Create a WS-CANCEL variable with a 88 level - "CANCEL-EVENT". All
: cancellable events must now check the variable before processing:

: IF CANCEL-EVENT
:     GO TO event-EXIT
: END-IF

: normal event code....

: Make the variable EXTERNAL if you have non-modal dialogs to allow for
: changing events accross multiple forms.

: Don't forget, there will have to be code in a non-cancellable event to
: ENABLE the events again!

: Hope this may help.

: Simon Cordingley
: Casegen Systems Ltd
: www.casegen.co.uk

: don ferrario wrote in message <7a4bpm$gr0$1@news1.epix.net>...
: >I am working with Fujitsu PowerCobol, which like some others
: >is an event driven cobol system.
: >
: >When a program statement (such as changing focus to a control)
: >generates an event, an event program is begun.
: >
: >When the event program ends, flow returns to the original point,
: >as expected.
: >
: >PROBLEM:  I don't always want the program flow to return.  Depending
: >on the user's actions, these returns can stack up, and the flow is
: sometimes
: >not as desired.
: >
: >Is there a statement, within a subprogram, which can be issued which
: >will cancel all previous  waiting subprograms?  This almost sounds
: >like the old ALTER statement, but not quite.  CANCEL comes to mind,
: >but so far as I know, you can only CANCEL programs under the current
: >one, not programs executing *above* the current program.
: >
: >This is becoming quite a problem for me, because depending on how
: >the user interacts with the program, it is almost impossible to code
: >anticipating
: >all the possible program return-flows.
: >
: >don ferrario
: >http://www.ferrario.com/don
: >
: >
```

<a id="t-36f5d99210"></a>
## Thread: Help: Variable Length Records--Special Case — 14 msgs

_Topics: [`VSAM, files, sorting`](topics.md#files) · [`Help requests and how-to`](topics.md#help)_

### Help: Variable Length Records--Special Case

- **From:** Andy Arnold <awarnold@cpcug.org>
- **Date:** 1999-02-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36CA34B7.E7D70FBE@cpcug.org>`

```
I need a new solution because my old one no longer works.

How do I access the LRECL with COBOL/MVS or COBOL/370 of a variable
length VSAM input file?

I've been doing COBOL since the early 70's so I do know a few tricks.  I
have frequently used negative indexing to get to the LRECL in the buffer
of a sequential file. But I've never needed to try VSAM and I've not
needed this trick since we moved from COBOL II to COBOL/MVS or 370 or
whatever IBM calls it now.  One of our programmers is trying to make
this work and she is getting a boundary error when she tries to run the
program.

Thanks in advance.
--AWA
```

#### ↳ Re: Help: Variable Length Records--Special Case

- **From:** Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>
- **Date:** 1999-02-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36CA9FFD.E5E5F9D8@zip.com.au>`
- **References:** `<36CA34B7.E7D70FBE@cpcug.org>`

```
Andy Arnold wrote:
> 
> I need a new solution because my old one no longer works.
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> --AWA

On the file statement there is a way to specify a working storage field
that is the record length.  It is something like 'record length is'. 
The field is best off being a PIC 9(04) COMp as this is what the RDW is.

The boundary error is probably because it is compile with SSRANGE
switched on.

Ken
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Help: Variable Length Records--Special Case

- **From:** "James King" <mangogwr@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1999-02-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<o7qA2.15018$OS5.14974959@news3.mia>`
- **References:** `<36CA34B7.E7D70FBE@cpcug.org> <36CA9FFD.E5E5F9D8@zip.com.au>`

```
You code this:

FD MYFILE
     record contains nn characters to nnn characters
     depending upon myfile-lrecl.
01  myrec10     pic x(10).
01  myrec20    pic x(20).
01  myrec200  pic x(200).

Working-storage section.
01  myfile-lrecl                     pic s9(04) comp.  (cannot be > 32768
  because then you have to use spanned records when then have a
limit of 32764 + 4 for the length (of last record))

Ken Foskey wrote in message <36CA9FFD.E5E5F9D8@zip.com.au>...
>Andy Arnold wrote:
>>
…[24 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Help: Variable Length Records--Special Case

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@inospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-02-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7atide$nj9@dfw-ixnews8.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<36CA34B7.E7D70FBE@cpcug.org> <36CA9FFD.E5E5F9D8@zip.com.au> <o7qA2.15018$OS5.14974959@news3.mia>`

```
Didn't I correct this last week?

The correct format of the ANSI'85 format FD that gives you back the length
of a variable length record is

   Record VARYING IN SIZE .... Depending on
      and not
   Record CONTAINS .... Depending on
```

#### ↳ Re: Help: Variable Length Records--Special Case

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@inospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-02-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7aep9g$4ud@dfw-ixnews3.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<36CA34B7.E7D70FBE@cpcug.org>`

```
In COBOL/for this-and-that (as with any ANSI '85 Standard compiler), it is
finally possible to get the length of a "variable length" record via
officially supported means.

In your FD code the

  Record varying in size depending on WS-item

phrase (rather than the older RECORD CONTAINS clause).

Then, after every READ, your WS-item will contain the length of the record
that was just read.  (You need to "update" it before a WRITE)
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Help: Variable Length Records--Special Case

- **From:** Roland Schiradin <schiradi@tap.de>
- **Date:** 1999-02-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36CB39DC.C89B5ED7@tap.de>`
- **References:** `<36CA34B7.E7D70FBE@cpcug.org> <7aep9g$4ud@dfw-ixnews3.ix.netcom.com>`

```
Bill,


> Then, after every READ, your WS-item will contain the length of the record
> that was just read.  (You need to "update" it before a WRITE)

let me extend your append a little bit, because it was my first producation
problem
early in the 80.

If you have a record structure like
01 Record.
     05 Rec-1    pic x(10).
     05 Len-1    pic s9(3) packed-decimal.
     05 Field     pic x occurs 1 to 500 depending on len-1-

Make sure that you move 500 to len-1 before moving somethink to
Field.

A very good old system-programmer help me to fix this . I would
say this was only nice (non-arrogant) system programmer I have seen
this days (1983).


roland
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Help: Variable Length Records--Special Case

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@inospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-02-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7aff55$qsd@dfw-ixnews4.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<36CA34B7.E7D70FBE@cpcug.org> <7aep9g$4ud@dfw-ixnews3.ix.netcom.com> <36CB39DC.C89B5ED7@tap.de>`

```
Roland Schiradin wrote in message <36CB39DC.C89B5ED7@tap.de>...
>Bill,
>
>
>> Then, after every READ, your WS-item will contain the length of the
record
>> that was just read.  (You need to "update" it before a WRITE)
>
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Ok, let's clear up two related issues - that may have been muddled by now.
Assume you have an FD with

FD MyFile
     Record Varying in Size from 10 to 100
       Depending on WS-Num1.
01  MyRec.
    05    Fixed-Part   Pic X(08).
    05    FD-Num2      Pic 9(02)
    05    Tabl.
             10 Each-Elem Occurs 1 to 90
                    Depending on FD-Num2.
                15  Elem    Pic X.

Later in Working-Storage you have
  05    WS-Num1  Pic  9(03).

****

OK,
  A few things to think about:

1) The item in the RECORD VARYING clause *must* be Working-Storage.

2) The item in the OCCURS clause can be in the FD or in Working-Storage.
(Traditionally, it has been in the file itself - but I could imagine a
"strange" application where it was in WS.  Writing such records would be
pretty easy, reading them would be quite a challenge)

3) The item in the OCCURS clause should be "updated" before you try to build
the record (which itself is before a WRITE)

4) The item in the RECORD VARYING clause doesn't need to be updated until
just before you do the WRITE statement (you can build the record without
updating it)

5) The item in the OCCURS clause needs to be "created" by the application
logic (before a WRITE) and you can treat it like a "normal" part of the
external record when you READ the record.

6) The item in the RECORD VARYING clause is sort-of a "magic" field in that
it gets updated by the system when you read a record (and need not actually
be stored anywhere in the file).

  ****

I hope this clears up some of the differences between these two related but
not identical functions of ANSI'85 Standard COBOL.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Help: Variable Length Records--Special Case

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Roland Schiradin <schiradi@tap.de>
- **Date:** 1999-02-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36CB4538.41E99ACE@tap.de>`


#### ↳ Re: Help: Variable Length Records--Special Case

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 1999-02-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7aeop2$qm$1@news-1.news.gte.net>`
- **References:** `<36CA34B7.E7D70FBE@cpcug.org>`

```

Andy Arnold wrote in message <36CA34B7.E7D70FBE@cpcug.org>...
>I need a new solution because my old one no longer works.
>
>How do I access the LRECL with COBOL/MVS or COBOL/370 of a variable
>length VSAM input file?
>
  Won't you get the LRECL if you make the FD something like:

RECORD CONTAINS 1 to 1000 CHARACTERS DEPENDING ON Foo-Length.

When you do the READ, foo-length should have what you need. (That will be
the COBOL length; add 4 for JCL length if RECFM = V[B])

MCM
```

```
Just because 2 of us posted with conflicting information,  I am really quite
certain that this needs to be a RECORD VARYING rather than a RECORD CONTAINS
clause - to get the record size back in a field.
```

```
Andy Arnold (awarnold@cpcug.org) wrote:

: One of our programmers is trying to make this work and 
: she is getting a boundary error when she tries to run the
: program.

I know your question is about VSAM but the shop I'm 
currently working in had SOC4 abends when going to COBOL
for MVS for *sequential* variable length files.  The solution
was to add some loadlibs (probably installation specific),
change the JCL on the DD statement, and change the FD statement
in the source code.

Regards, Charla
```

```
Try 
 www.infoseek.com
search for

on WEB pages
 1297 hits

take a while to discover relevant material)

you can use the radio buttons to do searches within current results say
   COBOL within the T27 results
or
   UNISYS within the T27 results
or 
   emul* within the T27 results

During the above experimentation you will actually be within the Web Site
search capability. You will need to back all the way out to do the following,
or just refetch www.infoseek.com.

You can use the pull down menu to search Newsgroups for, say, T27.  No doubt
there is useful info there but in small amounts. Such posting can also help you
contact people who have published a common interest, and who are currently
involved in that area.

Also, if you have not already done so, you may wish to use the dejanews search
engine. From your post and the subject it does not seem like you are new at
this, but in case you are, note that most news presentation software is only
showing you the most recent postings. You may not be aware therefore of
previous posts in the compl.sys.unisys group to which you have cross posted. 
Using dejanews search engine you can go back a long ways, and probably the
inquiry has useful responses already (don't take offense if all that is already
known to you, just trying to kick start the engine if not rolling already).

You might also use the search engines to find Unisys websites. The email
contacts can possibly get you some technical documents, sample programs or
leads to download sites.

Best Wishes,

Robert Rayhawk
RKRayhawk@aol.com
```

## Thread: move to field "justified LEFT" — 20 this month / 25 total

### move to field "justified LEFT"

- **From:** "don ferrario" <don@ferrario.com>
- **Date:** 1999-02-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7adgdp$fpo$1@news1.epix.net>`

```
I'm beating myself up - there has to be a simple way...

variable-A is described as PIC X(10).
     Records in the file contain text which is right-justified.
     There may or may not be leading spaces.
     There may or may not be spaces between the characters.


I need to move the contents to variable-B, which is PIC X(20).
    variable-B needs the data justified to the LEFT (normally default)

I need to squeeze out LEADING spaces (if any - some have none)
from variable-A during the move to variable-B.

For example:
                          variable-A  contains:      "    abc  def"
     after the move
                          variable-B contains:       "abc  def
"

I have tried INSPECTing variable-A, tallying leading spaces,
then using STRING with a POINTER to begin the after the leading
spaces.

I haven't gotten that to work consitently (probably don't have it coded
right),
but there has GOT to be a more elegant way to do this....

don ferrario
http://www.ferrario.com/don
```

- **Date:** 1999-02-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36CB97FE.A3AC0AA@san.rr.com>`
- **References:** `<7adgdp$fpo$1@news1.epix.net>`

```
ACUCOBOL has a routine to do this:

CALL ï¿½C$JUSTIFYï¿½ USING DATA-ITEM, JUSTIFY-TYPE

> I'm beating myself up - there has to be a simple way...
>
```

#### ↳ Re: move to field "justified LEFT"

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com>
- **Date:** 1999-02-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36CAED01.5ADB5E93@NOSPAMhome.com>`
- **References:** `<7adgdp$fpo$1@news1.epix.net>`

```
> For example:
>                           variable-A  contains:      "    abc  def"
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> spaces.

I don't know of a "best" way of doing this.  Your suggestion should have
worked, what were your results?  

The easiest thing to do is to use your INSPECT result to do a substring
move:
MOVE VARIABLE-A (start:) TO VARIABLE-B.

I have used the UNSTRING command to parse out words when I needed them,
but I think the easiest way is to redefine your variables as arrays of
characters.  

Then loop through, moving character by character.  This has the
advantage in that you could modify it to compress multiple spaces
between words or skip punctuation, should the specs change.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: move to field "justified LEFT"

- **From:** "Alan Russell" <RUSSELAH@apci.com>
- **Date:** 1999-02-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7aeu92$t1n@netnews1.apci.com>`
- **References:** `<7adgdp$fpo$1@news1.epix.net> <36CAED01.5ADB5E93@NOSPAMhome.com>`

```
We faced this problem a long time ago in our shop, so I wrote a relatively
short assembler subroutine which will left justifiy, right justify, or
center the contents of an field.  So instead of constantly reinventing the
wheel, we just insert
                CALL 'LEFT' USING field, field-length
(or RIGHT or CENTER) as appropriate.

Alan Russell, PhD, CCP
Work - Russelah@apci.com
Home - AHRussell@computer.org
Home page - http://members.aol.com/ahrussell
-------------------------------------------------------
The views expressed are mine alone and do not necessarily reflect those of
my employer
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: move to field "justified LEFT"

- **From:** "PAULO CRUZ" <scruz_paulo@mail.teleweb.pt>
- **Date:** 1999-02-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7afmod$m6c$2@fenix.maxitel.pt>`
- **References:** `<7adgdp$fpo$1@news1.epix.net> <36CAED01.5ADB5E93@NOSPAMhome.com> <7aeu92$t1n@netnews1.apci.com>`

```
Hi,

Alan Russell wrote<7aeu92$t1n@netnews1.apci.com>...
>We faced this problem a long time ago in our shop, so I wrote a relatively
>short assembler subroutine which will left justifiy, right justify, or
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>(or RIGHT or CENTER) as appropriate.


Since this is no Assembler n.g. and since reinventing the wheel has its own
value in a place so many learning people* are so often paying visit to, why
not posting some suggestions on how to...?

*) (and who is not learning anymore?)

Having a string of a given lenght and two variable subscripts, here's a way
of getting a left-justified string out of a right-justified one. The other
way round would similarly be easily achieved:

01 char-str pic x(20) value
   "           ola, la..".
01 sub-a pic 999 comp-3 value 0.
01 sub-b pic 999 comp-3 value 0.

if char-str not = spaces
   perform until char-str (1:1) not = space
      move 1 to sub-a
      perform varying sub-b from 2 by 1 until
         sub-b > 20
         move
         char-str(sub-b:1) to char-str(sub-a:1)
         add 1 to sub-a
      end-perform
      move space to char-str(sub-a:1)
   end-perform
else
   'non-valid input'
end-if

...and the 'thing' could also be made a callable object (oh, it lacks the
centering option!).

Rgds
P.Cruz

(snip)

>>> I have tried INSPECTing variable-A, tallying leading spaces,
>>> then using STRING with a POINTER to begin the after the leading
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: move to field "justified LEFT"

- **From:** Ed.Stevens@nmm.nissan-usa.com
- **Date:** 1999-02-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7ah6lu$9rf$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`
- **References:** `<7adgdp$fpo$1@news1.epix.net> <36CAED01.5ADB5E93@NOSPAMhome.com>`

```
In article <36CAED01.5ADB5E93@NOSPAMhome.com>,
  Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com> wrote:
> > For example:
> >                           variable-A  contains:      "    abc  def"
…[22 more quoted lines elided]…
>

With reference modification and in-line performs, there's no need to fool with
redefining the variable as an array of characters.

MOVE 1 TO SUB2
INSPECT SOURCE-STRING
   TALLYING SUB1 FOR LEADING SPACES
PERFORM VARYING SUB1 FROM SUB1 BY 1
        UNTIL SUB1 > LENGTH OF SOURCE-STRING
   IF SOURCE-STRING(SUB1:1).....
    .
    .    (whatever selective/bypassing logic you need)
    .
      MOVE SOURCE-STRING(SUB1:1) TO TARGET-STRING(SUB2:1)
   END-IF
END-PERFORM

Functionally no different than redefining the strings as arrays, but
eliminates the need for the redefinition itself. That, combined with the use
of the LENGTH OF function means the code self-adjusts if the length of
SOURCE-STRING ever changes.


Ed Stevens

-----------== Posted via Deja News, The Discussion Network ==----------
http://www.dejanews.com/       Search, Read, Discuss, or Start Your Own
```

- **From:** psychedelic-harry_SPAM_THIS_@mindless.com (Psychedelic Harry)
- **Date:** 1999-02-19T00:00:00

```
In article <7ah6lu$9rf$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>,
Ed.Stevens@nmm.nissan-usa.com wrote:

> In article <36CAED01.5ADB5E93@NOSPAMhome.com>,
>   Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com> wrote:
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
> END-PERFORM

In this case, why not the simple approach:

   01 field-a pic x(10) just right.
   01 field-b pic x(20) just left.
   01 sub1    pic s9(8) comp binary.
   ...
   inspect field-a tallying sub1 for leading ' '
   move field-a (sub1:length of field-a - sub1) to field-b
   ...
*** or even easier (but I cant remember if it is an extension)
   string 
     field-a delimited leading ' '
     into field-b
   end-string
```

```
> Yes but REMember the Length of is a COMPILE-TIME measurement.
>
<snip>

And your point is??  Yes, "LENGTH OF" is a compile time measurement, but the
length of the object to which it is applied is also fixed at compile time.

 01 my-string pic x(30).
 01 str-len   pic 9(4) comp.

      MOVE LENGTH OF MY-STRING TO STR-LEN.

*comment: the length of MY-STRING cannot be changed at run-time,
*         so it doesn't matter that "LENGTH OF" my string is fixed at
*         compile-* time.

Ed Stevens

-----------== Posted via Deja News, The Discussion Network ==----------
http://www.dejanews.com/       Search, Read, Discuss, or Start Your Own
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: move to field "justified LEFT"

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 1999-02-23T00:00:00

```

Ed.Stevens@nmm.nissan-usa.com wrote in message
<7ah6lu$9rf$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>...
[...]
>PERFORM VARYING SUB1 FROM SUB1 BY 1
>        UNTIL SUB1 > LENGTH OF SOURCE-STRING

LENGTH OF is an IBM extension. The original poster did not
specify the implementation.

FUNCTION LENGTH (SOURCE-STRING) would provide
ANSI 85 compatibility.
-------------------------------
Rick Smith
e-mail: < ricksmith@aiservices.com >
```

#### ↳ Re: move to field "justified LEFT"

- **From:** pduboisREMOVETHIS@enteract.com (PAL3000)
- **Date:** 1999-02-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36ca4f39.10583811@news.enteract.com>`
- **References:** `<7adgdp$fpo$1@news1.epix.net>`

```
Find the first non space character in the source field, then do a

	MOVE SOURCE-FLD (FIRST-NON-BLANK:) TO DEST-FLD.

When you use a subscript without a size as in (3:) it will move all
the characters starting with and after the subscript.

Paul

On Tue, 16 Feb 1999 23:27:48 -0500, "don ferrario" <don@ferrario.com>
wrote:

words REMOVETHIS from my email address
```

#### ↳ Re: move to field "justified LEFT"

- **From:** paulr@bix.com (paulr)

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7admso$mb8@lotho.delphi.com>`
- **References:** `<7adgdp$fpo$1@news1.epix.net>`

```

I dont have a manual sitting here so you will probably 
have to go check the syntax but here is the easy way...:) 

Inspect the-thing tallying space-counter for leading spaces
computer move-size = 10 - space-counter
move the-thing(space-counter:move-size)
  the-20-char-thing(0:move-size)       


Funny how often we run into the exact same problems isn't it?  
Someone isn't going to like the reference modification, but 
hey... so C me. :) 
-Paul


don ferrario (don@ferrario.com) wrote:
: I'm beating myself up - there has to be a simple way...

: variable-A is described as PIC X(10).
:      Records in the file contain text which is right-justified.
:      There may or may not be leading spaces.
:      There may or may not be spaces between the characters.


: I need to move the contents to variable-B, which is PIC X(20).
:     variable-B needs the data justified to the LEFT (normally default)

: I need to squeeze out LEADING spaces (if any - some have none)
: from variable-A during the move to variable-B.

: For example:
:                           variable-A  contains:      "    abc  def"
:      after the move
:                           variable-B contains:       "abc  def
: "

: I have tried INSPECTing variable-A, tallying leading spaces,
: then using STRING with a POINTER to begin the after the leading
: spaces.

: I haven't gotten that to work consitently (probably don't have it coded
: right),
: but there has GOT to be a more elegant way to do this....

: don ferrario
: http://www.ferrario.com/don
```

#### ↳ Re: move to field "justified LEFT"

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1999-02-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7aek21$qet$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<7adgdp$fpo$1@news1.epix.net>`

```
Unstring delimited by ALL " " will get rid of the spaces,
then re-string the same way.
IE:

01 data-in                picture x(25) value "THIS is TEXT with SPACE".
01 temp1                picture x(15).
01 temp2                picture x(15).
etc.
MOVE SPACE TO TEMP1 TEMP2 ....
UNSTRING DATA-IN DELIMITED BY ALL " "
INTO TEMP1 TEMP2 TEMP3 TEMP4 TEMP5 TEMP6.
MOVE SPACE TO DATA-IN.
STRING TEMP1 DELIMITED BY SPACE
                TEMP2 DELIMITED BY SPACE
                 ETC
                 INTO DATA-IN.

don ferrario wrote in message <7adgdp$fpo$1@news1.epix.net>...
>I'm beating myself up - there has to be a simple way...
>
…[30 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

```
Using COBOL sp2 with Acucobol will allow you to easily support
embedded Active X controls in a 32 bit Windows environment.  It's easy
to display the same Windows calendar to which you refer in your post

support.  Send me an e-mail and I'll be happy to describe it in more
detail.
```

## Thread: indexed files — 4 msgs

_Topics: [`VSAM, files, sorting`](topics.md#files)_

### indexed files

- **From:** smiley@izap.com (Smiley Mann)
- **Date:** 1999-02-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36c8e295.6674275@news.izap.com>`

```
dear friends,

i'm trying to create a program that takes input via the keyboard and
writes it to an indexed output file.  the program seems to take in all
the input i feed it, but when i try to display the file it shows only
4 records, when i inputted about 20.  what's going on?  where's the
other records?  why is it only taking in 4 records?  did i leave
something out?

well here's my source (don't mind the lack of structure)

       ID DIVISION.
       PROGRAM-ID. INPUT1.
       AUTHOR. ME.

       ENVIRONMENT DIVISION.
       INPUT-OUTPUT SECTION.
       FILE-CONTROL.

           SELECT OUTPUT-INDEX-FILE
               ASSIGN TO "D:\BRADEN\CBL2OIMF.DAT"
               ORGANIZATION IS INDEXED
               ACCESS MODE IS SEQUENTIAL
               RECORD KEY IS PART-NUMBER-OUT.

       DATA DIVISION.
       FILE SECTION.
       FD  OUTPUT-INDEX-FILE
           LABEL RECORDS ARE STANDARD
           BLOCK CONTAINS 0 RECORDS
           RECORD CONTAINS 30 CHARACTERS
           DATA RECORD IS OUTPUT-INDEX-REC.
       01  OUTPUT-INDEX-REC.
           05  LOCATION-OUT         	 	PIC X(02).
           05  PART-NUMBER-OUT         	PIC X(06).
           05  DESCRIPTION-OUT        	PIC X(15).
           05  QUANTITY-OUT            	PIC X(02).
           05  UNIT-PRICE-OUT          	PIC X(03).
           05  FILLER                  		PIC X(02) VALUE   	
						SPACES.
       WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
       77  EOF-SW                      		PIC X(01) VALUE 'N'.
           88  ITS-EOF                           	VALUE 'Y'.

       PROCEDURE DIVISION.
       001-MAIN-MODULE.
           OPEN OUTPUT OUTPUT-INDEX-FILE
           PERFORM UNTIL ITS-EOF
               DISPLAY 'LOCATION: '
               ACCEPT LOCATION-OUT
               IF LOCATION-OUT = '00'
                   MOVE 'Y' TO EOF-SW
               ELSE
                   DISPLAY 'PART NUMBER: '
                   ACCEPT PART-NUMBER-OUT
                   DISPLAY 'DESCRIPTION: '
                   ACCEPT DESCRIPTION-OUT
                   DISPLAY 'QUANTITY: '
                   ACCEPT QUANTITY-OUT
                   DISPLAY 'UNIT PRICE: '
                   ACCEPT UNIT-PRICE-OUT
               END-IF
               WRITE OUTPUT-INDEX-REC
           END-PERFORM.
           CLOSE OUTPUT-INDEX-FILE.

           MOVE 'N' TO EOF-SW.
           OPEN INPUT OUTPUT-INDEX-FILE.
           PERFORM UNTIL ITS-EOF
               READ OUTPUT-INDEX-FILE
                   AT END
                       MOVE 'Y' TO EOF-SW
                   NOT AT END
                       DISPLAY OUTPUT-INDEX-REC
               END-READ
           END-PERFORM.
           CLOSE OUTPUT-INDEX-FILE.
           STOP RUN.

SMILEY
```

#### ↳ Re: indexed files

- **From:** "Tom Morrison" <t.morrison@liant.com>
- **Date:** 1999-02-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7aeq8i$sgf$1@client2.news.psi.net>`
- **References:** `<36c8e295.6674275@news.izap.com>`

```
You don't tell us what compiler, etc. but here is a wild guess..

Since you have ACCESS MODE IS SEQUENTIAL, the IO subsystem should reject,
with an appropriate error status ("21"), any record you attempt to write the
key value of which is not greater than the previously written key value.  An
example:

PART-NO-OUT       Successful?
000023                    Yes
000021                    No
000025                    Yes
000029                    Yes
000028                    No

In this example, even though you "wrote" 5 records, only 3 would exist in
the file.

This might help.  Your program does not anticipate any IO error; it is not
quite ready for production :)

Regards,
Tom Morrison

Smiley Mann wrote in message <36c8e295.6674275@news.izap.com>...
>dear friends,
>
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
>               RECORD KEY IS PART-NUMBER-OUT.
[snip]
```

#### ↳ Re: indexed files

- **From:** rkrayhawk@aol.com (RKRayhawk)
- **Date:** 1999-02-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19990217143501.01002.00000061@ng-cr1.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<36c8e295.6674275@news.izap.com>`

```
Try to find FILE STATUS in your reference manual or book. All I/O statements
should be followed by a thorough examination of file status (whether it is I/O
through standard COBOL facilities or through advanced database or telecom
facilities ... if you do I/O then do status checking right then and there).


Also notice,( and this is not yet your current problem), that you will
inevitably do atleast one extra and erroneous WRITE, because of poor
positioning of the WRITE statement.


You posted with the polite and very exceptable note "...  (don't mind the lack
of structure) ...". You will benefit by structuring your code at an early stage
and always structuring your code.

The structure of nearly every COBOL program is management of I/O exceptions. 
The business or scientific information you process is secondary, tertiary or
even quarternary.

So, your structure is something like ..
IF the user says quit then quit
ELSE _!!!!!_PERFORM_!!!!_ do-something.  Use PERFORM and paragraphs. Structure
from the start, that is the fundamental activity of programming - organize. 
Movement of data and calculation, however impressive, is minor activity.

The details really are minor, organize, organize early, keep it organized.

In do-something you might have,
  take in the user data
  if that went well and the data is cool
  _!!!!!_PERFORM_!!!!!_ write-to-file


In write-to-file you should have structure, not later, _NOW_
   WRITE blah blah blah
      ON ERROR
          la ti dah and tell someone about it
or
   WRITE
   EVALUATE  Specific-FILE-SATUS
      WHEN okay
          bump count, tell the world
      WHEN particular anticipatable error
          log problem, perhaps attempt alternative
    * note sometimes errors are exceptable
      WHEN OTHER
          log log log, and then bomb
   END-EVALUATE.

The idea here is not to write your code, the idea is to show you that you do
need to structure, you need lots of structure. Structuring the program is the
essence of the work. The records, fields and contents thereof are very minor
concerns.

It is you that should mind the lack of structure.  You have to start with
structure, it is not an after thought.  Most of your time programming will be
spent trying to perceive and properly represent the structure of the process.

You can benefit by just listing the things a program does or might do.  Code
them as paragraph labels. And then try to figure which paragraph performs the
other paragraph and under what conditions.

Start at the list of things to do level. Try to conceive of how these actions
are related. Don't start inside the IFs. Under no cicumstance should you be
hesitant to create a new paragraph and _!!!!!_PERFORM_!!!!!_ it. Don't be
afraid to have lots of paragraphs.

One other small note, ALL I/O needs status checking, even the OPEN statement. 
This is an extremely valuable lesson to learn. In large applications even just
connecting to SQL services constitutes an I/O action that can fail, and
therefore needs to be checked.

The relevance to your current code, is that an unconditional CLOSE is
potentially harmful. You actually should only close a file that was in fact
successfully opened. Most COBOLs fail softly on this problem, but not all.  

The dificulty you get into is that if the OPEN failure is not detected and not
diagnosed with DISPLAY messages or some other kind of diagnostic then, your
program could proceed to the CLOSE. At which point you could have a very
confusing failure. You could inadvertantly send yourself off on a long and
useless analysis of the CLOSE failure. In a professional environment that could
be time spent in a production down situation when there is much at stake.

The place I have seen this mistake cost the most money is actually the READs of
a file, but it can happen with the CLOSE. Programmers neglect to check the
status of the connect to database services or the status of the open, and then
blithely sail into the READ routines which blow sky-high.  They spend a lot of
time examining the irrelevant READ paragraph and the paragraphs which control
it, searching and arduously finding the keys and control variables, and having
lots of talk about this and that might have happened.  Eventually, after
eliminating all other possibilities they percolate back up the program
structure and discover possibility that the program did not even start
correctly, it can cost alot money to eliminate all other possibilities by
process of elimination.

It is much cheaper, and a lot more fun, to check file status (or database
status) upon open (or upon connect). Screach to a halt if the file does not
open. That is do no WRITEs, no READS, and usually you should not do a CLOSE.

You have a good start with your posted code, let us know how you progress.
Remember to check the file status after every I/O operation.

Best Wishes, 


Robert Rayhawk
RKRayhawk@aol.com
```

## Thread: Printing With MF Cobol — 7 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### Printing With MF Cobol

- **From:** dcantua@my-dejanews.com
- **Date:** 1999-02-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7ac1pu$ppb$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
We are trying to print barcodes and regular text on a laser printer.  We are
using MF Compiler 3.2.46 for OS/2, for the printer we are send HPCL printer
escape commands. When we write the information to a file, then	send the file
to the printer using a Copy command or Print command it prints out with the
barcodes and text. When we write it straight to the printer it the barcodes
will not print. It looks like the printer is not getting the correct esc
commands when the cobol program sends it. But is getting the correct esc
commands when it is written to the file. Can anyone help us??

Daniel G. Cantua
EDP Senior Programmer
Los Angeles County - ISD

-----------== Posted via Deja News, The Discussion Network ==----------
http://www.dejanews.com/       Search, Read, Discuss, or Start Your Own
```

#### ↳ Re: Printing With MF Cobol

- **From:** rkrayhawk@aol.com (RKRayhawk)
- **Date:** 1999-02-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19990216114431.03165.00002437@ng18.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<7ac1pu$ppb$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
dcantua@my-dejanews.com
On Tue, 16 Feb 1999 15:11:02 GMT

wrote in part

<< ... When we write the information to a file, then	send the file
to the printer using a Copy command or Print command it prints out with the
barcodes and text. When we write it straight to the printer it the barcodes
will not print. ...>>


Can you be a little more specific about what does happen in the direct print
attempt.  Do you use an OPEN verb?  What is the status code upon open? How have
you coded your SELECT and ASSIGN clauses?

Do you have sufficient file STATUS code checking in the OPEN, WRITE and COSE
statement? 

Best Wishes

Robert Rayhawk
RKRayhawk@aol.com
```

#### ↳ Re: Printing With MF Cobol

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1999-02-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-WoSK5VBtkgy2@Dwight_Miller.iix.com>`
- **References:** `<7ac1pu$ppb$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
On Tue, 16 Feb 1999 15:11:02, dcantua@my-dejanews.com wrote:

> We are trying to print barcodes and regular text on a laser printer.  We are
> using MF Compiler 3.2.46 for OS/2, for the printer we are send HPCL printer
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> commands when it is written to the file. Can anyone help us??

When assiging your output to "Printer" or any print device, Micro 
Focus defaults to Line Sequential.  Line sequential files lose the 
X"1b" characters, or insert extra characters depending on your runtime
settings (+N I think might fix your problem).  An alternative is to 
expand the print records by two bytes, inserting X"0d0a" in these 
bytes and explicitly stating Organization is RECORD sequential in your
select statement.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Printing With MF Cobol

- **From:** dcantua@my-dejanews.com
- **Date:** 1999-02-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7aejt5$12l$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`
- **References:** `<7ac1pu$ppb$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-WoSK5VBtkgy2@Dwight_Miller.iix.com>`

```
In article <Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-WoSK5VBtkgy2@Dwight_Miller.iix.com>,
  redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell) wrote:
> On Tue, 16 Feb 1999 15:11:02, dcantua@my-dejanews.com wrote:
>
> > We are trying to print barcodes and regular text on a laser printer.  We are
> > using MF Compiler 3.2.46 for OS/2, for the printer we are send HPCL printer
> > escape commands. When we write the information to a file, then	send the
file
> > to the printer using a Copy command or Print command it prints out with the
> > barcodes and text. When we write it straight to the printer it the barcodes
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Yes, adding +N to the COBSW line solved our problem. Thank you for all your
help. We can now print our bar codes inside our document.

Daniel G. Cantua
Los Angeles County - ISD

-----------== Posted via Deja News, The Discussion Network ==----------
http://www.dejanews.com/       Search, Read, Discuss, or Start Your Own
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Printing With MF Cobol

- **From:** pduboisREMOVETHIS@enteract.com (PAL3000)
- **Date:** 1999-02-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36cbf8b5.9595002@news.enteract.com>`
- **References:** `<7ac1pu$ppb$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-WoSK5VBtkgy2@Dwight_Miller.iix.com> <7aejt5$12l$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
Thanks for posting your success!  I am just confused because my manual
says that +N is the default, so this should have been in effect. Also,
+N indicates to print the NULL stuff, which would likely be the cause
of your problems.  Can you let me know and clear this up?? 

Thanks,

Paul

On Wed, 17 Feb 1999 14:32:05 GMT, dcantua@my-dejanews.com wrote:

>Yes, adding +N to the COBSW line solved our problem. Thank you for all your
```

#### ↳ Re: Printing With MF Cobol

- **From:** pduboisREMOVETHIS@enteract.com (PAL3000)
- **Date:** 1999-02-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36cadf55.24293029@news.enteract.com>`
- **References:** `<7ac1pu$ppb$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
In your run command, you can add:
	-N

to your COBSW line.


This turns off the NULL switch,  You should read about this in your
manual. Basically it allows control characters to be read and written
to sequential files.
```

## Thread: bix.com question — 2 msgs

### bix.com question

- **From:** rkrayhawk@aol.com (RKRayhawk)
- **Date:** 1999-02-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19990216115423.03165.00002441@ng18.aol.com>`

```
This is a question for users of bix.com.  Is this not the byte magazine mail
service? (correct me if I am qrong about that).  What will happen to bix.com
now?

Best Wishes,

Robert Rayhawk
RKRayhawk@aol.com
```

#### ↳ Re: bix.com question

- **From:** paulr@bix.com (paulr)
- **Date:** 1999-02-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7acn8a$pm4@lotho.delphi.com>`
- **References:** `<19990216115423.03165.00002441@ng18.aol.com>`

```

BIX has not been associated with Byte directly now for many years, 
and is owned by Delphi Internet Corp.  

It is a very interesting place, and will probably continue plodding along
well into the future. It's pretty cheap too, something like $35/year
gets you a BIX and Delphi account, plus web space and all that jazz. 
Failry good SPAM filtering too. 

BIX is really underpopulated right now; but the folks there are people who
have massive amounts of experience in a lot of areas. (Want to know 
how to build a Solar Power Station? How about how to write a S.F. book,
or a screenplay? Or how to engineer a hunk of SCSI hardware?) 
We lack a bit in COBOL expertise right now; Don runs the COBOL 
conference, but is a very busy man, and there are only a half dozen 
or so Bixen who are active in the COBOL world. 

BIX, unlike Delphi, is a pure text based system as well.
We keep talking about webifying it, but never really seem to get 
around to doing it. : )

If anyone is interested in a Bix/Delphi account, let me know, and 
I'll see if there are any specials running. 

-Paul


RKRayhawk (rkrayhawk@aol.com) wrote:
: This is a question for users of bix.com.  Is this not the byte magazine mail
: service? (correct me if I am qrong about that).  What will happen to bix.com
: now?

: Best Wishes,

: Robert Rayhawk
: RKRayhawk@aol.com
```

<a id="t-163ac51208"></a>
## Thread: Q: squeezing blanks out of a text string? — 13 this month / 16 total

_Topics: [`Language features and syntax`](topics.md#syntax)_

### Q: squeezing blanks out of a text string?

- **From:** Anonymous <nobody@base.xs4all.nl>
- **Date:** 1999-02-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<17d1a7cb40135122c4ae73bc7340d3b0@base.xs4all.nl>`

```
Suppose you had a field that contained six bytes of alphanumeric data,
On every line the field can be begin with one or more letters
followed by either 1) one or more blanks or 2) one or more zeros
followed by at least one digit 1-9 followed by none, one or more spaces.

123456

CR10
CR   1
C    1
L1
L 2
L 20
L  21
VR 100
VR  10
VR   9

Suppose you wanted to compact these values, squeezing out all blanks
and *leading* zeros. Blanks padding out to the right are OK. Resulting
in:

CR10
CR1
C1
L1
L2
L20
L21
VR100
VR10
VR9

This would be pretty easy in some other languages but text-processing is
not COBOL's strong suite. So, any ideas on how to accomplish this?



Please reply to the following address after removing "antispam" and
reversing "tom" in the following address:
antip23spam610@email.tom.com

or simply reply to this article in the ng, I'll see it.


Thanks,

Mike Striefel
Motorola SSTG
```

#### ↳ Re: Q: squeezing blanks out of a text string?

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com>
- **Date:** 1999-02-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36CB2E2F.50199EF6@NOSPAMhome.com>`
- **References:** `<17d1a7cb40135122c4ae73bc7340d3b0@base.xs4all.nl>`

```
I would create two fields which are redefined as array of characters. 
Move your input field to one of these and spaces to the other.

Then do the following perform loops:
Move character from one array to the other array incrementing pointers,
stop when no longer alpha.
Increment the input pointer until numeric & > 0
Move character from one array to the other, incrementing pointers.
Either stop with space or stop at end.

Anonymous wrote:
> 
> Suppose you had a field that contained six bytes of alphanumeric data,
…[44 more quoted lines elided]…
> Motorola SSTG
```

#### ↳ Re: squeezing blanks out of a text string?

- **From:** "Alan Russell" <RUSSELAH@apci.com>
- **Date:** 1999-02-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7afblo$4v2@netnews1.apci.com>`
- **References:** `<17d1a7cb40135122c4ae73bc7340d3b0@base.xs4all.nl>`

```
Anonymous wrote in message
<17d1a7cb40135122c4ae73bc7340d3b0@base.xs4all.nl>...
>Suppose you had a field that contained six bytes of alphanumeric data,
>On every line the field can be begin with one or more letters
…[47 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Here's my reply:

01  work-fields.
      05  in-field.
            10  in-char pic x occurs 6 indexed by in-position.
      05  out-field.
             10  out-char pic x occurs 6 indexed by out-position.
      05  digit-found pic x.
.
.
*    assuming that in-field contains the string and out-field is what you
want
     move 'n' to digit-found.
     move spaces to out-field.
     set out-position to 1.
     perform varying in-position from 1 by 1 until in-position > 6
           if  in-char (in-position) = space
           or in-char (in-position) = zero and digit-found = 'n'
                 continue
           else
                 move in-char (in-position) to out-char (out-position)
                 set out-position up by 1
                 if in-char (in-position) numeric
                     move 'y' to digit-found
                 end-if
            end-if
       end-perform.

this should effectively skip all spaces and zeroes in advance of other
digits.  I don't think this is particularly difficult in COBOL either.

Alan Russell, PhD, CCP
Work - Russelah@apci.com
Home - AHRussell@computer.org
Home page - http://members.aol.com/ahrussell
-------------------------------------------------------
The views expressed are mine alone and do not necessarily reflect those of
my employer
```

#### ↳ Re: Q: squeezing blanks out of a text string?

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1999-02-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36cb9190.33198453@news1.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<17d1a7cb40135122c4ae73bc7340d3b0@base.xs4all.nl>`

```

wrote:


>This would be pretty easy in some other languages but text-processing is
>not COBOL's strong suite. So, any ideas on how to accomplish this?
>
```

#### ↳ Re: Q: squeezing blanks out of a text string?

- **From:** Ed.Stevens@nmm.nissan-usa.com
- **Date:** 1999-02-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7ah7ga$ann$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`
- **References:** `<17d1a7cb40135122c4ae73bc7340d3b0@base.xs4all.nl>`

```
In article <17d1a7cb40135122c4ae73bc7340d3b0@base.xs4all.nl>,
  Anonymous <nobody@base.xs4all.nl> wrote:
> Suppose you had a field that contained six bytes of alphanumeric data,
> On every line the field can be begin with one or more letters
…[45 more quoted lines elided]…
>

With in-line performs and reference modification, text processing in cobol is
just as easy, and very similar to, doing it in Basic.

MOVE ZERO TO SUB2
MOVE SPACES TO RESULT-STRING
PERFORM VARYING SUB1 FROM 1 BY 1
        UNTIL SUB1 > LENGTH OF RESULT-STRING
   IF SOURCE-STRING(SUB1:1) > ZERO
      MOVE SOURCE-STRING(SUB1:1) TO RESULT-STRING(SUB2:1)
   END-IF
END-PERFORM

That squeezes out your spaces and shows how easy string processing is in
cobol.  I'll leave it to you to flesh out how to drop the leading zeros.

Ed Stevens

-----------== Posted via Deja News, The Discussion Network ==----------
http://www.dejanews.com/       Search, Read, Discuss, or Start Your Own
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Q: squeezing blanks out of a text string?

- **From:** Anonymous <nobody@drule.org>
- **Date:** 1999-02-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<199902262101.PAA10329@crius.flash.net>`
- **References:** `<7ah7ga$ann$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
[snipped]

> With in-line performs and reference modification, text processing in cobol is
> just as easy, and very similar to, doing it in Basic.
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> cobol.  I'll leave it to you to flesh out how to drop the leading zeros.

I didn't know you could do substring manipulation like this. 

I solved my problem by defining a six-element array of one-byte elements,
moving a text string into it, used a PERFORM paragraph to step through
each element pair replacing any ' 0' with '  ' to clear out leading zeros.

I then used one PERFORM nested within another to compress out leading spaces.
The outer PERFORM steps through each element in the array and if it finds
a space calls the inner PERFORM which steps through further elements until
it finds a numeric then swaps elements. 

This approach resembles what some of the other respondees suggested though
your method looks cleaner. I suppose if I remembered more COBOL than I've
forgotten all this would be alot easier. Oh well. 

Thanks for your help.


Please reply to the following address after removing "antispam" and
reversing "tom" in the following address:
antip23spam610@email.tom.com

or simply reply to this article in the ng, I'll see it.

Thanks,

Mike Striefel
Motorola SSTG
```

<a id="t-1bbd9b104f"></a>
## Thread: How is *your* shop's Y2K work going? — 19 this month / 24 total

_Topics: [`Y2K and Year-2000 remediation`](topics.md#y2k)_

### How is *your* shop's Y2K work going?

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1999-02-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<HHzy2.7112$Xl5.11560999@news1.mia>`

```
I would like to start a thread which should be interesting to many of
us.  We've all heard enough Y2K opinions to make us sick, but that's
not what I'm asking for here.  In most of the Y2K posts I have seen in
the COBOL newsgroups, the professional programmers have expressed
opinions similar to my own: briefly, that Y2K may be a big problem,
but it won't kill us.  So let's get a little hard data, where our own
personal experience gives us direct knowledge.  I would like to hear
from as many of you as are able and free to answer "What shape is your
shop (or client's shops) for Y2K?"  In other words, where you have
direct knowledge, is your part of the world going to be ready?  If you
aren't a professional programmer, or you aren't involved in your shop's
Y2K work, then your knowledge is second hand; please refrain and let us
get some data from the actual troops doing the work here. :-)

I'll start out by saying that all my clients have either completed Y2K
remediation, or the work is underway or planned on a schedule which
should be finished well in time.  None of the schedules are overly
optimistic, and my conservative opinion is that they should all be
complete by the end of September, at the latest.  There is only one
area of concern, and that is one system that a client has contracted
with a company that I don't have confidence in.  It's an important
system, but I think my client has enough resources to get it done if
that contractor drops the ball.

In my little part of the world, I have full expectation that we will
be ready.  How about yours? :-)
```

#### ↳ Re: How is *your* shop's Y2K work going?

- **Message-ID:** `<36CB6524.1253E76E@fast.net>`
- **References:** `<HHzy2.7112$Xl5.11560999@news1.mia>`

```
We think we have finished all the mainframe batch and on-line stuff.  Their
working on the PC stuff now.  We thought we were finished with the
mainframe stuff until some 'incidents' errupted in Jan. 99.  Hope the rest
of it holds for Y2K.
```

```
Well Judson, I am like you.  I have just about finished up,
and have one system left.  Actually, I would like to see us
tackle a *real* Y2K problem, like world hunger.  It looks like

to the brush-fire stages.  Wouldn't it be nice to go into the
new millennium saying "no one in the world will ever starve
again"?
```

#### ↳ Re: How is *your* shop's Y2K work going?

- **From:** "Alan Russell" <RUSSELAH@apci.com>
- **Date:** 1999-02-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7aejvg$p4h@netnews1.apci.com>`
- **References:** `<HHzy2.7112$Xl5.11560999@news1.mia>`

```
I'd like to respond to Judson's challenge by quoting from our company's 10-Q
statement released last week
(http://www.airproducts.com/corp/year2000-sec10q.htm):
-------------------------------

"SEC Year 2000 Readiness Disclosure (10Q)
Filed in February 1999
Year 2000 Preparation
During the fiscal quarter ended 31 December 1998, the Company continued to
achieve the critical milestones in the Year 2000 readiness program. Progress
and exposure are essentially as planned and disclosed in the fiscal year
1998 Annual Report issued in December 1998. The $40 million cost estimate as
previously disclosed remains currently viable. The next phase, Year 2000
contingency planning, is in progress and is expected to be ready to address
Year 2000 issues as they arise.

Information Technology
Over 96% of the mission-critical infrastructure and applications portfolio
have been tested and certified as Year 2000 ready. This activity is expected
to be complete by the end of the second fiscal quarter. The Company
continues to believe that the combination of readiness certification and
contingency plans will result in no material adverse impact on the Company's
operations or financial condition due to Information Technology Systems.

Process Control and Embedded Chip Systems
Over 98% of the Company-owned or operated Non-Information Technology Systems
have been inventoried and risk assessment is complete. Year 2000
certification efforts continue as planned with over 50% of the mission
critical systems certified as Year 2000 ready.

Third Parties
Assessment of the Company's key suppliers is continuing as planned."

-------------------------------
This is a public statement and is totally accurate.  Note the phrase "tested
and certified", meaning that not only is 96% of the remediation work done
[in fact the remediation is nearly 100%], but that 96% has been tested and
certified by an independent audit.  This is good news and we are happy to
make it public.  Likewise there is good progress being made on the embedded
chip issue as testified to above.  I have been a part of this effort since
1995 and am pleased to stand behind this public statement on our 10-Q.

Alan Russell, PhD, CCP
Work - Russelah@apci.com
Home - AHRussell@computer.org
Home page - http://members.aol.com/ahrussell
-------------------------------------------------------
```

#### ↳ Re: How is *your* shop's Y2K work going?

- **From:** Paul Krikorian <pkrikorian@mail.cccd.edu>
- **Date:** 1999-02-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36CC5117.DCB3D34@mail.cccd.edu>`
- **References:** `<HHzy2.7112$Xl5.11560999@news1.mia>`

```
We are a community college district serving approximately 47,000 students
among three community colleges and a public television station in Orange
County, California.  Our Personnel system was modified to handle the Y2K
problem back in February of 1997, and our Financial system a little while
later.  Our Student system has been modified but not fully tested.  We will
be doing a full system test next month.  Payroll is done by the County
which is just finishing up their Y2K modifications and should be
implementing them in June.  End users employ a variety of off-the-shelf PC
software to handle small, local activities.  Since we are not responsible
for maintaining the software I don't know if they are Y2K ready, but these
are not show-stopping applications that will keep the district from doing
it's main business.  Major outside institutions that we deal with are the
Teacher's Credit Union, State's Chancellor's office, the SSA and the IRS.
Don't have any idea where they are as far as Y2K modifications.
```

#### ↳ Re: How is *your* shop's Y2K work going?

- **From:** "Denny Brouse" <denden@prodigy.net>
- **Date:** 1999-02-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7ahjop$9m4$1@newssvr04-int.news.prodigy.com>`
- **References:** `<HHzy2.7112$Xl5.11560999@news1.mia>`

```
Judson,

The Unisys side of our shop (where I work) is 99.5% good to go.  All I have
left to install is the Win95 Y2K patches on about 10 PCs.  Federal examiners
require us to do such things, even though I can't think of a soul who even
knows you can run "winfile.exe" under Win95, let alone actually uses it.
The IBM side of our shop should finish final testing in March, 1999.  Must
be all that JCL stuff :~).  We've already seen a couple of bounces in
January 1999, (i.e. tapes coming from the IBM side with 99000 expiration
dates), but we extinguished that flicker before it even became a flame.  We
expect some minor problems, but I'm sure we'll be able to react quickly with
as little disruption as possible.  We have our disaster recovery center
available, just in case there's no power for a period of time or we have
some weird system problem. Our local utility assures us that they are Y2K
ready at the present time.  Besides, if there's no power, people won't be
able to do any banking anyhow, since we won't be able to get into the branch
vaults <G>!  Fortunately, for us, the Unisys check side starts each day
brand new, so the files are cleared every morning.  January 3, 2000 tested
about the same as January 2, 1999 ran in production this year.  The float
calculations we use only allow a maximum of 3 days float on checks, so
testing was rather easy to verify.  We use many "dumb" terminals in our
department that are clueless as to dates, imbedded chips, etc.  We even
tested our software for the year 2015, even though the V-Series we're
running on will probably be replaced with an NX box sometime in the year
2000.

I might point out that 2 of the dates the examiners gave us to test we're
the famous all "9's" tests. I.E. 04/09/99 (the 99th day of 1999) and
9/09/99, which in their minds could be interpreted as all "9's".
Hmmm....I guess I need to learn more about dates.  In my mind, the zeros are
significant digits, but what do I know?  I'm just a programmer, not an
examiner.  So I guess if I use a 1 digit month and a 1 digit day and/or a 2
digit Julian day in my calculations, and check for all "9's" as an end of
file condition rather than the "old fashioned" COBOL "at end" condition, I
could be in trouble!

All in all, it's been a lot of work for us, but I feel comfortable with
where we're at.  It's kinda like riding a motorcycle, though.  You can feel
comfortable, but you should never feel TOO comfortable.

Denny
denden@prodigy.net

…[31 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

```
On Thu, 18 Feb 1999 17:38:46 -0500, "David W. Furin"
<dfurin@larich.com> wrote:


>Ditto.  I've never understood how 99099 or 090999 equals "all nines." 
>Apparantly I also need to learn more about dates...  Has anyone
>(programmers, not auditors) ever found this to be a problem?

I've seen it.  Why?  Dates were used as a FLAG, but the date had to be
VALID.  99 is the highest anyone could go, and the farthest out - so
9/9/99 is a natural choice.
```

```
In this case specifically, I recommend you contact the senior analyst
in charge of Payroll/Personnel for a client of mine who chose Projeni to
remediate their Payroll/Personnel system, to be up and running this past
January 1st.  Her name is Margaret, and if you e-mail me, I will be happy
to get you in contact with her.  If you are considering using Projeni, I
am certain Margaret will have things she would love to relate to you.  In
fact, Margaret has been quite vocal on this subject recently, in the tiny
bit of her time which isn't absorbed in dealing with, ahhhh, certain Y2K
related issues. ;-)  Is this the end of February already?  Almost March?
My, how time does fly!  I'll let Margaret fill you in on the details.  I
have known Margaret for over 15 years.  She is one of the most aggressively
competent people I know.  Her main 'fault' is that on occasion she has
pushed herself to the point of endangering her health in order to make
sure the job got done right, and on time.  Anybody who knows Margaret will
tell you the same thing, and she is one of the most highly respected and
valued people in my client's MIS department.  Everybody in their MIS dept.
will tell you that about Margaret.  I know, because I have heard them say
such things many times.  Just thought you should know that, in case you
talk with somebody like, ohhh, Projeni, for example. :-)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: How is *your* shop's Y2K work going?

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** mark0young@aol.com (Mark0Young)
- **Date:** 1999-02-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19990220152307.16016.00000421@ngol01.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<36CDE122.1C16@bellsouth.net>`

```

I work for a community college in Oregon that has generally about 42,000
students a quarter.

Our shop has all its in-house mainframe applications Y2k-compliant, except for
an occasional remediation bug we keep coming across. The Financial Aid package
we are running is the vendor's Y2k-compliant release. We are in the midst of
upgrading the General Ledger package, but we keep on running into bugs in the
conversion programs (many of them the vendor's bugs, some are errors in
interpreting their instructions) that prevent us from converting the data from
the version of their package we are running to the version they say is
Y2k-ready. (For budgeting purposes, we ended up using the current package but
claim that next fiscal year is 1910 instead of 2000. But in 5 months we start
Fiscal Year 2000 and we need to be running the new release, have the users
trained on how to use it, interface programs working with it, and have the
budgeting data converted or re-keyed.)  Fiscal year 2000 starts on July 1. On
the week of July 4 we plan on a week of testing the operating system and some
application testing with the system date rolling over and in the year 2000.
Even though our operating system is suppose to be Y2k-ready, I am planning on
installing the next release before the Y2k test, but whatever release we are
running when we do our Y2k test, if the test succeeds, we plan on keeping that
release through to at least the opening days of the year 2000. One thing I have
to do before then is to install a new release of Gener/OL (CICS
development/runtime environment) because we are running a back release that is
not Y2k-ready.

Mark A. Young
```

```
<snip the rest>

My shop is one of the bigger insurance companies worldwide. In our home
country on our mainframe, most of the Y2K work is done. We spent about 12 mio
US$ to do it. All we fear now is a potential lost of electricity at 12 PM on
1999-12-31 ;-) but when we start business on 2000-01-03 we do not expect
further problems.

There are some minor PC solutions which may or may not be Y2K ready, we don't
care much about. But I know that some of our subsidiaries in other countries
are not yet ready and we have to help them.

Daniel
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: How is *your* shop's Y2K work going?

- **From:** jcj0347@aol.com (JCJ0347)
- **Date:** 1999-02-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19990223070156.25932.00002185@ng118.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<919555045.658766@flux>`

```
Judson,

I work for a business involved in wholesale distribution of outdoor power
equipment.

All code has been corrected, data files expanded, and I'm into testing,
testing, and more testing right now.  Finding a few bugs, but nothing major. 
Some code was abandoned when I
couldn't find a user that actually used a program.

The benefits of Y2K are making life somewhat easier on the users as I took the
opportunity to add a couple of much-needed alternate keys to some files, add
additional fields to others, and in one instance combined 3 transaction files
into 1 which cut 45 minutes off the end-of-day processing.  In other words I
wrapped a lot of plain old maintenance around Y2K.

This has been an on-going project since September, 1997.

Other non-COBOL issues are still outstanding.  We're going
to change the backup software this spring, and I'm having
major problems getting APC to tell me what to do with the
software that contols the ups.  All PC's have had upgraded
BIOS installed (a lot easier than I thought) except for one 
386 that is going to be replaced.  We just purchased a new
PII 350 server which should go online next week.  NOS
is NT 4.0.  Old server will become my development machine.

So I'll stay pretty busy the rest of this year, although I'm
taking a week off in May to go to Pawley's Island in SC
come heck or high water (or Y2K).

JC Jones.
```

## Thread: PL/I to COBOL conversion — 9 msgs

### PL/I to COBOL conversion

- **From:** Josep Lluís <j.l.sanchez@vitalicio.es>
- **Date:** 1999-02-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36CA8BA1.FD298A38@vitalicio.es>`

```
 First sorry by english level

 In PL/I (V2R3M0, IBM OS/390)  is possible to work which booleans
fields. It must be defined
as :
        DCL SWITCH BIT(1) VALUE '0'B;

 A need to know if there are a correspondence in IBM VSCOBOL II Release
3.2. I remember
that in IBM COBOL for IMB systems 36 and AS/400 (i think too) this was
supported by defining :

       01 SWITCH PIC 1 INDICATOR.
            88 SWITCH-ON  VALUE '1'B.
            88 SWITCH-OFF VALUE '0'B.

 Now I can't found any reference on VSCOBOL II manuals about this kind
of variables.

 Can you help me ? Thank a lot
```

#### ↳ Re: PL/I to COBOL conversion

- **From:** WOB@my-dejanews.com
- **Date:** 1999-02-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7afe1v$q0n$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`
- **References:** `<36CA8BA1.FD298A38@vitalicio.es>`

```
In article <36CA8BA1.FD298A38@vitalicio.es>,
  Josep =?iso-8859-1?Q?Llu=EDs?= <j.l.sanchez@vitalicio.es> wrote:
>  First sorry by english level
>
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
>

I believe that a One-Bit field in PL/I occupies a single-byte of storage
anyway. Depending on how PL/I addresses the target bit, the three COBOL
equivilents would be a single PIC X(01) byte, with a value of X'80'
(high-order bit is turned-on), X'01' (low-order bit is turned-on) or the
Figurative Constant LOW-VALUE (all bits are turned-off). If you can't define
HEX values, then define a 9(04) BINARY field (known as a halfword, FIXED BIN
(15,0) in PL/I) and redefine it as two PIC X(01) bytes. To represent X'80',
move 128 to the halfword. To represent X'01', move 1 to the halfword. Moving
a value of ZERO to the halfword would represent X'00'(LOW-VALUE). You would
then use the halfword to test for the presence of the target bit.

HTH....

WOB

-----------== Posted via Deja News, The Discussion Network ==----------
http://www.dejanews.com/       Search, Read, Discuss, or Start Your Own
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: PL/I to COBOL conversion

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@inospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-02-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7afg2m$rkc@dfw-ixnews4.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<36CA8BA1.FD298A38@vitalicio.es> <7afe1v$q0n$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
After a week-end of saying "nasty" things about the draft Standard, this is
time for my usual "plug" for the good things in it.  Both "Boolean" (usually
a 1 byte "switch" field) and "bit" fields are defined in the draft Standard
(although there is still some work.debate going on concerning groups, bits,
redefines, and where a bit field can start).

If you are interested in your vendor providing support for
either of these features in a "timely" manner, I would suggest you contact
them and request that they provide support before the next Standard is
official.

NOTE: The original question was for the OS/390 or MVS environment - where
such fields are NOT currently supported.  IBM, does, however, support
partial but not identical functionality in their COBOL/400 product.  My
memory is that it is for Boolean items but not bit-fields - but I would
easily stand corrected by our more knowledgeable OS/400 readers.

P.S. Yes, Randall, as with so many cases - like the meaning of COMP - IBM is
quite inconsistent in their COBOL extensions across platforms - and since
the "death of AD/Cycle" there has been little demand and even less
indication that such discrepancies will ever be resolved.
```

#### ↳ Re: PL/I to COBOL conversion

- **From:** John Piggott <John_Piggott@compuserve.com>
- **Date:** 1999-02-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36CB1409.3351@compuserve.com>`
- **References:** `<36CA8BA1.FD298A38@vitalicio.es>`

```
Josep,

There's no PIC 1 available in VS COBOL II (or its successors) and there's 
no way of accessing a bit item directly.

If the size of the data is not critical, change them to PIC X and use, 
for example:

  01  SWITCH  PIC X.
    88  SWITCH-ON VALUE X"01".
    88  SWITCH-OFF VALUE X"00".

One advantage with this is that you could have more values in addition to 
just ON and OFF.

If the switch is alone and has character or binary items on each side, 
then it's taking up a whole byte anyway and you lose nothing by using a 
PIC X.

If, on the other hand, you have up to 8 bit switches together and you  
can't change them all to PIC X because of size or compatibility 
considerations, then all your options are a bit ugly I'm afraid. 
You'll have to define them as a PIC X, e.g.

  01  EIGHT-SWITCHES  PIC X.

then you could try:

1) using a subroutine to do a boolean AND and OR e.g. CALL "TESTSW" USING 
EIGHT-SWITCHES position REPLY (where position is 1 to 8) and CALL "SETSW" 
USING EIGHT-SWITCHES position.

2) using a subroutine to "UNPACK" and "PACK" to and from a PIC X item and 
a PIC X OCCURS 8 field in working storage.

3) move the EIGHT-SWITCHES to the lowest byte of a PIC S9(9) COMP field 
(with the higher bytes binary zeros) and then do a repeated divide by 2. 
(Well, I said it gets ugly.)

John Piggott.

Josep =?iso-8859-1?Q?Llu=EDs?= wrote:
> 
>  First sorry by english level
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
>  Can you help me ? Thank a lot
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: PL/I to COBOL conversion

- **From:** Rodger Whitlock <totototo+newsgroups@mail.pacificcoast.net>
- **Date:** 1999-02-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<O0WpNihlhKml@vmsmail.gov.bc.ca>`
- **References:** `<36CA8BA1.FD298A38@vitalicio.es> <36CB1409.3351@compuserve.com>`

```
John Piggott <John_Piggott@compuserve.com> wrote:

>There's no PIC 1 available in VS COBOL II (or its successors) and there's 
>no way of accessing a bit item directly.
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>just ON and OFF.

But there's also a nasty class of bug lurking in this code. What happens 
when the switch contains a value other than x"00" or x"01"? Are such 
values to be considered "true"/"on" or "false"/"off"?

One way of dealing with such ambiguous situations (and, thanks to bugs 
elsewhere in the code, they can be expected to arise) is to define only 
one 88-level and then test solely against that. I would, myself, keep the 
switch-off value and treat all other values as "on." This requires that 
all programmers involved understand the issue and toe the line. There is 
no room for originality once a convention of this nature is settled on.

In a shop I used to work in, for historic reasons Booleans were 
conventially defined as pic x(1) taking the values " " and "X". Tests were 
made, higgledly-piggledy, against both values. As you can imagine, a 
corrupted Boolean caused the most astonishing screwups. I think that 
eventually everyone realized that tests had to be made consistently 
against one or the other value.

The great advantage of using a true single bit field for a Boolean is that 
such corruption is impossible. If the field is corrupted, it is still 
unambiguously one or the other of the two possible values.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: PL/I to COBOL conversion

- **From:** WOB@my-dejanews.com
- **Date:** 1999-02-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7ai52v$6bi$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`
- **References:** `<36CA8BA1.FD298A38@vitalicio.es> <36CB1409.3351@compuserve.com> <O0WpNihlhKml@vmsmail.gov.bc.ca>`

```
In article <O0WpNihlhKml@vmsmail.gov.bc.ca>,
  Rodger Whitlock <totototo+newsgroups@mail.pacificcoast.net> wrote:

> But there's also a nasty class of bug lurking in this code. What happens

<Rest Snipped>

Roger,

I actually posted a question regarding this to NEWS:COMP.LANG.PL1 last night
and received two similar replies.

From what I can gather, PL/I can be compiled to have ALIGNED or UNALIGNED data
(similar to 370/ASSEMBLER). Unfortunately, I'm still not sure about data-
alignment when using ALIGNED and whether it's aligned on a byte, halfword or
fullword boundary. Your point regarding the significant bit is exacly what I
was unsure about as well. This would warrant further investigation.

Other folks have suggested shifting and what-not. I guess it all depends.
However, if it is aligned on a byte-boundary, then I'd have to say that the
byte would equal either X'80', X'01' or X'00'. If aligned on a halfword or
fullword, hopefully the significant byte is left or right justified, using one
of the above patterns. With unaligned, this adds some real difficulty and I
don't have an answer.

WOB

-----------== Posted via Deja News, The Discussion Network ==----------
http://www.dejanews.com/       Search, Read, Discuss, or Start Your Own
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: PL/I to COBOL conversion

- **From:** Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>
- **Date:** 1999-02-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36CC1353.61A94821@zip.com.au>`
- **References:** `<36CA8BA1.FD298A38@vitalicio.es> <36CB1409.3351@compuserve.com>`

```
Josep,

There was a reply about using Bit fields using LE calls.  A bit of a
mixture (I would recommend upgrading to cobol for MVS anyway)

to find the bits in a single byte

move zero to comp-field.
move byte to last-part-of-comp-field. 
perform 8 times
	divide comp-field by 2 giving comp-field 
			       remainder bit-array(count)
end-perform

You get the idea...
Ken
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: PL/I to COBOL conversion

- **From:** "James King" <mangogwr@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1999-02-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<WqqA2.15027$OS5.14986218@news3.mia>`
- **References:** `<36CA8BA1.FD298A38@vitalicio.es> <36CB1409.3351@compuserve.com> <36CC1353.61A94821@zip.com.au>`

```
COBOL 370/LE allows Bit manipulation withing the Language.

The div by 2 is pretty good.

You can also code
05  MY-BITMASK.
      10  HEX-80   PIC X  VALUE X'80'   (10000000)   7F
      10  HEX-00   PIC X  VALUE X'00'.                      FF
      10  HEX-00   PIC X  VALUE X'00'.                      FF
      10  HEX-00   PIC X  VALUE X'00'.                      FF
      10  HEX-00   PIC X  VALUE X'00'.                      FF
      10  HEX-00   PIC X  VALUE X'00'.                      FF
      10  HEX-01   PIC X  VALUE X'01'   (00000001)   FF
this is hex 8000001  ( or 2147483649)
also 7FFFFFFF is = -2147483648



Ken Foskey wrote in message <36CC1353.61A94821@zip.com.au>...
>Josep,
>
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: PL/I to COBOL conversion

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** rkrayhawk@aol.com (RKRayhawk)
- **Date:** 1999-02-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19990225090837.27212.00000035@ng-fc1.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<WqqA2.15027$OS5.14986218@news3.mia>`

```
Per discussion of code 
>to find the bits in a single byte

In addition to the fine discussion so far, you can consider just doing a lookup
in a table that has the 256 static values already hard coded. Just use the byte
as an index into the table. You will have to normalize 0 to 1 ... 255 to 256
for COBOL basis 1 tables.

Division is the slowest possible way. This kind of thing really does not need
to be 'calculated' dynamically.

Best Wishes,

Robert Rayhawk
RKRayhawk@aol.com
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Cross System Product?

- **From:** "Jon Santarelli" <root@127.0.0.1>
- **Date:** 1999-02-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ebbg.f7dkr80.pminews@nntp.interaccess.com>`
- **References:** `<7aensc$kig@freenet-news.carleton.ca> <36cb7b5c.27513305@news1.ibm.net>`

```
Cross System Product (CSP) is one of IBM's SAA languages.  As the name
implies it is "Cross System", it will run on all of IBM's platforms
from the
'mighty' MVS to the lowly 'PC-DOS' and everything in the middle too.

It is COBOL like, very easy to learn.  I walked into a shop, did a CBT
on it
and was comfortable with it within the first two weeks.

Should you take the job?  I can't tell you that.  Personally, I'm
staying away from it.  In the states it is not very popular. 
Apparently in Europe, more specifically Germany, it is much more
popular.  It was coded mostly in Germany and mostly in C.

Here in Chicago I know that Allstate and Kemper insurance companies use
it extensively.  Motorola has (maybe had) a system or two in it.  Nike
in Portland uses it too.  There are a few other places around the
country that I have heard about too that use it.  



On Thu, 18 Feb 1999 02:31:30 GMT, Thane Hubbell wrote:

>On 17 Feb 1999 15:39:56 GMT, ba600@FreeNet.Carleton.CA (Mike Kenzie)
>wrote:
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
>what are you talking about????
```

```

<root@127.0.0.1> wrote:

>Cross System Product (CSP) is one of IBM's SAA languages.  As the name
```
