```
We have CSP in my shop - and are busily in the process of getting rid of it
because though it's pretty easy to learn and nice & easy to write new applications
it's a son of a bitch to maintain them.  From what I hear other sites are getting
rid of it too - and I think I heard that IBM are no longer developing the product,
at least on the mainframe.  I've always tried to stay away from it.

Mike Kenzie wrote:

> OK what's this one?  Someone woke me up this morning asking if I wanted to
> do some work with it.  Does any one know of similar products?  What's the
> learning curve like?  Will I regret accepting this challenge?
> --
```

```
On Wed, 17 Feb 1999 23:14:58 GMT, Hamish <anadem@yahoo.com> wrote:

>I need to get a compiler to learn COBOL with. Can anyone tell me the "pros"
>and "cons" of MF versus Fujitsu please?  WOuld one be better for a beginner
>than the other?  Should I start with one then get the other when I know more?
> The idea of Fujitsu being free is appealing, but sometimes you get what you
>pay for ...

Fujitsu took a lot of heat for offering their product "Free".  People
wanted to know the "catch", what's the "deal", can it be any good?

The answer is - yes it's free.  (v 3)
There is no catch, other than they would like you to buy (at a
reasonable price even!) v4.  But you don't have to.
And yes it is good.  It's very good.
```

#### ↳ Re: MF COBOL or Fujitsu COBOL for learning?

- **From:** NoLonger@ix.netcom.com (OldUncleMe)
- **Date:** 1999-02-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36cb8ad8.44658186@news>`
- **References:** `<7afihb$tvc$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
It was: Wed, 17 Feb 1999 23:14:58 GMT and with STARTLING insight,  "Hamish <anadem@yahoo.com>"
posted "MF COBOL or Fujitsu COBOL for learning?" to "comp.lang.cobol" :

-->I need to get a compiler to learn COBOL with. Can anyone tell me the "pros"
-->and "cons" of MF versus Fujitsu please?  WOuld one be better for a beginner
-->than the other?  Should I start with one then get the other when I know more?
--> The idea of Fujitsu being free is appealing, but sometimes you get what you
-->pay for ...
-->
-->thanks

Your learning ramp is going to include installation and learning the quirks (methods) of use
for either one.

Both compilers seem to work well with the Ansi core of the cobol language.  Where such products
effectively differ is in the use of proprietary 'extensions' to the language.  Both will work
on x86/win32 pc's.  If you are mainly learning cobol to learn cobol and don't have a particular
environment in mind, or plan to use cobol applications in windows, either should be good.

I prefer Fujitsu.  I haven't gotten the animator working though, or the debugger.  I use a
separate editor that I prefer for actually writing the code, and compile and link with Fuji.  I
have used the MF animator on an AS/400 AIX environment, and it's a nice tool to have, at times.
That compiler is rather weak regarding error messages;  it frequently chokes without giving a
clue as to why.  It's horrible for debugging Report writer as just about any problem it
encounters it states patently that there is a problem, but not what it is.  I imagine Personal
Cobol is about the same.  We have not been given access to the product manuals....

I think that applications written with any pc/cobol compiler will require the runtime libraries
be installed before working. even though you produce an executable (.exe) file.  Thus you won't
be producing true 'stand alone' code, per se.  Most if not all larger sizes of iron have a
cobol product installed from the get-go.  Running cobol apps there would be a matter of
following the in place procedure, after using the installed compiler and linking in situ.  Neh?
(I'm here to learn, please correct errors and ommissions!)

Power Cobol seems like a pretty strong product.  It's about $300 right now.

TS


"It's important when you ride snorting, kicking 2000 pound horned animals
 for a living that you occasionally get away for a while"  Skiing Cowboys
                       tenox+-+at+-+home+-+dot+-+com
```

```
I prefer the Fujitsu. I had the free download but bought Thanes book with
```

#### ↳ Re: Converting COBOL COMP Fields to numbers

- **From:** WOB@my-dejanews.com
- **Date:** 1999-02-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7ag3sq$d00$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`
- **References:** `<7afk9n$9hm$1@news.interlog.com>`

```
In article <7afk9n$9hm$1@news.interlog.com>,
  "Steven Hammond" <steven@ausc.com> wrote:
>
> Does anyone have basic code to convert COBOL COMP Fields to numbers?
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Assuming you're speaking of Mainframe COBOL COMP (which is BINARY), are you
asking to convert (for example), a fullword binary to DISPLAY?

Just move the fullword to a 10-byte DISPLAY NUMERIC field. It MUST be 10-bytes
because the high-water mark of a fullword is 2,147,483,647 (X'7FFFFFFF'). I
think it would be wise to use the Compile Option TRUNC(BIN) so that you don't
lose significant high-order digits.

Or, are you asking to convert each 4-Bit binary nibble to its associated
1-Byte DISPLAY counterpart? For example, converting X'7FFFFFFF'(4-Bytes) to
C'7FFFFFFFF'(8-Bytes)?

WOB

-----------== Posted via Deja News, The Discussion Network ==----------
http://www.dejanews.com/       Search, Read, Discuss, or Start Your Own
```


#### ↳ Re: Converting COBOL COMP Fields to numbers

- **From:** dnikelshpu@aol.com (DNikelshpu)
- **Date:** 1999-02-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19990222145955.02874.00001567@ng-fx1.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<7afk9n$9hm$1@news.interlog.com>`

```
COMP  is simply hexidecimal  representation of  binary numbers. Compy fields
can be 2, 4, or 8 bytes depending on the number of digits(1-4, 5-9,
10-18)...But yeh, all that and then some you should have no trouble finding in
any COBOL book.
 
A MOVE to a DISPLAY or COMP-3 field  will convert the number for you (check
your manual), just be careful of truncation.

           Dmitry
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Converting COBOL COMP Fields to numbers

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 1999-02-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7aucqr$dpk$1@news-1.news.gte.net>`
- **References:** `<7afk9n$9hm$1@news.interlog.com> <19990222145955.02874.00001567@ng-fx1.aol.com>`

```
DNikelshpu wrote in message
<19990222145955.02874.00001567@ng-fx1.aol.com>...
>COMP  is simply hexidecimal  representation of  binary numbers. Compy
fields
>can be 2, 4, or 8 bytes depending on the number of digits(1-4, 5-9,
>10-18)...But yeh, all that and then some you should have no trouble finding
in
>any COBOL book.

No, COMP is *****NOT*****  "hexadecimal representation."

The COBOL COMP, COMP-1 -2 -3 -4- -5 -whatever, DISPLAY and all the other
data types are the definitions of how BITS are used to convey a numeric
value.

But as you say, you should have no problem finding that in a COBOL book.

MCM
```

<a id="t-7c4ca357e1"></a>
## Thread: IDCAMS anyone? — 11 this month / 12 total

### IDCAMS anyone?

- **From:** paulr@bix.com (paulr)
- **Date:** 1999-02-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7aenr5$thk@lotho.delphi.com>`

```
I'll never really get the swing of batch processing... (*sigh*)
Can anyone point out to me what I am doing wrong in the below
JCL that creates a VSAM file? 

The CYLINDERS(5 3) line seems to be absolutley untenable to 
IDCAMS, yet removing that line creates the file fine - with 
index space of 1 track. :/ 

Error IDC3505I just tells me that the SPACE parameter is 
incorrect. I can't see where though... 

I probably have been glaring at it too long.
I'd appreciate a 4-eyes look from someone who has tangled with 
this before; thanks!
-Paul

-------------- include JCL Listing ---------------------
[ Edited for brevity ]

                                                                                
   DEFINE CLUSTER                   -                                   00190087
          (NAME(D9425$.ITA.TLFILE)  -                                   00200087
          VOL(STGC23)               -                                   00210087
          KEYS(12,0)                -                                   00220087
          RECORDSIZE(31 31)         -                                   00230087
          SHAREOPTIONS(2)           -                                   00240087
          FREESPACE(20 15)          -                                   00250087
          CYLINDERS(10 5)           -                                   00260088
          INDEXED)                  -                                   00261088
          INDEX(NAME(D9425$.ITA.TLFILE.INDEX) -                         00270088
            CYLINDERS(5 3))                                             00272088
IDC3505I INCORRECT SPECIFICATION OF SPACE ALLOCATION                            
IDC3003I FUNCTION TERMINATED. CONDITION CODE IS 12                              
                                                                                
IDC0002I IDCAMS PROCESSING COMPLETE. MAXIMUM CONDITION CODE WAS 12
```

#### ↳ Re: IDCAMS anyone?

- **From:** Gilbert Saint-flour <gsf@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 1999-02-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36caf535$1001$tfs$mr2ice@news>`
- **References:** `<7aenr5$thk@lotho.delphi.com>`

```
In <7aenr5$thk@lotho.delphi.com>, on 17 Feb 1999 at 15:39, paulr@bix.com
(paulr) said:

>Error IDC3505I just tells me that the SPACE parameter is 
>incorrect. I can't see where though... 

>   DEFINE CLUSTER                   -
>          (NAME(D9425$.ITA.TLFILE)  -
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> IDC3003I FUNCTION TERMINATED. CONDITION CODE IS 12

I'm not sure, but I would guess that you're not allowed to specify space
info at the component level (in your case, in the INDEX definition) if you
already specified it at the CLUSTER level.  In other words, keep the
CYLINDERS(10 5) and delete CYLINDERS(5 3).

Gilbert Saint-flour <gsf@ibm.net>
```

#### ↳ Re: IDCAMS anyone?

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com>
- **Date:** 1999-02-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36CAF4B8.48303457@NOSPAMhome.com>`
- **References:** `<7aenr5$thk@lotho.delphi.com>`


#### ↳ Re: IDCAMS anyone?

- **From:** vbandke@ibm.net (Volker Bandke)
- **Date:** 1999-02-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36cc01a8.1286499@news3.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<7aenr5$thk@lotho.delphi.com>`

```
On 17 Feb 1999 15:39:17 GMT, paulr@bix.com (paulr) wrote:

>I'll never really get the swing of batch processing... (*sigh*)
>Can anyone point out to me what I am doing wrong in the below
…[35 more quoted lines elided]…
>
You have specified the SPACE on the CLUSTER, which includes DATA and INDEX.  Remove the
CZLINDERS from the CLUSTER definition, add a DATA component definition with the CYLINDERS
option, and you should be all set

with kind regards

Volker Bandke
(BSP GmbH)
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: IDCAMS anyone?

- **From:** mark0young@aol.com (Mark0Young)
- **Date:** 1999-02-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19990217152715.00116.00001384@ngol06.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<36cc01a8.1286499@news3.ibm.net>`

```

One can specify the space allocation at either the cluster level, or at the
component level (DATA, INDEX), but not both.

I generally prefer specifying space allocation at the component level because
it gives me better control on the allocation instead of relying on IDCAMS to
make its own assumptions.

Generally the index allocation doesn't have to be anywhere near as large as the
data allocation--I generally find a few cylinders of data need just a few
tracks for the index. However, I have used several cylinders for the index when
the data has occupied a thousand or more cylinders. Of course, the real answer
on allocation is "it depends" and I find the LISTCAT listings a real friend for
adjusting space allocations.

Mark A. Young
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: IDCAMS anyone?

- **From:** "Joel C. Ewing" <jcewing@acm.org>
- **Date:** 1999-02-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36CD15C0.A59037F4@acm.org>`
- **References:** `<7aenr5$thk@lotho.delphi.com> <36cc01a8.1286499@news3.ibm.net>`

```
Volker Bandke wrote:
> 
> On 17 Feb 1999 15:39:17 GMT, paulr@bix.com (paulr) wrote:
…[36 more quoted lines elided]…
> option, and you should be all set
...

Unless you have an "unusual" case or really know what you are doing, I
wouldn't recommend ever giving explicit space for the INDEX component
(or giving a NAME parameter for the INDEX component either--let it
default).  Either specify space on the CLUSTER (which is used for the
DATA component) or specify SPACE for the DATA component.  Normally let
IDCAMS decide what is reasonable space for the INDEX.  When we get JCL
from vendors with INDEX space, it is invariably grossly overallocated. 
In your case above, the initial dataset DATA size requires only 10 index
records (1 record per CA, which in this case equals one DATA cylinder)! 
Depending on INDEX CISZ chosen by IDCAMS, a full cylinder probably has
space for at least 180 index records, maybe more, so 5 cylinders would
be considerable overkill.  I notice no DATA CISZ specification.  This
probably means a default of 32768, which may be good for sequential
batch, but questionable for either online applications or if majority of
accesses are random.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: IDCAMS anyone?

- **From:** paulr@bix.com (paulr)
- **Date:** 1999-02-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7ajp00$imc@lotho.delphi.com>`
- **References:** `<7aenr5$thk@lotho.delphi.com> <36cc01a8.1286499@news3.ibm.net> <36CD15C0.A59037F4@acm.org>`

```


Thanks guys, go to show how much I do not know. :) 
I undersrtand that when allocating indexes, you should
allocate 5 + alternate-key-length + (bse-key-length * #-possible-dups).
I take it from the comments here that such is *not* the case with 
the initial index, or even close? 

ALso, specifing the index and data sets separately worked, and
because I became very unsure about the sizes, I told it to 
allocate in RECORDS instead. I figured this was sort of a cheat
way around figuring out all the spacing considerations? 

Thanks
-Paul
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: IDCAMS anyone?

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1999-02-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-DD5GnFsdhd1b@Dwight_Miller.iix.com>`
- **References:** `<7aenr5$thk@lotho.delphi.com> <36cc01a8.1286499@news3.ibm.net> <36CD15C0.A59037F4@acm.org> <7ajp00$imc@lotho.delphi.com>`

```
On Fri, 19 Feb 1999 13:29:36, paulr@bix.com (paulr) wrote:

> ALso, specifing the index and data sets separately worked, and
> because I became very unsure about the sizes, I told it to 
> allocate in RECORDS instead. I figured this was sort of a cheat
> way around figuring out all the spacing considerations? 

Allocating in records will result in a much smaller than optimum CA 
(Potentially a single track CA) resulting in poor read and insert 
performance - multiple CA splits.  You are better of (SPEED wise) to 
allocate at the cylinder level.

From the "Using Datasets" manual:

--- just above the heading liste below:

"If the control area is smaller than a cylinder, its size will be an 
integral multiple of tracks".

Under the heading "Advantages of a large Control Area Size"

"Control area size has significant performance implications.   
One-cylinder control areas have the following advantages:

There is a smaller probability of control area splits.

The index is more consolidated.  One index record addresses all the 
control intervals in a control area.  If the control area is large, 
fewer index records and index levels are required.  For sequential 
access, a large control area decreases the number of reads of index 
records.

(--- my comments now - this next one is the biggie!--)

There are fewer sequence set records.  The sequence set record for a 
control area is ALWAYS  (emphasis mine) read for you.  Fewer records 
means less time spent reading them.

........"

There's more but that's the gist of what I'm getting at.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: IDCAMS anyone?

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Joel C. Ewing" <jcewing@acm.org>
- **Date:** 1999-02-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36CE5299.BCC3D48D@acm.org>`
- **References:** `<7aenr5$thk@lotho.delphi.com> <36cc01a8.1286499@news3.ibm.net> <36CD15C0.A59037F4@acm.org> <7ajp00$imc@lotho.delphi.com>`

```
The formula you gave involving alt-key-length is the formula for
calculating the RECSZ values for the DATA component of an alternate
index -- doesn't direcly relate to the space for the INDEX component of
either the base CLUSTER or the AIX.   The INDEX component always
requires one INDEX CI (Control Interval), recorded in the stats as one
INDEX record, per DATA CA (Control Area).  Since IDCAMS knows at
creation time what the CA size is and exactly how many CA's are in the
DATA component's primary and secondary allocations, it can compute the
exact space requirements for the INDEX component more easily than you
can.  The only case where it makes sense to explicitly give the INDEX
space is when you know the DATA component will eventually expand to many
secondary allocations and you want to allocate the INDEX at max required
size just to avoid fragmenting your volume with a large number of tiny
index secondary extents.

I personally like using RECORDS allocation myself because it is device
independent.  Several things to watch for:  
* Unless the entire dataset is small, you may need to experiment to be
sure your secondary allocation is large enough to exceed one cylinder. 
If both the primary and secondary allocation are larger than one
cylinder, then you will get a CA size of one cylinder and file
allocation in cylinder units, which tends to be optimum for large VSAM
datasets (it makes no difference here whether the original allocation is
in RECORDS, TRACKS, or CYLINDERS).
* RECORDS allocation presumes that maximum use is made of each CA and CI
in the DATA component.  If you define the CLUSTER with nonzero CI or CA
FREESPC parameters and want to be able to do an initial load of "n"
records into the CLUSTER, then the requested space in RECORDS needs to
be proportionally adjusted to a higher value
 n' = n /((1-(CIFREESPC/100))(1-(CAFREESPC/100))
* If you have adjusted the RECORDS parameter properly and the average
record length is correct, but the dataset still goes into secondary
allocation on initial loading with far fewer records, you may have one
of those rare cases with large keys that compress poorly, where the
default INDEX CISIZE chosen by IDCAMS may not be large enough to allow
use of all the CI's in a CA.  You may need to force a larger INDEX CISZ
by explicitly requesting a larger CISZ in the INDEX component.
* For RECORDS allocation of an AIX cluster, remember that the number of
records in this context is the number of unique alternate key values and
the record length is not the original base cluster record length but
given by your 5+altkeylength + basekeylen*nrdups formula, which is
usually much shorter than the base cluster record size.


paulr wrote:
> Thanks guys, go to show how much I do not know. :)
> I undersrtand that when allocating indexes, you should
…[71 more quoted lines elided]…
> : Joel C. Ewing, Fort Smith, AR        jcewing@acm.org
```

#### ↳ Re: IDCAMS anyone?

- **From:** Peter W. Smith <Peter_W._Smith@LNOTES3.bankofny.com>
- **Date:** 1999-02-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7afeef$q91$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`
- **References:** `<7aenr5$thk@lotho.delphi.com>`

```
In article <7aenr5$thk@lotho.delphi.com>,
  paulr@bix.com (paulr) wrote:
> I'll never really get the swing of batch processing... (*sigh*)
> Can anyone point out to me what I am doing wrong in the below
…[32 more quoted lines elided]…
>
You are specifying space allocation on the cluster and the index.
You should be defining space on the index and the data components.

 Example:

    DEFINE CLUSTER -
         (NAME(D9425$.ITA.TLFILE) -
         VOLUMES(STGC23) -
         FREESPACE(20 15) -
         KEYS(12 0) -
         RECORDSIZE(31 31) -
         SHAREOPTIONS(2) -
         INDEXED) -
    DATA(NAME(D9425$.ITA.TLFILE.DATA) -
         CYLINDERS(10 5) -
         CISIZE(4096)) -
    INDEX(NAME(D9425$.ITA.TLFILE.INDEX) -
         CYLINDERS(1 1) -
         CISIZE(1024))

-----------== Posted via Deja News, The Discussion Network ==----------
http://www.dejanews.com/       Search, Read, Discuss, or Start Your Own
```

#### ↳ Re: IDCAMS anyone?

- **From:** psychedelic-harry_SPAM_THIS_@mindless.com (Psychedelic Harry)
- **Date:** 1999-02-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<psychedelic-harry_SPAM_THIS_-1902990124280001@192.168.0.2>`
- **References:** `<7aenr5$thk@lotho.delphi.com>`

```
You have a "cylinders" in both the cluster and the index command...lose
one and let AMS do its job...this is what it is trying to tell you....

Either specify a space for the data cluster and lose the "cluster" space
command....or lose the index cluster command and let the data be allocated
in "cylinders (10 5)" and the index be allocated as (10 5) in tracks....

BTW, do you have SMS at your site?

I have found that SMS and AMS together can do a better job than a
human...and they have the added benefit of adjusting to new DASD types
without headache or redefination....if all you want is 10 cylinders, let
it move the stuff around.

In article <7aenr5$thk@lotho.delphi.com>, paulr@bix.com (paulr) wrote:
>[snip]           
>    DEFINE CLUSTER                   -                                  
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>             CYLINDERS(5 3))
```

```
understanding of COBOL, including GUI application development,

month or two?
```

<a id="t-3221bd88d3"></a>
## Thread: Help! Micro Focus Cobol Dos Extender! — 4 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers) · [`Help requests and how-to`](topics.md#help)_

### Help! Micro Focus Cobol Dos Extender!

- **From:** Steven Savage <badger@infinet.com>
- **Date:** 1999-02-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<LfWy2.884$f2.1150@news2>`

```
I am trying to track down an obscure problem in Micro Focus version 3.2 
(yes, 3.2).  An error occurred in the Dos Extender, called from the batch 
program:

Unrecoverable system interrupt 0D error (followed by three numbers that 
differe depending on the code module invovled in the error).

Does this sound familiar to anyone?  Please post or better yet email me.
```

#### ↳ Re: Help! Micro Focus Cobol Dos Extender!

- **From:** "Calle" <markus.carl@fen.baynet.de>
- **Date:** 1999-02-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7ahka9$3jk$1@freenet-b.fen.baynet.de>`
- **References:** `<LfWy2.884$f2.1150@news2>`

```
Out of what kind of batch program do you call the XM ?
If You call it out of a COBOL batch program, you don���t need to use the
extender, cause it���s already loaded with the startprogram. Just call the SUB
and it should work.
I hope this will help you,

Calle

Steven Savage schrieb in Nachricht ...
>I am trying to track down an obscure problem in Micro Focus version 3.2
>(yes, 3.2).  An error occurred in the Dos Extender, called from the batch
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
>------------------------------------------------------------------
```

#### ↳ Re: Help! Micro Focus Cobol Dos Extender!

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1999-02-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36cc611e.86340524@news1.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<LfWy2.884$f2.1150@news2>`

```
On Thu, 18 Feb 1999 15:23:23 GMT, Steven Savage <badger@infinet.com>
wrote:

>I am trying to track down an obscure problem in Micro Focus version 3.2 
>(yes, 3.2).  An error occurred in the Dos Extender, called from the batch 
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>Does this sound familiar to anyone?  Please post or better yet email me.

Yep.  Usually used an XM switch setting to overcome the problem.
Sometimes this is caused by call parm mismatches too.

Try SET XM=/P1

through /P9

Sometimes just SET XM=+JRUU1 will do it.  There is a large variety of
switches to try.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Help! Micro Focus Cobol Dos Extender!

- **From:** scm@bolo.freeserve.co.uk (Shaun C. Murray)
- **Date:** 1999-02-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36cdc1cf.22930842@news.enterprise.net>`
- **References:** `<LfWy2.884$f2.1150@news2> <36cc611e.86340524@news1.ibm.net>`

```
On Thu, 18 Feb 1999 18:53:15 GMT, redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
wrote:

>
>Yep.  Usually used an XM switch setting to overcome the problem.
>Sometimes this is caused by call parm mismatches too.

Yes. If you can test the program without using XM you may get a better
clue as to why it's Trap 0D'ing. Check for things like data not
initialized, indices out of range etc.

Lastly try debugging with either the XM debugger or through Xilerator.


I'd also reccomend getting hold of the 3.4 XM which I find a bit more
tolerant of newer pentium based systems than 3.2. Talk to the company
formerly known as Micro Focus (that's how they answer the phone these
days!) about getting a newer version of XM.

>
>Try SET XM=/P1
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>switches to try.

K is always a good one too and there are a couple that have numbers
after them which may work but take a lot of playing with. Usually /Pn
does it though.
```

<a id="t-d2c61839c1"></a>
## Thread: MF Cobol RETURN-CODE and Oracle 7.3.4 — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers) · [`Databases and SQL`](topics.md#databases)_

### MF Cobol RETURN-CODE and Oracle 7.3.4

- **From:** "Bob Stacklin" <robstack@columbus.rr.com>
- **Date:** 1999-02-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.database.oracle,comp.databases.oracle.misc,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<SQ0z2.955$_2.5060307@storm.twcol.com>`

```
   At my company, we are experiencing problems related to Oracle 7.3.4
compilation of COBOL programs.
  Programs compiled using the Oracle 7.3.4 libraries are completing with a
non zero return codes.  This is not occurring with programs compiled using
the Oracle 7.3.2 libraries.  I have isolated the differences to the
RETURN-CODE after the call of the "SQLBEX" module (below).  The programs
were executed using an Oracle 7.3.4 database, HP-UX 10.20, Pro*Cobol
11.30(Microfocus 4.05).

                DISPLAY 'CAT=', RETURN-CODE UPON CONSOLE
                MOVE 0 TO SQL-SQLETY

                CALL "SQLBEX" USING
                    SQLCTX
                    SQLEXD
                    SQLFPN
                DISPLAY 'CTX=', RETURN-CODE UPON CONSOLE

                IF SQLCODE IN SQLCA IS LESS THAN 0
                    THEN PERFORM 900-ABEND END-IF.
                DISPLAY 'SQLCODE=', SQLCODE IN SQLCA UPON CONSOLE.
                DISPLAY 'RET=', RETURN-CODE UPON CONSOLE.

     Executed using  Oracle 7.3.4 database
     7.3.2 Compiled Program:          7.3.4 Compiled program:
     BEFORE=+0001                      BEFORE=+0001
     AFTER=+0000                         AFTER=+5028
     SQLCODE=+000000000          SQLCODE=+000000000
     $echo $?                                 $echo $?
     0                                            196

    We have contacted Oracle , but have received no resolution.  Any
assistance would be appreciated.
Thanks,
Bob Stacklin
```

- **From:** NoLonger@ix.netcom.com (OldUncleMe)
- **Date:** 1999-02-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36cb7048.37857114@news>`

```
We've reached the subject of object oriented Cobol, but our text is out of date, showing what 
must now be seen as a bygone explanation of the topic.  (It was incomplete and awkward anyway)

Could someone please point out any internet reference or discussion that would aid the student
on this topic, please!  Pointers to relevant books as well would be greatly appreciated.

Realisticallly, is it possible to study OO/C without needing to be specific to a particular
implementation?  Is the material currently extant relevant in light of the potentially rapid
change of 'near future' standards?

(The lab uses MF v.3.2   rev.037.  I use Fujitsu v.3 as well.)

TS

 for a living that you occasionally get away for a while"  Skiing Cowboys
                       tenox+-+at+-+home+-+dot+-+com
```

### Re: Help wanted

- **From:** Dan Jezek <danjezek@pixi.com>
- **Date:** 1999-02-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36CE4253.FDD2D53F@pixi.com>`

```
Howard Brazie wrote:

>Do Hawaiians still refer to the older 48 states as "the states"?  I
> would have thought they had become accustomed to being one by now.

>I would contact a major headhunter company such as Romac (888)883-1936.

Hawaiians refer to the older 48 states simply as "mainland".  
They refer to white people as "haole".  (Stupid people).  
It gets tiring living here for a while.  Hawaii is nice to come visit,
but not to live here.  With the crappy money I make working at First
Hawaiian Bank doing mainframe COBOL, I pay for rent, food, gas and 
have almost nothing left.
Working as a computer programmer in Hawaii is equivalent to working as a
janitor in Indiana.
```

### Builing Final Application with Mercant (sic) NetExpress 3.0

- **From:** nick@asheath.demon.co.uk (Nick & Carol Lucas-White)
- **Date:** 1999-02-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36cda67a.74820@news.demon.co.uk>`

```
I'm told by my programmer that the option to build an .exe with
Netexpress does just that - it creates an exe but there's nothing to
tell you what DLL' s to package with it. Does anyone have any
knowledge of what to do or has even managed to achieve this ?

I'm starting to have grave doubts that it's possible to make up a
distributable package from this product at all.

Mercant (MF's new name) say it's too difficult to explain on the phone
but are happy to send someone out at one thousand pounds per day !!! -
God knows what we pay support for if that's their attitude,
```

```
Bob
```

<a id="t-a5f64ecc01"></a>
## Thread: Calling an external program in MF NetExpress — 3 msgs

### Calling an external program in MF NetExpress

- **From:** Renee Poerschke <poerschke@ksn.de>
- **Date:** 1999-02-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36CD669E.D5B0BAA0@ksn.de>`

```
Hi

I want to execute a programm (e.g. Word) from Micro Focus
Net Express. Has anyone solved this problem before? I can't
find a library function to do this

Renee
```

#### ↳ Re: Calling an external program in MF NetExpress

- **From:** smb@mfltdz.co.ukz (Steve Biggs)
- **Date:** 1999-02-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7ajre6$tbk@hyperion.mfltd.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<36CD669E.D5B0BAA0@ksn.de>`

```
Renee Poerschke <poerschke@ksn.de> wrote:
>I want to execute a programm (e.g. Word) from Micro Focus
>Net Express. Has anyone solved this problem before? I can't
>find a library function to do this

Hi Renee,
If you just want to start up Word and not do anything with it, then you 
could either call the Windows API "CreateProcessA" (using the right 
call-convention and parameters) or use the COBOL call CBL_EXEC_RUN_UNIT. 

If you want to create/amend/save Word documents from your COBOL program, 
you can use OLE automation from COBOL - there's an example of that in
\netexpress\base\demo\oledemos\report.

(The demo also demonstrates calling Excel, and some more general Object 
COBOL work, but this is quite easy to comment out. The current version of 
the demo uses the Word95 automation interface, rather than the newer 
Word97 interface, but the latter is just as accessible from COBOL.)

Steve.
MERANT Micro Focus Development.
```

#### ↳ Re: Calling an external program in MF NetExpress

- **From:** bob7536@aol.com (Bob7536)
- **Date:** 1999-02-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19990220200936.12657.00000849@ng150.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<36CD669E.D5B0BAA0@ksn.de>`

```
Hi Renee,

If you would like an example of the create process API please e-mail me.

Thanks,
Bob
```

## Thread: Fujitsu Compiler — 3 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### Fujitsu Compiler

- **From:** haize <i@hate.spam>
- **Date:** 1999-02-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36CE29EC.1A61979C@hate.spam>`

```
Ok, I downloaded the Fujitsu cobol compiler, and I think I got it pretty
much figured out. The only problem i'm having is when I try to link the
object code I get the following error:

NMAKE : fatal error U1077: 'C:\COBOL\PCOBOL32\LINK' : return code '0x68'

Stop.

Microsoft (R) 32-Bit Incremental Linker Version 3.00.5270
Copyright (C) Microsoft Corp 1992-1995. All rights reserved.

/OUT:C:\COBOL\PCOBOL32\HOMEWRK1.EXE
C:\cobol\PCOBOL32\homewrk1.OBJ
C:\COBOL\PCOBOL32\PROJECT.RES
C:\COBOL\PCOBOL32\F3BICIMP.LIB
C:\COBOL\PCOBOL32\LIBC.LIB
C:\COBOL\PCOBOL32\KERNEL32.LIB
C:\COBOL\PCOBOL32\USER32.LIB
LINK : fatal error LNK1561: entry point must be defined
Linking files ended.

Anyone know whats up? Thanks
```

#### ↳ Re: Fujitsu Compiler

- **From:** haize <i@hate.spam>
- **Date:** 1999-02-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36CE4CB8.FBA3433D@hate.spam>`
- **References:** `<36CE29EC.1A61979C@hate.spam>`

```


haize wrote:

> Ok, I downloaded the Fujitsu cobol compiler, and I think I got it pretty
> much figured out. The only problem i'm having is when I try to link the
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
> Anyone know whats up? Thanks

In case anyone else has this problem I figured id post the answer here. When
compiling the source code with the Fujitsu compiler, you need to add a MAIN
clause to the compiler. This tells the compiler this is a stand-alone
program and isn't called by another one, and so it is compiled accordingly.
Once it is compiled right, there isn't any problem
linking it.
```

#### ↳ Re: Fujitsu Compiler

- **From:** "R. Hayes" <rmch@cadvision.com>
- **Date:** 1999-02-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36d0239b.0@news.cadvision.com>`
- **References:** `<36CE29EC.1A61979C@hate.spam>`

```
Did you make sure that your program is linked with the MAIN directive
(assuming there's no called programs).

haize wrote in message <36CE29EC.1A61979C@hate.spam>...
```

## Thread: Perform "Standards" question — 2 msgs

_Topics: [`Language features and syntax`](topics.md#syntax) · [`COBOL standards (ANS, ISO, 74/85/2002/2014)`](topics.md#standards)_

### Re: Perform "Standards" question

- **From:** Barticus@att.spam.net (Randall Bart)
- **Date:** 1999-02-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36cc8ef7.74834268@netnews>`
- **References:** `<MPG.112a77f51ef0dd60989682@news.texas.net> <36C34D40.8A8F6343@nospam.ibm.net> <36c38066.9968722@netnews> <7a7j2d$grq$1@fenix.maxitel.pt>`

```
'Twas Sun, 14 Feb 1999 22:21:50 +0100, when "PAULO CRUZ"
<scruz_paulo@mail.teleweb.pt> illuminated comp.lang.cobol thusly:

>1.st - I don't agree neither with a 'perform' performing the same paragraph
>it belongs to, nor the 'go to' the paragraph name.

<English>
"don't ... neither" is a double negative.  I take it you mean to say "I
agree with neither a 'perform' performing the same paragraph it belongs
to, nor the 'go to' the paragraph name."
</English>

>2nd. - I assume the problem here is lock control, isn't it ? 

No.  The cases where I most recently wrote a GO TO like this involved an
inquiry.  Actually there were multiple GOs because there were multiple
reasons for rejecting records.  This paragraph was called from two
different places, and they had each implemented some of this filtering
differently, so I moved more filtering down into the file reading
paragraph.
```

#### ↳ Re: Perform "Standards" question

- **From:** "PAULO CRUZ" <scruz_paulo@mail.teleweb.pt>
- **Date:** 1999-02-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7al03q$1iu$1@fenix.maxitel.pt>`
- **References:** `<MPG.112a77f51ef0dd60989682@news.texas.net> <36C34D40.8A8F6343@nospam.ibm.net> <36c38066.9968722@netnews> <7a7j2d$grq$1@fenix.maxitel.pt> <36cc8ef7.74834268@netnews>`

```

Randall Bart wrote<36cc8ef7.74834268@netnews>...
>'Twas Sun, 14 Feb 1999 22:21:50 +0100, when "PAULO CRUZ"
><scruz_paulo@mail.teleweb.pt> illuminated comp.lang.cobol thusly:
>
>>1.st - I don't agree neither with a 'perform' performing the same
paragraph
>>it belongs to, nor the 'go to' the paragraph name.
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
></English>

Hoping not to be betrayed by my english:

A - Yes, you took it correctly and you've just helped me improving
 my english, which not being my native tongue sometimes
corners me into difficult situations like the one you caught me into...
Finding myself very often insisting on the correctness of  the usage
of my mother tongue, I'm really glad to find purists here.
Thanks for your valuable criticism.

B - As for the COBOL (another English dialect among so many ones
in this monotonous world!) which brought us here, I personally would
have handled those record rejecting by means of a condition just
appended to the perform command. Anyway, I'd rather do GOTO the
paragraph name (even against my preferences) than PERFORM the
paragraph name under which I'm cycling (what seems actually awful).

Rgds
P.Cruz

>>2nd. - I assume the problem here is lock control, isn't it ?
```

## Thread: OO Cobol ? (Cobol compiler needed....) — 7 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers) · [`Object-oriented COBOL`](topics.md#oo)_

### OO Cobol ? (Cobol compiler needed....)

- **From:** NoLonger@ix.netcom.com (OldUncleMe)
- **Date:** 1999-02-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36ccee44.14660149@news>`

```
Our text is out of date, showing a bygone explanation of the topic.  (It was incomplete and
awkward anyway)  I'm having a lot of trouble understanding how the objects work together with
the main program and relate to each other.  (I bet this has never happened to anyone
before...heh)  Could someone please point out any internet reference or discussion that would
aid the student on this topic, please!  Pointers to relevant books as well would be greatly
appreciated.

Realisticallly, is it possible to study OO/C without needing to be specific to a particular
implementation?  Is the material currently out there relevant in light of the potentially rapid
change of 'near future' standards?

(The lab uses MF v.3.2   rev.037.  I use Fujitsu v.3 as well.)

TS


"It's important when you ride snorting, kicking 2000 pound horned animals
 for a living that you occasionally get away for a while"  Skiing Cowboys
                       tenox+-+at+-+home+-+dot+-+com
```

#### ↳ Re: OO Cobol ? (Cobol compiler needed....)

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@inospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-02-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7akaue$t3v@sjx-ixn10.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<36ccee44.14660149@news>`

```
I keep hoping someone else will respond to this note.  I tried to respond
privately, but can't get a good email address.

Let me say this much:

Micro Focus 3.2 and Fujitsu 3 support for OO are both quite out-of-date when
it comes to what is in the draft Standard (as is IBM's OO support).  Each of
the 3 is incompatible with the others.  There is currently a session
scheduled at the next J4 meeting when they hope to revise (yet again) the OO
specification in response to their last internal review.

Given all of this,
  I think that any text that matches a current compiler is OK to learn OO
COBOL from.  All you need to know is that some features may be added, other
may be deleted, and still others modified - before there is ever a truly
"Standard" definition of OO COBOL.

If you haven't seen it, there is PDF "white paper" on the OO features in the
draft Standard (as of a couple of years ago) that you can get via the
Fujitsu web page (www.adtools.com).  Also, you can send a note to the chair
of J4 - and ask him for a copy of the "concepts" section on OO.  He can be
reached at
      doncobol@mediaone.net
I don't know if you will get it in hardcopy or email - and it is out of date
too.

I hope something in here helps at least a little.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: OO Cobol ? (Cobol compiler needed....)

```
-->>                       tenox+-+at+-+home+-+dot+-+com

The anti-spam stuff, sorry, I apologize.  Since leaving netcom with >350KB per day of spam, I
have rigged all  my client's copies of my return addresses and now have -0-KB per day of spam!!
That's great.

I appreciate the information you've given me on the current status.  I'm considering launching
into study of Java, C and C++ at this point--nearly through with the Cobol course.  That's all
they offer at my current institution, 3 sections of cobol and one of CICS.  The cics looks
interesting, in a get-a-job sort of way  :)  Visual basic, v.4, is all the rest of their
offering!  (an intro course at that...)  I liked vb a lot, but found it limiting in terms of
creating new routines and deployment on computers without the runtimes installed.  The final
product is a little topheavy, and only viable on wintel systems.  It is a good front end for
any code, practically, on wintel.  

It seemed as we finish this section, Cobol 3, and are introduced to OO concepts, in small part,
that my new familiarity with cobol would be of assistance in learning and employing object
cobol.  But then the limitations of the text and the standards extant being only applicable by
vendor (and realistically, I don't have one!) make it seem that there is little course to
follow in this enterprise unless I start work in a shop that employs OO....  It is by
definition a shame that standards, such as the non-existant COBOL-97 standard cannot be agreed
upon and fielded by vendors in a timely fashion.  Just putting it out, get it out there so
people can use it.  Let vendors upgrade products on the fly like the pc world, and issue
updates to their products (and for free as well.)  

OK, mission critical requirements and arguments against (the above) aside for the moment, look
at another standards issue:  Was it the IETC or IIIE or whoever putzed about with the v.90
modem standard for so long, with debate between vendors and foot dragging by the "Committee"
taking so long to issue the standard that by the time it was sent out, everyone had separate
work arounds and modem customers didn't realize any value from the standard, because in the
end, it was the damn phone lines and CO switching and node implementations (filters and
modulation/remodulation) that prevent it from working in the majority of cases.  Smart
consumers with need or desire have found alternatives:  cable modem (I have had it from the
first day it was available on my street) or xDSL, or satellite or a string and two cans (an
actual improvement on v.90 in most areas!!)

Well, it's the concepts that I'm interested in so I think I'm bound to look into a language
that employs OO natively, and thus its use is defined.  It's silly to try this in cobol, except
to pass our last exam:  there is no road to follow, what roads there are lead to cliffs! 

I've been looking over the links on Cetus, very interesting.      /ts

PS:  my addy is in all my .sig's!  I hope it to be discernable:  tenox+-+at+-+home+-+dot+-+com
         as:  tenox@home.com



"It's important when you ride snorting, kicking 2000 pound horned animals
 for a living that you occasionally get away for a while"  Skiing Cowboys
                       tenox+-+at+-+home+-+dot+-+com
```

#### ↳ Re: OO Cobol ? (Cobol compiler needed....)

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1999-02-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7akjvo$qdv$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<36ccee44.14660149@news>`

```
I missed this the first time, but just read Bill's response.
I concur with what Bill said regarding standards/oops,
but would add a bit.  I think if you are going to learn oops,
I would buy a current version, not learn on one of the
earlier "student/give-away" versions.

For both MF and Fujitsu the current versions of the software
are much closer to "a standard" ... Bill is quite correct in that
the standard is not there.  However, things have firmed up a
whole lot since the early versions came out, and current
versions are bound to be closer to the emerging standard. You
have to keep in mind that OOPS is a multi-language standard.

Whatever emerges has to conform with calling conventions
that have come into being through VB, OCX, C++, etc.  The
current set do.  That is not true of Version 3 Fujitsu, however.
The MAJOR difference between 3 an 4 is in the OOPS. The
standard Cobol's are virtually identical.

I am not sure if student versions of current stuff are there or
not.

OldUncleMe wrote in message <36ccee44.14660149@news>...
>Our text is out of date, showing a bygone explanation of the topic.  (It
was incomplete and
>awkward anyway)  I'm having a lot of trouble understanding how the objects
work together with
>the main program and relate to each other.  (I bet this has never happened
to anyone
>before...heh)  Could someone please point out any internet reference or
discussion that would
>aid the student on this topic, please!  Pointers to relevant books as well
would be greatly
>appreciated.
>
>Realisticallly, is it possible to study OO/C without needing to be specific
to a particular
>implementation?  Is the material currently out there relevant in light of
the potentially rapid
>change of 'near future' standards?
>
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>                       tenox+-+at+-+home+-+dot+-+com
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: OO Cobol ? (Cobol compiler needed....)

- **From:** NoLonger@ix.netcom.com (OldUncleMe)
- **Date:** 1999-02-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36cde75f.18043457@news>`
- **References:** `<36ccee44.14660149@news> <7akjvo$qdv$1@news.igs.net>`

```
It was: Fri, 19 Feb 1999 16:19:47 -0500 and with STARTLING insight,  ""Donald Tees"
<donald@willmack.com>" posted "Re: OO Cobol   ? (Cobol compiler needed....)" to
"comp.lang.cobol" :

-->I missed this the first time, but just read Bill's response.
-->I concur with what Bill said regarding standards/oops,
-->but would add a bit.  I think if you are going to learn oops,
-->I would buy a current version, not learn on one of the
-->earlier "student/give-away" versions.
-->
-->For both MF and Fujitsu the current versions of the software
-->are much closer to "a standard" ... Bill is quite correct in that
-->the standard is not there.  However, things have firmed up a
-->whole lot since the early versions came out, and current
-->versions are bound to be closer to the emerging standard. You
-->have to keep in mind that OOPS is a multi-language standard.
-->
-->Whatever emerges has to conform with calling conventions
-->that have come into being through VB, OCX, C++, etc.  The
-->current set do.  That is not true of Version 3 Fujitsu, however.
-->The MAJOR difference between 3 an 4 is in the OOPS. The
-->standard Cobol's are virtually identical.
-->
-->I am not sure if student versions of current stuff are there or
-->not.
-->

Thanks for your reply.  I'm pretty sure that there are not any 'student' versions of OOcobol.
As we approached the topic at the end of our 3rd quarter of cobol, it looked very interesting.
Esp with the tie-in to the methodology of other languages.  But the text is hopelessly out of
date.  There is not really a cross-vendor standard.  There is no help from the Cobol (ahem,
'97) standards committee at this point in time.  It seems as futile to learn OO cobol unless
employed to use it as it would be to learn Microfocus screen extensions.  They are a dead end.

My intent in learning cobol in the first place was to be able to write utility and network
client/server programs for wintel, and secondarily, for other platforms.  There is still a lot
of room for this, just that perhaps cobol is not a very choice language for this application.
I considered that cobol would be like learning latin when applied to studying other, probably
more applicable languages--having familiarity with programming processes, logic and procedures
and with the reasoning dictated by use of such fancy abacuses.  Thus the study of OO Cobol
seemed a logical extension to this premise.  Guess not.  I'm pretty disappointed.


"It's important when you ride snorting, kicking 2000 pound horned animals
 for a living that you occasionally get away for a while"  Skiing Cowboys
                       tenox+-+at+-+home+-+dot+-+com
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OO Cobol ? (Cobol compiler needed....)

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@inospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-02-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7al5lo$6rb@sjx-ixn5.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<36ccee44.14660149@news> <7akjvo$qdv$1@news.igs.net> <36cde75f.18043457@news>`

```
OldUncleMe wrote in message <36cde75f.18043457@news>...
   <much snippage>
>-->
>-->I am not sure if student versions of current stuff are there or
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>Thanks for your reply.  I'm pretty sure that there are not any 'student'
versions of OOcobol.


I am 90% certain that there is an "Academic version" of NetExpress from
Merant (aka Micro Focus) - which does include "full" OO support.  It isn't
free and it does have other "academic" restrictions - but I think it might
meet your needs.
```

```
Dear All,

BM International Ltd have set up two new IT Training discussion lists for IT
Training companies/consultants* and IT customers to discuss IT Training
issues whatever the subject(CICS, DB2, A Series, NT, UNIX etc.).  One is for
the UK and there is another for all non-UK companies.

This is a brand new list (initialised last week) , and I hope it will be of
benefit to your company.  If you would like to join please visit our web site
at www.BMInternational.co.uk.

If you have any queries, please e-mail me at
James_Foster@BMInternational.co.uk.

Thanks for your time.


James Foster
BM International Ltd

Home Page: www.BMInternational.co.uk

*IT Training Companies/consultants, please e-mail me for terms and conditions
of applications.
```

## Thread: SORT error message with Fujitsu Compiler — 7 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers) · [`VSAM, files, sorting`](topics.md#files)_

### SORT error message with Fujitsu Compiler

- **From:** smiley@izap.com (Smiley Mann)
- **Date:** 1999-02-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36cf1356.1323148@news.izap.com>`

```
dear friends,

        this is a suprogram i'm doing for school.  i originally wrote
it on a MicroFocus compiler.   but my MicroFocus compiler has a bug so
i decided to use my Fujitsu Compiler.

this is the error i got when compiling:

A:\Input13.cbl 40: JMN2896I-S  SORT-MERGE FILE CAN ONLY SPECIFY 	
				FILE-IDENTIFIER IN ASSIGN CLAUSE.

i'm sure it's just a syntax thing.  could any of you enlighten me?
here is my source:

 ID DIVISION.
 PROGRAM-ID. INPUT13.
 AUTHOR. BRADEN BADUA.

 ENVIRONMENT DIVISION.
 CONFIGURATION SECTION.
 INPUT-OUTPUT SECTION.
 FILE-CONTROL.

     SELECT TRANSACTION-FILE
         ASSIGN TO "A:\CBL2TRN.DAT"
         ORGANIZATION IS LINE SEQUENTIAL.

     SELECT SORT-FILE
         ASSIGN TO "C:\SORT.DAT".

     SELECT INDEX-FILE
         ASSIGN TO "A:\CBL2OIMF.DAT"
         ORGANIZATION IS INDEXED
         ACCESS MODE IS SEQUENTIAL
         RECORD KEY IS PART-NUMBER-OUT.

 DATA DIVISION.
 FILE SECTION.
 FD  TRANSACTION-FILE
     LABEL RECORDS ARE STANDARD
     DATA RECORD IS TRANSACTION-REC.
 01  TRANSACTION-REC.
     05  LOCATION-TRANS          PIC 9(02).
     05  PART-NUMBER-TRANS.
         10  FIRST-PART          PIC X(01).
         10  REMAINING-PART      PIC X(05).
     05  DESCRIPTION-TRANS.
         10  FIRST-CHARACTER     PIC X(01).
         10  REMAINING-CHARACTER PIC X(14).
     05  QUANTITY-TRANS          PIC 9(02).
     05  UNIT-PRICE-TRANS        PIC 9(03).
     05  FILLER                  PIC X(02).

*********ERROR IS HIGHLIGHTED HERE*****
 SD  SORT-FILE.
 01  SORT-REC.
     05  FILLER                  PIC X(02).
     05  PART-NUMBER-SORT        PIC X(06).
     05  FILLER                  PIC X(22).


 FD  INDEX-FILE
     LABEL RECORDS ARE STANDARD
     BLOCK CONTAINS 0 RECORDS
     RECORD CONTAINS 30 CHARACTERS
     DATA RECORD IS INDEX-REC.
 01  INDEX-REC.
     05  LOCATION-OUT            PIC X(02).
     05  PART-NUMBER-OUT         PIC X(06).
     05  DESCRIPTION-OUT         PIC X(15).
     05  QUANTITY-OUT            PIC X(02).
     05  UNIT-PRICE-OUT          PIC X(03).
     05  FILLER                  PIC X(02).

 WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
 77  EOF-SW                      PIC X(01) VALUE 'N'.
     88  ITS-EOF                           VALUE 'Y'.

 01  80-BLANK                    PIC X(78) VALUE SPACES.

 01  VALID-WRITE                 PIC 9(01) VALUE 0.
 01  NULL-VARIABLE               PIC X(01) VALUE SPACE.

 LINKAGE SECTION.
 01  PUSH-VALUE                  PIC 9(02).

 SCREEN SECTION.
 01  SCREEN1 BACKGROUND-COLOR 0.
     05  BLANK SCREEN.

 01  SCREEN2 BACKGROUND-COLOR 5.
     05  LINE 1 COLUMN 2     PIC X(78) FROM 80-BLANK.
     05  LINE 2 COLUMN 2     PIC X(78) FROM 80-BLANK.
     05  LINE 3 COLUMN 2     PIC X(78) FROM 80-BLANK.
     05  LINE 4 COLUMN 2     PIC X(78) FROM 80-BLANK.
     05  LINE 5 COLUMN 2     PIC X(78) FROM 80-BLANK.

 01  SCREEN3 BACKGROUND-COLOR 0.
     05  LINE 3 COLUMN 29  VALUE 'CREATING NEW MASTER FILE'.
     05  LINE 6 COLUMN 2  PIC X(78) FROM 80-BLANK.

 PROCEDURE DIVISION.
 001-MAIN-MODULE.
*    PERFORM 800-OPEN-TRANSACTION.
*    PERFORM 100-TRANSACTION-FILE-ROUTINE UNTIL ITS-EOF.
*    PERFORM 850-CLOSE-TRANSACTION.
*    CLOSE TRANSACTION-FILE.
     PERFORM 200-SORT.
     PERFORM 300-DISPLAY-MASTER-FILE.
     EXIT PROGRAM.

 100-TRANSACTION-FILE-ROUTINE.
     DISPLAY SCREEN1
     DISPLAY SCREEN2
     DISPLAY SCREEN3
     DISPLAY ' IF THERE ARE NO MORE RECORDS'
     DISPLAY 'TYPE 00 IN LOCATION FIELD'
     DISPLAY 'LOCATION: '
     ACCEPT LOCATION-TRANS
     IF LOCATION-TRANS = '00'
         MOVE 'Y' TO EOF-SW
     ELSE
         DISPLAY 'PART NUMBER: '
         ACCEPT PART-NUMBER-TRANS
         DISPLAY 'DESCRIPTION: '
         ACCEPT DESCRIPTION-TRANS
         DISPLAY 'QUANTITY: '
         ACCEPT QUANTITY-TRANS
         DISPLAY 'UNIT PRICE: '
         ACCEPT UNIT-PRICE-TRANS
         INITIALIZE VALID-WRITE
         CALL 'VALID13' USING LOCATION-TRANS,
                              FIRST-PART,
                              REMAINING-PART,
                              FIRST-CHARACTER,
                              VALID-WRITE
         IF VALID-WRITE = 0
             WRITE TRANSACTION-REC
         ELSE
             CONTINUE
         END-IF
     END-IF.

 200-SORT.
     SORT SORT-FILE
         ON ASCENDING KEY PART-NUMBER-SORT
         USING TRANSACTION-FILE
         OUTPUT PROCEDURE IS 400-OUTPUT-PROCEDURE.

 300-DISPLAY-MASTER-FILE.
     PERFORM 1000-OPEN-INPUT-INDEX.
     DISPLAY SCREEN1.
     PERFORM UNTIL ITS-EOF
         READ INDEX-FILE
             AT END
                 MOVE 'Y' TO EOF-SW
             NOT AT END
                 DISPLAY INDEX-REC
         END-READ
     END-PERFORM.
     ACCEPT NULL-VARIABLE
     PERFORM 1050-CLOSE-INPUT-INDEX.

 400-OUTPUT-PROCEDURE.
     PERFORM 900-OPEN-OUTPUT-INDEX
     PERFORM UNTIL ITS-EOF
         RETURN SORT-FILE
             AT END
                 MOVE 'Y' TO EOF-SW
             NOT AT END
                 MOVE SORT-REC TO INDEX-REC
                 WRITE INDEX-REC
                     INVALID KEY DISPLAY 'ERROR'
                 END-WRITE
         END-RETURN
     END-PERFORM
     PERFORM 950-CLOSE-OUTPUT-INDEX.

 800-OPEN-TRANSACTION.
     MOVE 'N' TO EOF-SW.
     OPEN OUTPUT TRANSACTION-FILE.

 850-CLOSE-TRANSACTION.
     CLOSE TRANSACTION-FILE.

 900-OPEN-OUTPUT-INDEX.
     MOVE 'N' TO EOF-SW.
     OPEN INPUT INDEX-FILE.

 950-CLOSE-OUTPUT-INDEX.
     CLOSE INDEX-FILE.

 1000-OPEN-INPUT-INDEX.
     MOVE 'N' TO EOF-SW.
     OPEN INPUT INDEX-FILE.

 1050-CLOSE-INPUT-INDEX.
     CLOSE INDEX-FILE.
```

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@inospam.netcom.com>

```
The weekly comment about Fujitsu's non-Standard implementation of SORT.

Change the item in the ASSIGN part of the file associated with an SD from a
"literal" to a file-name (not in quotes)

and DEMAND a refund from Fujitsu for their not fixing their KNOWN
non-conformance to the ANSI/ISO Standard!!!
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: SORT error message with Fujitsu Compiler

- **From:** Barticus@att.spam.net (Randall Bart)
- **Date:** 1999-02-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36cfd5b4.101799203@netnews>`
- **References:** `<36cf1356.1323148@news.izap.com> <7an81j$ibv@dfw-ixnews9.ix.netcom.com>`

```
'Twas Sat, 20 Feb 1999 13:02:11 -0800, when "William M. Klein"
<wmklein@inospam.netcom.com> illuminated comp.lang.cobol thusly:

>The weekly comment about Fujitsu's non-Standard implementation of SORT.
>

>non-conformance to the ANSI/ISO Standard!!!

What standard?  The ASSIGN clause is 100% implementor defined.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: SORT error message with Fujitsu Compiler

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1999-02-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36d07d91.10971832@news1.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<36cf1356.1323148@news.izap.com> <7an81j$ibv@dfw-ixnews9.ix.netcom.com> <36cfd5b4.101799203@netnews>`

```
On 21 Feb 1999 21:04:06 GMT, Barticus@att.spam.net (Randall Bart)
wrote:


>What standard?  The ASSIGN clause is 100% implementor defined.
>

Actually the standard says that teh assign can be assigned to a
Literal or symbolic name.  Fujitsu chokes on assignment to a literal,
thus is non standard. (But I can see the arguement either way on
this). They treat this particular assignment as a comment anyway, and
create their own, verified unique, sort work file - reguardless of the
name you specify.  It bit me once, but it's really not an issue
anymore.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: SORT error message with Fujitsu Compiler

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@inospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-02-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7aq068$jlo@dfw-ixnews12.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<36cf1356.1323148@news.izap.com> <7an81j$ibv@dfw-ixnews9.ix.netcom.com> <36cfd5b4.101799203@netnews> <36d07d91.10971832@news1.ibm.net>`

```
Thane Hubbell wrote in message <36d07d91.10971832@news1.ibm.net>...
>On 21 Feb 1999 21:04:06 GMT, Barticus@att.spam.net (Randall Bart)
>wrote:
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>

I think this is what really BUGS me so much about this.  If Fujitsu were to
just fix their compiler to support the required syntax, there is really NO
need whatsoever that they do anything at all with the system-name specified
in the ASSIGN clause.  In fact, most of the compilers that I know that do
accept the syntax, do ignore it completely.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: SORT error message with Fujitsu Compiler

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@inospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-02-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7apvk6$me9@sjx-ixn6.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<36cf1356.1323148@news.izap.com> <7an81j$ibv@dfw-ixnews9.ix.netcom.com> <36cfd5b4.101799203@netnews>`

```
Randall Bart wrote in message <36cfd5b4.101799203@netnews>...
>'Twas Sat, 20 Feb 1999 13:02:11 -0800, when "William M. Klein"
><wmklein@inospam.netcom.com> illuminated comp.lang.cobol thusly:
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>>Change the item in the ASSIGN part of the file associated with an SD from
a
>>"literal" to a file-name (not in quotes)
>>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>--


I am afraid that I must say "wrong, Randall".

The *SEMANTICS* of the ASSIGN clause are totally implementor-defined.  The
SYNTAX is not at all implementor-defined.  A conforming implementation
ABSOLUTELY  POSITIVELY must support both a literal and a system-name.  (If
you want to see how the Standard documents a feature where the implementor
is allowed to support only one of multiple options, read the RERUN clause
syntax rules.)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: SORT error message with Fujitsu Compiler

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Barticus@att.spam.net (Randall Bart)
- **Date:** 1999-02-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36d91634.47278286@netnews>`
- **References:** `<36cf1356.1323148@news.izap.com> <7an81j$ibv@dfw-ixnews9.ix.netcom.com> <36cfd5b4.101799203@netnews> <7apvk6$me9@sjx-ixn6.ix.netcom.com>`

```
'Twas Sun, 21 Feb 1999 13:56:55 -0800, when "William M. Klein"
<wmklein@inospam.netcom.com> illuminated comp.lang.cobol thusly:

>Randall Bart wrote in message <36cfd5b4.101799203@netnews>...

>>What standard?  The ASSIGN clause is 100% implementor defined.

>I am afraid that I must say "wrong, Randall".
>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>syntax rules.)

AIUI the standard only makes it necessary to support at least one literal
and at least one system-name.  Maybe there is a valid literal but no one
has found it yet.  B^)
```

```
travel I've been doing.  But I've been able to catch up on my trade
magazine reading.  COBOL has been mentioned quite a bit.
```

## Thread: RM-COBOL runtime error #204 — 4 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### RM-COBOL runtime error #204

- **From:** Bert van de Bovenkamp <bert.van.de.bovenkamp@tip.nl>
- **Date:** 1999-02-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36CEAB2D.A34E3C05@tip.nl>`

```
Hello,

I receive a runtime error #204 while running a program.
(RM-COBOL under MS-DOS)

I know it has something to do with insufficient memory.
The program uses a couple of huge tables and it's not possible to make
them smaller.
The moment I call another program from within this program the error
#204 occurs.

Is it possible to use the upper or other memory (switch or parameter)
with RM-COBOL.

Greetings,

Bert van de Bovenkamp
The Netherlands.
```

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7an8me$9un$1@diana.bcn.ttd.net>`
- **References:** `<36CEAB2D.A34E3C05@tip.nl>`

```
Yes, using version 6 or upper.


Bert van de Bovenkamp escribi� en mensaje <36CEAB2D.A34E3C05@tip.nl>...
>Hello,
>
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

#### ↳ Re: RM-COBOL runtime error #204

- **From:** "PAULO CRUZ" <scruz_paulo@mail.teleweb.pt>
- **Date:** 1999-02-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7an165$ait$1@fenix.maxitel.pt>`
- **References:** `<36CEAB2D.A34E3C05@tip.nl>`

```

Bert van de Bovenkamp wrote <36CEAB2D.A34E3C05@tip.nl>...
>I receive a runtime error #204 while running a program.
>(RM-COBOL under MS-DOS)
>I know it has something to do with insufficient memory.
Just off the top of my head, I'd say that error has to do
with a called program not being accessible. I'm not sure
such an inaccessibility error status means scarcity
of memory - as far as I'm able to recall, it should mean
unresolved path or wrong compiled version (as seen
by the underlying runtime).

>The program uses a couple of huge tables and it's not possible to make
>them smaller.
Sure? Not even writing their contents down to a file (or files)?

>The moment I call another program from within this program the error
>#204 occurs.
See!?

>Is it possible to use the upper or other memory (switch or parameter)
>with RM-COBOL.
I lost contact with rm-cobol's *.cobs running under Dos for
sometime now, but remember that if you had your DOS
memory management well tuned, nammely if you had
free Upper Memory Blocks, then the runtime might use them
as well. But all this should be implemented on the Dos front.
As for the runtime parameters, you may reduce memory alloccated
to sorting (t=?/may be zero if you don't sort/merge), reduce the
accept/display buffering (b parameter) and little else. The terminfo
file can also be modified and give you something more of what you
need. You can also switch off the pop-up windows manager
if you don't use that feature.
Anyway I can tell you that once I had a couple hundred programs
running in Dos (Novell network), windowing at full steam(Panels),
sort/merging, lots of tables, permanent sub-program calling (and
cancelling) and I was even able to call a very interesting tool that
Liant sold at that time and that enabled you to display true
graphics of data supplied to an appropriate template. Well, this
utility, unlike Rm-runtime, depended on the extended memory
available. One feature I used not to be bothered by memory
complaints was 'overlay' sections.
Luckily all those programs (in a newer version) are running
under Unix and I had no more concerns on the subject.
Anyway , let's hope someone may turn out with more
helpful advice.


>
>Greetings,
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

#### ↳ Re: RM-COBOL runtime error #204

- **From:** nop06685@mail.telepac.pt (Frederico Fonseca)
- **Date:** 1999-02-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36cf31f2.1002138@news.indigo.ie>`
- **References:** `<36CEAB2D.A34E3C05@tip.nl>`

```
On Sat, 20 Feb 1999 13:31:41 +0100, Bert van de Bovenkamp
<bert.van.de.bovenkamp@tip.nl> wrote:

>Hello,
>
>I receive a runtime error #204 while running a program.
>(RM-COBOL under MS-DOS)
>
Error 204 has to do with calling a program that is either not
available to the runtime or that is not a valid RM/COBOL program
with an internal version supported by the runtime you are using.

This means that if you have a program that was created using
a newer compiler and if that programs used options not available
on the runtime you are using them you will get this error even
if the program is a valid RM/COBOL program.

Hope this clarify.


Frederico Fonseca

RM/COBOL technical support in Portugal
```

```
Can anyone give me an example of a simply array using Cobol. I'm trying to
learn this language and it would help to see an example,
thanks
abcall@sisna.com
```

#### ↳ Re: array

- **From:** terrywin.nospam@spectra.net (Terry Winchester)
- **Date:** 1999-02-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36cf0589.22563528@supernews.spectra.net>`
- **References:** `<36cedcf0.0@news.sisna.com>`

```
On Sat, 20 Feb 1999 09:09:54 -0700, "Arlene B. Call"
<abcall@sisna.com> wrote:

>Can anyone give me an example of a simply array using Cobol. I'm trying to
>learn this language and it would help to see an example,
>thanks
>abcall@sisna.com
>

Working-Storage..........
01  Idx                    Pic S9(3)  Value +0    Comp-3.
01  Nbr                    Pic  9(2)  Value 0.

01  The-Table.
    05  An-Array   occurs 10 times.
        10  An-Element     Pic  9(2). 

Procedure Division.......
Move Zeros to The-Table.

Perform Varying Idx from 1 by 1
Until Idx Greater Than 10
    Add 1 to Nbr
    Move Nbr to An-Element (Idx)
End-Perform.

Perform Varying Idx from 1 by 1
Until Idx Greater Than 10
    Display An-Element (Idx)
End-Perform.

HTH
Terry
```

#### ↳ Re: array

- **From:** vbandke@ibm.net (Volker Bandke)
- **Date:** 1999-02-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36cfdc99.69351402@news3.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<36cedcf0.0@news.sisna.com>`

```
On Sat, 20 Feb 1999 09:09:54 -0700, "Arlene B. Call" <abcall@sisna.com> wrote:

>Can anyone give me an example of a simply array using Cobol. I'm trying to
>learn this language and it would help to see an example,
>thanks
>abcall@sisna.com
>
Data Division.

01 German-Weekday-Table.
	05 PIC X(10) VALUE 'Montag'.
	05 PIC X(10) VALUE 'Dienstag'.
	05 PIC X(10) VALUE 'Mittwoch.
	05 PIC X(10) VALUE 'Donnerstag'.
	05 PIC X(10) VALUE 'Freitag'.
	05 PIC X(10) VALUE 'Sonnabend'.
	05 PIC X(10) VALUE 'Sonntag'.
01 REDEFINES German-Weekday-Table.
	05 G-Weekday PIC X(10) Occurs 7 TIMES.
77	DOW	PIC 9.

PROCEDURE DIVISION.
	ACCEPT DOW From Day-Of-Week
	DISPLAY 'The German weekday today is' G-Weekday(DOW)
	GOBACK
	.


HTH

Volker Bandke

(BSP GmbH)

with kind regards

Volker Bandke
(BSP GmbH)
```

### Micro Focus Personal Cobol student needs help

- **From:** lva7322772@aol.com (LVa7322772)
- **Date:** 1999-02-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19990219231349.07906.00000374@ng31.aol.com>`

```
I have a program that I wrote, and it is supposed to give me a report. 
However, when I run the job, it does not give me my report.  I am assumming it
has to do with the set up of my printers, but I still have not been able to get
it to print.  I am working in the windows 95 environment on a Hewlett Packard
PC and Printer is a deskjet 672C.  If there is someone out there that can help
me in setting this up I would be forever greatful.

Me
```

#### ↳ Re: Micro Focus Personal Cobol student needs help

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1999-02-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36ce4777.210863855@news1.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<19990219231349.07906.00000374@ng31.aol.com>`

```
On 20 Feb 1999 04:13:49 GMT, lva7322772@aol.com (LVa7322772) wrote:

>I have a program that I wrote, and it is supposed to give me a report. 
>However, when I run the job, it does not give me my report.  I am assumming it
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>me in setting this up I would be forever greatful.

If I remember right that's a WinPrinter - you can't print with DOS
print services.  HP did have a set of DOS drivers for that printer - I
think - but you have to explicitly load them - and they were for
Windows 3.1 - I don't know if they work from Win 95.  Even if your
Personal COBOL is the Windows version, if you are writing to PRN or
PRINTER, it's not using the Windows drivers and you output is in the
bit bucket.
```

#### ↳ Re: Micro Focus Personal Cobol student needs help

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 1999-02-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6gzz2.1$O_4.70@news19.ispnews.com>`
- **References:** `<19990219231349.07906.00000374@ng31.aol.com>`

```

LVa7322772 wrote in message <19990219231349.07906.00000374@ng31.aol.com>...
>I have a program that I wrote, and it is supposed to give me a report.
>However, when I run the job, it does not give me my report.  I am assumming
it
>has to do with the set up of my printers, but I still have not been able to
get
>it to print.  I am working in the windows 95 environment on a Hewlett
Packard
>PC and Printer is a deskjet 672C.  If there is someone out there that can
help
>me in setting this up I would be forever greatful.
>
I installed a 672C on Win95 and had no problems. Here are a few
things you can verify.

Find out how the printer is connected:

Press Start, Settings, Printers. In the Printers window right-click the
on the printer icon and select Properties. In the Properties Dialog,
select the Details Tab. The item, "Print to the folowing port" will
tell you the destination for printer output. It should show "LPT1: ..."

If the printer is not connected to LPT1, the program cannot print
to LPT1.

Find out if the printer port is available to DOS:

Press Start, Programs, MS-DOS Prompt. At the DOS prompt, enter
the command, "debug". At the hyphen, enter "d 40:0". the display will
have 8 lines showing 128 bytes in both hex and characters. The first
line will begin "0040:0000". Following this will be 8, 2-digit hexidecimal
numbers and a hyphen. The two pairs of hex digits after the hyphen
give the port address of LPT1 as it is available to DOS. If the value
is "00 00", the port is not available to DOS. Normal values are "BC 03"
or "78 03". At the hyphen, enter "q" to quit debug. Then "exit" to close
the window.

If the port is not available to DOS, a DOS program cannot use the
port. Though I would expect the MF run time to report error 027
"Device not available."

If the report was a single page, maybe it did print. The 672C is a
page printer. Before closing the file, force a page eject with the
following code.

    WRITE name-of your-print-record FROM " "
        BEFORE ADVANCING PAGE

This will ensure that the last command to the printer is a "form feed."
Though, if a partial page was sent to the printer, there would be a
flashing indicator to show that the page was not complete. Pressing
the page eject button should cause the partial page to print.

If these suggestions and others that may be provided are not sufficient
to resolve the problem, you might try one of the windows newsgroups.

HTH
-------------------------------
Rick Smith
e-mail: < ricksmith@aiservices.com >
```

#### ↳ Re: Micro Focus Personal Cobol student needs help

- **From:** bigmanong@aol.com (BIGMANONG)
- **Date:** 1999-02-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19990221153123.00963.00001335@ng-fa1.aol.com>`

### The Cobol-Analyzer for OS/390

- **From:** Roland Schiradin <schiradi@tap.de>
- **Date:** 1999-02-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36CF3801.66284826@tap.de>`

```
Hi all,

the next CBTTAPE ( www.cbttape.org ) contains a new version of the
Cobol-Analyzer (FILE321). Many user like and run this programm and the
latest
version is still in Beta. This program analyze your load-lib no need for

the cobol-source and help you to identify your quality standards or
some bad modules for LE migrating.

The new version supports:
COBOL for OS/390 and VM V2R1
MVS 4.3

The source code is Mainframe-Assembler and require therefore
basic knowledge about MVS/OS/390.

Sample report:

Can not established the program_language
Program Name= CMC22    was loaded into storage at EPA= 86200CE8

Number of instructions:       3

============ Options in effect =========================================


 ADV    APOST   DATA(31) NODECK  NODUMP NODYNAM    NOFASTSRT  NOFDUMP
 LIB    NOLIST  NOMAP    NONUM   OBJ    NOOFFSET   NOOPTIMIZE DDNAME(Def

 NUMPROC(MIG)   RENT     RES     NOSEQ  SIZE(MAX)  SOURCE     NOSSRANGE
 NOTEST TRUNC(OPT)       NOVBREF NOXREF ZWB        NONAME     NOCMPR2
 NUMCLS(PRIM)   NODBCS   NOAWO   NOEVENTS          NOCURRENCY

 Installation default res. word list
 RMODE(ANY)     NO TEST(STMT)    NO TEST(PATH)     NO TEST(BLOCK)
 INTDATE(ANSI)  NOT PGMNAME(LONGUPPER)  NOT PGMNAME(LONGMIXED)
 NODLL          NOEXPORTALL      NODATEPROC        YEARWINDOW(   0)

============ Additional information ====================================

============ about DATA DIVISION    ====================================


Program use no QSAM or SAM access method
Program use no VSAM sequential files
Program use no VSAM indexed files
Program use no VSAM rba files
Program use no CODE-SET (ASCII files)
Program use no spanned records
Program has no picture G (DBCS) items

Program has no SYNCHRONIZED clause
Program has no JUSTIFIED clause
Program has no POINTER items
Program has no complex ODO clause
Program has no external floating-point items
Program has no procedure-pointer items

============ Additional information    =================================

Program use no FILE-STATUS clause
Program use no RERUN clause in I/O-control
Program use no UPSI switches

============ Additional information    =================================

============ about PROCEDURE DIVISION  =================================


Following statements in control (check Y/N):
 Accept        N  Add           N  Alter         N  Call          Y  Can

 Compute       N  Delete        N  Display       N  Divide        N  End

 Entry         N  Exit          N  Go to         N  If            N  Ini

 Merge         N  Move          Y  Multiply      N  Open          N  Per

 Release       N  Return        N  Rewrite       N  Search        N  Set

 Start         N  Stop          N  String        N  Subt          N  Uns

 Write         N  Continue      N  End-Add       N  End-Cal       N  End

 End-Divide    N  End-Evaluate  N  End-If        N  End-Muliply   N  End

 End-Rewrite   N  End-Search    N  End-Start     N  End-String    N  End

 End-Write     N  Goback        N  Evaluate      N  Service       N  End

========================================================================


Procedure has no hexadecimal literals
No altered GO TO
No I/O error declarative
No Label declarative
No program segmentation
No program segmentation

No exit program
Call literal (static?), see options
No call identifier
No call on overflow
No call length of
No call address of

No exponentiation used
No floating point items used
No Copy-Statements
No Basis-Statements
No DBCS-Name in Program
No Shift-In/Shift-Out
============ End  ======================================================

Regards
Roland
```

<a id="t-19bb228927"></a>
## Thread: converting decimals into hex — 8 msgs

_Topics: [`Migration and conversion`](topics.md#migration)_

### converting decimals into hex

- **From:** melamedd@aol.com (Melamedd)
- **Date:** 1999-02-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19990221200124.09006.00001405@ng30.aol.com>`

```
Can someone explain how to convert decimals into hex for PIC S9(4) COMP
For example: -76 is HEX FFB4;
                     76 is HEX 004C
How was this done?

Tx.
```

#### ↳ Re: converting decimals into hex

- **From:** John Trifon <jtrifon@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-02-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36D0BD7A.7B7E4781@ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<19990221200124.09006.00001405@ng30.aol.com>`

```
First, convert the 76 in base 10 to base 16.
76 = 16*4 + 12 = 004C

Then convert to base 2:
004C= 0000 0000 0100 1100

To get the negative, you must convert to 2's complement. this is done in 2
steps:
first, flip all the 0's to 1's and the 1's to 0's.
flip    = 0000 0000 1011 0011

Now add 1 to the previous result.
+1    = 1111 1111 1011 0100

Change back to base 16.
b16  =  F        F       B      4

Hope this helped.

Melamedd wrote:

> Can someone explain how to convert decimals into hex for PIC S9(4) COMP
> For example: -76 is HEX FFB4;
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> Tx.
```

#### ↳ Re: converting decimals into hex

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com>
- **Date:** 1999-02-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36D17237.2936BF@NOSPAMhome.com>`
- **References:** `<19990221200124.09006.00001405@ng30.aol.com>`

