```
display a hex value?
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: converting decimals into hex

- **From:** melamedd@aol.com (Melamedd)
- **Date:** 1999-02-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19990222133445.23067.00001726@ng09.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<36D17237.2936BF@NOSPAMhome.com>`

```
This is  a question /answer type of a problem, and even though I am comfortable
in converting positive numbers, but still have a problem understanding the
negative convertion.

Thanks for your help
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: converting decimals into hex

- **From:** "Leif Svalgaard" <lsvalg@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 1999-02-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36d1f695@news3.us.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<36D17237.2936BF@NOSPAMhome.com> <19990222133445.23067.00001726@ng09.aol.com>`

```
Melamedd wrote in message <19990222133445.23067.00001726@ng09.aol.com>...
>This is  a question /answer type of a problem, and even though I am
comfortable
>in converting positive numbers, but still have a problem understanding the
>negative convertion.


To convert the negative numbers add them to 65526, then convert the result.
E.g. to convert -1234:
65536 - 1234 = 64302
64302 / 16 = 4018, remainder 14=E
4018 / 16 = 251, remainder 2
251 / 16 = 15=F, remainder 11=B
hex: FB2E
```

#### ↳ Re: converting decimals into hex

- **From:** "Leif Svalgaard" <lsvalg@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 1999-02-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36d152eb@news3.us.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<19990221200124.09006.00001405@ng30.aol.com>`

```

Melamedd wrote in message <19990221200124.09006.00001405@ng30.aol.com>...
>Can someone explain how to convert decimals into hex for PIC S9(4) COMP
>For example: -76 is HEX FFB4;
>                     76 is HEX 004C
>How was this done?
76 / 16 = 4, remainder 12. A=10 B=11 C=12, result 4C
Here is another: 1234 / 16 = 77 remainder 2
77 /16 = 4, remainder 13 (=D), result= 4D2
```

#### ↳ Re: converting decimals into hex

- **From:** <BARNfrog@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 1999-02-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7b16pl$9fr@bgtnsc03.worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<19990221200124.09006.00001405@ng30.aol.com>`

```
I assume you are referring to IBM MVS COBOL here.  If so the answer is:

Negative binary numbers are stored in a form called  2's compliment.  The
process is as follows:

1. take the binary positive number

0000  0000  0100  1100      (hex 004C, dec 76)

2. change 0's to '1's. 1's to 0's.

1111  1111  1011  0011

3.  add 1 to the result

1111  1111  1011 0100  (hex FFB4, dec -76)

>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>
>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>
>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>


Melamedd wrote in message <19990221200124.09006.00001405@ng30.aol.com>...
>Can someone explain how to convert decimals into hex for PIC S9(4) COMP
>For example: -76 is HEX FFB4;
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>Tx.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: converting decimals into hex

- **From:** "Jerry Peacock" <bismail@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 1999-02-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7bd82e$26o$1@birch.prod.itd.earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<19990221200124.09006.00001405@ng30.aol.com> <7b16pl$9fr@bgtnsc03.worldnet.att.net>`

```

BARNfrog@worldnet.att.net wrote in message
<7b16pl$9fr@bgtnsc03.worldnet.att.net>...
>
>1. take the binary positive number
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>
What's been left out is WHY IS THIS DONE? It is done so
the computer need only ADD logic and no subtraction hardware.
Let's see:  76 - 76 = ?
From above example:
    76 = 0000 0000 0100 1100
  -76 = 1111 1111 1011 0100
sum = 0000 0000 0000 0000

Neat, eh?
```

```

with NetExpress) to a new customer.  He wants to use ODBC to work with our
COBOL data files.  I believe he is going to use Visual Basic.  I have no idea
where to start can I please get some comments on how VB can communicate to our
COBOL data files using ODBC ?

Thanks,
Bob Hennessey
```

#### ↳ Re: ODBC to my NetExpress COBOL data files

```
Dear fellow programmers,

or
something like that. Would any of you please steer me in the right
direction on how to find this book.  I have looked in some of the
"BIG"
shops (i.e. Barnes and Noble, Waldens) and had no luck...Thank You. I
have a pretty good grasp on Cobol and would like to learn the C
programming family.  I hear this is a good book to have. Is this true?
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: from cobol to c

- **From:** coboljit@aol.com (COBOLJIT)
- **Date:** 1999-02-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19990227034238.17155.00000407@ngol07.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<wjrA2.15041$OS5.15014747@news3.mia>`

```
In article <wjrA2.15041$OS5.15014747@news3.mia>, "James King"
<mangogwr@bellsouth.net> writes:

>I've seen it a couple of times.  Its OK.  You can become a very good
>'C" pgmr w/o ever knowing COBOL.  They are so totally different
>and you have to think differently.  Get the 'C for Dummies' book
>as it is a very good textbook.  ALso pickup the little R&K 'C' primer
>

Yes, it's quite true you don't need to know COBOL to learn C.  The idea of the
Moving from COBOL to C was to take the way a COBOL programmer thinks and
explainhow he should be thinking in "C."  So it's written from the approach of
explaining how to write C to someone who knows COBOL.  

And it's always a good idea to read more than one book on a subject. Moving
from COBOL to C would be a good starting point for a COBOL programmer.  Just
like "Teach Yourself COBOL in 21 Days" is a good starting point for a beginning
programmer :)

Helen
Helen

------------------------------------------------------------------------------
King Computer Services, Inc.
COBOL Training for the Year 2000
"COBOL, Just in Time" Crash Course
"COBOL, Dates and the Year 2000" Technical Reference.
818-951-5240
```

## Thread: printing from version 2.0.02 dos — 3 msgs

### printing from version 2.0.02 dos

- **From:** marianne_mayberry@yahoo.com
- **Date:** 1999-02-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7ap7nl$vh1$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
Could you possibly help me with a problem I've been having with printing from
DOS version 2.0.02?

I'm a new user, so forgive me if this question has been asked.  I've been
creating output reports for printing.  I retrieve them in the COBOL editor and
print from there.  The output appears the way I intend it to, however, when I
print there is a problem. The first page of my report always skips a line
before printing.  The consecutive pages start on the first line of the page as
it should.

Do you know what I could do in order to get the first page to begin printing
on the first line as do all the other pages?  This is a problem even my
profesor cannot solve.

-----------== Posted via Deja News, The Discussion Network ==----------
http://www.dejanews.com/       Search, Read, Discuss, or Start Your Own
```

#### ↳ Re: printing from version 2.0.02 dos

- **From:** "James King" <mangogwr@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1999-02-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6mrA2.15042$OS5.15015918@news3.mia>`
- **References:** `<7ap7nl$vh1$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
View the file in HEX.  There is probably a CR/LF pair (HEX '0D0A')
at the top.  Check the source to see if there is an initial write
or improperly initialised data.
```

## Thread: Beginner — 10 msgs

_Topics: [`Tutorials, books, learning`](topics.md#learning)_

### Beginner

- **From:** synth@mindspring.com (David Mark Coplin)
- **Date:** 1999-02-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36cf6630.17480123@news.mindspring.com>`

```
Beginning Cobol Student,
	How many levels of occur statements does Cobol 85 support?
```

#### ↳ Re: Beginner

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1999-02-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36cf6da4.286184078@news1.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<36cf6630.17480123@news.mindspring.com>`

```
On Sun, 21 Feb 1999 01:50:49 GMT, synth@mindspring.com (David Mark
Coplin) wrote:

>Beginning Cobol Student,
>	How many levels of occur statements does Cobol 85 support? 

The answer can be found in Chapter 12 of Sams Teach Yourself COBOL in
24 Hours.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Beginner

- **From:** dnikelshpu@aol.com (DNikelshpu)
- **Date:** 1999-02-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19990222135903.02874.00001550@ng-fx1.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<36cf6da4.286184078@news1.ibm.net>`

```
Up to SEVEL levels COBOL 85 (up to 3 COBOL 74).
 
It's common practice to avoid using more than 3 levels for readability/sanity.
Use Evaluate instead.

                   Dmitry
```

#### ↳ Re: Beginner

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1999-02-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<N2Tz2.8103$Xl5.13794673@news1.mia>`
- **References:** `<36cf6630.17480123@news.mindspring.com>`

```
David Mark Coplin wrote:
>
> How many levels of occur statements does Cobol 85 support?

The COBOL 85 standard specifies a minimum of 3 levels, but many compilers
support more than that.  Unisys A Series, for example, supports up to 48
levels (OCCURS on every level number 02-49).  This is an extravagant limit,
for if each of 48 OCCURS were 2, then 2**48 = 281,474,976,710,656.  That's
a lot of RAM! :-)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Beginner

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@inospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-02-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7apv8q$m6i@sjx-ixn6.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<36cf6630.17480123@news.mindspring.com> <N2Tz2.8103$Xl5.13794673@news1.mia> <35CAA02707321B16.7C8B22A6907A68B1.1F39BB966E0D0E25@library-proxy.airnews.net> <36d07cf7.10818010@news1.ibm.net>`

```
I think we are confusing minimum/maximum "usages" here.  (Judson, was just
wrong about "3" - which was the '74 value.)

What the '85 Standard  says is that EVERY compiler must support tables which
include 7 levels.  It is "allowable" for an implementation to support more
than 7 levels, it is NOT conforming to support less than 7 levels - but
obviously, when you support 7 level tables, you can still have individual
conforming programs that use any number of OCCURS from 1 thru 7.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Beginner

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1999-02-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<JhcA2.2543$LN5.3395319@news2.mia>`
- **References:** `<36cf6630.17480123@news.mindspring.com> <N2Tz2.8103$Xl5.13794673@news1.mia> <35CAA02707321B16.7C8B22A6907A68B1.1F39BB966E0D0E25@library-proxy.airnews.net> <36d07cf7.10818010@news1.ibm.net> <7apv8q$m6i@sjx-ixn6.ix.netcom.com>`

```
Bill is correct.  The original question was "How many levels of occur
statements does Cobol 85 support?", and my answer was framed "The COBOL
85 standard specifies a minimum of 3 (should have been 7) levels, but
many compilers support more than that."  We were discussing the number
of levels required to be 'supported' by the COBOL 85 standard.

Of course a COBOL 85 program could use fewer levels, or even no OCCURS
at all. :-)
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Beginner

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com>
- **Date:** 1999-02-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36D17097.F41C7367@NOSPAMhome.com>`
- **References:** `<36cf6630.17480123@news.mindspring.com> <N2Tz2.8103$Xl5.13794673@news1.mia>`

```
> The COBOL 85 standard specifies a minimum of 3 levels, but many compilers
> support more than that.  Unisys A Series, for example, supports up to 48
> levels (OCCURS on every level number 02-49).  This is an extravagant limit,
> for if each of 48 OCCURS were 2, then 2**48 = 281,474,976,710,656.  That's
> a lot of RAM! :-)

I love it when the syntax limit is way larger than the practical limit. 
The compiler isn't that much more complicated than it would be with 5 or
6 levels, and it doesn't make sense to try to guess what is practical
and try to hit it - when things change so rapidly.  In this case, 48
wasn't even a "way large" selection, it was basically no limit at all
inside of the COBOL limit on level numbers.  Good programming design by
the compiler writers.
```

## Thread: microfocus cobol — 5 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### microfocus cobol

- **From:** bigmanong@aol.com (BIGMANONG)
- **Date:** 1999-02-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19990221152637.00963.00001330@ng-fa1.aol.com>`

```
Upon running my program there is an error message qoute  "STOP RUN ENCOUNTERED
WITH RETURN CODE = +00000" . How would you fix the problem. I need help.
```

#### ↳ Re: microfocus cobol

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@inospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-02-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7apvbq$m99@sjx-ixn6.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<19990221152637.00963.00001330@ng-fa1.aol.com>`

```
It sounds to be as if you have NO problem.  What makes you think that you
do?  Not every "message" is an "error message".  Is there something that
makes you think that your program has not executed as designed?
```

#### ↳ Re: microfocus cobol

- **From:** "George Zielinski" <georgezielinski@retired.airfoce.net>
- **Date:** 1999-02-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<5DC9C12A1F8637AE.E978B0DE802C8E24.824D6384DF149D6A@library-proxy.airnews.net>`
- **References:** `<19990221152637.00963.00001330@ng-fa1.aol.com>`

```
That is not a problem!!  It is telling you that it has reached your STOP RUN
statement in your program.  The return code of 0000 is a successful
completion.
```

#### ↳ Re: microfocus cobol

- **From:** dnikelshpu@aol.com (DNikelshpu)
- **Date:** 1999-02-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19990222135253.02874.00001549@ng-fx1.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<19990221152637.00963.00001330@ng-fa1.aol.com>`

```
In case you did not figure it out yet:

Your program ran fine, and did what you told it to...Your problem's in the
logic. Execute your program STEP by step. You should have no problem finding
exactly where your program reaches the stop run statement.
 
If you still can't figure it out let me know and I'll walk you through it.

                              Dmitry
```

#### ↳ Re: microfocus cobol

- **From:** as999@torfree.net (Adrian Boldan)
- **Date:** 1999-02-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<F7KLns.AI7.0.bloor@torfree.net>`
- **References:** `<19990221152637.00963.00001330@ng-fa1.aol.com>`

```
BIGMANONG (bigmanong@aol.com) wrote:
: Upon running my program there is an error message qoute  "STOP RUN ENCOUNTERED
: WITH RETURN CODE = +00000" . How would you fix the problem. I need help.

1. Remove the STOP statement from your program.
 or
2. Delete the message :-)
```

## Thread: OS/390 Emulator — 17 this month / 27 total

_Topics: [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe)_

### OS/390 Emulator

- **From:** "Torix" <Torixx@ameritech.net>
- **Date:** 1999-02-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6rmA2.14496$1h.2036556@nntp0.chicago.il.ameritech.net>`

```
Greetings all,

I am new here so please forgive me if I sound a bit ignorant :)

Is there such a thing as a OS/390 emulator for win98?  I would like to
replicate the environment I am being introduced to at work, at home.

Thank you for any help you can provide

Todd
```

#### ↳ Re: OS/390 Emulator

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@inospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-02-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7atilu$nsh@dfw-ixnews8.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<6rmA2.14496$1h.2036556@nntp0.chicago.il.ameritech.net>`

```
OS/390 "emulation" is a fairly broad question.  Both Merant (formerly Micro
Focus) and CA provide lots of emulation support.  (Interestingly enough,
IBM's VisualAge products emulate the mainframe COBOL - but not the
environment).  If you were really RICH you could get yourself a P/390 and
actually run OS/390 on a "PC".  Exactly what are you looking for?
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: OS/390 Emulator

- **From:** Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>
- **Date:** 1999-02-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36D296DC.369D0E74@zip.com.au>`
- **References:** `<6rmA2.14496$1h.2036556@nntp0.chicago.il.ameritech.net> <7atilu$nsh@dfw-ixnews8.ix.netcom.com>`

```
William M. Klein wrote:
> 
> OS/390 "emulation" is a fairly broad question.  Both Merant (formerly Micro
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> 


Sorry to do this again but...

Has anyone used Visual Age to support their mainframe development.

How does it go about supporting DB2 calls?

How does it go about supporting CICS calls (if at all)?

What about pure development for the PC using remote communication to the
host?

What about developing pretty gooey screens?

Just fishing to make me look god for the boss
Ken
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OS/390 Emulator

- **From:** paulr@bix.com (paulr)
- **Date:** 1999-02-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7b103l$5jb@lotho.delphi.com>`
- **References:** `<6rmA2.14496$1h.2036556@nntp0.chicago.il.ameritech.net> <7atilu$nsh@dfw-ixnews8.ix.netcom.com> <36D296DC.369D0E74@zip.com.au>`

```

if you pick up VisualAge COBOL, you will land a big one, so take
Photos. :) 

It handles DB2 calls, on open systems and mainframes just like a 
duck takes to water. 

Same to CICS.

It handles GUI screens with a VisualAge Generator flair, meaning,
you have to use the IBM paradigm, but if you do, it works great. 
(Think Visual Parts and NonVisual Parts.)

IN addition, you can do things like remote edit / compile / debug
on the mainframe, use VSAM files from the mainframe as if they
were local files, and just tons of other stuff.

Heck, it even comes with VisualAge CICS Development Environement,
which is the non-Open Systems version of Transaction Manager. (Think,
'OS/2 Transaction Manager Version 4.1', or CICS 4.1 on the mainframe.)

It also has the nice advantage of coming with both OS/2 and Windows NT
executables. One caveat, the compiler will run under WIndows 95 just fine,
but the Visual Builder (i.e. GUI parts) will *not*.

-Paul
```

```
Big problem with the IBM products - working in ASCII! I believe some of the
software will work in EBCIDIC - but not all.

MicroFocus Mainframe Express complete with integrated CICS, IMS, SQL,
Assember, testing tools, mainframe connectivity and you work in EBCIDIC - we
do all our testing and demos with the product and it is difficult to think
how it could be better.

Simon Cordingley
Casegen Systems Ltd - Cobol generation products.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OS/390 Emulator

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** paulr@bix.com (paulr)
- **Date:** 1999-02-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7b26mo$t6a@lotho.delphi.com>`
- **References:** `<6rmA2.14496$1h.2036556@nntp0.chicago.il.ameritech.net> <7atilu$nsh@dfw-ixnews8.ix.netcom.com> <36D296DC.369D0E74@zip.com.au> <7b103l$5jb@lotho.delphi.com> <36d46deb.0@mercury.nildram.co.uk>`

```


Wrongo - the IBM products work just as handily in ASCII, which is the 
default on open systems, as in EBCIDIC, which is the default on the      
mainframes. 

You can even MIX them with no problems, for example, having a lookup
table resident on a Mainframe and a few work tables resident on a PC
drive. 

Microfocus has a good product, but the Santa Theresa labs provide 
the best COBOL support I have ever seen. On all the platforms supported.
Besides, the CICS that comes with VisualAge isn't just CICS emulation,
it is real 100% usable CICS. 

Not to mention that if you want to use DB2, just use it. Why simulate it?
WHIch again, works across all the platforms, from a personal one user 
product on Windows 95, to full scale products under NT and OS/2, to
AS/400, to Mainframe.

-Paul
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OS/390 Emulator

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@inospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-02-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7b7l54$2d0@sjx-ixn9.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<6rmA2.14496$1h.2036556@nntp0.chicago.il.ameritech.net> <7atilu$nsh@dfw-ixnews8.ix.netcom.com> <36D296DC.369D0E74@zip.com.au> <7b103l$5jb@lotho.delphi.com> <36d46deb.0@mercury.nildram.co.uk> <7b26mo$t6a@lotho.delphi.com> <36d724b2.0@mercury.nildram.co.uk>`

```
Check that their (IBM's) EBCDIC support is "complete" for the environments
documented.  A couple of things to check out:

    - Are keys for LOCAL VSAM files supported in EBCDIC order?
    - Can you specify Hex values (e.g. X'40' as a space)?
    - Check out specifying CICS attribute bytes

Some of these may now be supported by IBM, but I don't think so.  (I am also
pretty certain that they still have no Assembler support)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OS/390 Emulator

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** paulr@bix.com (paulr)
- **Date:** 1999-02-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7b7qg2$ln8@lotho.delphi.com>`
- **References:** `<6rmA2.14496$1h.2036556@nntp0.chicago.il.ameritech.net> <7atilu$nsh@dfw-ixnews8.ix.netcom.com> <36D296DC.369D0E74@zip.com.au> <7b103l$5jb@lotho.delphi.com> <36d46deb.0@mercury.nildram.co.uk> <7b26mo$t6a@lotho.delphi.com> <36d724b2.0@mercury.nildram.co.uk> <7b7l54$2d0@sjx-ixn9.ix.netcom.com>`

```

Bill, I'm surprised(!)  
You really should check out VisualAge for COBOL, it would be a 
HELL of an eye opener for you. A pleasent one, I think. :) 

In order:

Local VSAM files supported in EDBDIC order........ yes
Can you specify Hex values........................ yes
Specifying CICS attribute bytes................... yes
 
If you are using EBCDIC, X'40' means space.
IN a CICS program, you *always* use the EBCDIC values in
attribute bytes, since BMS demands it anyway. 

For example, to specify and attribute of UNPROT-NUM-MDT, you would
use X'D1'. 

It is really *very* good stuff. I'm wondering if it used to be
some awfully bad product, but then I get cornfused, since it
used to be just Microfocus COBOL relabled I think. The new stuff
is very very good. 


Now, I do need to qualify that, my COBOL experience is limited,    
with serious projects on the mainframe, with AcuCOBOL, VisualAge,
CobolSet for AIX, AS/400, and UNisys 2200's, but the VisualAge
compiler feels 100 times as solid as say, AcuCOBOL. That *may*
be a little IBM predjudice slipping in there, as you can see
from the above. :)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OS/390 Emulator

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@inospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-02-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7b85bk$red@dfw-ixnews9.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<6rmA2.14496$1h.2036556@nntp0.chicago.il.ameritech.net> <7atilu$nsh@dfw-ixnews8.ix.netcom.com> <36D296DC.369D0E74@zip.com.au> <7b103l$5jb@lotho.delphi.com> <36d46deb.0@mercury.nildram.co.uk> <7b26mo$t6a@lotho.delphi.com> <36d724b2.0@mercury.nildram.co.uk> <7b7l54$2d0@sjx-ixn9.ix.netcom.com> <7b7qg2$ln8@lotho.delphi.com>`

```
Part of my confusion was that earlier in this thread someone mentioned the
COLLSEQ(EBCDIC) compiler option - which was all that was available with the
original IBM (not Micro Focus) product.

It appears that they have now also added a CHAR(EBCDIC) compiler option.

When BOTH are used, it appears that most of what Micro Focus has always
offered for EBCDIC support is now also available with VisualAge COBOL.

HOWEVER, when I look thru SC26-9050-04 which seems to be the current VA
Programming Guide, I still can't find any support for EBCDIC files that are
*LOCAL*.  All I could find were references to REMOTE (which could just be
across the LAN) EBCDIC file support.  It also appears that all EBCDIC
support requires Sdu.  Can someone point me to IBM documentation showing
local EBCDIC indexed file support?  (I couldn't see it in the CHAR
discussion, COLLSEQ discussion, or file sections - but it may be there
somewhere else.)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OS/390 Emulator

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** paulr@bix.com (paulr)
- **Date:** 1999-02-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7b90af$3f0@lotho.delphi.com>`
- **References:** `<6rmA2.14496$1h.2036556@nntp0.chicago.il.ameritech.net> <7atilu$nsh@dfw-ixnews8.ix.netcom.com> <36D296DC.369D0E74@zip.com.au> <7b103l$5jb@lotho.delphi.com> <36d46deb.0@mercury.nildram.co.uk> <7b26mo$t6a@lotho.delphi.com> <36d724b2.0@mercury.nildram.co.uk> <7b7l54$2d0@sjx-ixn9.ix.netcom.com> <7b7qg2$ln8@lotho.delphi.com> <7b85bk$red@dfw-ixnews9.ix.netcom.com>`

```
Slight confusion of terms - NT doesn't support real VSAM, and OS/2
VSAM is a completely different animal than Mainframe VSAM. (VSAM,
real VSAM, on a PC is always remote. Local indexed files are often 
reffered to by careless people like me as 'VSAM', but they are not.)
CICS under NT either uses DB2, which has its own mechanism to handle
EBCDIC informtion, or uses Encina SFS, which again, has it's own
method to handle EBCDIC.

However, from the COBOL point of view, the compiler handles local
files in EBCDIC format quite easily, especially with CICS. 

-Paul
```

```
On a related topic, does either Visual Age or MicroFocus support RSAM
file handling.  It is a proprietary standard (its either an Anderson
or Compuware product - I'm at home right now and don't have the
vendor's name handy) that allows restarting abended jobs at the point
in the files where reads/writes left off (kinda like a rollback in a
table).  The advantage is that you don't have to rerun the job.  It is
extensively used in my shop's production code.  It would be nice to be
able to use an IDE for code develpment - we're using Intall1 in TSO -
it's somewhat primitive compared to other platforms.  And since we are
kicking off a major redevelopment effort of all our systems it might
be a good time to have our coding standards enter the 80's.

James Johnson
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OS/390 Emulator

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** paulr@bix.com (paulr)
- **Date:** 1999-02-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7b7g8j$eue@lotho.delphi.com>`
- **References:** `<6rmA2.14496$1h.2036556@nntp0.chicago.il.ameritech.net> <7atilu$nsh@dfw-ixnews8.ix.netcom.com> <36D296DC.369D0E74@zip.com.au> <7b103l$5jb@lotho.delphi.com> <36d46deb.0@mercury.nildram.co.uk> <7b26mo$t6a@lotho.delphi.com> <36d724b2.0@mercury.nildram.co.uk>`

```
Simon Cordingley (simonc@casegen.co.uk) wrote:
: Paul - thanks for the update.

: Can you confirm that with the IBM products I can run CICS/COBOL/DB2/VSAM on
: a Windows 95/98/NT machine in EBCDIC (sorry about earlier typo) Any idea on
: cost? I have used this environment on OS/2 some time ago in ASCII.

Yes, consider it confirmed. To obtain EBCDIC collating sequence use in 
your programs, use the compiler directive

     PROGRAM COLLATING SEQUENCE IS EBCDIC 

or

     COLLSEQ(EBCDIC)       

as a compiler option.

PC's of course, want to hold integers in a format that is byte-reversed
from the mainframe. (Mainframes are BIG-ENDIAN).  For programs that
will use Mainframe binary data and rely on a mainframe internal
representation of integer values, use the BINARY(S390) compiler
option. (Note: Don't use COMP-5 in this case! You will get   
a native binary format, whatever that may be at the time...) 

Finally, PC's represent floating point numbers in IEEE format,
while mainframes use Hexadecimal. Use the FLOAT(S390) compiler
option to ensure floating point numbers are in mainframe
format. 

File status values can be a bit of a problem, since values in 
the 9x range are platform dependent, but that should not be 
much of a problem,  if any. 



: What about IMS DB/DC (IMS/TM now I think)  - can I run that and access DL/1
: databases? Is assembler supported?

DL/I and I are not aquainted, so I'll give you some of the dope from 
the programming guide below:


  Remote DL/I provides access to IMS full functin databases and GSAM
  databases from programs using Data Language I (DL/I) calls running
  on workstations. Remote DL/I provides the support to develop and 
  test mainframe COBOL programs on workstations that use DL/I calls
  in a subset of environments. Specfically, support for DL/I calls    
  will be provided for:

    - IMS batch applications that access IMS full function databases
      and GSAM databases.

  With remote DL/I support and IBM Visual Age for COBOL, customers that
  use CBLTDLI can devleop, compile, and test on workstations the
  following

   - COBOL programs that run in an IMS batch environment that use
     CBLTDLI calls.

  Note:
   - REmote DL.I does not provide access to IMS message queues or
     IMS fast path databases.

   - Remote DL/I runs using only S/390 data types as input and output. 
     It does not provide any data conversion functins.

HOW REMOTE DL/I Works
  remote DL/I uses APPC and and IMS batch environment to provide the 
remote DL/I call support. When Remote DL/I is first initialized on 
the workstation, it asks the user for the OS/390 uerid and password to 
be used when the remote job is started on OS/390. Then APPC is used
by Remote DL/I ti start a job on the OS/390 which brings up an IMS 
batch environment. One the IMS batch environment environment is available,
then remote DL/I calls can be processed. The DL/I calls on the workstation 
are sent to the IMS batch environment and executed. The results of the 
DL/I calls are then send back to the program running on the workstation. 
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OS/390 Emulator

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** William Lynch <wblynch@att.net>
- **Date:** 1999-02-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36D4E6CC.89D441D3@att.net>`
- **References:** `<6rmA2.14496$1h.2036556@nntp0.chicago.il.ameritech.net> <7atilu$nsh@dfw-ixnews8.ix.netcom.com> <36D296DC.369D0E74@zip.com.au> <7b103l$5jb@lotho.delphi.com> <36d46deb.0@mercury.nildram.co.uk> <7b26mo$t6a@lotho.delphi.com>`

```
paulr wrote:
> 
> Wrongo - the IBM products work just as handily in ASCII, which is the
> default on open systems, as in EBCIDIC, which is the default on the
> mainframes.

On a short but pedantic note, IBM mainframes use EBCDIC, as in Extended
Binary Coded Decimal Interchange (?) Code. "BCD" was a carryover from
the 1400-series days, Binary Coded Decimal. I think it was IBM's
response to "ASCII", where the "II" is Information Interchange, or words
to that effect. 

Bill Lynch
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OS/390 Emulator

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** Barticus@att.spam.net (Randall Bart)
- **Date:** 1999-02-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36d919f3.48233871@netnews>`
- **References:** `<6rmA2.14496$1h.2036556@nntp0.chicago.il.ameritech.net> <7atilu$nsh@dfw-ixnews8.ix.netcom.com> <36D296DC.369D0E74@zip.com.au> <7b103l$5jb@lotho.delphi.com> <36d46deb.0@mercury.nildram.co.uk> <7b26mo$t6a@lotho.delphi.com> <36D4E6CC.89D441D3@att.net>`

```
'Twas 25 Feb 1999 05:59:52 GMT, when William Lynch <wblynch@att.net>
illuminated comp.lang.cobol thusly:

>On a short but pedantic note, IBM mainframes use EBCDIC, as in Extended
>Binary Coded Decimal Interchange (?) Code. "BCD" was a carryover from
>the 1400-series days, Binary Coded Decimal. I think it was IBM's
>response to "ASCII", where the "II" is Information Interchange, or words
>to that effect. 

It's an odd inconsitancy.  "Binary Coded Decimal Interchange Code" was
abrreviated BCDIC or BCD.  BCDIC may have been prefered by IBM, but it
acquired the pronunciation "bee-see-dee" so the spelling BCD predominated.
"Extended Binary Coded Decimal Interchange Code" was abrreviated EBCDIC
and the pronunciation "eb-suh-dic" soon emerged, so no other spelling was
needed.
```

```
In article <7atqr8$scp$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>, daniel.jacot@winterthur.ch
writes:

>
>I don't know about a free emulator, but if you can spend some money, consult
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>

I had a dismal experience with using MVS Workbench.  Our shop deals primarily
with BATCH processing programs and was looking at microfocus as a potential
tool to off-load development from the mainframe and provide debugging tools. 
Microfocus was quick to send a trial copy, but when they determined that a bug
existed in their BATCH environment, we kinda got put to the back burner for
support as most of their users appear to be working in a CICS , online
environment.  After waiting six months for a resolution to being able to run
THEIR demo process in the batch environment and still not getting any response,
we dropped them and went to another vendor.  Our vender choice does not
emulatethe MVS enviironment, but the COBOL development environment does provide
everything we were looking for in terms of source code compatibility and
debugging tools.
```

<a id="t-2a5a4d671b"></a>
## Thread: Need HELP Desperately!!!! Micro Focus COBOL Netexpress 2.0 — 2 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers) · [`Help requests and how-to`](topics.md#help)_

### Need HELP Desperately!!!! Micro Focus COBOL Netexpress 2.0

- **From:** "Hugh Tran" <hugh_tran@hotmail.com>
- **Date:** 1999-02-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7arfq2$k43$1@news1.mpx.com.au>`

```
Hi,

I am using MF COBOL Netexpress 2.0 on Windows NT. I am having difficuties
with screen I/O. I have a screen defined with fields. I want to be able to
change these field attributes. For example, on a screen, if field_1 is
entered "Y" then I want to be able to protect or hightlight or blink field_2
on the screen.

How can I change the field attributes without to having defining mutli
screens????

Desperately need help. Tanks in advance

hugh_tran@hotmail.com
```

#### ↳ Re: Need HELP Desperately!!!! Micro Focus COBOL Netexpress 2.0

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1999-02-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-cX9JYmnp2FY2@Dwight_Miller.iix.com>`
- **References:** `<7arfq2$k43$1@news1.mpx.com.au>`

```
On Mon, 22 Feb 1999 11:34:01, "Hugh Tran" <hugh_tran@hotmail.com> 
wrote:

> Hi,
> 
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

There are at least 3 (maybe 4) user interface options with NetExpress.
 Are you using Dialog System, the HTML template, Screens, or the 
Screen Section?

With the Screen Section you can do what you are trying to do by using 
the "Control" option in the screen definition.  You assign control for
each field to a specific variable, then move the desired display 
attributes into this variable and re-display the screen.
```

<a id="t-0c1941b0a2"></a>
## Thread: Winsock Socket access from a COBOL program. — 3 msgs

### Winsock Socket access from a COBOL program.

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1999-02-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36d09e1d.19304607@news1.ibm.net>`

```
I've seen questions on this topic posted here a couple of times.
Recently I found I had a need to create a socket client under Windows.


So how to do it.... I found the Sockets API on the internet and
downloaded it, and I downloaded the documentation.  I've interfaced
with the 16 bit Windows API, but never anything as complicated as the
Windows API.  The sockets were not a new concept either - recently I
wrote a program on the OS/390 mainframe (Actually I ADAPTED a program
that someone else here wrote) that acts as a Server.  We are
interfacing to it from the DEC/VMS system.  At any rate, I was
impressed by it.  It's similar to what I wrote a while back (Async
streaming communications), and I figured it all out pretty fast.  

But this Windows API sometimes seems purposely obtuse.  I scanned
DejaNews and found a post by Dave Maxwell, who indicated he had some
source code for MicroFocus COBOL that accessed the Sockets API.  Dave
was kind enough to share that code with me, and I ported it to work
under the Fujitsu compiler.  He gave me permission to make his code
available on the internet, so... if you want some source code to
inteface with the sockets API, MicroFocus and Fujitsu versions are
available from my web site. 

Go here: http://www.geocities.com/Eureka/2006

and press the downloads button - You will have to scroll down to the
bottom.

This was my mini project I was talking about earlier.  It really helps
to understand C, and especially Pointers when interfacing with the
Windows API.
```

#### ↳ Re: Winsock Socket access from a COBOL program.

- **From:** NoLonger@ix.netcom.com (OldUncleMe)
- **Date:** 1999-02-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36d192da.2470326@news>`
- **References:** `<36d09e1d.19304607@news1.ibm.net>`

```
It was: Mon, 22 Feb 1999 00:07:20 GMT and with STARTLING insight,  "redsky@ibm.net (Thane
Hubbell)" posted "Winsock Socket access from a COBOL program." to "comp.lang.cobol" :

-->I've seen questions on this topic posted here a couple of times.
-->Recently I found I had a need to create a socket client under Windows.


-->So how to do it.... I found the Sockets API on the internet and
-->downloaded it, and I downloaded the documentation.  I've interfaced
-->with the 16 bit Windows API, but never anything as complicated as the
-->Windows API.  The sockets were not a new concept either - recently I
-->wrote a program on the OS/390 mainframe (Actually I ADAPTED a program
-->that someone else here wrote) that acts as a Server.  We are
-->interfacing to it from the DEC/VMS system.  At any rate, I was
-->impressed by it.  It's similar to what I wrote a while back (Async
-->streaming communications), and I figured it all out pretty fast.  

-->But this Windows API sometimes seems purposely obtuse.  I scanned
-->DejaNews and found a post by Dave Maxwell, who indicated he had some
-->source code for MicroFocus COBOL that accessed the Sockets API.  Dave
-->was kind enough to share that code with me, and I ported it to work
-->under the Fujitsu compiler.  He gave me permission to make his code
-->available on the internet, so... if you want some source code to
-->inteface with the sockets API, MicroFocus and Fujitsu versions are
-->available from my web site. 

-->Go here: http://www.geocities.com/Eureka/2006

-->and press the downloads button - You will have to scroll down to the
-->bottom.

-->This was my mini project I was talking about earlier.  It really helps
-->to understand C, and especially Pointers when interfacing with the
-->Windows API.


Thanks for the info.  I downloaded the zip file and I'm going to take some time and study it,
good reference, and probably a good intro to several topics.

Let me ask.  First, it looks like you are using features of new, cob'97 standard?  (such as
comments:  *> ), et al.  I have not seen EVALUATE used in this fashion, is this new usage also,
or is this from Cobol 85 and it just slipped by me? Also, I'm pretty new to this stuff, is this
file, client.cbl an object? (as in OO)

(From the file:  client.cbl)
<small snip of code>
              evaluate TRUE
                 when CMD = "ECHO"
                 when CMD = "echo"
                 when CMD = "RESE"
                 when CMD = "rese"
                    perform Receive-Data <<
                    display RecBuf at 0801<<--all 4 conditions above result in these actions?

                 when CMD = "FILE"
                 when CMD = "file"
                    move "rese" to CMD
                    move 0 to EOD-Flag
                    perform until EOD-Flag = 1
                      perform Receive-Data
</small snip of code>


In the Fujitsu version you don't use the COPY (I assume you're including only the necessary
portions directly.  Why?  Won't COPY work, or is this just done differently, perhaps to only
include necessary parts of the file?                         Thanks,          /ts



"It's important when you ride snorting, kicking 2000 pound horned animals
 for a living that you occasionally get away for a while"  Skiing Cowboys
                       tenox+-+at+-+home+-+dot+-+com
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Winsock Socket access from a COBOL program.

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1999-02-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36d1eaf8.104528577@news1.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<36d09e1d.19304607@news1.ibm.net> <36d192da.2470326@news>`

```
On Mon, 22 Feb 1999 17:42:42 GMT, NoLonger@ix.netcom.com (OldUncleMe)
wrote:


>Let me ask.  First, it looks like you are using features of new, cob'97 standard?  (such as
>comments:  *> ), et al.  I have not seen EVALUATE used in this fashion, is this new usage also,
>or is this from Cobol 85 and it just slipped by me? Also, I'm pretty new to this stuff, is this
>file, client.cbl an object? (as in OO)

The comments and the Symbolic constants are the only non 85 standard
stuff.  Several EXTENSIONS that are Fujitsu specific are used to
interface with the Windows Sockets API.  The syntax, however, is very
similar to the MicroFocus syntax.  

The Evaluate below is standard.  

>
>(From the file:  client.cbl)
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
>

The copy that was used in the MicroFocus example was generated (I
beleive) via a MicroFocus tool that will take C header files and
convert them to COBOL - but it used several MicroFocus specific
featuers that made it eaiser for MF to interface with the API - these
include the 78 levels (which Fujitsu will support in the next release)
and dynamic field typing.  ie:  New usages were created by these
headers.  They were implemented then as external variables in nested
programs.  Since Fujitsu didn't support the 78 levels or Dynamic
typing - AND I AM NOT SUGGESTING THAT THEY SHOULD!  I find #DEFINE
after #DEFINE after #DEFINE to be confusing and unnecessary.  For
example in the API Windows redefines and Unsigned Integer to WORD -
WHY?  Why not just call it an unsigned integer! <G>.  Anyway, I just
tried to make it as clear as possible, while understanding it and
porting it.
```

<a id="t-468da99507"></a>
## Thread: Visual Basic calls Cobol EXE (NetExpress) — 2 msgs

### Visual Basic calls Cobol EXE (NetExpress)

- **From:** OKLEIN@online.de
- **Date:** 1999-02-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7arges$qn1$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
Hallo,

I'm working with a modul written in Cobol. I have made a OLE-EXE Projekt out
of this code with NetExpress. Now I call this Cobol-EXE from a Visual Basic
Program. So far everything works fine. Only the performance is not so good.
It seems that the "invoke" from Cobol takes a lot of time. I'm using quite a
lot of those "invokes". I think it has something to do wit the the fact, that
VB calls Cobol over the so called Process Border. Is there anybody who has
some experience with that stuff ?

Oliver Klein

-----------== Posted via Deja News, The Discussion Network ==----------
http://www.dejanews.com/       Search, Read, Discuss, or Start Your Own
```

#### ↳ Re: Visual Basic calls Cobol EXE (NetExpress)

- **From:** smb@mfltdz.co.ukz (Steve Biggs)
- **Date:** 1999-02-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7arjih$jcr@hyperion.mfltd.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<7arges$qn1$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
OKLEIN@online.de wrote:
>Hallo,
>
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>some experience with that stuff ?

Hi Oliver,
Dynamic (late) bound method calls across process boundaries are always 
going to incur some overhead no matter what the language. There's always 
going to be a balance between the number of calls you have to make verses 
the number of parameters used on each call, too.

However, NetExpress 3.0 supports dual interfaces for COBOL OLE objects. 
This means that your VB program can early-bind to your COBOL object, and 
this will improve the call speed.

To do this, you need to create a type library which includes your 
COBOL object (marked as dual), register it with your object and import it 
into VB. There are various tools to help you to create this library (which 
are too technical to go into here, but are fully documented in the 
product). 

Steve.
MERANT Micro Focus Development.
```

```
run Micro Focus cobol gens and compiles.  I am trying to analyze a call 
to run, and am not helped by our documentation.  If anyone can point me 
to a source of information or is willing to help me dissect this, I 
appreciate it:

xm run $imsdir\pcims PSBGEN U:\%2\PSB\%1.PSB LIST(%1.LST) NOECHO VERIFY 
<U:\BIN\CRFILE

Please email me if possible.
```

<a id="t-1141c3b4c2"></a>
## Thread: Reading/Writing Memory Locations with NetExpress — 6 msgs

### Reading/Writing Memory Locations with NetExpress

- **From:** bob7536@aol.com (Bob7536)
- **Date:** 1999-02-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19990223071514.02259.00002064@ng-fq1.aol.com>`

```
Good Morning,

With MicroFocus Version 3.2 I used X"85" and X"86" to read and write memory
locations.  I am unsure how to do this with NetExpress.  I am using a computer
that has a appications-specific intergrated circuit (ASIC).  There are two I/O
ports located at I/O addresses 240h and 241h.  I need to be able to read read
and write a byte of data to these two addressed.  Can anyone make a suggetion
on how to accomplish this ?  The purpose of this is to control a cash drawer
that is attached to an IBM SureOne Point of Sale terminal.

Thanks,
Bob Hennessey
```

#### ↳ Re: Reading/Writing Memory Locations with NetExpress

- **From:** "Gael Wilson" <gw@mfltd.co.uk>
- **Date:** 1999-02-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7auabj$i3u@hyperion.mfltd.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<19990223071514.02259.00002064@ng-fq1.aol.com>`

```
Bob,

This may be oversimplifying what you are trying to do, but if you know the
memory address you could set a pointer to it, then assign the address of a
linkage item to the pointer and modify the linkage item directly.

eg
        working-storage section.
        01 ptr pointer.
        linkage section.
        01 mem pic x.
        procedure division.
            set ptr to null
            set ptr up by h"240"
            set address of mem to ptr.



Bob7536 wrote in message <19990223071514.02259.00002064@ng-fq1.aol.com>...
>Good Morning,
>
>With MicroFocus Version 3.2 I used X"85" and X"86" to read and write memory
>locations.  I am unsure how to do this with NetExpress.  I am using a
computer
>that has a appications-specific intergrated circuit (ASIC).  There are two
I/O
>ports located at I/O addresses 240h and 241h.  I need to be able to read
read
>and write a byte of data to these two addressed.  Can anyone make a
suggetion
>on how to accomplish this ?  The purpose of this is to control a cash
drawer
>that is attached to an IBM SureOne Point of Sale terminal.
>
>Thanks,
>Bob Hennessey
```

```
I haven't had the 'opportunity' to use NetExpress and the Windows
```

#### ↳ Re: Reading/Writing Memory Locations with NetExpress

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1999-02-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-FzkfkxlxudIR@Dwight_Miller.iix.com>`
- **References:** `<19990223071514.02259.00002064@ng-fq1.aol.com>`

```
On Tue, 23 Feb 1999 12:15:14, bob7536@aol.com (Bob7536) wrote:

> Good Morning,
> 
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> 


Bob,

I did this at my last job - but I did it differently.  You are going 
to laugh.   What is really cool about the SURE ONE is that you can 
configure the darn ports anyway you like.  We did it two ways.

The best way was to put the PRINTER on the serial port and the cash 
drawer on the COM port.  We then used the MicroFocus Windows printing 
calls to print to the default printer which we attached to COM1 - and 
sent the code to open the cash drawer to a file assigned to: "LPT1".  

The other way was to setup the cash drawer on the serial port and send
the code to a file assigned to "COM3" or whatever.  We just didn't 
make the calls to the memory addresses anymore, and I'm not sure you 
can even do it with the Windows stuff.
```

<a id="t-f80065a658"></a>
## Thread: MF Cobol & ibm3151 Delete EOL key — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### MF Cobol & ibm3151 Delete EOL key

- **From:** Philip Ross <pross@freeway.net>
- **Date:** 1999-02-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7av4se$f65@news.freeway.net>`

```
I just upgraded my MicroFocus Cobol from v3.0 to v4.1 running on an AIX
4.3.2 platform. Since then, my EOL (Erase to End of Line)  key on my
ibm3151 terminals does not operate properly.  The terminfo file looks
fine. I re-tic'd the file. Using keybcf to set the ADIS keys seems to
accept the keystroke. But instead of  deleting from the cursor to the
end of the line, it clears the field and puts a capital "I" in the first
space. The sequence for Erase to End of Line is "ESC I".  At a unix
prompt, pressing the key sends an "ESC I" as it is suppose to.

Why is Cobol not handling it properly?  An suggestions appreciated.

Phil Ross
pross@freeway.net
```

---

<a id="t-968e72b8ed"></a>
## Thread: RMCOBOL and Copy Replace — 2 this month / 4 total

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers) · [`Language features and syntax`](topics.md#syntax)_

### RMCOBOL and Copy Replace

- **From:** davidbm@ix.netcom.com (David)
- **Date:** 1999-02-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36d31a8c.20857062@nntp.ix.netcom.com>`

```
I am running RMCOBOL v. 6.1 and I am attempting to use the  COPY
REPLACING option.  The command I use is:

COPY "FILE.CPY" 
    replacing ==FAX== by ==FAX1==.

The compiler either ignores the REPLACING option or tells me that 'BY'
is required and aborts.  Can somone give me the correct syntax for
this command?

Thanks
```

#### ↳ Re: RMCOBOL and Copy Replace

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@inospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-02-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7av951$qth@dfw-ixnews8.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<36d31a8c.20857062@nntp.ix.netcom.com>`

```
Assuming you aren't trying to do "partial word" replacement, what you have
coded looks correct.  If you are trying to change a "prefix" in data-names
like

   05 FAX-THIS  PIC X.

then your syntax should be "ignored" - but if you are trying to change
something like

  05  FAX  PIC X.

it should work.
```

---

<a id="t-60602b0551"></a>
## Thread: Twiddle binary bit in Cobol? — 10 msgs

### Twiddle binary bit in Cobol?

- **From:** Mark Juchems <Juchems_mark_e@cat.com>
- **Date:** 1999-02-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36D2B6C9.7E2119A1@cat.com>`

```
I work at Caterpillar and we are trying to pack data in a hex comp-3
field.  Is there a way to set individual bits in a hex field?  In C you
can use the << operator to look and set them, however, I don't know
about COBOL?  I am using IBM mainframe cobol.

What we have is a string of On-Offs that need to be stored very
effeicently.
```

#### ↳ Re: Twiddle binary bit in Cobol?

- **From:** john@watkins.cix.[nospam]co.uk (John Watkins)
- **Date:** 1999-02-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<memo.19990223230256.38891A@jwatkins.compulink.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<36D2B6C9.7E2119A1@cat.com>`

```
In article <36D2B6C9.7E2119A1@cat.com>, Juchems_mark_e@cat.com (Mark 
Juchems) wrote:

> I work at Caterpillar and we are trying to pack data in a hex comp-3
> field.  Is there a way to set individual bits in a hex field?  In C you
> can use the << operator to look and set them, however, I don't know
> about COBOL?  I am using IBM mainframe cobol.

You can use Language Environment (LE/370, LE/MVS, etc) routines to 
manipulate bits on the mainframe .

| John Watkins | mailto:john@watkins_dot_cix.co.uk  |
|              | http://www_dot_cix.co.uk/~watkins/ |
+--------------+------------------------------------+
```

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36D344F9.5BD9@compuserve.com>`
- **References:** `<36D2B6C9.7E2119A1@cat.com>`

```
Mark, It can't be done without an Assembler* subroutine, e.g. to set / 
unset / test bit "n" or to pack / unpack bits to / from an 8-byte array. 
(* It CAN be written in COBOL but it's really messy.)

Mark Juchems wrote:
> 
> I work at Caterpillar and we are trying to pack data in a hex comp-3
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> effeicently.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Twiddle binary bit in Cobol?

- **From:** "Leif Svalgaard" <lsvalg@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 1999-02-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36d34869@news3.us.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<36D2B6C9.7E2119A1@cat.com> <36D344F9.5BD9@compuserve.com>`

```
The BITPK routine from ETK can manipulate bits.
Go to http://www.etk.com then downloads, then ETKPAK. Read the
documentation for ETKPAK.

John Piggott wrote in message <36D344F9.5BD9@compuserve.com>...
>Mark, It can't be done without an Assembler* subroutine, e.g. to set /
>unset / test bit "n" or to pack / unpack bits to / from an 8-byte array.
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>> effeicently.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Twiddle binary bit in Cobol?

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@inospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-02-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7autrt$7mg@sjx-ixn6.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<36D2B6C9.7E2119A1@cat.com> <36D344F9.5BD9@compuserve.com>`

```
I don't mean to argue (too much) with John, but if you are running under LE,
there are specific "bit-twiddling" callable services that you can use to
test or set bits.  If you are under LE, they are documented in the
Programmers Guide under "Bit Manipulation".  If you have trouble finding
them, let me know.  (These are not available to COBOL applications that are
pre-LE, so then you would probably want to call Assembler, PL/I, or
something else to do it for you)
```

#### ↳ Re: Twiddle binary bit in Cobol?

- **From:** swiegand@neo.rr.com (SkippyPB)
- **Date:** 1999-02-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<323DA499B441EEA1.65ACE556CF39D8BE.1CE9BB3E81761A42@library-proxy.airnews.net>`
- **References:** `<36D2B6C9.7E2119A1@cat.com>`

```
On Tue, 23 Feb 1999 08:10:18 -0600, Mark Juchems
<Juchems_mark_e@cat.com> enlightened us:

>I work at Caterpillar and we are trying to pack data in a hex comp-3
>field.  Is there a way to set individual bits in a hex field?  In C you
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>effeicently.

On an IBM mainframe running MVS or VSE, comp-3 is not binary data, but
packed data.  Example, if you have a field defined as PIC S999 COMP-3
and it had a value of +465, it would appear in storage as  00465C.
What you need are fields defines as COMP or USAGE IS BINARY.

HTH

Regards,

          ////
         (o o)
-oOO--(_)--OOo-

In prison there are wardens who are often sadistic and psychotic.
At work we call them managers.

 Steve
```

#### ↳ Re: Twiddle binary bit in Cobol?

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 1999-02-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7aut2e$6vj$1@news-1.news.gte.net>`
- **References:** `<36D2B6C9.7E2119A1@cat.com>`

```
Mark Juchems wrote in message <36D2B6C9.7E2119A1@cat.com>...
>I work at Caterpillar and we are trying to pack data in a hex comp-3
>field.  Is there a way to set individual bits in a hex field?  In C you
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Assuming your machine's COMP-3 is BCD (one decimal digit per nibble), if
what you want to do is store a series of boolean values (true/false,
on/off), COMP (binary) might be more efficient.

Using binary storage, you could then set/unset/interrogate bits using basic
multiply-or-divide-by-two and check-the-remainder arithmetic.

I have a text and graphics tutorial I wrote on the various COBOL data types;
it describes bit patterns and allows you to display the numeric value plus
hex and binary images of selected data values in various PICTURE formats on
the screen. (MS-DOS program).

If you are interested in this tutorial, drop me a note and I'll send you a
copy. (Freeware). Or better still, maybe someone can suggest a suitable Web
site and I'll post the file for anyone else who who like to use it.

MCM
Racine WI
```

#### ↳ Re: Twiddle binary bit in Cobol?

- **From:** Claude Pellerin <pclaude@GIL.COM.AU>
- **Date:** 1999-02-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36D3815F.93AB89F3@GIL.COM.AU>`
- **References:** `<36D2B6C9.7E2119A1@cat.com>`

```
On Unisys 2200s, the COBOL compiler allows PIC 1 to define a single bit eg:

01  SOME_DATA.
        03 FIRST_BIT   PIC 1 VALUE 0.
        03 SECOND_BIT PIC 1 VALUE 1.
        03 FILLER PIC 1(4).

etc...
Isn't there something like that on IBM?
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Twiddle binary bit in Cobol?

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@inospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-02-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7b057k$ang@dfw-ixnews9.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<36D2B6C9.7E2119A1@cat.com> <36D3815F.93AB89F3@GIL.COM.AU>`

```
No, this there is nothing like that (yet) on IBM S/390 mainframes.  The next
Standard will support such constructs and (finally) IBM mainframe COBOL
programmers won't have to convert to hex and play with items that way - or
even use LE callable services.
```

#### ↳ Re: Twiddle binary bit in Cobol?

- **From:** Mark Juchems <Juchems_mark_e@cat.com>
- **Date:** 1999-02-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36D402DC.FD7E9B5A@cat.com>`
- **References:** `<36D2B6C9.7E2119A1@cat.com>`

```
Thanks for all of the help.   I found out we have an inhouse routine -
CTBYTBIT and CTBITBYT that can do what I need.  I asked a few gurus around
here and they had no idea how to do it without the subroutine....

> I work at Caterpillar and we are trying to pack data in a hex comp-3
> field.  Is there a way to set individual bits in a hex field?  In C you
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
```

```
range is still the same, which in turn, makes the labor shortage look
worse than it really is.
```

<a id="t-2effff14ba"></a>
## Thread: Cobol OS/390 to C OS/390 V2R6 — 3 msgs

_Topics: [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe)_

### Cobol OS/390 to C OS/390 V2R6

- **From:** Roland Schiradin <schiradi@tap.de>
- **Date:** 1999-02-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36D1F48F.3BD75D87@tap.de>`

```
Hi,

just to let you know I have made some test and can confirm that IBM
drop the "#pragma linkage(xxxx,COBOL)" requirement with C OS/390 V2R6.

This mean you don't have to tell C who is the calling program and how
the parameters
list looks.

I'll expand my test-programs from time to time. Next step is C to Cobol
and
PL1 to Cobol. After that I 'll release my source/jcl on www.cbttape.org
File321


Roland
```

#### ↳ Re: Cobol OS/390 to C OS/390 V2R6

- **From:** "James King" <mangogwr@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1999-02-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<uzrA2.15045$OS5.15022241@news3.mia>`
- **References:** `<36D1F48F.3BD75D87@tap.de>`

```
well NO DUH.  That's why it called LE - Language Environment.
All programs COMPILED (not necessarily assembled) expect
to find the address of passed parms in R1 and return them in R1
and return any return-code in R15.  ALSO, for variable number of parms
the LAST item in the list has it's 0 bit (leftmost) set ON.  That way
all system SVC routines and system subs / compiled subs can
see the 'end of the list'

You can stop your 'testing'.  As long as your on OS/390, it'll work

Roland Schiradin wrote in message <36D1F48F.3BD75D87@tap.de>...
>Hi,
>
…[20 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

- **From:** Roland.Schiradin@t-online.de (Roland Schiradin)
- **Date:** 1999-02-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7aucmk$vqc$1@news00.btx.dtag.de>`
- **References:** `<36D1F48F.3BD75D87@tap.de> <uzrA2.15045$OS5.15022241@news3.mia>`

```
James King schrieb in Nachricht ...
>well NO DUH.  That's why it called LE - Language Environment.
>All programs COMPILED (not necessarily assembled) expect
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>see the 'end of the list'
This is no true for ILC between Cobol and  C OS/390 V2R4.
C have a different calling convention.

Roland
```

<a id="t-bc34e4e288"></a>
## Thread: WS prefixes — 15 msgs

### WS prefixes

- **From:** tmiller16@my-dejanews.com
- **Date:** 1999-02-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7b1eph$fl$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
We are having a debate over the proper use of prefixes when naming certain
Working-Storage items.	In the past, we have used short, commonly used
prefixes such as:

PRT-  (or PRT2- in the case of two print files) for print items
SWK- (or SWKx-) for sort-work items
DWK- (or DWKx-) for disk work items

There are some who feel that we should use (and mandate as a standard) the
full 7 or 8 character file name specified in the select statement.  I have
always assumed that the sort prefix was the most common, but maybe I'm wrong.

How do you assign prefixes in your shop?

Thanks.

-----------== Posted via Deja News, The Discussion Network ==----------
http://www.dejanews.com/       Search, Read, Discuss, or Start Your Own
```

#### ↳ Re: WS prefixes

- **From:** Warren Porter <warrenp@ASPMnetdoor.com>
- **Date:** 1999-02-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36D44928.29BC57AF@ASPMnetdoor.com>`
- **References:** `<7b1eph$fl$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
For items in a print line, we use PRT-;  other items in working-storage
occasionally start with WS-, but with many items in working storage placed there
by a script that prefix is sometimes omitted.  FD's and items in it are much more
standardized.  In a file named TM-SUBS-FILE, its key is named TM-SUBS-KEY, but
all other fields start with TMS-whatever.  In any program, any data name starting
with TMS- can immediately be traced to only one source.

tmiller16@my-dejanews.com wrote:

> We are having a debate over the proper use of prefixes when naming certain
> Working-Storage items.  In the past, we have used short, commonly used
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> How do you assign prefixes in your shop?
```

#### ↳ Re: WS prefixes

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com>
- **Date:** 1999-02-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36D4503B.E54D1C4F@NOSPAMhome.com>`
- **References:** `<7b1eph$fl$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
I don't reference data in FD or SD statements.  Instead I use
working-storage.  In this case, the short prefix obviously works.  

tmiller16@my-dejanews.com wrote:
> 
> We are having a debate over the proper use of prefixes when naming certain
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
> http://www.dejanews.com/       Search, Read, Discuss, or Start Your Own
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: WS prefixes

- **From:** paulr@bix.com (paulr)
- **Date:** 1999-02-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7b2735$t6a@lotho.delphi.com>`
- **References:** `<7b1eph$fl$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <36D4503B.E54D1C4F@NOSPAMhome.com>`

```
Interesting... and it shows our different backgrounds.

FD filenames in my stuff are always long: 
A master control file is probably going to 
be named:  
             MASTER-CONTROL-FILE

The associated 01 record will be - yep, you guess it: 
             MASTER-CONTROL-FILE-RECORD.

I don't use SD's all that much, so I would usually just name it 
SD-SOME-FILE-NAME.

Working storage is usually designated two or three ways, 
depending upon what it is. Local working stuff invariably 
has a WS in front if it. 

Record definitions are usually the same as in the FD's, but
with an 01 name of WS-MASTER-CONTROL-FILE-RECORD. Makes it
handy to use MOVE CORRESPONDING. 

-Paul


Howard Brazee (brazee@NOSPAMhome.com) wrote:
: I don't reference data in FD or SD statements.  Instead I use
: working-storage.  In this case, the short prefix obviously works.  

: tmiller16@my-dejanews.com wrote:
: > 
: > We are having a debate over the proper use of prefixes when naming certain
: > Working-Storage items.  In the past, we have used short, commonly used
: > prefixes such as:
: > 
: > PRT-  (or PRT2- in the case of two print files) for print items
: > SWK- (or SWKx-) for sort-work items
: > DWK- (or DWKx-) for disk work items
: > 
: > There are some who feel that we should use (and mandate as a standard) the
: > full 7 or 8 character file name specified in the select statement.  I have
: > always assumed that the sort prefix was the most common, but maybe I'm wrong.
: > 
: > How do you assign prefixes in your shop?
: > 
: > Thanks.
: > 
: > -----------== Posted via Deja News, The Discussion Network ==----------
: > http://www.dejanews.com/       Search, Read, Discuss, or Start Your Own
```

#### ↳ Re: WS prefixes

- **From:** "Alex the Man" <wod_charon@hotmail.com>
- **Date:** 1999-02-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<919888848.590888@news.netactive.co.za>`
- **References:** `<7b1eph$fl$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
In FD filename is aaannntn
where aaa is the abbreviation for system
where nnn is a numeric value
where t = type (p = print file, k = control file, u = output (uitvoer))
where n is a numeric value so we can have more than one of a type sometimes
even go into double digits

then we use the tn as start of file layout
lets take xxx001p1. where record is p1-record.

01 p1-record.
     03  p1-description     pic x(40).
     03  p1-date                pic 9(08).
     03  p1-person.
           05  p1-surname   pic x(20)
           05  p1-name        pic x(20).
etc.

In working storage it is named w01-xxxxxxxxxxxxxx, w02-xxxxxxxxxxxxx, ...,
w99-xxxxxxxxxxxxxx or even past w99-
where x is a descriptive name the  w  is to say that it is in working
storage section
These layouts handled same as in fd.
```

#### ↳ Re: WS prefixes

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1999-02-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<hs%A2.3648$LN5.4958330@news2.mia>`
- **References:** `<7b1eph$fl$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
tmiller16@my-dejanews.com wrote:
>We are having a debate over the proper use of prefixes when naming certain
>Working-Storage items. In the past, we have used short, commonly used
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>How do you assign prefixes in your shop?

I don't try to make all my prefixes the same size, I don't see any
benefit in doing so, I think clarity is more important.  I use 'WS-'
for all 77 level items, and only 77 level entries (or 01 level items
with a picture, when used like a 77).  For all other data names, I
take the name of the 01 level item and make an acronym from the name
and use it on all data items subordinate to that 01.  For file names
I usually use something like 'PRINT-FILE' or 'WORK-FILE' and name
the records 'PRINT-RECORD' and 'WORK-RECORD'.  The data items in the
records would have prefixes of 'PR-' and 'WR-'.  My prefixes are
usually two to five characters, whatever it takes for clarity and
uniqueness.  Larger programs often require longer prefixes to make
them both unique and clear.

I go to great lengths to make the remainder of the data name after
the prefix to be consistent throughout the whole system.  When I am
designing a new system, I create both a data name dictionary and an
abbreviation dictionary, so new data names can easily be created
using consistent abbreviations.  I hate to see 'number' abbreviated
'NO', 'NBR', 'NUM' and 'NR' all in the same system.  Chaos!  one of
the marks of excellence is consistency, consistency, consistency.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: WS prefixes

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 1999-02-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Pe1B2.4$pK4.152@news19.ispnews.com>`
- **References:** `<7b1eph$fl$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <hs%A2.3648$LN5.4958330@news2.mia>`

```

Judson McClendon wrote in message ...
[...]
>...  I hate to see 'number' abbreviated
>'NO', 'NBR', 'NUM' and 'NR' all in the same system.  Chaos!  one of
>the marks of excellence is consistency, consistency, consistency.
>--
I hate "number" in any form. I found such inconsistent abbreviations
in the systems I inherited. I decided to use the consistent form, "id".
Hence, "employee-id' is the identifier for an employee, this employee
works at a company, which is identified by its "company-id"; is
assigned to a department, which is identified by its "department-id";
and works on jobs, which are identified by their "job-id"; etc.

I also found cases where department numbers and job numbers
contained letters, which is inconsistent with using number as part
of the variable name.
-------------------------------
Rick Smith
e-mail: < ricksmith@aiservices.com >
```

```
it varies, but most common i've seen is everything in working-storage
starts WS-, linkage LS-  Communication CS-.
given COPYBOOK named FDFILE01 with
01  file01-record.
      05   :file01:-emp-id-no           pic x(16).
      05   :file01:-emp-hire-date     pic s9(08) comp.

we COPY FDFILE01 replacing ==:FILE01:== by ==WS-FILE01==
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: WS prefixes aren't needed

- **From:** "John Hicks (Remove \\"nospam\\" from address before replying)" <johnhicks@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 1999-02-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36D4D891.6A3C0506@ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<7b1eph$fl$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <Xw0B2.3665$LN5.4996580@news2.mia>`

```
It does appear that most shops prefer unique datanames everywhere, but I
prefer Paul's (paulr@bix.com) approach: Define the data fields in a
given record type one time and use those names consistently whenever a
record reoccurs in the file section, working storage, etc. In other
words, take full advantage of COBOL's data qualification feature.

For each record type, I set up a copybook containing the data elements
in that record (but not the 01 level). Then I invoke it following a
hard-coded 01 level wherever that record occurs. 

All of this makes maintenance much easier, particularly in a large
system. It also makes our COBOL records a step closer to the records of
Pascal, the structs of C, and, yes, the classes of C++.

--John Hicks
johnhicks at ibm dot net

> 
> Interesting... and it shows our different backgrounds.
…[45 more quoted lines elided]…
> : > http://www.dejanews.com/       Search, Read, Discuss, or Start Your Own

…[28 more quoted lines elided]…
> >http://www.dejanews.com/       Search, Read, Discuss, or Start Your Own
```

```
Simple way is WSI for IN and WSO for OUT, WS if either way.\
FDI and FDO if working from buffer
SD is fine for sorts
DWK?? I usually know whether the program is disk or tape and wouldnt want
to make the program locked (syntactally) to a certain device.

Just my personal 2cents
Chuck
```

```
: It's interesting to know how you guys can get a job without not only having
```

#### ↳ Re: how does it happen?

- **From:** "Jeff" <a@a.com>
- **Date:** 1999-02-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<EIoB2.356$bk.158214@storm.twcol.com>`
- **References:** `<7b12rf$a4@bgtnsc02.worldnet.att.net>`

```
>It's interesting to know how you guys can get a job without not only having
>a clue haw to do it but not even reading a book to convince an interviewer
>you have 'marginal' knowledge?   Please enlighten me?
>
>30 years in US immigrant.


Remember most of the managers are men and since we are all chauvinist pigs
it makes it hard for a woman to get a job.

People are also more likely to hire someone they know rather than Joe Blow
off the street, regardless of gender or experience. I as a former consultant
worked with several "company men" who had no formal computer education but
had worked for the company for many years. Sometimes lack of education can
be good incentive to keep a person from moving on to a better location.

In the end you don't necessarily have to convince the interviewer of the
company, just the interviewer at the consulting agency. Allot of the
consulting agencies are hiring people with little experience to fill
positions they are not quite qualified for just to fill the positions which
they can receive their all-important percentage of the hourly rate which
they are charging the client for. Although I was qualified for all the
positions I took as a consultant, many I took without an interview. My
account manager was very good at pimping me to the clients. My former
consulting agency was getting paid almost twice what I was receiving for my
services. Their incentive to place a consultant in a position far
overshadows the consultants abilities to perform the job. To give the
consultants credit, most people don't know what they can do until they are
given assignments they don't think they can accomplish. If you just shut up
and do the best you can, most of the time that is enough.

I spent 9 months sending resumes to regular companies looking for jobs.
Literally hundreds of resumes. I sent just 7 to consulting agencies and my
answering machine was filled when I got home the next day.

No matter how disappointing the job search gets, remember you can always
sell your soul to a consulting agency.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: how does it happen?

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com>
- **Date:** 1999-02-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36D6B097.9A573DDB@NOSPAMhome.com>`
- **References:** `<7b12rf$a4@bgtnsc02.worldnet.att.net> <EIoB2.356$bk.158214@storm.twcol.com>`

```
Jeff wrote:

> I spent 9 months sending resumes to regular companies looking for jobs.
> Literally hundreds of resumes. I sent just 7 to consulting agencies and my
> answering machine was filled when I got home the next day.

Oh yes. 
 
> No matter how disappointing the job search gets, remember you can always
> sell your soul to a consulting agency.

Why is this selling your soul?  I was placed in my current position
after a phone interview - I was supposed to work 3 months, expanded to
the maximum legal 6 months, and then allowed to expand for a year
before.  A couple of times the my employer offered me a regular job but
I turned them down - the consultant paid much more.  This last time it
was within $20,000 plus benefits and I accepted their offer.  I had to
apply, and then I was told I had already had my interview.  Unlike most
hires - they knew what I could do and how well I worked with other
employees.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: how does it happen?

- **From:** "Jeff" <a@a.com>
- **Date:** 1999-02-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<hXGB2.413$bk.789416@storm.twcol.com>`
- **References:** `<7b12rf$a4@bgtnsc02.worldnet.att.net> <EIoB2.356$bk.158214@storm.twcol.com> <36D6B097.9A573DDB@NOSPAMhome.com>`

```
>Why is this selling your soul?

As a consultant I found that most managers don't like hearing suggestions
from their consultants. They don't want consultants, they want
contractors/indentured servants. They want you to do what you are told and
stop finding errors in their suggestions. Sometimes this is due to just
plain incompetence, but most times this is just due to the amount of money
they have to spend on your hourly rate as a contractor. Thus a
consultant/contractor/indentured servant will do whatever it takes to get it
done, and get it done quickly. Regardless of how wrong, inefficient, or what
it will affect in the long run. Thus giving up your beliefs and just a
little piece of your soul.

If you aren't being paid as much managers are more willing to allow you to
spend the time to do something right, rather than quick. The risk is lower
and almost always saves trouble in the long run.

Doing quality work should never mean they have to pay you allot of money.
The incentive should be just to do a good job, not to make a ridiculous
amount of money.
```

```
done using MS visual Studio.

it looks like I will have to define templates / and language settings (unless
there is somewhere I could download these). I does lack syntax higlighing/
checking, which would be nice.

Daniel
```

```
block comment/uncomment upper case/lower case and total undo/redo +
find/replace cut/copy/paste etc.
```

```
Does the PIC 1 on COBOL/400 actually give you bit-level access?  I thought

```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Twiddle binary bit in Cobol?

- **From:** "Gary S. Drummond" <gdrumm@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-02-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36D4F0D8.D7E2E92C@ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<36D2B6C9.7E2119A1@cat.com> <c44_9902242145@hccfido.hcc.nl> <36d48eb0.11471267@news.indigo.ie> <7b25ha$kos@dfw-ixnews12.ix.netcom.com>`

```
Fujitsu allows a pic 1 also, but it's not explained very well.

The Unisys 1100/2200 pic 1 is just that, bit fields. 
It's not standard, but it lets me write some things in COBOL and 
skip ASM/MASM for some of my utilities. (i.e. Hard code screen formats
and send anything to a terminal/periperal device.)
....
04  a-9bit-byte.
    06 filler pic 1.
    06 upper-nibble pic 1(4).
    06 upper-two    pic 1(2).
    06 lower-two    pic 1(2).
....
36 bit word, 4 x 9-bit bytes internal, 8 or 9 bit bytes can be used
during I/O if desired, and medium supports it. 

Gary
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Sort record

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@inospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-02-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7b4pk6$m2b@dfw-ixnews7.ix.netcom.com>`

```
Yes, you do need a SELECT/ASSIGN statement for your SD.
  NO, the chances are whatever you put in the ASSIGN statement *will* not be
used by the compiler

If you are using Fujitsu COBOL (and no other compiler), you need to make
certain you use the filename format of the Assign and not the "literal"
format.

Show the whole NG what you have, and we should be able to help you.
```
