###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Sort record

- **From:** "Jeff" <a@a.com>
- **Date:** 1999-02-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<N7oB2.320$bk.136225@storm.twcol.com>`
- **References:** `<36d4deea.8592302@news.rmi.net> <36D55C1D.D03D6750@ASPMnetdoor.com> <7b4krb$p6e$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
>> Put your SD(s) (Sort Description) after your FD's.  Good luck on your
>> next exam.
>
>I tried that, but it chokes on the code.  Essentially telling me operation
>SORT-FILE is undefined.


Some versions of COBOL allow for dynamic allocation of temporary files.
Check the DYNAM compiler option. Assuming you are working under MVS, you may
need to define a temporary DSN in the JCL associated with the sort file
DDNAME for the NODYNAM compiler option:

//SORTFILE DD DSN=&&SORTFILE,DISP=(NEW,DELETE,DELETE)

Your SD should look something like:

       SD  SORT-FILE.
       01  SORT-FILE-RECORD.
           05 SORT-KEY ........
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Sort record

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@inospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-02-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7b777c$35h@dfw-ixnews11.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<36d4deea.8592302@news.rmi.net> <36D55C1D.D03D6750@ASPMnetdoor.com> <7b4krb$p6e$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <N7oB2.320$bk.136225@storm.twcol.com>`

```
Just to "correct" something in the attached note (or at least as I read it).
If you are talking MVS (or OS/390), the DYNAM compiler option has absolutely
NOTHING to do with "dynamic file allocation".  There may be some compiler
somewhere where this is true, but certainly not from IBM.

FYI,
  The file specified in the ASSIGN clause for an SD (for most but NOT ALL
compilers) has little or nothing to do with where you physical "sort work"
files are allocated.  There is nothing in the Standard that says that these
shouldn't be related, but neither is there anything that says they should.
Therefore, (as I have stated in other posts) most compilers basically just
ignore the information in the Select/Assign clause for an SD.

Out of curiosity, does anyone know a compiler that DOES pay attention to the
Select/Assign clause for an SD?  I am certain that there are some, so I am
genuinely interested in hearing the answer.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Sort record

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>
- **Date:** 1999-02-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36D936A5.1417E8C3@zip.com.au>`
- **References:** `<36d4deea.8592302@news.rmi.net> <36D55C1D.D03D6750@ASPMnetdoor.com> <7b4krb$p6e$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <N7oB2.320$bk.136225@storm.twcol.com> <7b777c$35h@dfw-ixnews11.ix.netcom.com>`

```
William M. Klein wrote:
> 
> Just to "correct" something in the attached note (or at least as I read it).
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

There is a parameter that says this in the compiler set up.  I cannot
remember the name but it does exist.  I had a problem with an empty
dataset after Fujitsu to MVS conversion, the DDname was supposed to be
XYZO1 (XYZ output 1) instead it was defined as XYZ01 (XYZ zero 1). 
After some hunting we found the parameter and turned it off and a lot of
interesting features appeared in our conversion.  I prefer it to be OFF
for this reason, I consider dynamic allocation a bad thing for everyone.

I never use internal sort so I cannot say that flipping the flag will or
will not affect it.

Happy hunting
Ken
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Sort record

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@inospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-02-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7bcfcv$t08@sjx-ixn6.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<36d4deea.8592302@news.rmi.net> <36D55C1D.D03D6750@ASPMnetdoor.com> <7b4krb$p6e$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <N7oB2.320$bk.136225@storm.twcol.com> <7b777c$35h@dfw-ixnews11.ix.netcom.com> <36D936A5.1417E8C3@zip.com.au>`

```
The option is NOT a compiler option but is the LE
   CBLQDA
run-time option.   If you take what USED to be the LE default, it would
create a file - that would not get cataloged and would disappear at the end
of the job step.

With the latest release of LE (OS/390 2.6), the default has now been changed
to the not-ANSI conforming but what most MVSers want of
  NOCBLQDA

Note:  IBM compilers on VM, OS/2, and NT (I am not certain about OS/400) all
create "usable" files in conformance with the ANSI/ISO Standard - and you
can use them without any difficulty.  It is only OS/390 and MVS where IBM
implemented this in an unusable manner.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Sort record

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** John Trifon <jtrifon@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-02-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36D761DF.72091F53@ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<36d4deea.8592302@news.rmi.net> <36D55C1D.D03D6750@ASPMnetdoor.com> <7b4krb$p6e$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <N7oB2.320$bk.136225@storm.twcol.com>`

```
Under MVS, there is no need to define an //SORTFILE. The compiler requires the
SD's SELECT/ASSIGN clause only to satisfy syntax but never uses the the
assigned_name at all. All sorting occurs in the //SORTWKxx files as required.
Rather than define a slew of  //SORTWKxx's in your JCL, it would be better to
tell SORT to dynamically allocate  sort space as needed. This can be done with:
//$ORTPARM DD *     << syncsort
    OPTION DYNALLOC

//IGZSRTCD  DD * << dfsort
     OPTION DYNALLOC

These parameters are not related to the compiler DYNAM option.


Jeff wrote:

> >> Put your SD(s) (Sort Description) after your FD's.  Good luck on your
> >> next exam.
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
>            05 SORT-KEY ........
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Sort record

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Frank Yaeger <fyaeger@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 1999-02-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36D8084D.93C7F2A8@ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<36d4deea.8592302@news.rmi.net> <36D55C1D.D03D6750@ASPMnetdoor.com> <7b4krb$p6e$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <N7oB2.320$bk.136225@storm.twcol.com> <36D761DF.72091F53@ix.netcom.com>`

```


John Trifon wrote:

> Rather than define a slew of  //SORTWKxx's in your JCL, it would be better to
> tell SORT to dynamically allocate  sort space as needed. This can be done with:
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>

 Actually, the DFSORT equivalent of Syncsort's //$ORTPARM DD would be:

 //DFSPARM DD *
    OPTION DYNALLOC
 /*

 Or you can even use //$ORTPARM if you set DFSORT's PARMDDN installation option
 to PARMDDN=$ORTPARM (the shipped default is PARMDDN=DFSPARM).

 However, OPTION DYNALLOC is not normally required since DFSORT will
 automatically use dynamically allocated work data sets by default if you don't
 have any JCL SORTWKs (set DFSORT's DYNAUTO=IGNWKDD installation option if you
 want DFSORT to use dynamically allocated work data sets INSTEAD of JCL SORTWKs).

Frank Yaeger - DFSORT Team (Specialties: Y2K, Symbols, ICETOOL, OUTFIL :-)
fyaeger@vnet.ibm.com
DFSORT/MVS is on the World Wide Web at URL:
     http://www.ibm.com/storage/dfsort/
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Sort record

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1999-02-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36d84660.895875@news1.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<36d4deea.8592302@news.rmi.net> <36D55C1D.D03D6750@ASPMnetdoor.com> <7b4krb$p6e$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <N7oB2.320$bk.136225@storm.twcol.com> <36D761DF.72091F53@ix.netcom.com> <36D8084D.93C7F2A8@ibm.net>`

```
On Sat, 27 Feb 1999 06:59:25 -0800, Frank Yaeger <fyaeger@ibm.net>
wrote:

> However, OPTION DYNALLOC is not normally required since DFSORT will
> automatically use dynamically allocated work data sets by default if you don't
> have any JCL SORTWKs (set DFSORT's DYNAUTO=IGNWKDD installation option if you
> want DFSORT to use dynamically allocated work data sets INSTEAD of JCL SORTWKs).

it's FAST.  I'm very impressed with DFSORT in it's present
incarnation.

## Thread: Power-Cobol Table-Item — 4 msgs

### Power-Cobol Table-Item

- **From:** "Hajo Schepker" <schepker@geocities.com>
- **Date:** 1999-02-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7b44av$vkf$1@namib.north.de>`

```
Hello,
I need help.

How can I make it, that in a table-item always the last line is visible.

Thanks for help.
Hajo
```

#### ↳ Re: Power-Cobol Table-Item

- **From:** "don ferrario" <don@ferrario.com>
- **Date:** 1999-02-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7b4soi$44n$1@news1.epix.net>`
- **References:** `<7b44av$vkf$1@namib.north.de>`

```
You can set the "ScrollLock" property to make a *column*
always visible, but can't do it with a row.

Possibly you could switch your rows/columns to make
the data you want to lock, in a column, instead of a row.
```

#### ↳ Re: Power-Cobol Table-Item

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com>
- **Date:** 1999-02-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36D5AF4A.F5C68B06@NOSPAMhome.com>`
- **References:** `<7b44av$vkf$1@namib.north.de>`

```
Hajo Schepker wrote:
> 
> Hello,
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> Hajo


I'm not sure what you're saying, but could this be what you want?  My
table is indexed by a TABLE-INDEX and a TABLE-SIZE, I never change
table-size.  Variable length tables work almost the same way.

    SET TABLE-SIZE TO 1
    SET TABLE-SIZE UP BY 1
    READ TABLE-FILE INTO TABLE-RECORD
        AT-END SET TABLE-EOF TO TRUE.
    PERFORM 0100-BUILD-TABLE 
        UNTIL TABLE-EOF.
    DISPLAY TABLE-VALUE (TABLE-SIZE).

0100-BUILD-TABLE.
    SET TABLE-SIZE UP BY 1.
    MOVE TABLE-RECORD TO TABLE-VALUE (TABLE-SIZE)
    READ TABLE-FILE INTO TABLE-RECORD
        AT-END SET TABLE-EOF TO TRUE.
```

#### ↳ Re: Power-Cobol Table-Item

- **From:** "Hajo Schepker" <schepker@geocities.com>
- **Date:** 1999-02-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7b9ckk$4fb$1@namib.north.de>`
- **References:** `<7b44av$vkf$1@namib.north.de>`

```
Perhaps I can make it clearler

In a Sheet ( I use Power-Cobol V3) I placed a table-Item with 4 rows.
When I added values into the table using the SETROW-Command and the value of
SETROS is 5 then a scrollbar appears and only the four rows I defined are
visible.
the fifth row is only visible, when I scroll.
I now want that the program automatic scrolls to the last row.

Thanks
H. Schepker
```

<a id="t-480902334d"></a>
## Thread: Urgent Help Needed On Indexed Files I/O — 14 this month / 15 total

_Topics: [`VSAM, files, sorting`](topics.md#files) · [`Help requests and how-to`](topics.md#help)_

### Urgent Help Needed On Indexed Files I/O

- **From:** Adnan Hussain <ah6@coventry.ac.uk>
- **Date:** 1999-02-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Pine.OSF.3.91.990225114206.5343A-100000@leofric>`

```

For a collage course I have to program a small database type application 
for a Video rental company, and I'm having a few problems. Here is my 
customer file:

       FD  CUSTOMER-FILE
	       LABEL RECORDS ARE STANDARD.
	   01  CUSTOMER-RECORD.
	   05  CUSTOMER-NO         PIC 9(6)    VALUE ZERO.
	   05  CUSTOMER-SUR        PIC A(20)   VALUE SPACE.
	   05  CUSTOMER-FIRST      PIC X(30)   VALUE SPACE.
	   05  CUSTOMER-ADDRESS    PIC X(30)   VALUE SPACE.
	   05  NUMBER-VIDEO        PIC 9       VALUE 0.

Whenever a customer rents a video the NUMBER-VIDEO should increment.
However, it won't let me do an ADD 1 TO NUMBER-VIDEO. It will compile,
but then crash at that point.

What I *can* do is:
               ADD 1 TO WS-NUMBER-VIDEO.
               MOVE WS-NUMBER-VIDEO TO NUMBER-VIDEO.

However, WS-NUMBER-VIDEO is initalised each time, and therefore only the 
number 1 will be moved to NUMBER-VIDEO, even if the customer rents 2 or 3 
videos.

I then tried:
               MOVE NUMBER-VIDEO TO WS-NUMBER-VIDEO.
               ADD 1 TO WS-NUMBER-VIDEO.
               MOVE WS-NUMBER-VIDEO TO NUMBER-VIDEO.

and the same thing happens, compiles, but then crashes at that point. 
Both times, the error was:

Execution error : file 'ass2a.int'
error code: 163, pc=B4E, call=1, seg=0
163     Illegal character in numeric field 

It seems to me that I can't manipulate the fields of a particular record 
directly, it has to be done via a variable. Nor can I read a field *into*
a variable. How do I get around this ?

BTW, I'm sure I'm using the READ CUSTOMER-FILE commands correctly, 
because I *can* do a DISPLAY NUMBER-VIDEO, I just can't ADD TO, or MOVE 
from the field.

Please help. BTW I'm using Micro Focus COBOL for UNIX V3.1 revision 049.

Thanks in advance.
```

#### ↳ Re: Urgent Help Needed On Indexed Files I/O

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 1999-02-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<mPbB2.1$Af2.43@news19.ispnews.com>`
- **References:** `<Pine.OSF.3.91.990225114206.5343A-100000@leofric>`

```

Adnan Hussain wrote in message ...
>
>For a collage course I have to program a small database type application
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
>
[...]
>Execution error : file 'ass2a.int'
>error code: 163, pc=B4E, call=1, seg=0
>163     Illegal character in numeric field
>

In a FD, VALUE clauses are treated as comments. NUMBER-VIDEO
likely contains a SPACE. A SPACE is an "Illegal character in (a) numeric
field".

After opening the file, do one of the following:
    INITIALIZE CUSTOMER-RECORD
or
    MOVE ZERO TO NUMBER-VIDEO.
Either of these instructions will ensure that NUMBER-VIDEO contains
a legal value.

On another point, you have triggered a pet peeve of mine by using
CUSTOMER-NO and NUMBER-VIDEO. I avoid the use of "number"
in any form, I would suggest CUSTOMER-ID and
VIDEOS-RENTED-COUNT as replacements. See the ng thread
"WS prefixes" posts by Judson McClendon and myself.

HTH
-------------------------------
Rick Smith
e-mail: < ricksmith@aiservices.com >
```

#### ↳ Re: Urgent Help Needed On Indexed Files I/O

- **From:** donald@willmack.com (Donald Tees)
- **Date:** 1999-02-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7b3l1c$27v$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<Pine.OSF.3.91.990225114206.5343A-100000@leofric>`

```
Your problem is that the VIDEO-NUMBER in the record
was not set to zero when you created the record. It
probably contains a NULL instead of the ASCII ZERO.

For the arithmetic to work, it *must* contain a valid
numeric value --- IE: one of the digits 0-9.  The correct
way to create the record (and this should go into the routine
where you originally create that record) is to initialize
each field before you write it.  The alpha fields should
be set to spaces (Or a name like "new csutomer") and the
numeric fields should be set to zero, or some other legal
value.

Some Cobols automatically initialize working storage, even
though that is not to spec.  Either that is the case, or you
set the WS value to zero with a value statement, which is
why it works in WS. The move of a WS value will aslo work, as
that is how you initialize it.


In article <Pine.OSF.3.91.990225114206.5343A-100000@leofric>, 
ah6@coventry.ac.uk says...
>
>
…[49 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

#### ↳ Re: Urgent Help Needed On Indexed Files I/O

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@inospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-02-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7b47em$na7@dfw-ixnews3.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<Pine.OSF.3.91.990225114206.5343A-100000@leofric>`

```
There have been several posts about trying to do arithmetic on fields in
your FD, but I think that either they - or I - have missed the point of your
application.  Am I correct that each record is suppose to have a higher
number than the previous record?  If so, then you need to define your
"counter" in Working-Storage any way and move it to your FD.  You can do
this explicitly - or you could do it by doing a WRITE FROM.  In this case,
your whole record description would be in working-storage; you would
initialize all the fields before ANY record was written; and you would build
each record in working-storage - including an ADD 1 to your counter.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Urgent Help Needed On Indexed Files I/O

- **From:** paulr@bix.com (paulr)
- **Date:** 1999-02-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7b4frg$fs8@lotho.delphi.com>`
- **References:** `<Pine.OSF.3.91.990225114206.5343A-100000@leofric> <7b47em$na7@dfw-ixnews3.ix.netcom.com>`

```
Just a little offtopic, but why do people prefer to use ADD, SUBTRACT,
etc., rather than COMPUTE? 

The assembly code for COMPUTE usually comes out the same or even a
little more efficient than the standalones, and the infix notation 
is more common, albeit, not 'English.'

I occasionally do use FD data items for computational purposes,
usually when incrementing counters. 

   READ MASTER-CONTORL-FILE ...
   COMPUTE THIS-INDEX = THIS-INDEX + 1
   MOVE THIS-INDEX to WS-STORAGE-SOMEWHERE
   WRITE MASTER-CONTROL-FILE-RECORD...

(With appropriate error checking on the reads and such.) 

Common wisdom has this as being a BAD thing, but most modern compilers
have no problem with it. I do note that people sometimes expect the 
FD storage to be valid *after* the write, and *that* isn't so.

Unless there is something I don't know about (possible! :) FD space
is nothing special, just an allocated memory area like any other. 
I don't see a lot of advantage to moving a value to WS, computing
it there, then moving it back to FD storage. (?) 

-Paul

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Urgent Help Needed On Indexed Files I/O

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1999-02-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7b4per$oah$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<Pine.OSF.3.91.990225114206.5343A-100000@leofric> <7b47em$na7@dfw-ixnews3.ix.netcom.com> <7b4frg$fs8@lotho.delphi.com>`

```
As far as the non-use of compute goes, for me it is
simply personal preference.  I do use compute, but usually
only when there are several terms involved, or I want to
use brackets to make the computation clearer.

For a single operand, I like the English ... I always feel
it reads better.

paulr wrote in message <7b4frg$fs8@lotho.delphi.com>...
>Just a little offtopic, but why do people prefer to use ADD, SUBTRACT,
>etc., rather than COMPUTE?
…[30 more quoted lines elided]…
>: your FD, but I think that either they - or I - have missed the point of
your
>: application.  Am I correct that each record is suppose to have a higher
>: number than the previous record?  If so, then you need to define your
>: "counter" in Working-Storage any way and move it to your FD.  You can do
>: this explicitly - or you could do it by doing a WRITE FROM.  In this
case,
>: your whole record description would be in working-storage; you would
>: initialize all the fields before ANY record was written; and you would
build
>: each record in working-storage - including an ADD 1 to your counter.
>
…[27 more quoted lines elided]…

3
>: >videos.
>: >
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…

*into*

…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ FD vs Working-Storage (was Re: Urgent Help Needed On Indexed Files I/O

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@inospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-02-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7b4psm$m5n@dfw-ixnews7.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<Pine.OSF.3.91.990225114206.5343A-100000@leofric> <7b47em$na7@dfw-ixnews3.ix.netcom.com> <7b4frg$fs8@lotho.delphi.com>`

```
Check out DejaNews.
  There are about monthly "discussions" on when the information under an FD
are

    A) available
    B) correct

We never seem to "end" the argument; some think the Standard is clear and
some think it is not; there are still reports of some compilers allowing
access at various times that others don't.

The bottom-line:
  To be absolutely positively "certain" that you have what you think you
should have, use WRITE FROM and READ INTO.  If the performance "hit" from
this is to great for your particular compiler and it DOES let you access the
FD when your coding style "likes" doing so, then go for it.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Urgent Help Needed On Indexed Files I/O

- **From:** "Warren Simmons" <w.g.simmons@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 1999-02-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7b4ri8$prq@bgtnsc01.worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<Pine.OSF.3.91.990225114206.5343A-100000@leofric> <7b47em$na7@dfw-ixnews3.ix.netcom.com> <7b4frg$fs8@lotho.delphi.com>`

```
Why don't people use COMPUTE instead of ADD,
SUBTRACT, MULTIPLY?
:In the following off topic portion the question about preferences can be
cleared up, if you really want to know, as follows.

COBOL was specified as a common business oriented language.  Early on, the
math educated  designers were sure that ment that the users were
only trained to add, subtract, etc.  They felt that FORTRAN took care of the
COMPUTE users.
This is just one of the many things that had to be overcome over the years.
It was a long time before any discussion about functions would be tolerated
after COMPUTE was added.

The short answer is that what business wants to do with computers changes in
definition as time goes by.  Who knew what business math entailed? Certainly
not the early designers. COBOL was and continues to be a work in progress.

Warren Simmons
paulr wrote in message <7b4frg$fs8@lotho.delphi.com>...
>Just a little offtopic, but why do people prefer to use ADD, SUBTRACT,
>etc., rather than COMPUTE?
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

>Just a little offtopic, but why do people prefer to use ADD, SUBTRACT,
>etc., rather than COMPUTE? 
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Depends on the compiler.  I avoid DIVISION in a compute for accuracy.
I avoid COMPUTE altogether with CA-Realia COBOL - it's very
inefficient.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Urgent Help Needed On Indexed Files I/O

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com>
- **Date:** 1999-02-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36D6AF43.EFB24D56@NOSPAMhome.com>`
- **References:** `<Pine.OSF.3.91.990225114206.5343A-100000@leofric> <7b47em$na7@dfw-ixnews3.ix.netcom.com> <7b4frg$fs8@lotho.delphi.com> <36d60e2f.89263134@news1.ibm.net>`

```
>Just a little offtopic, but why do people prefer to use ADD, SUBTRACT,
>etc., rather than COMPUTE?

Unless I have a performance issue, I use whichever construct fits best
with the function of what I'm doing.   If I am incrementing, I use ADD
(or more likely use varying or even set).  Complicated arithmetic is
done with a COMPUTE.

I only use DIVIDE when I am looking for a remainder or modulus.

Sometimes there will be a difference in the following code:

MOVE ZERO TO MY-FIELD.
COMPUTE MY-FIELD = ZERO.

This is dependent on your environment, and the difference (if it exists)
has to do with internal representation of signs.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Urgent Help Needed On Indexed Files I/O

- **From:** William Lynch <wblynch@att.net>
- **Date:** 1999-02-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36D641A1.42A8B559@att.net>`
- **References:** `<Pine.OSF.3.91.990225114206.5343A-100000@leofric> <7b47em$na7@dfw-ixnews3.ix.netcom.com> <7b4frg$fs8@lotho.delphi.com>`

```
paulr wrote:
> 
(snip)
> Unless there is something I don't know about (possible! :) FD space
> is nothing special, just an allocated memory area like any other.

True, but the key here is *when*. Your W-S is always present, before,
after & during OPEN/READ/WRITE/CLOSE.

Bill Lynch
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Urgent Help Needed On Indexed Files I/O

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1999-02-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<vYwB2.318$FN5.438989@news3.mia>`
- **References:** `<Pine.OSF.3.91.990225114206.5343A-100000@leofric> <7b47em$na7@dfw-ixnews3.ix.netcom.com> <7b4frg$fs8@lotho.delphi.com>`

```
paulr wrote:
>Just a little offtopic, but why do people prefer to use ADD, SUBTRACT,
>etc., rather than COMPUTE?

I know of three reasons why people may avoid COMPUTE.

 1. On some compilers COMPUTE is not very efficient.

 2. There are times when you need extra control over intermediate
    results, such as rounding.

 3. If the particular COBOL compiler's COMPUTE logic isn't great,
    you can get intermediate underflow/overflow on chained multiply
    or divide operations unless you are careful.  For example, it is
    a good idea to code B * C / D rather than B / D * C, especially
    if you are carrying the result to several decimal places.

Personally, I use whichever construct seems most appropriate for the
specific calculation.  If the calculation is a single simple operation
I usually use the operation directly rather than COMPUTE, because it
seems to make the logic more obvious.  For more complex computations,
particularly when the expression makes it clearer, I use usually use
COMPUTE, except when the reasons above apply.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Urgent Help Needed On Indexed Files I/O

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "John Hicks (Remove \\"nospam\\" from address before replying)" <johnhicks@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 1999-02-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36D75F23.D21DC02D@ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<Pine.OSF.3.91.990225114206.5343A-100000@leofric> <7b47em$na7@dfw-ixnews3.ix.netcom.com> <7b4frg$fs8@lotho.delphi.com> <vYwB2.318$FN5.438989@news3.mia>`

```
I agree with everything Judson said and would add one more point:

For simple calculations (such as the increment of the video rental count
in the problem we are considering), it is both simpler and clearer to
use ADD instead of COMPUTE.

Consider:

   add 1 to rental-cnt

   vs

   compute rental-cnt = rental-cnt + 1

So superior is the COBOL approach that most languages invented since
have been careful to follow its example:

   rental-cnt += 1

   or   

   rental-cnt++

--John Hicks
johnhicks at ibm dot net


Judson McClendon wrote:
> 
> paulr wrote:
…[26 more quoted lines elided]…
> whoever believes in Him should not perish but have everlasting life."


Judson McClendon wrote:
> 
> paulr wrote:
…[26 more quoted lines elided]…
> whoever believes in Him should not perish but have everlasting life."

<a id="t-7a8d209aa5"></a>
## Thread: Processing linked list in IMS with COBOL — 3 this month / 4 total

_Topics: [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe)_

### Processing linked list in IMS with COBOL

- **From:** HyperReal-Anon <nobody@sind.hyperreal.art.pl>
- **Date:** 1999-02-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19990225234003.29417.qmail@serek.arch.pwr.wroc.pl>`

```
Here's another question for those who speak IMS with COBOL, a bit trickier
than the last.

Suppose you had an IMS database defined to hold Bills Of Material, a common
manufacturing task. Structurally the database is made up of two segments:
  A001   root segment, made up of one field KEY-A001   pic x(12)
                 and
  A002   child segment, made up of one field KEY-A002  pic x(12)

Occurances in the database can represent one of two types: assemblies
or components. An assembly is defined as a combination of components and
subassemblies. A component cannot be made up of anything. An assembly may
itself be a subassembly of another assembly.

Thus, all A001 occurances are assemblies. A002 occurances beneath a given
A001 occurance may be subassemblies or components. 

Question: how would you go about generating an exploded Bills Of Material
report, listing each component and recursively each subassembly, given a
"top assembly" ?  

Positioning on the top assembly and GNPing would easily get all components
and subassemblies, but how would you recursively list out the subassemblies?
Keep in mind that subassemblies can be nested to an unknown, variable depth.

I can see how as you GNP you through the A002s you could test each value to
see if it existed as an A001 thus determing whether it was indeed a subassembly -
but once you do that you lose your positioning. You could stash your positioning
keys before making the test but that'd work only for one level of nexting - if
the test was positive but it too contained subassemblies you'd lose your path
back to where you started.

This is a classical problem but I'm stumped. Any clues/hints/ideas would be
greatly appreciated.



Please reply to the following address after removing "antispam" and
reversing "tom" in the following address:
antip23spam610@email.tom.com


Thanks,

Mike Striefel
Motorola SSTG
```

#### ↳ Re: Processing linked list in IMS with COBOL

- **From:** "Jeff" <a@a.com>
- **Date:** 1999-02-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<NpoB2.323$bk.148257@storm.twcol.com>`
- **References:** `<19990225234003.29417.qmail@serek.arch.pwr.wroc.pl>`

```
>I can see how as you GNP you through the A002s you could test each value to
>see if it existed as an A001 thus determing whether it was indeed a
subassembly -
>but once you do that you lose your positioning. You could stash your
positioning
>keys before making the test but that'd work only for one level of nexting -
if
>the test was positive but it too contained subassemblies you'd lose your
path
>back to where you started.
>
>This is a classical problem but I'm stumped. Any clues/hints/ideas would be
>greatly appreciated.


A second PCB would definitely be the way to go. But asuming you don't do
that, you can use the concatenated key of the PCB to determine your position
in the database. If you parse out the keys you can use GU's to reposition.
With some creative working storage tables you could stack up the keys and
pull them back out to recurse back up the tree.

Another possibility would be to just use GN's and check the status code to
dtermine when you change parents/levels.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Processing linked list in IMS with COBOL

- **From:** rkrayhawk@aol.com (RKRayhawk)
- **Date:** 1999-02-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19990226221457.19485.00000718@ng156.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<NpoB2.323$bk.148257@storm.twcol.com>`

```
The situation you have described is non-trivial, to say the least. There are
many demons lurking in this supposed simple recurse of yours.

First off, let me mention that the well intentioned, and also well phrased,
recommendations that you get a PSB with a second PCB with access to the same
data base will not solve your problem.  If your first PCB provides you access
to level-1 and level-2 of a bill of material structure, then the second PCB is
only going to give you level-2 and level-3. ((Generalized, n PCBs provide n+1
levels of the structure)).

As mentioned, you can use working storage to save off some intermediate levels,
and perhaps bounce down to level-n and level-m.  But you then need a kind of
stack push and stack pop algorthm, 

Thus with any simple program you will need limits to how deep a structure can
go (to match depth of your stack-like data structure in WS).  For class work,
that actually is achievable. You can just reject structures that are too deep. 
In the real world that will not work too well in many manufacturing
applications (or design applications).

Also, you need nice tools to attach to this innocent algorithm to detect
circles
   PART-1 ->> PART-2
   PART-2 ->> PART-3
   PART-3 ->> PART-4
   PART-4 ->> PART-2
   peg that CPU needle.

In the real world it might be better to conceive of this application as a multi
pass algorithm, spining recurse lists off to external storage rather than main
memory.

Either way you need logic that scans the list more than a pair of levels at a
time to detect problems.

It is not unusual for such part structures to be logically stratified, so that
initial scans can have some sense of how much deeper they have to go by the
strata (or depth) they have currently reached.  For example, a wing is part of
an airplane, just as a tail is: either one may have many other parts.  But a
fin is not so high-level, and a servo-motor is getting real low.

Another way to look at this is to ask what kind of B-O-Ms are really practical.
 We could list out a whole airplane, but who would read that?!

Structure scans have other issues, too - such as depth first or breadth first
explosion, left-to-right or vice versa.

Some times your key sequencing, as in the ordinal values of your A001 and A002
segments will determine this (or limit your options).

Anyway, you may need depth limit, and breadth limit checks to prevent runaway
sequences. Even small databases can fill memory fast, and if you are writing
the push and pop logic in COBOL, it could take a lot of time to execute.

Try to cut it down to size, with perhaps a two pass concept: pass one could
recurse to perhaps a depth of five, marking nodes that go any deeper. Pass two
could be similar.

Try to conceive of your presentation logic in practical terms, what kind of
views of the part explosion are comprehendible.  If you have linear pages that
are indenting 35 or 40 levels deep to show structure, and the parent and
grand-parent are 17 pages back, what does that page really mean? ((That is
especially true online)).  Try to define your output in practical terms, that
will communicate useful limits back to your explosion logic.

Pass two, and presentation logic should have loop detection (that is recursive
in itself, even across a fixed number of records).  If your data had logical
stratification, you could detect faults in the second pass and / or
presentation logic: a tail should not be part of a wing (I am being simplistic
here, no doubt some one knows of a contradiction to that, this is the internet
after all). This kind of fault would be encoded in a type field or modulated
into the key field structure.

Anyway, two PCBs is not likely to be enough, although it will be a lot better
than one. A DBA would not like it, but if your pass one is designed to handle
five levels, then four PCBs would be better.

Also note that you can use logical relationships (in full function databases)
for this stuff as well. That logical relation ship can point back to roots,
such that you would have a 
structure of 

  A001
     :
  A002 / A001 concatenated segment

that will require even more support from DBA. When pros implement B-O-Ms with
logical relations, they try real hard to prevent loops or structure faults from
ever getting recorded, which is supposed to relieve scanners (parts exploders)
from having to check for that.  Some times applications have loop/fault seekers
(that do clean up logic).

Yet it is true that your scanner can still encounter them, especially if trying
an all out explosion in a single phase scan.  Early implementations, and
student implementations, would do best if they have artificially forced limits
on depth, breadth, and total parts per single parent.

Before an application gets to an intermediate stage of development it has some
kind of type code on segments to prevent invalid structures. The idea is that
perhaps any kind of an A002 'could' get inserted under an A001, but that does
not mean that you will chase it.
 
Once you get accustomed to that idea, you realize easily that the notion that
we should only present to a certain level (or in a certain manner) for a
presentation to certain viewers, is just a special case of not chasing
inappropriate siblings.  So you can avoid a chase if it is not appropriate for
the current presentation view, or not appropriate at all.


Best Wishes,













Robert Rayhawk
RKRayhawk@aol.com
```

### Problem in EXAMINE verb

- **From:** chowdhury@vsnl.com
- **Date:** 1999-02-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7b35nk$e2l$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
I'm using MS-COBOL 5.0 on a i486DX machine. I've created the following
program:

       IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
       PROGRAM-ID. L3P30.

       ENVIRONMENT DIVISION.

       DATA DIVISION.
       WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
       77  A  PIC X(5) VALUE is "EERIE".

       PROCEDURE DIVISION.
       PARA1.
           EXAMINE A TALLYING ALL "E".


       IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
       PROGRAM-ID. L3P30.

       ENVIRONMENT DIVISION.

       DATA DIVISION.
       WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
       77  A                 PIC X(5) VALUE is "EERIE".

       PROCEDURE DIVISION.
       PARA1.
    EXAMINE a TALLYING ALL "E".

My problem is that this program when compiled gives the following message

Microsoft (R) COBOL Optimizing Compiler Version 5.0

COBOL software by Micro Focus
Copyright (C) Microsoft Corporation 1984,1993. All rights reserved.
Copyright (C) Micro Focus Ltd. 1984,1993. All rights reserved.

Object file-name [L3P30.OBJ]:
Source listing [NUL]:
Object listing [NUL]:
    12 EXAMINE a TALLYING ALL "E".
* 641-S************* **
** '.' missing following procedure name declaration, or unrecognized verb
* Checking complete - errors found

I've read in a book that the identifier used in EXAMINE must be having a
DISPLAY USAGE. I tried that also but still not possible to remove the error.

Also how will i display the result (book says result stored in TALLY register;
what's it? and how to use it ?)

I'd be extremely thank full to anyone for an urgent solution.

Thanks

-----------== Posted via Deja News, The Discussion Network ==----------
http://www.dejanews.com/       Search, Read, Discuss, or Start Your Own
```

#### ↳ Re: Problem in EXAMINE verb

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@inospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-02-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7b38ut$blu@sjx-ixn10.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<7b35nk$e2l$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
In order to use the EXAMINE verb, your compiler must be set to support a
dialect with it.  (I think that with MS COBOL 5.0 - this would be either
OSVS or MF.)  Have you chosen one of those dialects?  If not, it won't
understand the EXAMINE verb at all.

The TALLY special register is also an extension that is only available with
certain dialects set. You should just be able to use it as a sending field
after the EXAMINE statement.

NOTE: You may well want to look at the INSPECT verb instead of the EXAMINE
verb - so you can get more "portable" COBOL code.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Problem in EXAMINE verb

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com>
- **Date:** 1999-02-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36D55E03.63227035@NOSPAMhome.com>`
- **References:** `<7b35nk$e2l$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <7b38ut$blu@sjx-ixn10.ix.netcom.com>`

```
> NOTE: You may well want to look at the INSPECT verb instead of the EXAMINE
> verb - so you can get more "portable" COBOL code.

Are there compilers which accept both words?  ANSI 68 uses one and ANSI
72 uses the other, while both use TRANSFORM which went away later.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Problem in EXAMINE verb

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@inospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-02-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7b474f$n1d@dfw-ixnews3.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<7b35nk$e2l$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <7b38ut$blu@sjx-ixn10.ix.netcom.com> <36D55E03.63227035@NOSPAMhome.com>`

```
Howard Brazee wrote in message <36D55E03.63227035@NOSPAMhome.com>...
>> NOTE: You may well want to look at the INSPECT verb instead of the
EXAMINE
>> verb - so you can get more "portable" COBOL code.
>
>Are there compilers which accept both words?  ANSI 68 uses one and ANSI
>72 uses the other, while both use TRANSFORM which went away later.

You bet,
   Micro Focus accepts both in the same program (you can actually use the
TRANSFORM statement in the same routine as an intrinsic function - or even
in an object oriented program).  I am NOT recommending this,  just saying
that they NEVER take anything away - unless it directly "conflicts" with a
newer feature.
```

#### ↳ Re: Problem in EXAMINE verb

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1999-02-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<YlcB2.3767$LN5.5322845@news2.mia>`
- **References:** `<7b35nk$e2l$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
chowdhury@vsnl.com wrote:
>I'm using MS-COBOL 5.0 on a i486DX machine. I've created the following
>program:
…[26 more quoted lines elided]…
>    EXAMINE a TALLYING ALL "E".
     ^
Should start in Area B (column 12).  Because the sentence starts in
Area A, the compiler thinks 'EXAMINE' is a paragraph (procedure name)
and is complaining that you left out the period.  Note the message:

>    12 EXAMINE a TALLYING ALL "E".
>* 641-S************* **
>** '.' missing following procedure name declaration, or unrecognized verb
>* Checking complete - errors found

If 'EXAMINE' starts to the left of Area A, you could get into a whole
host of other possible error messages.  When I cut and pasted your
program and compiled it with MF COBOL 3.2 (a close relative of MS COBOL
5.0), I got a lot of errors, but the first one flagged 'EXAMINE' with
"Missing Period".
```

## Thread: Need assistance with CICS to HP PCL printer problem — 3 msgs

_Topics: [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe)_

### Need assistance with CICS to HP PCL printer problem

- **From:** slider_rj@my-dejanews.com
- **Date:** 1999-02-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7b51fc$4et$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
I need to change my CICS COBOL program to print a document on an HP 4 Plus
Laser.	It currently prints the document on an old IBM 4019 laser.  I removed
the old IBM PCL (hexadecimal) string (which worked fine) and replaced it with
the HP PCL (hexadecimal) string to access the lower paper tray.  It appears
that the HP did not recognize the string or the PCL command was garbled up
somehow as it passed thru CICS---> VTAM (DRS,VPS) ---> HP printer.  The
printer appears to print the PCL string rather than execute the desired
command.

Note:  I'm using PCL found in an HP LaserJect 4Si Printer User's Reference
Manual.  I'm assuming that the HP 4 Plus LaserJet uses the same PCL code. . .
. Right???

The set up I used in my CICS program is as follows:

WS-TRAY    PIC   X(5)   VALUE   X'1B266C3448'     (Lower Tray PCL Hex Code)
WS-TRAY-LENGTH   PIC  S9(04)  COMP VALUE +5

EXEC CICS SEND TEXT
    FROM (WS-TRAY)
    LENGTH(WS_TRAY_LENGTH)
    ACCUM
    PAGING
    RESP (RESPONSE CODE)
END-EXEC.

Any assistance or suggestions as to why the HP PCL string doesn't work would
be greatly appreciated.

Thanks in advance.

Kathy

-----------== Posted via Deja News, The Discussion Network ==----------
http://www.dejanews.com/       Search, Read, Discuss, or Start Your Own
```

#### ↳ Re: Need assistance with CICS to HP PCL printer problem

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1999-02-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36d61c2b.92844609@news1.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<7b51fc$4et$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
On Fri, 26 Feb 1999 02:38:37 GMT, slider_rj@my-dejanews.com wrote:

>I need to change my CICS COBOL program to print a document on an HP 4 Plus
>Laser.	It currently prints the document on an old IBM 4019 laser.  I removed
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Sounds like the ESC character X"1b" on the PC is being lost.  Can you
check your ASCII/EBCDIC translation to ensure that the proper EBCDIC
character is being used and translated into X"1b" in ascii?
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Need assistance with CICS to HP PCL printer problem

- **From:** Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>
- **Date:** 1999-02-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36D93AD8.1AC6B38C@zip.com.au>`
- **References:** `<7b51fc$4et$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <36d61c2b.92844609@news1.ibm.net>`

```
Thane Hubbell wrote:
> 
> On Fri, 26 Feb 1999 02:38:37 GMT, slider_rj@my-dejanews.com wrote:
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> character is being used and translated into X"1b" in ascii?

Spot on:  ESC character in EBCDIC is Hex 27.  Be careful to use the
character based commands, do not use hex strings.

01 PCL-select-tray.
  10 filler  pic x(01) value x'27'.
  10 filler  pic x(04) value '&l4H'.

ampersand, lowercase l, 4 and Capital H.   I am assuming that the hex
strings were correct.

Ken
```

<a id="t-9ff938abb3"></a>
## Thread: comunications — 8 this month / 10 total

### comunications

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1999-02-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7b6d6f$mir$1@news.igs.net>`

```
This is not a Cobol problem, but maybe some people in here can
help anyway.

I have a flat ASCII file, (Cobol produced, no binary) that I have to
move from one PC to another.  The existing system does it using
ProComm scripts under DOS, then shells, and runs a program (also
Cobol) that merges it with a master input file.  Data is received from
several locations.  This is very low volume stuff, and only done once
per day, but it has to be done over a modem, and it must be completely
automatic.

It will not work under windows, even in a DOS box.  I would like to
replace it in the simplest way possible.  I thought of using dial in
networking, and a batch file, but I cannot seem to find any way for
windows95 to answer the phone.  Does anybody have a simple
solution?
```

#### ↳ Re: comunications

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 1999-02-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<HEzB2.3$gY4.9@news19.ispnews.com>`
- **References:** `<7b6d6f$mir$1@news.igs.net>`

```

Donald Tees wrote in message <7b6d6f$mir$1@news.igs.net>...
>This is not a Cobol problem, but maybe some people in here can
>help anyway.
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>It will not work under windows, even in a DOS box.

I assume you mean the ProComm scripts will not work.

>  I would like to
>replace it in the simplest way possible.  I thought of using dial in
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Use VB 5 (or 6). Communications is fairly straight forward (no API
calls necessary) and it can launch the COBOL program (DOS or
Windows) when the file(s) have been successfully transferred.

Place the program in the StartUp folder.

The downside is that the Com Port will not be available for any
other applications.
-------------------------------
Rick Smith
e-mail: < ricksmith@aiservices.com >
```

#### ↳ Re: comunications

- **From:** paulr@bix.com (paulr)
- **Date:** 1999-02-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7b6gvt$5nn@lotho.delphi.com>`
- **References:** `<7b6d6f$mir$1@news.igs.net>`

```

Well, you have several options actually. The simplest option 
may be to simply use NT at your central site, and let all the 
remote machines dial into it. (The NT machine supports remote
dial in very easily.)  

You can then just have a batch file on the remote systems that
copies the datafiles to a 'hot folder' on the NT system. 
(literraly - 
  copy c:\mylocalfile //name-of-nt-server/shared-folder/filename)
)

Or you can get sophisticated and use something like NDm (Connect
Direct), or you can get slick and write a C or VB program that
opens a socket and accepts the information directly. 

I recommend *against* doing this out of COBOL, though there 
are many PC COBOL implementations that can do this. The reason is
that I don' trust PC COBOL's to keep up in the Windows / NT world,
but almost all the C compilers will, and VB is very good for 
doing things like this. (Gee- it comes out to something like 
15 or 20 lines of code... just the right size for a BASIC program. :) 

But the key to all of that working very simply is just to use NT
as your server. (You could use OS/2, but probably not politically
viable. :) Heck, you can even use secure / non secure internet 
connections with the NT machine at that point. Just make sure it
has a nice stable internet address...

-Paul

: per day, but it has to be done over a modem, and it must be completely
: automatic.

: It will not work under windows, even in a DOS box.  I would like to
: replace it in the simplest way possible.  I thought of using dial in
: networking, and a batch file, but I cannot seem to find any way for
: windows95 to answer the phone.  Does anybody have a simple
: solution?
```

- **From:** paulr@bix.com (paulr)
- **Date:** 1999-02-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7b6p80$s4m@lotho.delphi.com>`
- **References:** `<7b6d6f$mir$1@news.igs.net> <7b6gvt$5nn@lotho.delphi.com>`

```
Oh, and I forgot, you can do dial in networking with Windows 95,
but the software to do it is on the Microsoft PLUS disk for Windows
95. Again, NT Workstation is probably your best bet here....

#### ↳ Re: comunications

- **From:** "Ramon Gonzalez" <kp4tr@leading.net>
- **Date:** 1999-02-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7b86hr$t4p@news2.southeast.net>`
- **References:** `<7b6d6f$mir$1@news.igs.net>`

```
I can suggest innumerable methods of capturing this file, but Ill suggest
this:

1. Disable the MODEM/COM port from Win 95 and let PROCOMM for DOS talk
directly to the MODEM/COM port. Procomm for DOS wants to talk directly to
the MODEM/COM port, not thru Win 95 drivers.
2. Get Procomm Plus for Windows and migrate your ASPECT scripts.
3. Get a cheap 486 PC, install DOS, and dedicate this PC for this job. Have
this PC connect thru a newtork card (Novell or LAN Manager) to the PC where
you will do your file crunching.

A more sophisticated way is to install Linux and use (PPP) to establish a
TCP/IP connection, then FTP your files to Linux, and use SAMBA between Linux
and Win 95 over a network.


Donald Tees wrote in message <7b6d6f$mir$1@news.igs.net>...
>This is not a Cobol problem, but maybe some people in here can
>help anyway.
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

#### ↳ Re: comunications

- **From:** "Ramon Gonzalez" <kp4tr@leading.net>
- **Date:** 1999-02-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7b86ki$t4s@news2.southeast.net>`
- **References:** `<7b6d6f$mir$1@news.igs.net>`

```
I can suggest innumerable methods of capturing this file, but Ill suggest
this:

1. Disable the MODEM/COM port from Win 95 and let PROCOMM for DOS talk
directly to the MODEM/COM port. Procomm for DOS wants to talk directly to
the MODEM/COM port, not thru Win 95 drivers.
2. Get Procomm Plus for Windows and migrate your ASPECT scripts.
3. Get a cheap 486 PC, install DOS, and dedicate this PC for this job. Have
this PC connect thru a newtork card (Novell or LAN Manager) to the PC where
you will do your file crunching.

A more sophisticated way is to install Linux and use (PPP) to establish a
TCP/IP connection, then FTP your files to Linux, and use SAMBA between Linux
and Win 95 over a network.


Donald Tees wrote in message <7b6d6f$mir$1@news.igs.net>...
>This is not a Cobol problem, but maybe some people in here can
>help anyway.
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

- **Date:** 1999-02-27T00:00:00

- **Message-ID:** `<7b86m2$t7o@news2.southeast.net>`
- **References:** `<7b6d6f$mir$1@news.igs.net>`

## Thread: Web Site for Tools? — 2 msgs

_Topics: [`Web, XML, modern integration`](topics.md#web)_

### Web Site for Tools?

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 1999-02-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7b6gtr$8nn$1@news-1.news.gte.net>`

```
2/26/99

I have, over the past several years, produced two FREE tools for COBOL
programmers. While these are available on Compuserve, I would like to make
them more broadly available by posting them "somewhere" on the Internet.

Can someone suggest a suitable for site or sites for me to contact?

Here are the summaries of the files as they appear on Compuserve:

COBDATA.ZIP
Text and graphics introduction to COBOL numeric data types. Designed for
intermediate to advanced programmers not familiar with the COBOL language or
COBOL numeric data storage. Windows(r) Write or compatible word processor
recommended (but ASCII text file included). Includes executable program file
as well as PB 3.2 source code. Copyright but free. From Michael Mattias.
A compiler is NOT required to use this product.

COBFD.ZIP
Tal Systems' COBFD COBOL FD Analyzer v 1.40 (Nov '98). Freeware for
PC/MS-DOS;
parses COBOL source or copylibs and reports the size and offset within each
'01' level of
all datanames (group and elementary). Supports any level OCCURS/REDEFINES
and most
specialty compiler directives. EXE, documentation and demo file included.
Version 1.40 adds
command-line options for use in batch files. Conversions, sort keys, new
record definitions:
Why do it by hand? From Michael Mattias.


Thanks in advance,
Michael Mattias dba Tal Systems
Racine WI USA

PS: I'm still kind of new to the newsgroup software, so if you want to
really make sure I get your message, please send e-mail.
```

### Cobol data file

- **From:** "xpto" <xpto@xpto.pt>
- **Date:** 1999-02-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7b6q3g$vfk$1@duke.telepac.pt>`

```
I would like to know if anyone have or knows a tool that, giving a cobol
datafile, prints the structure of the file.

Jos� Oliveira
```

#### ↳ Re: Cobol data file

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com>
- **Date:** 1999-02-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36D6F9DC.B224C5DC@NOSPAMhome.com>`
- **References:** `<7b6q3g$vfk$1@duke.telepac.pt>`

```
xpto wrote:
> 
> I would like to know if anyone have or knows a tool that, giving a cobol
> datafile, prints the structure of the file.
> 
> Josï¿½ Oliveira

I am trying to figure out what you're wanting.  What is "a cobol
datafile"?  To me, this is a file with data in it.  It could have been
created by anything but COBOL programs can access it.  Since COBOL has
no delimiters, there's no way to determine where a field starts or ends
except by asking whoever wrote that file.

Now if you mean it reads a cobol program to determine the structure -
then I would print the COBOL program.  COBOL displays the structure
quite nicely.
```

## Thread: COBOL Data ----> DB4? — 8 this month / 15 total

### COBOL Data ----> DB4?

- **From:** "Kevin Gagnon" <kmg@connectnet.com>
- **Date:** 1999-02-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01be625b$69edf120$fb02fccc@kevin-laptop>`

```
I am tasked with TRYING to pull data from an existing COBOL program
(programmer unknown) and convert it to a DB4 database.  I know nothing
about a COBOL data structure.  How is the data stored..... Customers,
Invoices..... etc?


Is it possible to pull data from a COBOL program and map into a DB4
structure??
```

#### ↳ Re: COBOL Data ----> DB4?

- **From:** scomstock@aol.com (S Comstock)
- **Date:** 1999-02-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19990227102530.15313.00001026@ng-fv1.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<01be625b$69edf120$fb02fccc@kevin-laptop>`

```
Kevin Gagnon writes ...

>I am tasked with TRYING to pull data from an existing COBOL program
>(programmer unknown) and convert it to a DB4 database.  I know nothing
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>structure??

Sorry, but what is DB4?

Regards,



Steve Comstock
Telephone: 303-393-8716
www.trainersfriend.com
email: steve@trainersfriend.com
256-B S. Monaco Parkway
Denver, CO 80224
USA
```

#### ↳ Re: COBOL Data ----> DB4?

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 1999-02-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7b9ask$7gm$2@news-2.news.gte.net>`
- **References:** `<01be625b$69edf120$fb02fccc@kevin-laptop>`

```
It doesn't matter what the target database is: you must have the source data
record layout.

COBOL does not define what is stored in various files (customers, invoices,
etc); that is defined by the system designer, as are all the field
definitions (is this data alpha? Numeric? Packed? Binary? BCD?).

There is virtually no way to solve your problem - regardless of the target -
unless you can obtain the record layouts of the files. These are called
"File Descriptions" (FD's) in COBOL, and are either in the COBOL source code
of the application or in what is called a "copylib."

If you have the COBOL source, any quality COBOL consultant should be able to
help you extract the data. (He, a quality COBOL consultant, said, with no
hint of modesty).

Without those definitions,you, sir, are well and truly up Fecal Matter Creek
sans paddle.

MCM


Kevin Gagnon wrote in message <01be625b$69edf120$fb02fccc@kevin-laptop>...
>I am tasked with TRYING to pull data from an existing COBOL program
>(programmer unknown) and convert it to a DB4 database.  I know nothing
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL Data ----> DB4?

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@inospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-02-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7b9ghb$3uk@sjx-ixn6.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<01be625b$69edf120$fb02fccc@kevin-laptop> <7b9ask$7gm$2@news-2.news.gte.net>`

```
I would add one thing to the "attached" note.  You also need to know what
COMPILER was used.  (You also need to be aware that the actual file
description - as it is broken down into specific fields of different types -
may be in the Working-Storage of your program not just under the FD.

Bottom-line:
  You need to have someone who knows COBOL, can find the source code, and
knows what compiler was used.

There is a "trial and error" method if your COBOL program still "runs" and
you can control (via known input) what data is in the output - however, this
is pretty error-prone and fairly unlikely to work well.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL Data ----> DB4?

- **From:** "PAULO CRUZ" <scruz_paulo@mail.teleweb.pt>
- **Date:** 1999-02-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7b9rel$d4q$1@fenix.maxitel.pt>`

```
Am I wrong or have already heard about cobol utilities that, given a
Cobol file, can map out its description in terms of size of records,
how many records in the file, how may keys, their starting
positions and lenghts ?
Still, as the posters said, without knowing the cobol trademark and
at least the non-key fields arrangements, it'll be a tough task - but
an impossible one ?
Rgds
P.Cruz

>description - as it is broken down into specific fields of different
types -

>Bottom-line:
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…

>>Without those definitions,you, sir, are well and truly up Fecal Matter

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL Data ----> DB4?

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@inospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-02-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7ba2bh$7ut@dfw-ixnews12.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<01be625b$69edf120$fb02fccc@kevin-laptop> <7b9ask$7gm$2@news-2.news.gte.net> <7b9ghb$3uk@sjx-ixn6.ix.netcom.com> <7b9rel$d4q$1@fenix.maxitel.pt>`

```
I think that the recent posts have been talking about products that can take
an FD (or Working-Storage record description) and produce a
COBOL-independent "map".  There are also some products (like file-aid) that
take the record description and let you edit the file intelligently.
However, I don't recall (but could be wrong) any product that will "read the
file" and produce a field mapping -  I just don't see how that would be
possible.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL Data ----> DB4?

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 1999-02-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<yt2C2.11$n12.79@news19.ispnews.com>`
- **References:** `<01be625b$69edf120$fb02fccc@kevin-laptop> <7b9ask$7gm$2@news-2.news.gte.net> <7b9ghb$3uk@sjx-ixn6.ix.netcom.com> <7b9rel$d4q$1@fenix.maxitel.pt>`

```

PAULO CRUZ wrote in message <7b9rel$d4q$1@fenix.maxitel.pt>...
>Am I wrong or have already heard about cobol utilities that, given a
>Cobol file, can map out its description in terms of size of records,
>how many records in the file, how may keys, their starting
>positions and lenghts ?

Some indexed file rebuild utilities supply that type of data. However,
it may not supply information about the composition of the key fields.

>Still, as the posters said, without knowing the cobol trademark and
>at least the non-key fields arrangements, it'll be a tough task - but
>an impossible one ?

The level of difficulty varies inversely with the amount of experience
of the programmer and the programmer's knowledge of the COBOL
implementation and the application. With a starting point of
"know(ing) nothing about COBOL data structure," the task would
appear to be nearly impossible. However, nothing was said of how
much time was available. For example, given four weeks to do work
requiring six months makes a tough task impossible. We know that
some people will not wait forever; hence, impossible seems to be
a reasonable conclusion.

[...]
>William M. Klein wrote in msg<7b9ghb$3uk@sjx-ixn6.ix.netcom.com>...
>>I would add one thing to the "attached" note.  You also need to know what
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>>knows what compiler was used.
[...]
>>Michael Mattias wrote in message <7b9ask$7gm$2@news-2.news.gte.net>...
>>>It doesn't matter what the target database is: you must have the source
>>data record layout.
[...]
>>>Kevin Gagnon wrote in message
<01be625b$69edf120$fb02fccc@kevin-laptop>...
>>>>I am tasked with TRYING to pull data from an existing COBOL program
>>>>(programmer unknown) and convert it to a DB4 database.  I know nothing
>>>>about a COBOL data structure.  How is the data stored..... Customers,
>>>>Invoices..... etc?
[...]
-------------------------------
Rick Smith
e-mail: < ricksmith@aiservices.com >
```

#### ↳ Re: COBOL Data ----> DB4?

- **From:** "Jerry Peacock" <bismail@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 1999-02-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7bd75n$at$1@birch.prod.itd.earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<01be625b$69edf120$fb02fccc@kevin-laptop>`

```

Kevin Gagnon wrote in message <01be625b$69edf120$fb02fccc@kevin-laptop>...

>Is it possible to pull data from a COBOL program and map into a DB4
>structure??


If you are trying to decode a FILE without access to the original program
source code (and probably the compiler), you will fail. A COBOL program
has no pre-defined output nor can the output be determined as with a
DB2 file. A COBOL program can read, modify, then output an EXE file,
for example. Or the program can output a simple ASCII text file. No rhyme,
no reason.

If, OTOH, you have the program listing, with a COBOL manual, you should
be able to ferret out the basic file layout. Then, in combination with
knowledge of how the particular compiler represents certain data (mostly
numbers - and there are many ways to represent numbers), you may
be able to deduce the actual innards of the file in question.

Next is the matter of writing a conversion program to massage the
suspect file into the format you desire. This may prove quite messy
for some data types unsupported in the language of the translating
program (i.e., packed decimal).

Altogether, a dark and thankless job.
```

<a id="t-203203665a"></a>
## Thread: Test your knowledge of CICS at new site! — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe) · [`Meta: FAQs, group policy, charter`](topics.md#meta)_

### Test your knowledge of CICS at new site!

- **From:** Michael Littman <mikelittman@erols.com>
- **Date:** 1999-02-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.databases.ibm-db2,alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36D81B85.3D37D87A@erols.com>`

```
Dear CICS user,

We are currently beta testing our web-based 'CICS' knowledge test.  The
format is multiple choice.  To take the test, simply go to
www.tekmetrics.com and select the link, "Beta Testers", along the bottom
left side of the page.  The rest should be self-explanatory.  Before
taking the test, check out
the 'top ten' list to see the best scores to date!

Upon completion of the test, you will be scored 1-5 (1-Novice,
2-Beginner, 3-Intermediate, 4-Advanced and 5-Master) and be provided the
percentile that you fell into, based on the others who took the test.
The test is actually comprised of two tests of 16 questions each (that's
two chances to make the top ten!).  Any feedback you can provide us on
the quality of the questions and the test itself would be greatly
appreciated.  You will be given the opportunity to provide feedback on
each question as it is administered as well as at the end of the test.

The test is delivered in our production environment, so things might
seem a little 'strange' prior to taking the test.  However, any
information you provide will be kept anonymous
(unless you make the top ten - we figured the bragging rights would make
it worth it!).

If you have any problems getting to the site, or have any questions in
general, please feel free to contact me.  We hope that you enjoy the
test and look forward to your feedback!

Also, feel free to try out any of the other beta tests while you're
there.  Thanks in advance for your help and good luck!

Mike
```

<a id="t-3a72515112"></a>
## Thread: Overpunch, versions? — 2 this month / 6 total

### Overpunch, versions?

- **From:** bkwilson@isoc.net
- **Date:** 1999-02-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7b9os6$2bf$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
I wrote a program that converts overpunched numbers to signed numbers for a
Pick system, however, my brother warned me that there might be different
versions of overpunching.  The rules I found convert the last digit for both
positive ((0-9 = {,A-I ) and negative numbers (0-9= },J-R).

What are the different versions of overpunching?

How can I tell them apart?

What formula's am I missing?

Thanks,
Ken Wilson
bkwilson@isoc.net

-----------== Posted via Deja News, The Discussion Network ==----------
http://www.dejanews.com/       Search, Read, Discuss, or Start Your Own
```

#### ↳ Re: Overpunch, versions?

- **From:** "Leif Svalgaard" <lsvalg@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 1999-02-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36d89eea@news3.us.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<7b9os6$2bf$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
on some systems (e.g. AS/400) positive numbers are not overpunched.
Negative numbers are overpunched with J thru R.

both

<a id="t-a031b790f9"></a>
## Thread: Processing linked list in IMS with COBOL — 2 this month / 6 total

_Topics: [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe)_

### Re: Processing linked list in IMS with COBOL

- **From:** HyperReal-Anon <nobody@sind.hyperreal.art.pl>
- **Date:** 1999-02-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19990227231005.16140.qmail@serek.arch.pwr.wroc.pl>`
- **References:** `<36d5fffe.47764401@news>`

```
>> Here's another question for those who speak IMS with COBOL, a bit
>> trickier than the last.

[snipped]

> Many more of these IMS questions, and you'll need to supply a
> purchase order for our expertise :)

I gladly would, if my PHB wasn't so cheap. I should post the email he
sent out mandating the stockroom order only blue pens because other
colors are more expensive. 

I'm not kidding! :-/
 
> Actually, there are several possibilities, but the simplest one is to
> ask your DBA to gen your PSB with *two* PCBs for the database. Use
> the first PCB to GN through the database, and the second PCB for
> GU'ing any subassemblies found.

Oh yeah, our DBA, she got laid off two RIFs ago. Did I mention I'm the
only one left here who even knows how to compile a COBOL program here?

Whining aside, I can see how two PCBs would work for the case where
nesting is only one layer deep, but what about the case with more
layers? Seems to me you'd need a PCB for each layer and this means you
must guess in advance the number of layers you'll find.

Another way might be to create an array in working storage to hold
concatenated keys and use it like a FIFO stack - while GNPing if an
A002 is also an A001 push its c.k. onto the stack. Once GNP ends with
GE pop a c.k. off stack and load into SSA-A001, do GU then begin GNPing
again - this would be recursive and only require one PCB.

For example, given a BOM where each letter is an assembly and each assembly
is made up of some subassemblies (shown) and detail components (not shown).

                      | A
                      |
       _______________|________________________________
      | B       | C        | D         | E             | F      
      |         |          |           |               |
 _____|__      _|___      _|_____      |_______      __|______
| G      | H  | I   | J  | K     | L   | M     | N  | O       | P

To traverse the BOM, first load A into the FIFO stack.

Then call an outer loop (PERFORM -- UNTIL paragraph) that pops a key off
the FIFO stack, GU's it for positioning then calls inner loop PERFORM.
The inner loop PERFORM repetitively GNPs through each A002 occurance.
(now for the beauty part!) and if the A002 occurance is itself an assembly
pushs that key onto the stack.

By my thinking, after processing A, the stack contents should be:

	B - C - D - E - F

pop off B and process again, at end stack contents should be:

	G - H - C - D - E - F

pop off G and process again, at end stack contents should be:

	H - C - D - E - F

and so on and so on until stack is empty.

This would need only one PCB and one FIFO stack to work.

So of course the next question is, how do I implement such a FIFO stack
in COBOL?

Thank you for your response and help. 


Please reply to the following address after removing "antispam" and
reversing "tom" in the following address:
antip23spam610@email.tom.com


Thanks,

Mike Striefel
Motorola SSTG
```

<a id="t-ee43de6dcd"></a>
## Thread: Errors in Cobol for MVS — 2 msgs

_Topics: [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe)_

### Errors in Cobol for MVS

- **From:** Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>
- **Date:** 1999-02-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36D92D71.5FB3673A@zip.com.au>`

```
Bill you offered some help a while ago eliminating some messages for me. 

3825  IGYPS2168-W   "CORRESPONDING" was specified, but subordinate item 
"FILLER" within "TS-DATE" did not qualify according to the rules for the
"CORRESPONDING" phrase.  Subordinate item "FILLER" was ignored.

253  IGYGR1216-I   A "RECORDING MODE" of "F" was assumed for file
"CDR-FILE"

I did state that the recording mode message was a warning.  It is only
an informational.  It would be nice to eliminate this one totally as it
is 'outside the standard'.

I investigated a compile option FLGSTD as this one seemed promising
flagging obsoleted code.  The problem is that this flags everything
COMP-3, FUNCTION, etc.  How do you get a minimise this to truly obsolete
code

Thanks
Ken
```

#### ↳ Re: Errors in Cobol for MVS

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@inospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-02-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7bcef4$oeq@dfw-ixnews10.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<36D92D71.5FB3673A@zip.com.au>`

```
OK, let's do the easy part first. the FLAGSTD compiler option is the only
way to get OBSOLETE (as defined in the '85 Standard) features flagged.
UNfortunately, it is designed to ALWAYS flag IBM extensions - even if what
you really want is just to see obsolete items.

As for the I-level message for RECORDING MODE F being assumed, what I would
suggest is to "normally" compile with the FLAG(W,W) compiler option and NOT
to get the I-level messages.  I would suggest that you do at least one
compile with FLAG(I) before moving to production - just in case there is
actually something "interesting" - but I have never heard of an I-level IBM
message that would actually get me to change my code.

As for the W-level message on the CORRESPONDING, the obvious (but probably
unworkable) solution is to change to explicit individual MOVE statements.
This fits in with the general "I hate MOVE CORRESPONDING" sentiment in the
world - and the W-level message actually does point out something that COULD
be a maintenance problem.  However, I will pass this note on to someone else
who may have some other ideas on what to do about it.
```

<a id="t-597b810603"></a>
## Thread: Sorts - who internal VS external — 2 msgs

### Sorts - who internal VS external

- **From:** Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>
- **Date:** 1999-02-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36D92F5D.E3EEE484@zip.com.au>`

```
The results were not what I expected.  However I only received 5
responses directly and 2 on the newsgroup.

PCs everyone said they used internal.  No real surprise here as there is
not a decent external SORT tool available.

VMS (1 response)  used a mixture of internal and external sorts.

MVS all said that they basically did both with a internals being common.

I must admit that the use of the internal sort in MVS was a little
surprising.  I have worked at a few shops and they all had a standard of
no internal sorts.  They considered it easier to debug with an external
sort and it gives the process a divide and conquer approach.

Thanks to all for their 2 cents.
Ken
```

#### ↳ Re: Sorts - who internal VS external

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@inospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-02-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7bcf6d$sti@sjx-ixn6.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<36D92F5D.E3EEE484@zip.com.au>`

```
I don't remember reading the "survey" - but I did want to point out when it
comes to IBM mainframes, the FASTSORT compiler  option (available with VS
COBOL II and beyond) has really turned around a lot of shops. Most of the
shops that still don't like/want internal SORTs - don't know about this
option (and that it works with both DFSort and SyncSort - and can be set as
a shop default without programmers even needing to worry about it).
```

## Thread: Illegal File name Problem — 3 this month / 4 total

### Illegal File name Problem

- **From:** "Jim" <jimyt@REMOVEMEyahoo.com>
- **Date:** 1999-02-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<2ngC2.25133$yv3.12227@news2.giganews.com>`

```
OS: MS Windows 98
Compiler: Microfocus Animator 2

Dear Group,

I am having problems with a file in the following program, the error I am
getting suggests that I have illegal characters in the file name
"CUSTMAST.MF". I have tried renaming the file, rewriting the code, changing
case etc but an unable to find an answer, please help.

Error: illegal character in filename (004)

Relevant sections of code:

----------------------------------------------------------------------------
--------------
000050 FILE-CONTROL.
000060     SELECT SORTED-FILE ASSIGN TO "MT25SD.DAT"
000070         ORGANIZATION IS LINE SEQUENTIAL.
000090     SELECT CUSTOMER-MASTER ASSIGN TO "CUSTMAST.MF"
000100         ORGANIZATION IS LINE SEQUENTIAL.
000090     SELECT NEW-CUST-MASTER ASSIGN TO "MT25NF.DAT"
000100         ORGANIZATION IS LINE SEQUENTIAL.
               SELECT STOCK-MASTER-FILE ASSIGN TO "STCKMAST.IDX"
                   ORGANIZATION IS INDEXED
                   ACCESS MODE RANDOM
                   RECORD KEY IS  S-PART-NUM.
000080    SELECT PRINT-FILE ASSIGN TO PRINTER.
----------------------------------------------------------------------------
----------------
001551 INITIAL-ROUTINE.
001560     OPEN INPUT  CUSTOMER-MASTER    *error reported here*
                       SORTED-FILE
                       STOCK-MASTER-FILE
001570     OUTPUT NEW-CUST-MASTER
001580                 PRINT-FILE
001590     PERFORM PRINT-REPORT-HDGS
001621     PERFORM READ-SORTED-FILE
               PERFORM READ-MASTER-FILE.


Thanks

Jim.
```

#### ↳ Re: Illegal File name Problem

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@inospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-02-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7bcdrn$o2a@dfw-ixnews10.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<2ngC2.25133$yv3.12227@news2.giganews.com>`

```
I can't tell for certain, but what column does the quote at the end of
"CUSTMAST.MF" get coded in.  It looks like you MIGHT be over column 72 (or
even 80 - depending on where you start).  There are ways to specify "free
format" source code, but it doesn't look to me like you have done that
either.
```

#### ↳ Re: Illegal File name Problem

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 1999-02-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<40kC2.3$iZ5.350@news19.ispnews.com>`
- **References:** `<2ngC2.25133$yv3.12227@news2.giganews.com>`

```

Jim wrote in message <2ngC2.25133$yv3.12227@news2.giganews.com>...
>OS: MS Windows 98
>Compiler: Microfocus Animator 2
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>---------------------------------------------------------------------------
-
[...]
>               SELECT STOCK-MASTER-FILE ASSIGN TO "STCKMAST.IDX"

IDX suffix cannot be used for an indexed file in Micro Focus.
"filename.IDX" is used by Micro Focus to contain the actual index
to the corresponding data file.

>                   ORGANIZATION IS INDEXED
[...]
>001560     OPEN INPUT  CUSTOMER-MASTER    *error reported here*
>                       SORTED-FILE
>                       STOCK-MASTER-FILE
The error is reported on the OPEN statement, any of the files
could be the source of the problem. In this case, it is the last one.
[...]
-------------------------------
Rick Smith
e-mail: < ricksmith@aiservices.com >
```

## Thread: Germans Find Pentium III Flaw - Your privacy - Expires 2/28/99 — 1 msgs

### Germans Find Pentium III Flaw - Your privacy - Expires 2/28/99

- **From:** swintel@badchips.com (Boycott Swintel)
- **Date:** 1999-02-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.groupware.lotus-notes.programmer,comp.lang.basic.visual.misc,comp.lang.c,comp.lang.clarion,comp.lang.cobol,comp.lang.javascript,comp.lang.pascal.delphi.misc,comp.lang.perl.misc,comp.lang.python,comp.lang.tcl
- **Message-ID:** `<36da9c39.304681@news.earthlink.net>`

```
	Pentium III chip with the individual serial number that can
track your web surfing and buying habits can now have the ID number
turned on and off by software.  Following some links I found the
www.fightdivx.com website and noticed that they have a Intel Boycott
page with links, quotes and info on why you should boycott the
invasion of privacy Pentium III chips. Just like everyone suspected,
the ID number can be taken without a customers knowledge. Just like
cellular phone fraud, once someone has your unique ID number, they
could pose as you on the internet.  Do not be fooled by reports that
this problem is fixed because Intel disabled this feature by software
on their up coming chips.  Information is power. They want to know
your surfing and buying habits. That is what this is all about. Here
is the link to the page with the boycott info and links. 

http://www.fightdivx.com/intelboycott.htm

Also you will find a Boycott Intel screen saver and banner on their
page above. Spread it around.




Take the Pentium III Boycott Survey
http://mail.infotrieve.com/isurvey/index.cfm?vendorid=6045&formid=F0006045
```

        in module  file USER1:[MHINES1]CEX1.COB;5

%DCL-W-ACTIMAGE, error activating image CEX1.COB
-CLI-E-IMGNAME, image file VMS1$DKB100:[USER1.][MHINES1]CEX1.COB;5
-IMGACT-F-NOTNATIVE, image is not an OpenVMS Alpha image
```

#### ↳ Re: Weird error help!!!

- **From:** "Leif Svalgaard" <lsvalg@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 1999-02-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36d9e762@news3.us.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<7bchd0$l7u@portal.gmu.edu>`

```
the error is not while running the program (so the data file is
irrelevant) but during linking it. (That is what the LINK in the
error messages refers to). Check the RCRUN procedure...

Myke wrote in message <7bchd0$l7u@portal.gmu.edu>...
>I am getting this weird error in my program i would really like help with.
>The data file is set up right... First is the program, then the data file,
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>       IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.

>
>$ TYPE PDATA2.DAT
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
>%LINK-W-USRTFR, image USER1:[MHINES1]CEX1.EXE;8 has no user transfer
address
>%DCL-W-ACTIMAGE, error activating image CEX1.COB
>-CLI-E-IMGNAME, image file VMS1$DKB100:[USER1.][MHINES1]CEX1.COB;5
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

This is for anyone interested in subscribing to COBOL Developer News, a
newsletter for the COBOL development community from Fujitsu Software
Corporation?

Volume 1, 1999, covers the following topics:

* Win a Fujitsu LifeBook computer in the Best of COBOL 1999 Design
Competition
* PowerCOBOL V4L20 Beta Update
* Book Review: Teach Yourself COBOL in 24 Hours by Thane Hubbell
* User Success Story: InterNetworking Solutions Finds a Solution to Share
* Hints and Tips: Using PowerFORM � Bitmaps, Hopper Selection and Duplex
Printing.

You can download Volume 1, 1999 (as well as previous issues) at
www.adtools.com/news/newletter/index.htm.

You can subscribe to future issues of COBOL Developer News at
www.adtools.com/news/newsletter/subscribe.htm

Regards,
Fujitsu Software Corporation
mailto:cobol@adtools.com

- **Message-ID:** `<7bb3qk$sal$1@remarQ.com>`

```
A lot of folks on comp.lang.cobol have expressed interest in Fujitsu COBOL
so we thought you might like to know the following:

�Mastering COBOL� is a book that was written for those who want to gain an
in-depth understanding of COBOL, including GUI application development,
object-oriented programming, legacy code, internal data representations, and
traditional mainframe development with IMS, CICS, SQL, and JCL.

Starting April 1st (no fooling!), our prices are increasing so if you're
thinking of buying Fujitsu COBOL, you�ll want to act now. Our prices will
still be highly competitive but why pay more by delaying your purchase a
month or two? You can learn more about Fujitsu COBOL by visiting our website
at: http://www.adtools.com

Thanks as ever for your interest - we enjoy comp.lang.cobol's lively
discussion of COBOL-related topics.

Regards,
Fujitsu Software Corporation
mailto:cobol@adtools.com
```
